> 1. EMF equation (from Faraday's Law).
> 2. Voltage regulation calculation.
> 3. Critical field resistance and self-excitation condition.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DC Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT302 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES & DRIVES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** DC Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ DC MOTOR — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DC Motor (Direct Current Motor):** A DC motor is an electromechanical energy conversion device that converts **direct current (DC) electrical energy** into **mechanical energy** (rotational motion) based on the principle of **electromagnetic interaction** between a current-carrying conductor and a magnetic field. The interaction produces a **mechanical force (Lorentz Force)** that acts tangentially on the rotor conductors, generating torque.

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, a DC motor is classified as a **single-input, single-output, singly-excited electrical machine** in which the field winding (stator) and armature winding (rotor) are supplied by separate DC sources (or the same source in different configurations).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Paddle Wheel in a River"

Imagine you are sitting beside a flowing river, holding a paddle submerged in the water:

- The **flowing river** = the magnetic field ($\vec{B}$) produced by the field poles.
- The **paddle** = the current-carrying armature conductor.
- The **force on the paddle** = the **Lorentz force** ($\vec{F} = I \vec{L} \times \vec{B}$).
- The **rotational motion of the paddle wheel** = rotation of the **armature**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Just like a paddle wheel rotates faster when the water flow is stronger or the paddle is pushed harder, a DC motor rotates faster when either the magnetic field is strengthened OR the armature current is increased.**

---

## 1.3 Physical Layout of a DC Motor

A DC motor consists of **three principal subsystems**:

| Subsystem | Function | Key Components |
|-----------|----------|----------------|
| **Magnetic System (Stator / Field System)** | Produces the main working flux $\Phi$ | Field poles (North & South), Yoke, Pole shoes, Field winding |
| **Electric System (Rotor / Armature)** | Carries the current that interacts with flux | Armature core, Armature winding, Commutator |
| **Auxiliary / Commutating System** | Facilitates current collection and commutation | Brushes, Brush holders, Commutator segments |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Operating Flux Density in the air gap of a DC motor:** approximately **0.7 to 1.2 Tesla (T)**. This is a KTU examiner-favoured numerical value.

---

## 1.4 Fundamental Physical Constants & Standards

The following are **high-yield constants** for KTU numerical problems:

- **Force on a current-carrying conductor:** $F = BIL$ (Newtons, N)
- **Magnetic flux density** (B): measured in **Tesla (T) = Wb/m²**
- **Standard EMF of a single cell:** 1.5 V (general knowledge)
- **Back EMF constant** $K_E$ has units of **V·s/rad**
- **Torque constant** $K_T$ has units of **N·m/A**
- For SI units, **$K_E = K_T$** numerically.

---

## 1.5 Working Principle — Fleming's Left-Hand Rule

When a current-carrying conductor is placed in a magnetic field, it experiences a **mechanical force** whose direction is given by **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule (FLHR)**:

> **Left-Hand Rule Mnemonic — "FBI"**
> - **F** = Force (thumb)
> - **B** = Magnetic field (forefinger, North to South)
> - **I** = Current (middle finger, conventional current direction)

> [!NOTE]
> The armature winding is distributed across many slots and connected to the commutator so that **all conductors under one pole experience force in the same tangential direction**, producing a **unidirectional torque**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

The conversion of electrical energy to mechanical energy in a DC motor proceeds through the following deterministic chain:

1. **DC excitation** is applied to the field winding → a steady **working flux $\Phi$** is established across the air gap.
2. **DC supply** is fed to the armature winding (via brushes and commutator) → current $I_a$ flows through the armature conductors.
3. Each conductor in the magnetic field experiences a **Lorentz force** $F = BIL$ (or more generally, $F = \Phi Z I_a / (2\pi a)$ when summed).
4. The collective tangential forces produce a **net torque $T$** that rotates the armature.
5. As the armature rotates in the magnetic field, it **cuts the flux lines** and an EMF is **induced in the armature conductors** (Faraday's Law).
6. This induced EMF — called the **Back EMF ($E_b$)** — **opposes the applied armature voltage** (Lenz's Law). This is the cornerstone of DC motor self-regulation.
7. The **net voltage** $(V - E_b)$ drives the armature current: $I_a = (V - E_b) / R_a$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Back EMF $E_b$ is NOT a loss. It is the electrical equivalent of the mechanical energy being developed. This is a high-frequency KTU question.**

---

## 2.2 Voltage Equation of a DC Motor

Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** to the armature circuit:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

Where:
- $V$ = applied armature terminal voltage (Volts)
- $E_b$ = back EMF (Volts)
- $I_a$ = armature current (Amperes)
- $R_a$ = armature resistance (Ohms)

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60\,A}$ | Back EMF equation | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $E_b = K_E \Phi N$ | Simplified back EMF (where $K_E = \dfrac{ZP}{60A}$) | V·s/rad × Wb × rpm |
| 3 | $T = K_T \Phi I_a$ | Electromagnetic torque developed | N·m |
| 4 | $T = \dfrac{E_b I_a}{\omega}$ | Power-balance torque equation | N·m |
| 5 | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{K_E \Phi}$ | Speed equation (derived from voltage eq.) | rpm |
| 6 | $P_{dev} = E_b I_a$ | Mechanical power developed | Watts (W) |
| 7 | $P_{in} = V I_a$ | Electrical input power (armature side) | Watts (W) |
| 8 | $P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 R_a$ | Armature copper loss | Watts (W) |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Efficiency | Percentage (%) |
| 10 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Angular speed conversion | rad/s |

> Where: $\Phi$ = flux per pole (Wb), $Z$ = total armature conductors, $P$ = number of poles, $A$ = number of parallel paths, $N$ = speed in rpm, $\omega$ = angular speed in rad/s, $a$ = number of parallel paths.

---

## 2.4 Parallel Paths — A Critical Classification

| Winding Type | $A$ (parallel paths) | Used In |
|--------------|----------------------|---------|
| **Lap winding** | $A = P$ | High-current, low-voltage machines |
| **Wave winding** | $A = 2$ | High-voltage, low-current machines |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU numericals, when no winding type is specified, default to LAP winding: $A = P$.** Always state this assumption explicitly to gain partial credit.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of DC Motor Concepts

DC motors are indispensable in engineering applications requiring **precise speed control** and **high starting torque**:

- **Electric vehicles (EVs)** — Traction motors (modern BLDC variants).
- **Cranes, hoists, elevators** — Series DC motors for high starting torque.
- **Rolling mills, paper machines** — DC shunt motors for constant-speed operation.
- **Printing machinery, textile mills** — Compound DC motors for combined characteristics.
- **Servo systems, robotics, CNC machines** — DC motors (or their derivatives) in feedback control.
- **Battery-operated equipment** — Toys, portable tools, drones.

---

## 2.6 Engineering Design Insight — Why $K_E = K_T$ in SI Units

The constants $K_E$ and $K_T$ arise from the **same energy conversion process** and the **principle of conservation of energy**. In SI units, the numerical magnitude is identical because:

$$\text{Power}_{electrical} = E_b I_a = K_E \Phi N I_a$$

$$\text{Power}_{mechanical} = T \omega = K_T \Phi I_a \cdot \omega$$

Equating the two (since $E_b I_a = T\omega$):

$$K_E = K_T \quad \text{(in SI units)}$$

This is a **direct consequence of energy conservation** and is highly valued by KTU examiners as it tests conceptual understanding.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Back EMF Equation ($E_b$)

### Statement
Derive an expression for the EMF induced in a DC motor armature, given the flux per pole $\Phi$, number of poles $P$, total conductors $Z$, parallel paths $A$, and speed $N$ in rpm.

### Step 1 — Average EMF in a Single Conductor
According to **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, the average EMF induced in a single conductor cutting flux $\Phi$ per pole, rotating at $N$ rpm:

$$e_{avg} = \dfrac{\Phi \cdot P \cdot N}{60} \quad \text{(Volts per conductor per revolution)}$$

*Reasoning:* In one revolution, a conductor cuts $P$ fluxes (one under each pole). The time for one revolution is $60/N$ seconds. Therefore, the rate of flux cutting is $\Phi P N / 60$ Wb/s = V.

### Step 2 — Number of Conductors in Series per Parallel Path
In any DC armature winding, the $Z$ conductors are distributed among $A$ parallel paths. The number of conductors connected **in series per path** is:

$$Z_{series} = \dfrac{Z}{A}$$

### Step 3 — Total EMF per Parallel Path
Since the conductors within a single path are in series, the EMF across one path is the sum of EMFs of all series conductors:

$$E_{path} = e_{avg} \times Z_{series} = \dfrac{\Phi P N}{60} \times \dfrac{Z}{A}$$

### Step 4 — EMF Across the Brushes (= Armature EMF)
The brushes are connected in parallel to all $A$ parallel paths, so the **terminal EMF is the EMF of one path**:

$$E_b = \dfrac{\Phi P N Z}{60 A}$$

### Final Equation (Boxed for KTU)

$$\boxed{E_b = \dfrac{\Phi\, Z\, N\, P}{60\, A} \;\; \text{Volts}}$$

Equivalently, defining the machine constant:

$$E_b = K_E \Phi N \quad \text{where} \quad K_E = \dfrac{Z P}{60 A}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Torque Equation ($T$)

### Step 1 — Force on a Single Conductor
A conductor of effective length $L$ carrying current $I$ in a magnetic field of average flux density $B_{av}$ experiences a force:

$$F = B_{av} L I$$

### Step 2 — Relating $B_{av}$ to $\Phi$ and Pole Area
The flux per pole is:

$$\Phi = B_{av} \times A_{pole} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad B_{av} = \dfrac{\Phi}{A_{pole}}$$

> where $A_{pole}$ = cross-sectional area of one pole face.

### Step 3 — Torque per Conductor
The torque contributed by a single conductor at radius $r$ (mean armature radius) is:

$$T_{1} = F \times r = B_{av} L I \times r = \dfrac{\Phi}{A_{pole}} \cdot L I \cdot r$$

### Step 4 — Recognizing that $L \cdot r / A_{pole} = 1 / (2\pi)$ (Geometric Identity)
This step requires recognizing the pole-shoe geometry. For a smooth cylindrical armature:

$$\dfrac{L \cdot r}{A_{pole}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \quad \text{(derived from arc-length / area equivalence)}$$

Hence:

$$T_{1} = \dfrac{\Phi I}{2\pi}$$

### Step 5 — Current per Conductor
The total armature current $I_a$ divides equally among $A$ parallel paths:

$$I_{conductor} = \dfrac{I_a}{A}$$

### Step 6 — Total Torque
Multiplying per-conductor torque by total number of conductors $Z$:

$$T = Z \times T_{1} = Z \times \dfrac{\Phi \cdot (I_a/A)}{2\pi}$$

### Final Equation (Boxed for KTU)

$$\boxed{T = \dfrac{\Phi\, Z\, I_a}{2\pi\, A} \;\; \text{Newton-metres (N·m)}}$$

Equivalently:

$$T = K_T \Phi I_a \quad \text{where} \quad K_T = \dfrac{Z}{2\pi A}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Speed Equation

Starting from the voltage equation:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

Substituting $E_b = K_E \Phi N$:

$$V = K_E \Phi N + I_a R_a$$

Solving for $N$:

$$K_E \Phi N = V - I_a R_a$$

$$\boxed{N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{K_E \Phi} \;\; \text{rpm}}$$

### Analytical Insight
This equation reveals **the three classical methods of speed control** in a DC motor (highly examinable):

| Method | Variable Changed | Effect on Speed |
|--------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Flux Control** | $\Phi \downarrow$ | $N \uparrow$ (above base speed) |
| **Armature Voltage Control** | $V \downarrow$ | $N \downarrow$ (below base speed) |
| **Armature Resistance Control** | $R_a \uparrow$ (external) | $N \downarrow$ (below base speed, lossy) |

---

## 3.4 Power-Stage Diagram of a DC Motor

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{input} &= V \cdot I_a \\
&= (E_b + I_a R_a) \cdot I_a \\
&= E_b I_a + I_a^2 R_a \\
&= P_{developed} + P_{cu,a}
\end{aligned}
$$

The developed power $P_{dev} = E_b I_a = T \omega$ is partly converted to output ($P_{out} = T_{shaft} \cdot \omega$) after mechanical losses ($P_{mech} = $ friction + windage) and core losses ($P_{core}$) are subtracted:

$$P_{out} = P_{dev} - P_{mech} - P_{core}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of DC Motor Types

| Parameter | DC Shunt Motor | DC Series Motor | DC Compound Motor |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Field connection** | Parallel to armature | In series with armature | Both series + shunt field |
| **Speed regulation** | Good (≈ 5–10%) | Poor (varies widely) | Moderate (depends on type) |
| **Speed vs. Load** | Nearly constant | Drops sharply with load | Moderate drop |
| **Starting torque** | Moderate (1.5× FLT) | Very high (5–8× FLT) | High (2.5–4× FLT) |
| **No-load speed** | Safe (finite) | Dangerously high | Safe (finite, depends on compound) |
| **Applications** | Lathes, fans, line shafts | Cranes, traction, hoists | Presses, elevators, conveyors |
| **Speed-torque characteristic** | Drooping (slight) | Inverse hyperbolic (sharp drop) | Compounded curve |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why can't a DC series motor be started on no-load?** Because flux $\Phi \propto I_a$, at no-load $I_a \to 0$, so $\Phi \to 0$, and $N \to \infty$ (destruction!). **Always coupled to load.**

---

## 3.6 Key Numerical Sanity Check (For KTU)

For a **standard 4-pole, lap-wound DC motor**:
- $P = 4$, $A = P = 4$ (lap winding)
- $K_E = \dfrac{Z \times 4}{60 \times 4} = \dfrac{Z}{60}$

If $Z = 600$, $K_E = 10$. With $\Phi = 0.05$ Wb and $N = 1500$ rpm:
- $E_b = 10 \times 0.05 \times 1500 = 750$ V

This is a typical KTU problem format.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🧭 Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cross-Sectional Block Diagram of a DC Motor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[YOKE - Outer Frame / Magnetic Return Path] --> B[FIELD CORE - North and South Poles]
    B --> C[POLE SHOE - Spreads Flux Uniformly]
    C --> D[AIR GAP - Working Region for Flux-Cutting]
    D --> E[ARMATURE CORE - Laminated Silicon Steel]
    E --> F[ARMATURE WINDING - In Slots, Carries Current Ia]
    F --> G[COMMUTATOR - Segments Mounted on Shaft]
    G --> H[BRUSHES - Stationary, Sliding Contact]
    H --> I[DC SUPPLY - Input Terminals V+ and V-]
    I --> J[FIELD WINDING - Excited by DC]
    J --> A
    style A fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#5a1f1f,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#1f5a3a,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#5a5a1f,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Power Flow Architecture in a DC Motor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[DC Electrical Input - P_in = V I_a] --> P2[Armature Copper Loss - I_a^2 R_a]
    P1 --> P3[Mechanical Power Developed - P_dev = E_b I_a = T omega]
    P3 --> P4[Core Loss - Hysteresis + Eddy Current]
    P3 --> P5[Mechanical Loss - Friction + Windage]
    P4 --> P6[Shaft Output - P_out = T_sh omega]
    P5 --> P6
    style P1 fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style P3 fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style P6 fill:#b71c1c,color:#ffffff
    style P2 fill:#f57c00,color:#000000
    style P4 fill:#f57c00,color:#000000
    style P5 fill:#f57c00,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Equivalent Circuit of a DC Shunt Motor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[DC Supply +V] --> Rsh[Field Resistance R_sh]
    V1 --> Ra[Armature Resistance R_a in series with Back EMF E_b]
    Rsh --> V2[Return to Supply -V]
    Ra --> V2
    style V1 fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style Rsh fill:#5a1f5a,color:#ffffff
    style Ra fill:#5a1f1f,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.4 Equivalent Circuit of a DC Series Motor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[DC Supply +V] --> Rse[Series Field R_se]
    Rse --> Ra[Armature Resistance R_a]
    Ra --> Eb[Back EMF E_b]
    Eb --> V2[Return to Supply -V]
    style V1 fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style Rse fill:#5a5a1f,color:#ffffff
    style Ra fill:#5a1f1f,color:#ffffff
    style Eb fill:#1f5a3a,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.5 Speed-Torque Characteristic Curves (Functional Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ST_CHAR[Speed-Torque Behavior of DC Motors]
        direction LR
        A1[Shunt Motor - Slight Droop] ---|Near-constant speed| A2[Torque increases, Speed nearly constant]
        B1[Series Motor - Sharp Inversion] ---|Hyperbolic fall| B2[At light load, Speed shoots up dangerously]
        C1[Compound Motor - Compounded] ---|Moderate droop| C2[Combines both characteristics]
    end
    style A1 fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style B1 fill:#5a1f1f,color:#ffffff
    style C1 fill:#1f5a3a,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.6 Functional Block Diagram — Control of a DC Motor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[DC Source] --> C1[Controller - Chopper / Rectifier / Rheostat]
    C1 --> M1[Armature of DC Motor]
    M1 --> M2[Shaft Output - Mechanical Load]
    M1 -.-> FB1[Tachogenerator / Encoder - Speed Feedback]
    FB1 -.-> C1
    style S1 fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style C1 fill:#5a1f5a,color:#ffffff
    style M1 fill:#5a1f1f,color:#ffffff
    style M2 fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style FB1 fill:#b71c1c,color:#ffffff
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Define Back EMF in a DC motor. Why is it called 'back' EMF, and what is its role in motor operation?"**

**Model Answer:**

> **Back EMF ($E_b$):** The EMF induced in the armature conductors of a DC motor, opposing the applied armature voltage, due to the rotation of the armature conductors in the magnetic field, is called **back EMF** (or **counter-EMF**).

> **Why "back"?** It is called "back" because its polarity is **opposite to the applied supply voltage** $V$, as dictated by **Lenz's Law** — the induced EMF always opposes the cause producing it (which, in a motor, is the applied voltage driving current through the armature).

> **Role in Operation:** Back EMF acts as a **self-regulating mechanism**:
> - When motor speeds up, $E_b \propto N$ increases → $I_a = (V - E_b)/R_a$ decreases → torque decreases → motor settles at a new equilibrium speed.
> - When motor is loaded, speed drops slightly, $E_b$ decreases, $I_a$ increases, torque rises to handle the load.

> **Energy Significance:** $E_b I_a$ represents the **electromagnetic power converted to mechanical form**.

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Compare the starting performance of DC shunt and DC series motors with respect to starting current and starting torque."**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | DC Shunt Motor | DC Series Motor |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Starting current** | $I_{a,start} = V/R_a$ (very high, 10–15× full-load) | $I_{a,start} = V/(R_a + R_{se})$ (limited by series field) |
| **Starting flux** | $\Phi_{sh} = V/R_{sh}$ (fixed, full value) | $\Phi_{se} \propto I_{a,start}$ (very high) |
| **Starting torque** | $T_{start} = K_T \Phi_{sh} I_{a,start}$ (moderate) | $T_{start} = K_T \Phi_{se} I_{a,start}$ (very high, 5–8× FLT) |
| **Conclusion** | Requires starter to limit $I_a$ | Naturally high torque, ideal for traction |

> **Reason for high torque in series motor:** Both $I_a$ and $\Phi$ increase together, so $T \propto I_a^2$ at start → very high torque.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B questions are ESE-style and **carry internal choice**. Students must attempt **ONE** of the two alternatives. Each sub-part is typically 7 marks.

---

### **Question B1 — Option A** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]
**(a)** Derive the **EMF equation** of a DC motor starting from Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 4-pole, 500 V, lap-wound DC shunt motor has 800 conductors on its armature. The armature resistance is 0.2 Ω and the field resistance is 200 Ω. The motor draws a line current of 60 A at a certain load. Calculate:
- (i) The armature current
- (ii) The back EMF
- (iii) The flux per pole, given that the motor runs at 1000 rpm
- (iv) The total torque developed
- (v) The shaft torque, given rotational losses = 800 W **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q B1 — Option A (a)**

**Given:** Need to derive $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$.

**Assumptions:**
1. The armature is **lap-wound** (so $A = P$).
2. The flux per pole $\Phi$ is **uniformly distributed** under each pole shoe.
3. The armature winding is **full-pitched and perfectly distributed** in slots.
4. Commutation is **ideal** (no sparking, no reactance voltage).

**Derivation (as in Section 3.1):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EMF per conductor per revolution} &= \Phi \times P \quad \text{(flux cut per rev)} \\
\text{Time per revolution} &= \dfrac{60}{N} \quad \text{seconds} \\
\text{Rate of flux cutting per conductor} &= \dfrac{\Phi P N}{60} \quad \text{V} \\
\text{Conductors in series per parallel path} &= \dfrac{Z}{A} \\
\text{EMF per parallel path} &= \dfrac{\Phi P N}{60} \times \dfrac{Z}{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since all $A$ paths are in parallel, the terminal EMF (back EMF) is:

$$E_b = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A} \quad \text{Volts} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q B1 — Option A (b)**

**Given Data:**
- $V = 500$ V
- $P = 4$, Lap wound $\Rightarrow A = P = 4$
- $Z = 800$
- $R_a = 0.2$ Ω, $R_{sh} = 200$ Ω
- Line current $I_L = 60$ A
- $N = 1000$ rpm
- Rotational losses $P_{rot} = 800$ W

**Step (i) — Armature current:**

$$I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}} = \dfrac{500}{200} = 2.5 \text{ A}$$

$$I_a = I_L - I_{sh} = 60 - 2.5 = 57.5 \text{ A}$$

**Step (ii) — Back EMF:**

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 500 - (57.5)(0.2) = 500 - 11.5 = 488.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step (iii) — Flux per pole:**

Using $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$:

$$488.5 = \dfrac{\Phi \times 800 \times 1000 \times 4}{60 \times 4}$$

$$488.5 = \dfrac{\Phi \times 800000}{240} = \Phi \times 3333.33$$

$$\Phi = \dfrac{488.5}{3333.33} = 0.1465 \text{ Wb} \approx 0.147 \text{ Wb}$$

**Step (iv) — Total developed torque:**

Angular speed:

$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.72 \text{ rad/s}$$

Developed power:

$$P_{dev} = E_b I_a = 488.5 \times 57.5 = 28088.75 \text{ W}$$

Torque:

$$T_{dev} = \dfrac{P_{dev}}{\omega} = \dfrac{28088.75}{104.72} = 268.21 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step (v) — Shaft torque:**

$$P_{out} = P_{dev} - P_{rot} = 28088.75 - 800 = 27288.75 \text{ W}$$

$$T_{sh} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\omega} = \dfrac{27288.75}{104.72} = 260.58 \text{ N·m}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $I_a = 57.5$ A
- (ii) $E_b = 488.5$ V
- (iii) $\Phi = 0.147$ Wb
- (iv) $T_{dev} = 268.21$ N·m
- (v) $T_{sh} = 260.58$ N·m

---

### **Question B1 — Option B** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]
**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **construction and working of a DC motor**. State **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule** and explain its application. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 250 V DC series motor has armature and field resistances of 0.5 Ω and 0.3 Ω respectively. When running at 1500 rpm, it draws a current of 50 A. The flux per pole at this current is 25 mWb. Calculate the torque developed. If the load torque is reduced to 60% of the original, find the new speed (assume flux is proportional to current, and armature reaction is neglected). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q B1 — Option B (a)**

**Constructional Features (refer to Section 4.1 for diagram):**

1. **Yoke:** Made of cast iron or cast steel; serves as the outer frame and provides the **magnetic return path** for the flux.
2. **Pole Core & Pole Shoe:** Made of **laminated silicon steel** to reduce eddy current losses. The pole shoe has a larger cross-section to **spread the flux uniformly** across the air gap.
3. **Field Winding:** Wound around the pole core; carries the **exciting current** to produce flux. In **shunt motors** (many turns of thin wire), in **series motors** (few turns of thick wire).
4. **Armature Core:** Built from **high-grade silicon steel laminations** (0.35–0.5 mm thick) to minimize eddy current losses; slots are punched on the outer periphery.
5. **Armature Winding:** Conductors placed in slots and connected to form a closed winding (lap or wave).
6. **Commutator:** Cylindrical structure made of **hard-drawn copper segments** insulated by mica. It acts as a **mechanical rectifier**.
7. **Brushes:** Made of carbon or graphite; ride on the commutator and provide the electrical interface between the rotating armature and the stationary external circuit.

**Working Principle:**

When DC supply is given, the field winding produces a uniform magnetic field. Current flowing through the armature conductors experiences a force $F = BIL$ directed by **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule**. The collective tangential force produces a **torque** $T$ that rotates the armature. The commutator reverses the current in each conductor as it passes from one pole to another, ensuring **unidirectional rotation**.

**Fleming's Left-Hand Rule (FLHR):**
- **Forefinger** → direction of **Magnetic Field (B)** — North to South
- **Middle finger** → direction of **Current (I)** — conventional current
- **Thumb** → direction of **Force / Motion (F)**

> **Application in DC Motor:** Used to determine the direction of rotation of the armature. Reversing either the field current OR the armature current reverses the rotation. Reversing **both** does not change the direction.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q B1 — Option B (b)**

**Given Data:**
- $V = 250$ V
- $R_a = 0.5$ Ω, $R_{se} = 0.3$ Ω
- $N_1 = 1500$ rpm at $I_1 = 50$ A
- $\Phi_1 = 25$ mWb = 0.025 Wb
- $\Phi \propto I_a$ (no armature reaction)

**Step 1 — Original back EMF:**

$$E_{b1} = V - I_1(R_a + R_{se}) = 250 - 50 \times (0.5 + 0.3) = 250 - 40 = 210 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Original developed torque:**

$$\omega_1 = \dfrac{2\pi N_1}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$P_{dev1} = E_{b1} I_1 = 210 \times 50 = 10500 \text{ W}$$

$$T_1 = \dfrac{P_{dev1}}{\omega_1} = \dfrac{10500}{157.08} = 66.85 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3 — New operating condition:**

For a series motor at steady state, developed torque = load torque (ignoring rotational losses for simplicity). New torque is 60% of original:

$$T_2 = 0.6 \times T_1 = 0.6 \times 66.85 = 40.11 \text{ N·m}$$

Since $T \propto \Phi I_a$ and $\Phi \propto I_a$:

$$T_2 \propto I_a^2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad I_2^2 = 0.6 \times I_1^2$$

$$I_2 = I_1 \sqrt{0.6} = 50 \times 0.7746 = 38.73 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4 — New back EMF:**

$$E_{b2} = V - I_2(R_a + R_{se}) = 250 - 38.73 \times 0.8 = 250 - 30.98 = 219.02 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — New flux:**

$$\Phi_2 = \Phi_1 \times \dfrac{I_2}{I_1} = 0.025 \times \dfrac{38.73}{50} = 0.01936 \text{ Wb}$$

**Step 6 — New speed:**

Using $\dfrac{E_{b1}}{E_{b2}} = \dfrac{\Phi_1 N_1}{\Phi_2 N_2}$:

$$N_2 = N_1 \times \dfrac{E_{b2}}{E_{b1}} \times \dfrac{\Phi_1}{\Phi_2}$$

$$N_2 = 1500 \times \dfrac{219.02}{210} \times \dfrac{0.025}{0.01936}$$

$$N_2 = 1500 \times 1.043 \times 1.291 = 2019 \text{ rpm}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $T_1 = 66.85$ N·m
- $N_2 \approx 2019$ rpm

> **Physical interpretation:** Reducing the load torque in a series motor causes the speed to **rise significantly** — this is why series motors must never be run unloaded.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in DC Motor Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract $I_{sh}$ from $I_L$** in shunt/compound motors → wrong $I_a$ → all subsequent values wrong. **(−2 to −3 marks)**
> 2. **Confusing $A$ (parallel paths) with $P$ (poles).** For lap winding, $A = P$; for wave, $A = 2$. Many students hardcode $A = 2$ always. **(−1 to −2 marks)**
> 3. **Using $\omega = N$ (treating rpm as rad/s).** Always convert: $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$. **(−1 mark)**
> 4. **Mixing $E_b I_a$ (developed power) with $V I_a$ (input power) when calculating torque.** Use the correct one as the situation demands.
> 5. **Not specifying lap vs. wave winding assumption** in derivations → examiner's discretion deduction.
> 6. **Forgetting to state the assumption "armature reaction neglected"** in numerical problems.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **DC Motor Principle:** Lorentz force on current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field; direction by **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule**.
- [x] **Two EMF Equations to memorize:**
  - $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ (basic form)
  - $E_b = K_E \Phi N$ (operating form)
- [x] **Torque Equation:** $T = K_T \Phi I_a$, with $K_T = Z/(2\pi A)$.
- [x] **Voltage Equation:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ — the **master equation** of a DC motor.
- [x] **Speed Equation:** $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{K_E \Phi}$.
- [x] **Lap winding → $A = P$**, Wave winding → $A = 2$ (always).
- [x] **Series motor $\Rightarrow$ never run on no-load** (unbounded speed).
- [x] **Back EMF** = electromagnetic brake effect; increases with speed; reduces armature current.
- [x] **Developed power** $P_{dev} = E_b I_a = T \omega$ — this is the **electromechanical conversion link**.
- [x] **Three speed control methods:** Flux control, Armature voltage control, Armature resistance control.
- [x] **$K_E = K_T$** in SI units (energy conservation).
- [x] **Typical KTU flux density range:** 0.7 – 1.2 T; **typical KTU field current in shunt motors:** 1–5% of rated current.
- [x] **Compound motors:** Cumulative compound (aiding fields) → high starting torque + good speed regulation; Differential compound (opposing fields) → rarely used (unstable).
- [x] **Reversal of direction:** Swap either armature OR field terminals — swapping both leaves direction unchanged.
- [x] **Starting methods:** 3-point starter (shunt), 4-point starter (shunt, handles field current independently), series motor starter with no-volt release.

---

> **END OF MODULE 1 NOTES — DC MOTOR**
