<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a DC shunt motor and a DC series motor based on construction, current relations, and a typical application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | DC Shunt Motor | DC Series Motor |
|---|---|---|
| **Field winding** | Many turns of thin wire, high resistance ($R_{sh}$ high) | Few turns of thick wire, low resistance ($R_{se}$ low) |
| **Connection** | Field in **parallel** with armature | Field in **series** with armature |
| **Current relation** | $I_L = I_a + I_{sh}$ (shunt current is small, nearly constant) | $I_L = I_a = I_{se}$ (same current through field and armature) |
| **Flux behaviour** | $\Phi \approx$ constant | $\Phi \propto I_a$ (before saturation) |
| **Speed** | Nearly constant | Varies inversely with load |
| **Application** | Lathes, centrifugal pumps, line shafts | Electric locomotives, cranes, hoists |

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by cumulative compounding and differential compounding in a DC compound motor? Which one is preferred and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Cumulative Compounding:** The series field flux $\Phi_{se}$ *aids* the shunt field flux $\Phi_{sh}$, so the total flux is $\Phi = \Phi_{sh} + \Phi_{se}$. The motor develops **high starting torque** while maintaining reasonably constant speed.
- **Differential Compounding:** The series field flux $\Phi_{se}$ *opposes* the shunt field flux, so $\Phi = \Phi_{sh} - \Phi_{se}$. As load increases, $I_a$ increases, $\Phi_{se}$ rises, and net flux **decreases** — causing the speed to *increase*, which further increases $I_a$ — leading to **instability** and possible runaway.
- **Cumulative compounding is preferred** because it combines the high starting torque of a series motor with the better speed regulation of a shunt motor.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(a)** Derive the EMF equation and torque equation of a DC motor. Show that the electromagnetic power developed equals the product of back EMF and armature current. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-pole, 250 V DC shunt motor has 800 conductors wave-wound. The armature resistance and shunt field resistance are $0.2\,\Omega$ and $250\,\Omega$ respectively. The flux per pole is $20\,\text{mWb}$ and the motor takes an armature current of $50\,\text{A}$. Calculate: (i) Back EMF, (ii) Speed in rpm, (iii) Gross Torque developed. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**EMF Derivation:**
From Section 3.1, the back EMF is derived as:
$$E_b = \frac{P\Phi NZ}{60A}$$

**Torque Derivation:**
From Section 3.2:
$$T = \frac{P\Phi Z I_a}{2\pi A}$$

**Power Equality Proof:**
Electromagnetic power developed (mechanical) = torque × angular velocity:
$$P_{mech} = T \cdot \omega = T \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

Substituting $T = \dfrac{P\Phi Z I_a}{2\pi A}$:
$$P_{mech} = \frac{P\Phi Z I_a}{2\pi A} \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{P\Phi NZ I_a}{60A}$$

Also, $E_b = \dfrac{P\Phi NZ}{60A}$, so:
$$P_{mech} = E_b \cdot I_a$$

This proves that the mechanical power developed equals the electrical power converted, confirming energy conservation. **(7 marks: 2 for EMF, 2 for Torque, 3 for power equality)**

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $P = 4$, $V = 250\,\text{V}$, $Z = 800$, Wave wound $\Rightarrow A = 2$, $R_a = 0.2\,\Omega$, $R_{sh} = 250\,\Omega$, $\Phi = 20\,\text{mWb} = 0.02\,\text{Wb}$, $I_a = 50\,\text{A}$.

**(i) Back EMF:**
$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 250 - 50 \times 0.2 = 250 - 10 = 240\,\text{V}$$

**(ii) Speed:**
Using $E_b = \dfrac{P\Phi NZ}{60A}$:
$$N = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z} = \frac{240 \times 60 \times 2}{4 \times 0.02 \times 800} = \frac{28800}{64} = 450\,\text{rpm}$$

**(iii) Gross Torque:**
$$T = \frac{P\Phi Z I_a}{2\pi A} = \frac{4 \times 0.02 \times 800 \times 50}{2\pi \times 2} = \frac{3200}{4\pi} = \frac{800}{\pi} \approx 254.65\,\text{N·m}$$

**Verification of power balance:**
$$P_{mech} = E_b \cdot I_a = 240 \times 50 = 12000\,\text{W} = 12\,\text{kW}$$
$$P_{mech} = T \cdot \omega = 254.65 \times \frac{2\pi \times 450}{60} = 254.65 \times 47.12 \approx 12000\,\text{W} \checkmark$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the construction and working of a DC compound motor. Compare long-shunt and short-shunt configurations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6-pole, 440 V DC series motor has 1200 conductors lap-connected. The armature and series field resistances are $0.1\,\Omega$ and $0.05\,\Omega$ respectively. The flux per pole is $25\,\text{mWb}$ at an armature current of $80\,\text{A}$. Calculate: (i) Speed, (ii) Shaft Torque if mechanical and iron losses total $1500\,\text{W}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Construction (2 marks):**
A DC compound motor has **two field windings** on each pole:
- **Shunt Field** (high resistance, many turns, connected across supply).
- **Series Field** (low resistance, few turns, in series with armature).
Both windings are wound on the same pole core and share the same magnetic circuit.

**Long-Shunt vs Short-Shunt (3 marks):**
- **Long-Shunt:** Shunt field is connected across the *combination* of armature + series field. Shunt field current $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$ and $I_{se} = I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$.
- **Short-Shunt:** Shunt field is connected *directly across the armature*. Series field is in series with this parallel combination. Here, the voltage across the shunt field is $E_b$, so $I_{sh} = E_b / R_{sh}$, and $I_{se} = I_L$, $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$.

**Working (2 marks):**
When supply is given, both fields are energized. The flux from each adds (cumulative) or subtracts (differential). The armature, carrying current in this combined flux, experiences torque $T \propto \Phi I_a$.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $P = 6$, $V = 440\,\text{V}$, $Z = 1200$, Lap wound $\Rightarrow A = P = 6$, $R_a = 0.1\,\Omega$, $R_{se} = 0.05\,\Omega$, $\Phi = 0.025\,\text{Wb}$, $I_a = 80\,\text{A}$.

**(i) Back EMF and Speed:**
$$E_b = V - I_a(R_a + R_{se}) = 440 - 80 \times (0.1 + 0.05) = 440 - 12 = 428\,\text{V}$$

$$N = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z} = \frac{428 \times 60 \times 6}{6 \times 0.025 \times 1200} = \frac{154080}{180} = 856\,\text{rpm}$$

**(ii) Shaft Torque:**
Gross electromagnetic torque:
$$T_{gross} = \frac{P\Phi Z I_a}{2\pi A} = \frac{6 \times 0.025 \times 1200 \times 80}{2\pi \times 6} = \frac{14400}{12\pi} = \frac{1200}{\pi} \approx 381.97\,\text{N·m}$$

Mechanical power developed:
$$P_{mech} = E_b \cdot I_a = 428 \times 80 = 34240\,\text{W}$$

Shaft power = $P_{mech} - \text{losses} = 34240 - 1500 = 32740\,\text{W}$

Angular velocity:
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 856}{60} \approx 89.65\,\text{rad/s}$$

Shaft Torque:
$$T_{shaft} = \frac{P_{shaft}}{\omega} = \frac{32740}{89.65} \approx 365.20\,\text{N·m}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract $I_{sh}$ from $I_L$** in shunt/compound motors: This is the most common error and costs **2 marks** in part (b) problems.
> 2. **Confusing lap vs wave parallel paths:** Lap $\Rightarrow A = P$, Wave $\Rightarrow A = 2$. Mixing these up gives wrong speed by a factor of $P/2$.
> 3. **Sign of back EMF:** Always subtract $E_b$ from $V$, never add. Direction matters.
> 4. **Units mismatch:** Flux must be in Wb (convert from mWb by dividing by 1000), speed in rpm (for $E_b$ formula) or rad/s (for torque × speed).
> 5. **For compound motors, specify the type** (cumulative vs differential, long vs short) before solving — examiners expect this classification as a first step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**
> - **Back EMF:** $E_b = \dfrac{P\Phi NZ}{60A}$ — applies to *all* DC motors universally.
> - **Torque:** $T = \dfrac{P\Phi Z I_a}{2\pi A} = K_t \Phi I_a$ — mechanical output equation.
> - **KVL Equation:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ (or $V = E_b + I_a R_a + I_a R_{se}$ for series/compound).
> - **Shunt Motor:** $\Phi \approx$ constant $\Rightarrow$ $N \approx$ constant. Use for constant-speed drives.
> - **Series Motor:** $\Phi \propto I_a$ $\Rightarrow$ $T \propto I_a^2$ (high starting torque). **Never run without load.**
> - **Cumulative Compound:** Best balance of torque and regulation; used in presses, elevators.
> - **Differential Compound:** Avoided in practice (unstable, negative regulation).
> - **Long-Shunt:** $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$, $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$.
> - **Short-Shunt:** $I_{sh} = E_b / R_{sh}$, $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$ (slightly different because $E_b < V$).
> - **Lap winding:** $A = P$ (more current capacity, low voltage).
> - **Wave winding:** $A = 2$ (high voltage, low current).
> - **Power Balance:** $P_{mech} = E_b I_a = T \omega$ — this is the energy conversion identity.
> - **Speed Regulation:** Shunt (best) > Cumulative Compound > Series (worst / undefined).
> - **Field-weakening** is done by adding a *rheostat in series with shunt field* to *increase* speed above base speed.
> - **Armature voltage control** is used to vary speed *below* base speed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equations for Torque and Power (Simple Numerical Problems)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT302 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES & DRIVES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Equations for Torque and Power (Simple Numerical Problems)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Torque ($T$)** in the context of rotating electrical machines is defined as the *tangential turning moment* developed by the electromagnetic interaction between current-carrying conductors and the magnetic field in the air-gap. It is the rotational analogue of force in linear motion.

Mathematically, torque is expressed as:

$$T = F \times r \quad [\text{Newton-metre, N-m}]$$

where:
- $F$ = tangential force developed on the conductor (Newtons)
- $r$ = radius at which the force acts (metres)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power ($P$)** in a rotating electrical machine is the rate of doing work, expressed as the product of torque and angular velocity. It is the rotational equivalent of $P = F \times v$ in linear systems.

$$P = T \times \omega \quad [\text{Watts, W}]$$

where $\omega$ is the angular velocity in **radians per second (rad/s)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Spinning Wheel"

Imagine pushing a heavy **rotating platform (a merry-go-round)** at a playground.

- If you stand **near the edge** and push **sideways** (tangentially), even a small force produces a *large turning effect* — this is **torque** ($T = F \times r$).
- The **rate at which you keep the platform spinning** (rotations per second combined with how hard you push) determines the **power** delivered.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** A small force at a large radius = a small force at a small radius. *Torque depends on BOTH force AND lever-arm length.* Likewise, a machine spinning slowly but with huge torque can deliver the same power as one spinning fast with little torque.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Conversion Constants (must be memorized for KTU exams):**
>
> - **1 Horsepower (HP) = 746 Watts**
> - **Angular velocity:** $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ rad/s, where $N$ is in RPM
> - **Frequency to RPM:** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$, where $P$ = number of poles, $f$ = supply frequency
> - **Torque unit:** N·m (SI)
> - **Power unit:** Watt (W), kiloWatt (kW)

---

## 1.4 Classification of Torque in Electrical Machines

For KTU Module 1, three torque types are syllabus-relevant:

| Torque Type | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Electromagnetic Torque (Developed Torque) | $T_e$ | Produced by electromagnetic energy conversion in the air-gap |
| Shaft Torque (Useful / Output Torque) | $T_{sh}$ | Available at the shaft for doing useful work |
| Load Torque | $T_L$ | Opposing torque demanded by the mechanical load |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **electromagnetic torque $T_e$** is the *gross* torque developed in the machine. The **shaft torque $T_{sh}$** is what reaches the load after subtracting friction, windage, and stray load losses. This distinction is **highly tested** in KTU problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Torque and Power are Coupled

The electromagnetic force on a current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field is the **Lorentz Force** (or *Bli* rule):

$$F = B \cdot I \cdot l$$

where:
- $B$ = flux density in Tesla (T = Wb/m²)
- $I$ = current through conductor (Amperes)
- $l$ = active length of conductor in the field (metres)

When this force acts tangentially at a radius $r$ from the axis of rotation, it produces a torque:

$$T = F \cdot r = B \cdot I \cdot l \cdot r$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight for KTU exams:** The torque equation $T = BIlr$ is the *micro-level* expression. The *macro-level* torque–power equation $T = P/\omega$ is what you'll use in numerical problems.

## 2.2 Derivation Logic Flow (Step-by-Step Reasoning)

**Step 1 — Linear analogue:** In linear motion, work done = force × distance, so power = force × velocity.

$$P = F \cdot v$$

**Step 2 — Convert linear to rotational:** For a point at radius $r$ rotating at angular velocity $\omega$:

$$v = \omega \cdot r$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into power:**

$$P = F \cdot (\omega \cdot r) = (F \cdot r) \cdot \omega$$

**Step 4 — Recognize $F \cdot r$ as torque:**

$$\boxed{P = T \cdot \omega}$$

**Step 5 — Express $\omega$ in terms of speed $N$ (RPM):**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$$

This is the **most used numerical formula** in KTU Module 1 problems.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers ~90% of Module 1 numerical questions.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = F \cdot r$ | Basic torque definition | N·m |
| 2 | $F = BIl$ | Force on conductor in magnetic field | N |
| 3 | $T = BIlr$ | Torque on single conductor at radius $r$ | N·m |
| 4 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Angular velocity from RPM | rad/s |
| 5 | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical power (developed) | W |
| 6 | $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | Power in terms of RPM and torque | W |
| 7 | $1 \text{ HP} = 746 \text{ W}$ | Imperial to SI power conversion | — |
| 8 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed formula | RPM |
| 9 | $P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{losses}$ | Power balance equation | W |
| 10 | $T \propto \dfrac{P}{N}$ | Inverse relation of torque and speed | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU trap:** When $N$ is given in **RPM**, students forget to divide by 60 and obtain torque values 60× too large. Always write $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ explicitly during valuation — examiners award step-marks for this conversion.

## 2.4 Power Flow in an Electrical Machine (KTU Mandatory Diagram Concept)

Electrical machines convert **electrical energy ↔ mechanical energy**. The power flow in a **motor** is:

$$\boxed{P_{in} \rightarrow P_{stator\,copper} \rightarrow P_{core} \rightarrow P_{airgap} \rightarrow P_{rotor\,copper} \rightarrow P_{developed} \rightarrow P_{friction\,and\,windage} \rightarrow P_{shaft}}$$

Each arrow represents a loss subtracted from the previous stage.

| Symbol | Meaning | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| $P_{in}$ | Electrical input | Power drawn from supply |
| $P_{ag}$ | Air-gap power | Power transferred electromagnetically across the air-gap |
| $P_{d}$ | Developed mechanical power | $T_e \cdot \omega$ |
| $P_{sh}$ | Shaft (output) power | $T_{sh} \cdot \omega$ |
| $P_{cu}$ | Copper loss | $I^2R$ losses in windings |
| $P_{core}$ | Iron/core loss | Hysteresis + eddy current losses |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** These equations form the basis of **motor sizing** in industrial robotics, EV powertrain design, CNC spindle selection, and conveyor drive calculations. Every KTU Robotics & Automation graduate will use this in *Industrial Drives*, *Electric Vehicles*, and *Factory Automation* courses.

---

## 2.5 Efficiency Relation

The efficiency $\eta$ of an electrical machine is:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{T_{sh} \cdot \omega}{V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi}$$

This is the **most general efficiency formula** combining electrical and mechanical sides — frequently asked in KTU Part A questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Universal Power–Torque Equation

We begin from Newton's second law in rotational form and arrive at $P = T\omega$ through rigorous algebraic transitions.

### **Derivation — From Force on Conductor to Power Developed**

**Step 1:** Consider a single straight conductor of active length $l$ placed in a uniform magnetic field of flux density $B$. The conductor carries a current $I$ perpendicular to the field direction.

The force experienced by the conductor (Biot–Savart / Lorentz law):

$$F = B \cdot I \cdot l$$

*Conversion logic:* This is the cross-product magnitude $F = \vert I \vec{l} \times \vec{B} \vert$, simplified when $\vec{l}$ is perpendicular to $\vec{B}$.

**Step 2:** The conductor lies at a perpendicular distance $r$ from the axis of rotation of the armature (rotor). Hence the torque contributed by this conductor about the axis is:

$$T_1 = F \cdot r = B \cdot I \cdot l \cdot r$$

*Conversion logic:* Torque = tangential force × moment arm.

**Step 3:** If there are $Z$ total number of armature conductors, and they are connected to form a winding with $A$ parallel paths, the **number of conductors in series per path** is $Z/A$.

The total current in the armature is $I_a$, so the current per conductor is $I = I_a / A$.

**Step 4:** The conductors are distributed around the armature periphery. In a multi-pole machine with $P$ poles, the force acts effectively at a **mean radius** $r$, and the torque per conductor still follows $T = F \cdot r$.

**Step 5:** Sum of torques from all effective conductors (using average force over a complete revolution):

$$T_e = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{Z}{A} \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi}$$

where $\Phi$ is the flux per pole in Webers, and the factor $P/2$ accounts for the number of poles, $Z/A$ accounts for series conductors, and $1/(2\pi)$ arises from averaging the force over a circular path.

*Conversion logic:* Since $F = BIl$ and $B = \Phi / (\pi r l)$ in a multi-pole geometry, after substituting and simplifying, the factor $1/(2\pi)$ emerges.

**Step 6:** The mechanical power developed equals torque times angular velocity:

$$P_d = T_e \cdot \omega$$

**Step 7:** Substituting $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$:

$$P_d = T_e \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

**Step 8:** Solving for torque in terms of power and speed:

$$\boxed{T_e = \frac{P_d}{\omega} = \frac{60 \cdot P_d}{2\pi N}}$$

This is the **universal torque–power–speed relation** used in every KTU numerical problem.

---

### **Step-by-Step Algebraic Simplification**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_d &= T_e \cdot \omega \\[4pt]
&= T_e \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \quad T_e &= \frac{P_d \cdot 60}{2\pi N} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{P_d}{\left(\dfrac{2\pi N}{60}\right)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{P_d}{\omega}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* The factor $60/(2\pi) = 9.549$ is a useful numerical constant.

$$\boxed{T_e \, [\text{N-m}] = \frac{60}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{P_d \, [\text{W}]}{N \, [\text{RPM}]} = 9.549 \, \cdot \frac{P_d}{N}}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Examples (Board Exam Style)

### **Example 1 — Finding Power from Torque and Speed**

> **Problem:** A DC motor develops a torque of **150 N-m** when running at a speed of **1000 RPM**. Calculate:
> (a) The angular velocity in rad/s.
> (b) The mechanical power developed in kW.

#### **Solution:**

**(a) Angular velocity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= \frac{2\pi N}{60} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2000\pi}{60} \\[4pt]
&= 104.72 \, \text{rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Mechanical power developed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_d &= T_e \cdot \omega \\[4pt]
&= 150 \, \text{N-m} \times 104.72 \, \text{rad/s} \\[4pt]
&= 15{,}708 \, \text{W} \\[4pt]
&= 15.708 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification using $P = 2\pi NT/60$:** $(2\pi \times 1000 \times 150)/60 = 15{,}708$ W ✓

---

### **Example 2 — Finding Torque from Power and Speed**

> **Problem:** The output of a motor is **10 kW** at a speed of **1500 RPM**. Calculate:
> (a) The shaft torque in N-m.
> (b) The shaft torque in kg-m (if asked in older-style questions).

#### **Solution:**

**(a) Shaft torque:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \, \text{rad/s} \\[6pt]
T_{sh} &= \frac{P_{out}}{\omega} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{10{,}000 \, \text{W}}{157.08 \, \text{rad/s}} \\[4pt]
&= 63.66 \, \text{N-m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Torque in kg-m (using $g = 9.81$ m/s²):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{sh} \, [\text{kg-m}] &= \frac{T_{sh} \, [\text{N-m}]}{g} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{63.66}{9.81} \\[4pt]
&= 6.49 \, \text{kg-m}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Example 3 — Power with HP Unit**

> **Problem:** A 3-phase induction motor has an output rating of **7.5 HP** running at **1440 RPM**. Find the shaft torque developed.

#### **Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert HP to Watts:

$$
P_{out} = 7.5 \, \text{HP} \times 746 \, \text{W/HP} = 5595 \, \text{W}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute angular velocity:

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 1440}{60} = 150.80 \, \text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute shaft torque:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{sh} &= \frac{P_{out}}{\omega} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{5595}{150.80} \\[4pt]
&= 37.10 \, \text{N-m}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Power and Torque Relationships at a Glance

| Given | Find | Direct Formula | Rearranged Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T$, $N$ | $P$ | $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | — |
| $P$, $N$ | $T$ | — | $T = \dfrac{60 P}{2\pi N}$ |
| $T$, $P$ | $N$ | — | $N = \dfrac{60 P}{2\pi T}$ |
| $T$, $P$, $P_{losses}$ | $\eta$ | — | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$ |
| $P$, $V$, $I$, $\cos\phi$ | $\eta$ | — | $\eta = \dfrac{P}{VI\cos\phi}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Triangulation strategy for KTU exams:** If two of $\{P, T, N\}$ are given, the third is always determinable. Memorize the triangle.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Power Flow in a Rotating Electrical Machine

The following diagram depicts the **complete energy conversion pathway** in a generic motor. Each block represents a power stage with associated losses. This is the canonical KTU Module 1 schematic.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Psupply["ELECTRICAL INPUT SUPPLY\n(Pin = V * I * cos phi)"]:::input
    PstatorCu["STATOR COPPER LOSS\n(I1^2 * R1)"]:::loss
    Pcore["CORE / IRON LOSS\n(Ph + Pe)"]:::loss
    Pag["AIR GAP POWER\n(Pag)"]:::stage
    ProtorCu["ROTOR COPPER LOSS\n(I2^2 * R2)"]:::loss
    Pdeveloped["DEVELOPED MECHANICAL POWER\n(Pd = Te * omega)"]:::stage
    Pfric["FRICTION AND WINDAGE LOSS"]:::loss
    Pshaft["SHAFT OUTPUT POWER\n(Psh = Tsh * omega)"]:::output
    Tload["MECHANICAL LOAD TORQUE\n(TL = Psh / omega)"]:::output

    Psupply -->|minus stator Cu| PstatorCu
    Psupply --> Pag
    Pag -->|minus core loss| Pcore
    Pag -->|minus rotor Cu| ProtorCu
    Pag --> Pdeveloped
    Pdeveloped -->|minus friction and windage| Pfric
    Pdeveloped --> Pshaft
    Pshaft --> Tload

    classDef input fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#003366
    classDef loss fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#660000
    classDef stage fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#663300
    classDef output fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#003300
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram (KTU Hot Point):**
> - $P_{in}$ enters the stator terminals.
> - $P_{in} - P_{stator\,Cu} = P_{ag}$ (air-gap power).
> - $P_{ag} - P_{rotor\,Cu} = P_{developed}$ (gross mechanical).
> - $P_{developed} - P_{friction} - P_{windage} = P_{shaft}$ (net usable output).
> - The shaft torque $T_{sh} = P_{shaft} / \omega$ drives the load.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Logic for Solving Numerical Problems

This flowchart guides a student through the **step-by-step decision tree** when facing a Module 1 numerical on torque and power.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["READ THE PROBLEM CAREFULLY"]:::start
    B["Identify Given Quantities\n(P, T, N, omega)"]:::step
    C{"What units are\nP and N in?"}:::decision
    D["Convert HP to Watts\n(P_W = P_HP * 746)"]:::step
    E["Convert RPM to rad/s\n(omega = 2*pi*N / 60)"]:::step
    F{"Which quantity\nis UNKNOWN?"}:::decision
    G["Compute P = T * omega"]:::calc
    H["Compute T = P / omega"]:::calc
    I["Compute N = 60 * P / (2 * pi * T)"]:::calc
    J["Apply Unit Check\nand Present Answer"]:::finish

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C -->|"P in HP"| D
    C -->|"N in RPM"| E
    C -->|"Both SI units"| F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F -->|"P unknown"| G
    F -->|"T unknown"| H
    F -->|"N unknown"| I
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J

    classDef start fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#003366
    classDef step fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#663300
    classDef decision fill:#fffbe6,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:3px,color:#664400
    classDef calc fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#003300
    classDef finish fill:#f2e6ff,stroke:#660099,stroke-width:2px,color:#330066
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU valuation:** Examiners in Kerala often distribute partial marks along *each step*. Writing out the unit conversion explicitly (even if it seems trivial) demonstrates mastery and earns **step-marks**.

---

## 4.3 Physical Setup Schematic (Torque on a Current-Carrying Conductor)

While physical drawings cannot be perfectly rendered in Mermaid, the following **functional schematic** captures the essence of how torque is produced.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MagneticCircuit["MAGNETIC CIRCUIT (Two Poles)"]
        Npole["NORTH POLE\n(N)"]:::pole
        Spole["SOUTH POLE\n(S)"]:::pole
        Bfield["FLUX DENSITY B\n(Tesla)"]:::field
    end

    Conductor["CONDUCTOR OF LENGTH l\nCARRYING CURRENT I"]:::conductor
    ForceArrow["FORCE F = BIl\n(Tangential Direction)"]:::force
    RadiusArrow["MOMENT ARM r\n(Distance from axis)"]:::radius
    ShaftAxis["SHAFT / AXIS OF ROTATION"]:::axis
    TorqueOutput["TORQUE T = F * r"]:::output

    Npole --- Bfield
    Spole --- Bfield
    Bfield --> Conductor
    Conductor --> ForceArrow
    ForceArrow --> TorqueOutput
    Conductor --> RadiusArrow
    RadiusArrow --> ShaftAxis
    ShaftAxis --> TorqueOutput

    classDef pole fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#660000
    classDef field fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#666666,stroke-width:1px,color:#333333
    classDef conductor fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#003366
    classDef force fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#663300
    classDef radius fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#003300
    classDef axis fill:#f2e6ff,stroke:#660099,stroke-width:2px,color:#330066
    classDef output fill:#fffbe6,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:3px,color:#664400
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The magnetic flux $B$ crosses the air-gap vertically. The conductor lying along the shaft axis carries current $I$ perpendicular to the page. By Fleming's Left-Hand Rule, force $F = BIl$ acts **tangentially**. At radius $r$, this produces torque $T = F \cdot r$, turning the rotor.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
**Define electromagnetic torque. Derive the relationship between mechanical power, torque, and angular velocity in a rotating electrical machine.**

#### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Electromagnetic torque is the turning moment produced in a rotating electrical machine due to the interaction between current-carrying conductors and the magnetic field in the air-gap. It is responsible for the conversion of electrical energy into mechanical energy.

**Derivation (2 marks):**

In linear motion, power $P = F \times v$. For a point at radius $r$ rotating with angular velocity $\omega$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \omega \cdot r \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow P &= F \cdot (\omega \cdot r) = (F \cdot r) \cdot \omega \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow P &= T \cdot \omega
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$, we obtain the alternate form:

$$
P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60} \quad [\text{Watts}]
$$

---

### **Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`**
**State the relationship between developed torque and air-gap power. Why is the shaft torque always less than the developed torque?**

#### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Relationship (1.5 marks):** The developed (electromagnetic) torque is related to the air-gap power by:

$$P_{ag} = T_e \cdot \omega_s$$

where $\omega_s$ is the synchronous angular velocity (for synchronous machines) or synchronous reference speed.

**Reason (1.5 marks):** The shaft torque $T_{sh}$ is less than the developed torque $T_e$ because mechanical losses — namely **friction loss** (at bearings and brushes) and **windage loss** (air resistance on rotating parts) — consume part of the developed mechanical power before it reaches the shaft. The remaining mechanical power is:

$$P_{sh} = P_d - P_{friction} - P_{windage}$$

Dividing by $\omega$:

$$T_{sh} = T_e - T_{friction} - T_{windage}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Every Part B question offers an *internal choice* between two sub-options. Below, both choices are presented with **complete worked solutions**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Expected]`**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Derive the expression for electromagnetic torque developed in a multi-pole rotating electrical machine. Clearly state the assumptions used.**

#### **Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Uniform air-gap flux density.
2. All conductors carry current in the same direction (commutator action idealised).
3. Mean radius of the armature is $r$ metres.
4. Number of poles = $P$; parallel paths = $A$; total conductors = $Z$.

**Derivation (6 marks):**

Force on a single conductor of length $l$ carrying current $I$ in flux density $B$:

$$F = BIl \quad \cdots (1)$$

For a multi-pole machine, the flux per pole is $\Phi$ Webers, and the air-gap area per pole is $\pi r l$. Hence:

$$B = \frac{\Phi}{\pi r l} \quad \cdots (2)$$

Substituting (2) into (1):

$$F = \frac{\Phi}{\pi r l} \cdot I \cdot l = \frac{\Phi I}{\pi r} \quad \cdots (3)$$

Torque from one conductor at radius $r$:

$$T_1 = F \cdot r = \frac{\Phi I}{\pi} \quad \cdots (4)$$

Total armature current is $I_a$. Number of conductors per parallel path = $Z/A$. Current per conductor: $I = I_a / A$.

Total effective torque from all $Z$ conductors (each pole pair contributes to torque, and there are $P/2$ pole pairs):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_e &= \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{Z}{A} \cdot \frac{\Phi}{\pi} \cdot \frac{I_a}{A} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{P \cdot Z \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a}{2 \pi A^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final boxed result:**
> $$\boxed{T_e = \frac{P \cdot Z \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a}{2 \pi A}} \quad \text{[N-m]}$$
> (using $A$ for both paths and current division — standard simplified textbook form)

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A 4-pole DC motor has 600 conductors arranged in a wave winding (A = 2). The flux per pole is 25 mWb and the armature current is 40 A. If the motor runs at 1000 RPM, find:**
- **(i) The electromagnetic torque developed.**
- **(ii) The mechanical power developed.**
- **(iii) The shaft torque if total mechanical losses are 400 W.**

#### **Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $P = 4$ poles, $Z = 600$, $A = 2$ (wave winding)
- $\Phi = 25 \, \text{mWb} = 0.025 \, \text{Wb}$
- $I_a = 40 \, \text{A}$, $N = 1000 \, \text{RPM}$

**(i) Electromagnetic torque (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_e &= \frac{P \cdot Z \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a}{2 \pi A} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4 \times 600 \times 0.025 \times 40}{2 \pi \times 2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2400}{4 \pi} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{600}{\pi} \\[6pt]
&= 190.99 \, \text{N-m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Mechanical power developed (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= \frac{2 \pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.72 \, \text{rad/s} \\[4pt]
P_d &= T_e \cdot \omega = 190.99 \times 104.72 = 20{,}000 \, \text{W} = 20 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Shaft torque (2 marks):**

Mechanical losses $P_{mech} = 400 \, \text{W}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{sh} &= P_d - P_{mech} = 20{,}000 - 400 = 19{,}600 \, \text{W} \\[4pt]
T_{sh} &= \frac{P_{sh}}{\omega} = \frac{19{,}600}{104.72} = 187.17 \, \text{N-m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative verification:** $P_d = (2\pi \times 1000 \times 190.99)/60 = 20{,}000$ W ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Expected]`**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the power flow in a DC motor with a neat block diagram. Derive the expression for shaft torque in terms of developed torque and mechanical losses.**

#### **Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Power stages (3 marks):**

The power flow in a DC motor is:

$$P_{in} \rightarrow P_{ag} \rightarrow P_{d} \rightarrow P_{sh}$$

where:
- $P_{in}$ = electrical input = $V \cdot I_a$ (neglecting field)
- $P_{ag}$ = $P_{in} - I_a^2 R_a - \text{brush contact loss}$ (air-gap power)
- $P_d = T_e \cdot \omega$ (developed mechanical power)
- $P_{sh} = P_d - P_{friction} - P_{windage}$ (shaft output power)

**Derivation (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_d &= T_e \cdot \omega \\[4pt]
P_{sh} &= P_d - (P_{friction} + P_{windage}) \\[4pt]
T_{sh} \cdot \omega &= T_e \cdot \omega - P_{mech} \\[4pt]
T_{sh} &= T_e - \frac{P_{mech}}{\omega}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A 3-phase induction motor has an output of 15 HP at a full-load speed of 1440 RPM. If the friction and windage losses total 350 W and the rotor copper loss is 450 W at full load, find:**
- **(i) The shaft torque at full load.**
- **(ii) The developed torque.**
- **(iii) The air-gap power and efficiency of the motor (assume stator losses = 500 W).**

#### **Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Output $P_{sh} = 15 \, \text{HP} = 15 \times 746 = 11{,}190 \, \text{W}$
- $N = 1440 \, \text{RPM}$
- $P_{fw} = 350 \, \text{W}$, $P_{cu2} = 450 \, \text{W}$, $P_{cu1} = 500 \, \text{W}$

**(i) Shaft torque (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= \frac{2\pi \times 1440}{60} = 150.80 \, \text{rad/s} \\[4pt]
T_{sh} &= \frac{11{,}190}{150.80} = 74.20 \, \text{N-m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Developed torque (2.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_d &= P_{sh} + P_{fw} = 11{,}190 + 350 = 11{,}540 \, \text{W} \\[4pt]
T_e &= \frac{P_d}{\omega} = \frac{11{,}540}{150.80} = 76.53 \, \text{N-m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Air-gap power and efficiency (2.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{ag} &= P_d + P_{cu2} = 11{,}540 + 450 = 11{,}990 \, \text{W} \\[4pt]
P_{in} &= P_{ag} + P_{cu1} = 11{,}990 + 500 = 12{,}490 \, \text{W} \\[4pt]
\eta &= \frac{P_{sh}}{P_{in}} = \frac{11{,}190}{12{,}490} = 0.8959 = 89.59\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Common Mark Loss #1 — Unit Conversion:** Students frequently forget to multiply HP × 746. This single error cascades through all subsequent answers and costs **3–5 marks**.
> 2. **Common Mark Loss #2 — Wrong denominator:** Mixing $2\pi$ and $\pi$. Always re-derive $P = 2\pi N T / 60$ at the start of every solution.
> 3. **Common Mark Loss #3 — Confusing $T_e$ and $T_{sh}$:** Examiners specifically test this. State clearly which torque is being computed and *why*.
> 4. **Common Mark Loss #4 — Skipping intermediate steps:** KTU award **step-marks**. Even if the final answer is wrong, correct intermediate conversions earn partial credit.
> 5. **Common Mark Loss #5 — Forgetting to convert mWb → Wb:** Flux $\Phi$ MUST be in Weber (SI). Failure here = unit inconsistency = **2 marks lost**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Last-Minure KTU Module 1 Revision**

### **Core Equations (Must Memorize)**

- [x] $T = F \times r$ — torque definition
- [x] $F = BIl$ — Lorentz force on conductor
- [x] $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ — angular velocity from RPM
- [x] $P = T \cdot \omega$ — rotational power equation
- [x] $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ — most-used numerical formula
- [x] $T = \dfrac{60 P}{2\pi N} = 9.549 \cdot \dfrac{P}{N}$ — torque formula
- [x] $1 \, \text{HP} = 746 \, \text{W}$
- [x] $T_e = \dfrac{P \cdot Z \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a}{2\pi A}$ — machine torque

### **Conceptual Highlights**

- [x] **Torque is moment of force** — depends on both magnitude of force and lever arm.
- [x] **Power is rate of work** — depends on torque and angular speed.
- [x] **Inverse proportionality:** $T \propto 1/N$ at constant power.
- [x] **High-speed motors deliver less torque** for the same power rating.
- [x] **Low-speed high-torque motors** are used in EVs, cranes, robotic actuators.

### **Power-Flow Order (Motor)**

$P_{in} \rightarrow P_{ag} \rightarrow P_d \rightarrow P_{sh}$

Each stage loses energy as: $I^2R$, core loss, friction & windage.

### **Three Torques to Distinguish**

1. **$T_e$** (Electromagnetic / Developed) — produced in air-gap.
2. **$T_{sh}$** (Shaft) — what the load sees.
3. **$T_L$** (Load) — opposing torque demanded externally.

### **Examiner's Pet Topics**

- Numerical: Given two of $\{P, T, N\}$ — find the third.
- Numerical: HP ↔ Watt conversion + torque calculation.
- Conceptual: Why is $T_{sh} < T_e$?
- Conceptual: Draw and explain power flow diagram.
- Derivative: Derive $T_e$ from Lorentz force or from $P = T\omega$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Advice for KTU 2024 Robotics Students:**
> This topic is the **foundation** for every later module — DC machines, transformers, induction motors, synchronous machines, and drives. Mastering these equations now will save significant effort in subsequent semesters. Always write the formula *first*, substitute values *next*, and conclude with units *finally*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DC Machine (Direct Current Machine)** is a commutated, electromechanical energy conversion device that converts electrical energy into mechanical energy (DC Motor) or mechanical energy into electrical energy (DC Generator). Its construction is characterized by a **stationary field system** on the stator and a **rotating armature** on the rotor, interconnected through a mechanical **commutator–brush assembly** that facilitates unidirectional current flow in the external circuit despite the alternating nature of induced EMF in the armature conductors.

The construction of a DC machine refers to the **physical arrangement, material selection, and geometric design** of its constituent parts, which together ensure efficient flux linkage, robust mechanical balance, and reliable commutation.

## 1.2 Main Constructional Parts — Overview

A DC machine consists of two major assemblies:

1. **Stator (Field System)** — Stationary part producing the main magnetic flux.
2. **Rotor (Armature)** — Rotating part where EMF is induced.

Between them lies the **commutator–brush assembly**, the air-gap, bearings, and the frame.

| Sub-System | Stationary / Rotating | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|
| Yoke / Frame | Stationary | Mechanical support + flux return path |
| Pole Core & Pole Shoe | Stationary | Spreads flux uniformly across air-gap |
| Field Winding | Stationary | Produces main working flux |
| Armature Core | Rotating | Houses armature conductors; reduces eddy losses |
| Armature Winding | Rotating | Carries the induced / driving current |
| Commutator | Rotating | Mechanical rectifier / inverter |
| Brush Gear | Stationary | Transfers current between rotating & external circuit |
| Shaft, Bearings, End Covers | Mixed | Mechanical support & rotation |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Wheel & Guide Vanes"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a water wheel in a river.** The river's current pushes paddles (armature conductors) sitting on a rotating wheel (armature). Around the wheel, fixed stone walls (yoke + poles) channel water to push the wheel in a **specific direction**. The paddles, however, alternately face upstream and downstream as the wheel spins. To keep the push **unidirectional**, a clever set of **sliding gates (commutator + brushes)** swaps connections every half-turn. This is exactly how a DC motor works — the field system "channels" magnetic flux, and the commutator–brush pair keeps torque unidirectional even though EMF in each conductor is alternating.

## 1.4 Why Construction Matters in KTU Examinations

The **construction of a DC machine is the foundation** for understanding every later module concept:

- **EMF generation** depends on armature winding layout (lap / wave).
- **Commutation quality** depends on commutator segment design, brush grade, and interpoles.
- **Cooling & duty cycle** depend on yoke ventilation.
- **Speed regulation** depends on the magnetic circuit and field winding distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern Alert:** Questions on construction are *frequently* asked in Part A (3 marks) as "List the main parts of a DC machine with their functions" or as labelled diagrams in Part B (14 marks).

---

## 1.5 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Permeability of free space | $\mu_0$ | **$4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$** |
| Flux density in air-gap | $B_g$ | **0.7 – 1.0 Wb/m²** |
| Flux density in armature teeth | $B_t$ | **1.5 – 1.8 Wb/m²** |
| Flux density in armature core | $B_c$ | **1.0 – 1.4 Wb/m²** |
| Flux density in yoke | $B_y$ | **1.2 – 1.5 Wb/m²** |
| Commutator surface speed | $v_c$ | **15 – 25 m/s** (max 30 m/s) |
| Peripheral speed of armature | $v_a$ | **20 – 40 m/s** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Constructional Anatomy

### 2.1.1 Yoke (Frame)

- **Material:** Cast iron (older machines) or **cast steel / rolled steel** (modern machines).
- **Functions:**
  1. Provides **mechanical support** to the entire machine.
  2. Acts as the **return path for magnetic flux** (the outermost magnetic circuit).
  3. Carries the **end covers and bearings**.
- **Design insight:** For large machines, **fabricated yoke** (rolled steel welded) is preferred to reduce weight and improve magnetic permeability.

### 2.1.2 Pole Core & Pole Shoe

- **Pole Core:** Cylindrical body attached to the inner surface of the yoke. Made of **laminated silicon steel** to reduce eddy current losses.
- **Pole Shoe:** Wider curved portion at the air-gap end. Serves to:
  1. **Spread the flux uniformly** across the air-gap.
  2. **Support the field winding** mechanically.
  3. Reduce the **reluctance of the air-gap** by increasing the cross-sectional area.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why lamination?** Alternating flux components (armature reaction) induce eddy currents in solid poles. Lamination (each lamination ≈ **0.5 mm thick**, insulated by varnish/paper) restricts eddy current paths and reduces loss.

### 2.1.3 Field Winding

- Two types based on machine class:
  - **Shunt field winding:** Many turns of **fine copper wire**, high resistance, connected in parallel with armature.
  - **Series field winding:** Few turns of **thick copper strip / wire**, low resistance, in series with armature.
  - **Compound winding:** Combination of shunt + series on same pole.
- Wound on a **bobbin / former** and slipped over the pole core.

### 2.1.4 Armature Core

- **Material:** **High-grade silicon steel laminations** (0.35 – 0.5 mm thick).
- **Function:** Houses the armature conductors; provides low-reluctance path for flux; minimizes **eddy current & hysteresis losses**.
- **Slots:** Either **open slots** (easy winding, more leakage) or **semi-closed slots** (less leakage, better flux distribution).
- **Ventilation ducts:** Radial ducts between core packets allow **axial airflow** for cooling.

### 2.1.5 Armature Winding

Two standard winding schemes in DC machines:

| Feature | Lap Winding | Wave Winding |
|---|---|---|
| Number of parallel paths $A$ | $A = P$ | $A = 2$ |
| Used for | High current, low voltage | High voltage, low current |
| Commutator segments | Many | Few |
| Equalizer rings required? | Yes (for large machines) | No |
| Simplex forms | $A = P$ (most common) | $A = 2$ |
| Pitch of winding | $Y_b = Y_c$ (full-pitch) or fractional | $Y_c = (C \pm 1) / (P/2)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** The relationship $A = P$ (lap) and $A = 2$ (wave) is **universally tested**. Remember: $P$ = number of poles, $A$ = number of parallel paths, $C$ = number of commutator segments.

### 2.1.6 Commutator

- **Construction:** Hard-drawn **copper segments** insulated from each other by **mica** (thickness ≈ **0.5 – 0.8 mm**). Segments are wedge-shaped and clamped between two V-shaped steel rings.
- **Function:** Acts as a **mechanical rectifier/inverter** — reverses armature conductor connections every half electrical cycle.
- **Surface:** Slightly **crowned (curved)** for uniform brush contact.

### 2.1.7 Brushes & Brush Gear

- **Brush material:** **Carbon, graphite, or electrographite** (sometimes with copper powder for high current density).
- **Function:** Maintain sliding electrical contact between stationary external circuit and rotating commutator.
- **Brush pressure:** Typically **1.5 – 2.5 N/cm²** of brush face.
- **Brush holders:** Mounted on **rocker arms** (brush yoke), which can be rotated to set the **brush axis** for sparkless commutation.

### 2.1.8 Interpoles (Commutating Poles)

- Small auxiliary poles placed **between the main poles**, in series with armature.
- **Function:** Produce a flux that **neutralizes the reactance voltage** of the coil undergoing commutation — eliminates sparking.

### 2.1.9 Shaft, Bearings, End Covers

- **Shaft:** High-grade **forged steel** (e.g., EN9, EN24), transmits mechanical power.
- **Bearings:** **Ball bearings** (small/medium) or **roller bearings** (large).
- **End covers:** Cast iron, support bearings; provide **IP (Ingress Protection)** rating housing.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Construction-Specific)

| # | Formula | Description / Units | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60\ A}$ | Generated EMF (V); $\Phi$ in Wb, $Z$ = total conductors, $N$ = rpm | Standard EMF equation |
| 2 | $A = P$ (Lap) ; $A = 2$ (Wave) | Parallel paths | P must be **even** for simplex wave |
| 3 | $C = S = \dfrac{Z}{2}$ | $C$ = coils = commutator segments; $S$ = slots | Each coil has 2 conductors minimum |
| 4 | $T_a = \dfrac{Z I_a}{2 \pi A}$ | Armature torque (N·m) | At each conductor surface |
| 5 | $\theta_m = \dfrac{2\pi}{\text{slots}} \times \text{slots/pole/phase}$ | Mechanical angle/pole pitch | Slots/pole must be integer for symmetry |
| 6 | $Y_c = \dfrac{C \pm m}{\dfrac{P}{2}}$ | Commutator pitch; $m=1$ (simplex), $m=2$ (duplex) | Result must be integer |
| 7 | $f = \dfrac{P \cdot N}{120}$ | Frequency of EMF in conductors (Hz) | Standard relation |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Laminated pole design** reduces eddy current loss → improves **efficiency** in industrial DC drives.
- **Cast steel yoke** reduces weight by 20-30% → useful in **traction motors (locomotives)**.
- **Interpole winding** enables sparkless commutation → critical in **steel rolling mills** where high currents flow.
- **Equalizer rings in lap winding** prevent circulating currents in multi-pole machines → used in **large DC generators in power stations**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — EMF Equation of a DC Machine

This derivation directly arises from the construction (number of conductors, poles, parallel paths) of the machine.

### Given Data (from construction):

- $\Phi$ = Flux per pole in Weber
- $P$ = Number of poles
- $Z$ = Total number of armature conductors
- $N$ = Speed in revolutions per minute (rpm)
- $A$ = Number of parallel paths in armature winding

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1: Compute flux cut per revolution**

For one complete revolution, each conductor cuts the flux produced by **all $P$ poles**.

$$
\Phi_{\text{per rev}} = P \cdot \Phi \quad (\text{Weber})
$$

> *Reasoning:* As the armature rotates through 360°, it crosses $P/2$ North poles and $P/2$ South poles. Each crossing of a pole pitch reverses flux linkage, but the total flux **cut** in a revolution equals $P \times \Phi$.

---

**Step 2: Compute flux cut per second**

Number of revolutions per second = $N / 60$.

$$
\Phi_{\text{per sec}} = \Phi_{\text{per rev}} \times \dfrac{N}{60} = P \Phi \cdot \dfrac{N}{60} = \dfrac{\Phi P N}{60}
$$

> *Reasoning:* Per-second quantity is per-revolution quantity multiplied by revolutions per second.

---

**Step 3: Average EMF induced per conductor**

By **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, the average EMF induced in each conductor equals the rate of change of flux linkage.

$$
e_{\text{avg per cond}} = \dfrac{\Phi P N}{60} \quad (\text{Volts})
$$

---

**Step 4: Total EMF across brush terminals**

The $Z$ conductors are divided among $A$ parallel paths. Each parallel path has $Z/A$ conductors in series.

$$
E_{\text{series per path}} = \dfrac{Z}{A} \times e_{\text{avg per cond}} = \dfrac{Z}{A} \times \dfrac{\Phi P N}{60}
$$

> *Reasoning:* Since paths are in parallel, the terminal voltage equals the EMF across **one** path.

---

**Step 5: Final EMF Equation**

$$
E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N}{60} \cdot \dfrac{P}{A}
$$

Or, in the compact KTU 2024 board form:

$$
E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60\ A} \quad (\text{Volts})
$$

---

### Verification:

For a **wave-wound** machine, $A = 2$ → the equation reduces to:
$$
E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{120}
$$

For a **lap-wound** machine, $A = P$ → the equation reduces to:
$$
E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N}{60}
$$

Both match standard textbook results. ✔

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Commutator Pitch for Simplex Wave Winding

The construction of wave winding requires a specific **commutator pitch $Y_c$** that ensures the winding closes after passing through all poles.

### Given:

- $C$ = number of commutator segments
- $P$ = number of poles (must be even for simplex wave)

### Derivation:

**Step 1: Number of parallel paths in simplex wave winding**

$$
A = 2
$$

> *Reasoning:* A simplex wave winding connects one commutator segment to the next segment after passing **once** under a North and once under a South pole. Two such paths in parallel exist at all times.

---

**Step 2: Number of brushes needed**

$$
\text{Brushes} = A = 2 \quad \text{(one positive, one negative set)}
$$

> *Note:* Even with $P = 4, 6, 8$ poles, **only 2 sets of brushes** are needed because the winding progressively connects all points of the same polarity to one brush.

---

**Step 3: Commutator pitch**

The winding must connect $C$ segments, advancing $Y_c$ segments each time, after traversing all $P$ poles. For the winding to close properly:

$$
P \cdot Y_c = C \pm 1
$$

> *Reasoning:* After passing under $P/2$ North poles and $P/2$ South poles (total $P$ pole crossings), the winding must reach the segment **adjacent** to the start (either ahead by 1 or behind by 1, depending on direction).

---

**Step 4: Solve for $Y_c$**

$$
Y_c = \dfrac{C \pm 1}{\dfrac{P}{2}}
$$

The '**+**' gives **progressive winding**; '**−**' gives **retrogressive winding**.

> *Constraint:* $Y_c$ must be an integer for physical feasibility of the winding.

---

**Step 5: General form for simplex ($m = 1$) and duplex ($m = 2$)**

$$
Y_c = \dfrac{C \pm m}{\dfrac{P}{2}}
$$

where $m = 1$ for simplex, $m = 2$ for duplex (if a closed-form solution is desired).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Winding Construction Comparison

| Construction Parameter | Simplex Lap | Simplex Wave | Duplex Lap | Duplex Wave |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Parallel paths $A$ | $P$ | $2$ | $2P$ | $4$ |
| Number of brushes | $2P$ | $2$ | $2P$ | $4$ |
| Commutator pitch $Y_c$ | $\pm 1$ | $(C \pm 1)/(P/2)$ | $\pm 2$ | $(C \pm 2)/(P/2)$ |
| Current capacity | High | Moderate | Higher | High |
| Voltage rating | Low | High | Low | High |
| Typical application | Traction, mills | Exciter, small genset | Electroplating | Special high-current |
| Need equalizer rings? | Yes (large machines) | No | Yes | No |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since DC machine construction is inherently physical, the following Mermaid diagrams represent the **functional block-level architecture** mapping the interactions between parts, validated for syntax safety (alphanumeric node IDs only, no markdown inside labels).

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Main Constructional Architecture of a DC Machine

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Yoke Frame Cast Steel] --> B1[Pole Core Laminated Si Steel]
    B1 --> B2[Pole Shoe Flux Spreader]
    B2 --> B3[Field Winding Shunt or Series]
    A1 --> C1[End Cover Left]
    A1 --> C2[End Cover Right]
    C1 --> D1[Ball Bearing]
    C2 --> D2[Ball Bearing]
    D1 --> E1[Shaft Forged Steel]
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Armature Core Laminated Si Steel]
    F1 --> F2[Armature Winding Lap or Wave]
    F1 --> G1[Commutator Copper Segments Mica Insulated]
    G1 --> H1[Brushes Carbon or Graphite]
    H1 --> I1[Brush Holder Rocker Arm]
    I1 --> A1
    F1 --> J1[Air Gap Working Flux Path]
    B2 --> J1
    A1 --> K1[Interpoles Series With Armature]
    K1 --> F1
```

## 4.2 Flow Diagram — Functional Path of Magnetic Flux & Current

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Field Winding Excited] --> P2[Main Flux Phi Produced]
    P2 --> P3[Crosses Air Gap]
    P3 --> P4[Enters Armature Core]
    P4 --> P5[Flux Density B in Teeth and Core]
    P5 --> P6[Induces EMF in Conductors]
    P6 --> P7[EMF Appears at Commutator Segments]
    P7 --> P8[Brushes Collect EMF]
    P8 --> P9[External Load Current I]
    P9 --> P10[Motor Mode Torque Produced]
    P10 --> P11[Mechanical Rotation of Shaft]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Block Layout — Physical Cross-Section (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Z1[Stator Side Left] --> Z2[Left Main Pole N]
    Z2 --> Z3[Left Interpole Np]
    Z3 --> Z4[Air Gap Working Zone]
    Z4 --> Z5[Armature Core Rotating]
    Z5 --> Z6[Armature Slots Conductors Inside]
    Z5 --> Z7[Commutator Right End]
    Z7 --> Z8[Right Brush Positive]
    Z6 --> Z9[Commutator Connection Risers]
    Z5 --> Z10[Right Interpole Sp]
    Z10 --> Z11[Right Main Pole S]
    Z11 --> Z12[Stator Side Right]
    Z12 --> Z1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagrams reflect the **functional and topological arrangement** of DC machine parts. In physical cross-section, the North and South poles alternate around the inner periphery of the yoke, with the armature occupying the central rotating region and the commutator mounted on the shaft extension at one end.

## 4.4 Constructional Sub-Assembly Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT[DC Machine] --> STATOR[Stator Sub-Assembly]
    ROOT --> ROTOR[Rotor Sub-Assembly]
    ROOT --> AUX[Auxiliary Sub-Assembly]
    STATOR --> S1[Yoke]
    STATOR --> S2[Main Pole Cores]
    STATOR --> S3[Field Windings]
    STATOR --> S4[Interpoles]
    STATOR --> S5[Brush Holder Rocker]
    ROTOR --> R1[Armature Core]
    ROTOR --> R2[Armature Winding]
    ROTOR --> R3[Commutator]
    ROTOR --> R4[Shaft]
    AUX --> A1[Bearings]
    AUX --> A2[End Covers]
    AUX --> A3[Terminal Box]
    AUX --> A4[Cooling Fan]
    AUX --> A5[Name Plate]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> *List the main parts of a DC machine with their functions.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Yoke / Frame** – Provides mechanical support and acts as a flux return path.
2. **Pole Core and Pole Shoe** – Support field winding and spread flux uniformly across the air-gap.
3. **Field Winding** – Produces the main working magnetic flux.
4. **Armature Core** – Houses armature conductors; made of laminated silicon steel to reduce eddy loss.
5. **Armature Winding** – Carries induced current / driving current.
6. **Commutator** – Mechanical rectifier that converts AC to DC (in generator) or DC to AC (in motor).
7. **Brushes and Brush Gear** – Transfer current between rotating commutator and stationary external circuit.
8. **Bearings, Shaft, End Covers** – Mechanical support, rotation, and enclosure.

*(Any **six** parts with functions = 3 marks; each pair = 0.5 mark.)*

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
> *Compare lap winding and wave winding in a DC machine (any 4 points).*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Sl. No. | Lap Winding | Wave Winding |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Parallel paths $A = P$ | Parallel paths $A = 2$ |
| 2 | Used for high current, low voltage | Used for high voltage, low current |
| 3 | Requires $2P$ brushes | Requires only 2 brushes |
| 4 | Needs equalizer rings in large machines | Does not need equalizer rings |
| 5 | Simplex form has $Y_c = \pm 1$ | Simplex form has $Y_c = (C \pm 1)/(P/2)$ |

*(Any 4 points × 0.75 mark each = 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction of a DC machine** in detail, naming all major parts and their materials.
> **(b)** Derive the **EMF equation** of a DC machine and state the meaning of each term. A 4-pole lap-wound DC generator has 480 conductors and runs at 1000 rpm. The flux per pole is 0.05 Wb. Find the EMF generated.

#### Solution:

**(a) Construction (7 marks):**

The construction of a DC machine consists of the following major parts:

1. **Yoke** — Made of cast iron (or cast steel/fabricated steel in modern machines). Provides mechanical support and acts as the outer magnetic flux return path.
2. **Pole Cores and Pole Shoes** — Made of laminated silicon steel (0.5 mm laminations). The pole shoe spreads the flux uniformly and supports the field winding.
3. **Field Winding** — Wound on bobbins/former; either shunt (fine wire) or series (thick wire).
4. **Armature Core** — Built of 0.35–0.5 mm thick laminated silicon steel discs, slotted to house the conductors; provides low-reluctance path and reduces eddy losses.
5. **Armature Winding** — Lap or wave type; insulated copper conductors placed in slots.
6. **Commutator** — Hard-drawn copper segments insulated by mica; clamped by V-rings. Acts as a mechanical rectifier.
7. **Brushes and Brush Gear** — Carbon/graphite brushes held in brush holders mounted on a rocker arm.
8. **Shaft, Bearings, End Covers** — Forged steel shaft, ball/roller bearings, cast iron end covers.
9. **Interpoles** — Small auxiliary poles between main poles, wound in series with armature for commutation.

*(Neat sketch = 2 marks; List + materials + functions = 5 marks.)*

---

**(b) EMF Equation Derivation and Numerical (7 marks):**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Flux cut per revolution = $P \Phi$

Flux cut per second = $\dfrac{P \Phi N}{60}$

EMF per conductor (Faraday's Law) = $\dfrac{P \Phi N}{60}$

Conductors per parallel path = $\dfrac{Z}{A}$

Total EMF:
$$
E_g = \dfrac{Z}{A} \times \dfrac{P \Phi N}{60} = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60\ A}
$$

**Numerical (3 marks):**

Given:
- $P = 4$
- Lap wound → $A = P = 4$
- $Z = 480$
- $N = 1000$ rpm
- $\Phi = 0.05$ Wb

Substituting:
$$
E_g = \dfrac{0.05 \times 480 \times 1000 \times 4}{60 \times 4}
$$

Simplifying:
$$
E_g = \dfrac{0.05 \times 480 \times 1000}{60}
$$

$$
E_g = \dfrac{24000}{60} = 400\ \text{V}
$$

**Answer: Generated EMF = 400 V** ✔

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**
> **(a)** Explain the **construction and types of armature windings** in DC machines. Distinguish between simplex lap and simplex wave windings in detail.
> **(b)** A 6-pole, simplex wave-wound DC generator has 60 slots, 12 conductors per slot, and runs at 800 rpm. The flux per pole is 0.04 Wb. Compute the generated EMF. Comment on the number of brushes required and justify why equalizer rings are not necessary.

#### Solution:

**(a) Armature Windings (7 marks):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
The armature winding is a **closed winding** formed by interconnecting coils placed in slots. Each coil has one or more turns, with two active sides per coil embedded in the slots. The coil ends are connected to commutator segments.

**Types (2 marks):**
1. **Lap Winding** — The commutator pitch $Y_c = \pm 1$. Number of parallel paths $A = P$.
   - Simple Lap, Duplex Lap, Multiplex Lap.
2. **Wave Winding** — The commutator pitch $Y_c = (C \pm 1)/(P/2)$. Number of parallel paths $A = 2$.
   - Simple Wave, Duplex Wave, Multiplex Wave.

**Distinction Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Simplex Lap | Simplex Wave |
|---|---|---|
| Parallel paths | $A = P$ | $A = 2$ |
| Brushes | $2P$ | $2$ |
| Commutator pitch | $\pm 1$ | $(C \pm 1)/(P/2)$ |
| Use | High current | High voltage |
| Equalizer rings | Required for large P | Not required |

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute total conductors $Z$:**
$$
Z = \text{slots} \times \text{conductors per slot} = 60 \times 12 = 720
$$

**Step 2: Number of parallel paths $A$:**
For simplex wave, $A = 2$.

**Step 3: Apply EMF equation:**
$$
E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60\ A}
$$

$$
E_g = \dfrac{0.04 \times 720 \times 800 \times 6}{60 \times 2}
$$

**Step 4: Simplify:**
$$
E_g = \dfrac{0.04 \times 720 \times 800 \times 6}{120}
$$

$$
E_g = \dfrac{0.04 \times 720 \times 40}{1} \quad \left[\text{Since } \frac{800 \times 6}{120} = 40\right]
$$

$$
E_g = 0.04 \times 720 \times 40 = 1152\ \text{V}
$$

**Answer: Generated EMF = 1152 V** ✔

**Brush Count Justification (1 mark):**
Simplex wave winding requires **only 2 sets of brushes** regardless of the number of poles. This is because the winding is so arranged that all points of the same polarity (positive or negative) come under a single brush set.

**Equalizer Rings Justification (1 mark):**
Equalizer rings are **not needed** in wave winding because all conductors in any one parallel path are not under the influence of the same pole at the same time — therefore no circulating currents are induced due to unequal flux densities.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Construction Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting materials** — Examiner deducts 1–2 marks if you list parts but not their materials (e.g., silicon steel for armature, cast iron for yoke).
> 2. **Confusing lap & wave formulas** — Many students write $A = 2$ for lap and $A = P$ for wave. **Memorize with mnemonics: "L**ap = **L**ots of paths (P); **W**ave = **W**hich is 2."
> 3. **Skipping the sketch** — In Part B, a labelled diagram is **mandatory** for full marks. If absent, deduct 2 marks.
> 4. **Wrong $A$ substitution in EMF equation** — Always confirm lap/wave before substituting.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency** — Use $N$ in **rpm** (the formula already has /60). Mixing rad/s loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Not mentioning lamination thickness** — Examiner expects 0.35–0.5 mm lamination thickness in the armature core answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- DC machine = **Stator (field system)** + **Rotor (armature)** + **Commutator–Brush assembly**.
- **Yoke** → cast iron / cast steel → mechanical support + flux return.
- **Pole core & shoe** → laminated Si steel → flux spreading.
- **Armature core** → laminated Si steel (0.35–0.5 mm) → low eddy loss.
- **Lap winding** → $A = P$, $Y_c = \pm 1$, used for high current.
- **Wave winding** → $A = 2$, $Y_c = (C \pm 1)/(P/2)$, used for high voltage.
- **EMF equation** (the most tested formula in KTU 2024):
  $$
  E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60\ A}
  $$
- **Commutator** → copper segments + mica insulation.
- **Brushes** → carbon / graphite, mounted on rocker arm.
- **Interpoles** → in series with armature; neutralize reactance EMF.
- **Frequency of EMF in conductors:** $f = PN/120$.
- **Torque equation:** $T_a = \Phi Z I_a / 2\pi A$.
- **Conductors per path** = $Z/A$; **Coils** = $C = Z/2$ (for single-turn coils).
- Always draw a **labelled diagram** for full marks in Part B.
- Always state **materials** alongside the part name.
- For KTU 2024 NEP-aligned scheme, emphasize **rotating AC nature of EMF** vs. **DC output** through commutator action — this conceptual clarity scores high.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Core and Shell Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT302 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES & DRIVES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Core and Shell Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Transformer Construction Types

In the design of static electromagnetic energy conversion devices (transformers), the magnetic core and the associated windings can be physically arranged in two fundamentally distinct topological configurations. Under the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **Electrical Machines & Drives (PCMRT302)**, these are classified as:

### **A. Core Type Construction**
A transformer is designated as **Core Type** when the windings (primary and secondary) **surround a significant portion of the magnetic core**. The core forms the central structural spine, and the copper coils are wrapped around the two vertical limbs of the rectangular core. The magnetic flux path is primarily through a **single closed loop** of laminated steel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Exact):** "A core type transformer is one in which the windings envelop (surround) a considerable part of the magnetic core, with the core forming a single rectangular magnetic circuit."

### **B. Shell Type Construction**
A transformer is designated as **Shell Type** when the magnetic core **surrounds a significant portion of the windings**. The core forms a protective outer magnetic "shell," and the windings are sandwiched (sandwiched construction) between the core limbs. The magnetic flux divides into **two parallel paths** through the outer core limbs, completing the magnetic circuit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Exact):** "A shell type transformer is one in which the magnetic core envelops a major part of the windings, providing a return flux path through outer limbs and creating a double magnetic circuit."

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

### **Analogy 1: The Bookshelf vs. The Box**
- **Core Type** is like a **bookshelf with books on both sides of a central vertical divider**. The divider (core) is the structural spine, and the books (windings) sit on either side. Easy to manufacture, modular, and accessible.
- **Shell Type** is like **a sandwich placed inside a tightly fitting protective case**. The sandwich (winding) is fully enclosed by the bread slices and outer walls (core) on multiple sides. It is mechanically robust and provides excellent short-circuit withstand strength.

### **Analogy 2: Water Flow Through Pipes**
- Imagine magnetic flux as **water** flowing in a closed pipe.
- In **Core Type**, water flows through **one single thick pipe (single flux path)**.
- In **Shell Type**, water enters a junction and **splits into two parallel smaller pipes (two parallel flux paths)**, recombining at the exit. This is more efficient for high-capacity flows.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Material Standards

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard Values to Memorize:**
> - **Flux Density (B_m)**: **1.1 to 1.4 Tesla** for CRGO (Cold Rolled Grain Oriented) silicon steel laminations.
> - **Lamination Thickness**: **0.35 mm** (standard) or **0.27 mm** (high-grade).
> - **Stacking Factor (K_i or S.F)**: **0.90 to 0.95** (typically 0.9 for standard design, 0.95 for closely stacked).
> - **Lamination Insulation**: Varnish or oxide coating (one-side or both-side).
> - **Window Space Factor (K_w)**: **0.15 to 0.5** depending on conductor size and insulation.

---

## 1.4 Classification Summary Table

| Parameter | Core Type | Shell Type |
|-----------|-----------|------------|
| Winding Position | Surrounds core | Surrounded by core |
| Number of Limbs | 2 (with yokes) | 3 or more (central + outer) |
| Flux Paths | 1 | 2 (divided) |
| Cooling Surface | More (better) | Less (relatively) |
| Mechanical Strength | Lower | Higher |
| Repair & Maintenance | Easier | Difficult |
| Typical Application | Distribution & Power | High-current, special transformers |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Core Type Construction — Detailed Analysis

### **2.1.1 Constructional Sequence (Step-by-Step)**
1. **Lamination Stamping**: Silicon steel sheets (typically **0.35 mm** thick) are stamped into **L-shaped strips** (for single-phase) or **E-I strips** (more commonly).
2. **Insulation Coating**: Each lamination is coated with **varnish or paper** on one side to minimize eddy current losses. The coating thickness is typically **0.025 mm**.
3. **Stacking**: The L-shaped laminations are stacked alternately to form a rectangular frame with two vertical limbs and two horizontal yokes.
4. **Winding Placement**: **Half of the primary and half of the secondary windings** are placed on **each limb** (split winding arrangement). This is done to:
   - Reduce leakage flux.
   - Utilize both limbs effectively.
   - Balance the magnetic circuit.
5. **Clamping & Tanking**: The core is clamped with steel frames, then placed in a tank filled with **mineral oil** for cooling and insulation.

### **2.1.2 Why Half-Half Winding?**
- In single-phase core type, the primary is split as **N1/2 + N1/2** on two limbs, similarly for the secondary. The total series turns remain **N1** and **N2**.
- This **distributed winding** arrangement drastically reduces the **magnetizing reactance leakage** and ensures uniform flux distribution.

### **2.1.3 Why L-shaped (or E-I) Laminations?**
- The single-frame **rectangular core** cannot be assembled around pre-wound cylindrical coils. Hence, the core must be built in **two pieces** (L-L or E-I), with the coils pre-wound on a former, and then the core is assembled around them.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The **L-L type** and **E-I type** are the two most common stamping patterns. For 3-phase core type, **E-E-I** laminations are used to build a 3-limb core.

---

## 2.2 Shell Type Construction — Detailed Analysis

### **2.2.1 Constructional Sequence (Step-by-Step)**
1. **Lamination Stamping**: Silicon steel sheets are stamped into **E-shaped** and **I-shaped** strips.
2. **Pre-wound Coils**: The primary and secondary windings are wound as **thin cylindrical pancakes** (sandwiched layers) on a former.
3. **Layered Assembly**: The laminations are stacked such that the **E and I strips interleave** around the windings, creating a **central limb** carrying the entire flux and **two outer limbs** each carrying half the flux.
4. **Flux Distribution**:
   - Central limb flux: **Φ**
   - Each outer limb flux: **Φ/2**
   - The two outer fluxes recombine at the top and bottom yokes to form the closed magnetic circuit.
5. **Sandwich Winding**: The primary and secondary are arranged in **alternating sandwich layers** (P-S-P-S or S-P-S-P) to minimize leakage reactance.

### **2.2.2 Why Sandwich Winding?**
- Placing primary and secondary layers alternately **reduces the distance between them**, which **decreases leakage flux** (since leakage reactance `X_l ∝ distance²`).
- This is critical for **high-voltage, low-voltage rectifier transformers** and **welding transformers** where low leakage is mandatory.

### **2.2.3 Mechanical Superiority**
- The core completely **encloses the windings**, providing excellent protection against **radial short-circuit forces**.
- This is why **shell type** is preferred for **very high current** applications (e.g., electric arc furnace transformers, traction transformers).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they form 70% of the numerical answers in KTU University Exams.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Description | Units | Application |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_m$ | EMF per turn (RMS) | Volts | EMF Equation |
| 2 | $\Phi_m = B_m \times A_i$ | Flux per limb | Weber | Flux calculation |
| 3 | $A_i = a \times b$ | Gross iron area (rectangular) | m² | Core area |
| 4 | $A_i' = 0.9 \times A_i$ | Net iron area (after lamination) | m² | Effective area |
| 5 | $A_w = h \times w$ | Window area | m² | Window dimension |
| 6 | $Q = 3.33 \, f \, B_m \, A_i \, A_w \, K_w \, K_i \times 10^{-3}$ | Output rating (1-phase core type) | kVA | Output equation |
| 7 | $Q = 3 \times 3.33 \, f \, B_m \, A_i \, A_w \, K_w \, K_i \times 10^{-3}$ | Output rating (3-phase core type) | kVA | Output equation |
| 8 | $N = \frac{E}{4.44 \, f \, B_m \, A_i}$ | Number of turns | dimensionless | Turns calculation |
| 9 | $V_t = \frac{E}{4.44 \, f \, B_m \, A_i}$ | Volts per turn | V/turn | Design constant |
| 10 | $I_{fl} = \frac{Q \times 10^3}{V_{rated}}$ | Full-load current | A | Current rating |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Output Equation Derivation:** The constant **3.33** arises from $2.22 \times 1.5$, where 2.22 comes from $\sqrt{2} \times \pi$ in the EMF equation and 1.5 is the empirical design constant for the relationship between window area and gross core area.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application | Preferred Type |
|-------|-------------|---------------|
| Power Distribution | Pole-mounted distribution transformers (≤ 500 kVA) | Core Type |
| Power Generation | Generator step-up transformers (GSU) | Core Type |
| High-current Industrial | Arc furnace transformers, traction | Shell Type |
| High-voltage Testing | Cascade testing transformers | Shell Type |
| Electronics | Small signal/audio transformers | Core Type |
| Welding | Low-voltage high-current welding transformers | Shell Type |
