# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Voltage Transformation Ratio. State its significance in the operation of a transformer.**

**Model Answer:**

The Voltage Transformation Ratio (K) is defined as the ratio of the number of turns in the secondary winding to the number of turns in the primary winding of a transformer.

$$
K = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{I_1}{I_2}
$$

**Significance:**
1. It determines whether a transformer acts as a step-up (K > 1) or step-down (K < 1) device.
2. It is the primary design parameter that allows transformers to step up voltage for efficient long-distance power transmission and step it down for safe distribution to consumers.
3. It governs the current and voltage relationships on both sides without any physical electrical connection (only magnetic coupling).
4. It enables power conservation — the apparent power rating (in VA) remains nearly identical on both sides in an ideal transformer.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Derive the relation $V_1 / V_2 = N_1 / N_2$ for an ideal single-phase transformer.**

**Model Answer:**

For an ideal transformer with sinusoidal mutual flux $\phi = \phi_m \sin(\omega t)$:

By Faraday's Law, the EMF induced in the primary winding:
$$
e_1 = N_1 \frac{d\phi}{dt} = N_1 \, \phi_m \, \omega \cos(\omega t)
$$

The EMF induced in the secondary winding:
$$
e_2 = N_2 \frac{d\phi}{dt} = N_2 \, \phi_m \, \omega \cos(\omega t)
$$

Taking the ratio:
$$
\frac{e_1}{e_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2}
$$

For an ideal transformer, the terminal voltages equal the induced EMFs ($V_1 = e_1$, $V_2 = e_2$). Hence:
$$
\frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2}
$$

This proves that the voltage ratio equals the turns ratio.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**(a)** A single-phase, 50 Hz transformer has 800 turns on the primary and 1600 turns on the secondary. The primary is connected to a 220 V AC supply. Calculate:
   1. The Voltage Transformation Ratio (K)
   2. The secondary voltage
   3. The maximum flux in the core
   4. The primary current when the secondary supplies a 5 A load. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A 25 kVA, 1100/220 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer has 80 turns on the secondary. Calculate:
   1. The primary and secondary full-load currents
   2. The transformation ratio K
   3. The number of primary turns
   4. The maximum flux in the core. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Solution:**

**Given:** $N_1 = 800$, $N_2 = 1600$, $V_1 = 220$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $I_2 = 5$ A.

**(a.1) Transformation Ratio:**
$$
K = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{1600}{800} = 2
$$
This is a **step-up transformer** (since K > 1).

**(a.2) Secondary Voltage:**
$$
V_2 = K \times V_1 = 2 \times 220 = 440 \text{ V}
$$

**(a.3) Maximum Flux:**

Using the EMF equation $V_1 = 4.44 \, f \, \phi_m \, N_1$:
$$
220 = 4.44 \times 50 \times \phi_m \times 800
$$
$$
220 = 177600 \, \phi_m
$$
$$
\phi_m = \frac{220}{177600} = 1.2387 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Wb} \approx 1.24 \text{ mWb}
$$

**(a.4) Primary Current:**
$$
I_1 = K \times I_2 = 2 \times 5 = 10 \text{ A}
$$

---

**(b) Solution:**

**Given:** $S = 25$ kVA, $V_1 = 1100$ V, $V_2 = 220$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $N_2 = 80$.

**(b.1) Full-Load Currents:**
$$
I_1 = \frac{S}{V_1} = \frac{25000}{1100} = 22.727 \text{ A}
$$
$$
I_2 = \frac{S}{V_2} = \frac{25000}{220} = 113.636 \text{ A}
$$

**(b.2) Transformation Ratio:**
$$
K = \frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{220}{1100} = 0.2
$$

**(b.3) Primary Turns:**
$$
N_1 = \frac{N_2}{K} = \frac{80}{0.2} = 400 \text{ turns}
$$

**(b.4) Maximum Flux:**

Using the EMF equation on the secondary:
$$
V_2 = 4.44 \, f \, \phi_m \, N_2
$$
$$
220 = 4.44 \times 50 \times \phi_m \times 80
$$
$$
220 = 17760 \, \phi_m
$$
$$
\phi_m = \frac{220}{17760} = 0.01239 \text{ Wb} \approx 12.39 \text{ mWb}
$$

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the construction and working principle of a single-phase transformer. Derive the EMF equation $E = 4.44 \, f \, \phi_m \, N$. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A 100 kVA, 4400/220 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer has 60 turns on the secondary. Calculate:
   1. The transformation ratio K
   2. The number of primary turns
   3. The maximum flux in the core
   4. The primary and secondary full-load currents. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Construction & EMF Equation:**

**Construction (brief):** A single-phase transformer consists of a laminated silicon steel core (to reduce eddy current losses) with two windings — primary (input) and secondary (output) — wound on a common magnetic circuit. The windings are insulated from each other and from the core.

**Working Principle:** When an alternating voltage is applied to the primary, it draws a small magnetizing current that produces an alternating flux in the core. This flux links with both windings and induces EMFs in them according to Faraday's Law.

**EMF Derivation:**

Assume sinusoidal flux: $\phi = \phi_m \sin(\omega t)$

Instantaneous EMF in a winding of N turns:
$$
e = N \frac{d\phi}{dt} = N \, \phi_m \, \omega \cos(\omega t)
$$

Maximum EMF:
$$
E_m = N \, \phi_m \, \omega = 2\pi f N \phi_m
$$

RMS EMF:
$$
E_{rms} = \frac{E_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2\pi f N \phi_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2} \, \pi \, f \, N \, \phi_m
$$
$$
E_{rms} = 4.44 \, f \, \phi_m \, N
$$

---

**(b) Solution:**

**Given:** $S = 100$ kVA, $V_1 = 4400$ V, $V_2 = 220$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $N_2 = 60$ turns.

**(b.1) Transformation Ratio:**
$$
K = \frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{220}{4400} = 0.05
$$

**(b.2) Primary Turns:**
$$
N_1 = \frac{N_2}{K} = \frac{60}{0.05} = 1200 \text{ turns}
$$

**(b.3) Maximum Flux:**

Using $V_1 = 4.44 \, f \, \phi_m \, N_1$:
$$
4400 = 4.44 \times 50 \times \phi_m \times 1200
$$
$$
4400 = 266400 \, \phi_m
$$
$$
\phi_m = \frac{4400}{266400} = 0.01652 \text{ Wb} \approx 16.52 \text{ mWb}
$$

**(b.4) Full-Load Currents:**
$$
I_1 = \frac{S}{V_1} = \frac{100000}{4400} = 22.727 \text{ A}
$$
$$
I_2 = \frac{S}{V_2} = \frac{100000}{220} = 454.545 \text{ A}
$$

**Verification using current ratio:** $I_1 = K \times I_2 = 0.05 \times 454.545 = 22.727$ A ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Notation Confusion:** Students often confuse $K = N_2/N_1$ with $a = N_1/N_2$. The final numerical answer may be inverted by a factor of $K^2$. **Always verify** whether your answer gives $V_2 > V_1$ or $V_2 < V_1$ consistent with the problem statement.
>
> 2. **Units in EMF Equation:** Forgetting that $V_1$ must be in **Volts** (not kV) when using the 4.44 constant. If $V_1 = 6.6$ kV is given, either convert to 6600 V first OR adjust the constant accordingly.
>
> 3. **Power vs Apparent Power:** kVA is apparent power. To find currents, use $I = S/V$, NOT $I = P/V$ (where P is real power in kW).
>
> 4. **Three-Phase Factor:** In delta-star or star-delta, remember to divide the line voltage by $\sqrt{3}$ to get the per-phase voltage.
>
> 5. **Significant Figures:** KTU expects 3-4 significant figures in final answers — avoid rounding too early in intermediate steps.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Voltage Transformation Ratio**

- **Definition:** $K = N_2/N_1 = V_2/V_1 = I_1/I_2$ for an ideal transformer.
- **Step-up:** K > 1, voltage increases from primary to secondary.
- **Step-down:** K < 1, voltage decreases from primary to secondary.
- **Isolation:** K = 1, voltage unchanged (used for safety).
- **Universal EMF Equation:** $E = 4.44 \, f \, \phi_m \, N$ — the **bridge** between voltage rating and core design.
- **Power Conservation:** $V_1 I_1 = V_2 I_2$ in the ideal case.
- **Current Inversion:** When voltage steps up, current steps down proportionally (and vice versa).
- **Notation Trap:** KTU books may use **K** or **a** — always confirm the textbook convention.
- **Three-Phase Factor:** Always remember the $\sqrt{3}$ and per-phase distinction.
- **Iron Core Material:** Standard CRGO silicon steel lamination; flux density B typically 1.1–1.4 Tesla.
- **Frequency Standard:** India uses **50 Hz**; ensure correct value in EMF equation.
- **Final Answer Rule:** Always quote numerical answers with **correct units** (V, A, Wb, mWb, turns).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Ideal Transformer, Transformer on No-Load, Short Circuit and Open Circuit Test on Transformer (Basic Concept Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT302 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES & DRIVES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concept of Ideal Transformer, Transformer on No-Load, Short Circuit and Open Circuit Test on Transformer (Basic Concept Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Ideal Transformer — Formal KTU Definition

An **Ideal Transformer** is a hypothetical, lossless static electrical device that transfers alternating-current (AC) electrical energy from one circuit to another through **electromagnetic induction**, without any form of power loss. By KTU 2024 Scheme convention, an ideal transformer satisfies the following three simultaneous conditions:

1. **Zero Ohmic Resistance** in both primary and secondary windings — meaning **no copper (I²R) loss**.
2. **Infinite Magnetic Permeability** of the core material — meaning **zero magnetizing current** is required to establish the working flux.
3. **Zero Leakage Flux** — the entire flux produced by the primary links the secondary, and vice-versa. No stray flux lines close through air.

Under these ideal conditions, the transformer becomes a **pure, lossless, perfectly coupled** electromagnetic coupler, and the law of conservation of energy is satisfied exactly:

$$P_{in} = V_1 I_1 \cos\phi_1 = V_2 I_2 \cos\phi_2 = P_{out}$$

The efficiency of such a device is therefore $\eta = 100\%$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Syllabus Highlight:** The ideal transformer is treated as a *pedagogical starting point*. Every real transformer is analyzed by adding controlled "imperfections" (winding resistance, leakage reactance, core loss) to the ideal model.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water-Pressure Gearbox"

Imagine a hydraulic gearbox connecting two water pipes of different diameters. The water (electrical energy) entering the large pipe (primary) under low pressure and high flow rate emerges from the small pipe (secondary) as **high pressure, low flow rate** — without a single drop being lost. The gearbox has no friction, no leaks, and no heat.

This is precisely the ideal transformer:
- **Voltage (Pressure)** and **Current (Flow rate)** trade-off via the **turns ratio** (gear ratio).
- The **magnetic core** is the frictionless shaft that transmits power.
- **Frequency** remains unchanged (the water's pulsation rate).

## 1.3 Turns Ratio — The Master Variable

Let the primary have $N_1$ turns and the secondary $N_2$ turns. The **turns ratio** (also called transformation ratio) is defined as:

$$a = \frac{N_1}{N_2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> - If $a > 1$ → **Step-Down** transformer ($V_2 < V_1$, $I_2 > I_1$).
> - If $a < 1$ → **Step-Up** transformer ($V_2 > V_1$, $I_2 < I_1$).
> - If $a = 1$ → **Isolation** (1:1) transformer.

The standard rated frequency in India is **50 Hz**, and standard commercial distribution voltages are **415 V (3-phase) / 240 V (1-phase)** on the LV side.

## 1.4 Transformer on No-Load — Physical Setup

When the secondary terminals of a real transformer are kept **open-circuited** and rated sinusoidal AC voltage $V_1$ is applied to the primary, the transformer is said to be operating under **"No-Load"** (or **"Open-Circuited Secondary"**) conditions.

Under no-load:
- A small current $\mathbf{I_0}$ (typically **2% to 10%** of full-load current) flows in the primary.
- This current has two components: the **magnetizing component $I_m$** (purely reactive, $90°$ lagging flux $\phi$) and the **core-loss component $I_w$** (in phase with $V_1$).
- A sinusoidal alternating flux $\phi(t)$ is established in the core, with peak value $\phi_m$.
- No current flows in the secondary ($I_2 = 0$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Terminology:** The no-load current $I_0$ is also called the **exciting current** or **magnetizing current** in older textbooks. The KTU 2024 scheme uses **"no-load current $I_0$"** consistently.

## 1.5 The Two Foundational Tests — Why We Perform Them

KTU 2024 Module 1 mandates a *basic conceptual understanding* of two standard tests used to determine the equivalent-circuit parameters of a real transformer:

| Test Name | Purpose | Key Quantity Measured |
|---|---|---|
| **Open-Circuit (OC) Test** | To determine **no-load (core) losses** and the **shunt-branch parameters** $R_0, X_0$ | No-load power $P_0$ (W), no-load current $I_0$ (A), rated voltage $V_1$ (V) |
| **Short-Circuit (SC) Test** | To determine **full-load copper losses** and the **equivalent series impedance** $R_{01}, X_{01}$ | Short-circuit power $P_{sc}$ (W), short-circuit current $I_{sc}$ (A), short-circuit voltage $V_{sc}$ (V) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these tests?** Measuring the *entire* loss of a large transformer under direct loading is expensive, wasteful, and often impossible (you'd need a giant resistive load bank). These two indirect tests replicate loading conditions using **fractional rated instruments** on the LV/HV side, making them efficient and economical.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ideal Transformer — Governing Relationships

For an ideal transformer, three fundamental relations hold simultaneously, governed by **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** and the **Law of Conservation of Energy (Ampere-Turns Balance)**:

**Relation 1 — Voltage Ratio (from Faraday's Law):**

The EMF induced in a coil of $N$ turns linking an alternating flux $\phi(t) = \phi_m \sin(\omega t)$ is $e = -N \dfrac{d\phi}{dt}$. The RMS magnitude of the induced EMF per phase is therefore:

$$E = 4.44 \, f \, \phi_m \, N$$

Applying this to primary and secondary:

$$\frac{E_1}{E_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = a \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{V_1}{V_2} = a$$

> *Why?* In an ideal transformer with zero winding resistance, the applied voltage $V_1$ exactly equals the back-EMF $E_1$, and the secondary terminal voltage $V_2$ equals the secondary induced EMF $E_2$.

**Relation 2 — Current Ratio (from MMF Balance):**

The net MMF in the core must remain zero (since $\phi$ is set by $V_1$ and $f$). Thus $N_1 I_1 = N_2 I_2$:

$$\frac{I_1}{I_2} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{1}{a} \quad\Rightarrow\quad I_1 \, a = I_2$$

**Relation 3 — Impedance Reflection (from Voltage & Current Ratios):**

If a load impedance $Z_L$ is connected to the secondary, the impedance "seen" by the primary source is:

$$Z_{in} = \frac{V_1}{I_1} = \frac{a \, V_2}{I_2 / a} = a^2 \, Z_L$$

This phenomenon is called **impedance transformation** or **impedance reflection** — a critical concept in audio matching, amplifier design, and power-system analysis.

## 2.2 The Real Transformer on No-Load — Decomposition of $I_0$

A real transformer has finite core permeability and finite core loss. The no-load current $I_0$ is no longer purely magnetizing. It is resolved into two orthogonal components on the no-load phasor diagram:

- **$I_w$ — Core-loss (working) component:** In phase with applied voltage $V_1$. It supplies the hysteresis and eddy-current losses in the core.
- **$I_m$ — Magnetizing component:** Lags $V_1$ by $90°$ (actually lags flux $\phi$ by $90°$, and flux lags $V_1$ by $90°$ in a lossless inductor, so $I_m$ lags $V_1$ by $90°$ in the lossless component analysis). It establishes the working flux.

The magnitudes are related by:

$$I_0 = \sqrt{I_w^2 + I_m^2}$$

The **no-load power factor** is:

$$\cos\phi_0 = \frac{I_w}{I_0} = \frac{P_0}{V_1 I_0}$$

where $P_0$ is the total no-load power read by the wattmeter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The no-load power factor of a typical power transformer is in the range **0.15 to 0.30** (very poor, lagging). This is because the magnetizing component $I_m$ dominates over the small core-loss component $I_w$. Power engineers account for this when designing power-factor correction banks at substations.

## 2.3 Equivalent Circuit Parameters (No-Load Side)

The no-load branch of the transformer equivalent circuit is a **parallel R-L combination** representing the core:

- **$R_0$** — Equivalent resistance representing total core (iron) loss.
- **$X_0$** — Equivalent magnetizing reactance representing flux production.

These are computed from the OC test as:

$$R_0 = \frac{V_1^2}{P_0} \qquad\qquad X_0 = \frac{V_1^2}{Q_0} = \frac{V_1^2}{\sqrt{(V_1 I_0)^2 - P_0^2}}$$

The **no-load reactive power** consumed is $Q_0 = V_1 I_0 \sin\phi_0 = \sqrt{(V_1 I_0)^2 - P_0^2}$.

## 2.4 Short-Circuit Test — Series Parameters

When the secondary is shorted and a *reduced* voltage (typically **5%–10%** of rated) is applied to circulate full-load current, the equivalent circuit reduces to a simple **series impedance** $R_{01} + jX_{01}$ (since the shunt branch $R_0 \parallel jX_0$ is negligible at this low voltage).

$$R_{01} = \frac{P_{sc}}{I_{sc}^2} \qquad\qquad Z_{01} = \frac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}} \qquad\qquad X_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^2 - R_{01}^2}$$

## 2.5 ⚡ KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Variable Definition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = 4.44 \, f \, \phi_m \, N$ | Induced EMF per phase (RMS), $f$ = frequency, $\phi_m$ = peak flux, $N$ = turns | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = a$ | Voltage & turns ratio | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\dfrac{I_1}{I_2} = \dfrac{1}{a}$ | Current ratio (inverse of turns ratio) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $Z_{in} = a^2 \, Z_L$ | Impedance reflected to primary | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 5 | $I_0 = \sqrt{I_w^2 + I_m^2}$ | No-load current components | Amperes (A) |
| 6 | $\cos\phi_0 = \dfrac{P_0}{V_1 I_0}$ | No-load power factor | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $P_0 = V_{rated} \cdot I_0 \cdot \cos\phi_0$ | No-load (core) power input | Watts (W) |
| 8 | $R_0 = \dfrac{V_1^2}{P_0}$ | Core-loss resistance (OC Test) | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 9 | $X_0 = \dfrac{V_1}{\sqrt{I_0^2 - (P_0/V_1)^2}} = \dfrac{V_1}{I_m}$ | Magnetizing reactance (OC Test) | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 10 | $R_{01} = \dfrac{P_{sc}}{I_{sc}^2}$ | Equivalent resistance (SC Test) | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 11 | $Z_{01} = \dfrac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}}$ | Equivalent impedance (SC Test) | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 12 | $X_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^2 - R_{01}^2}$ | Equivalent leakage reactance (SC Test) | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 13 | $\eta = \dfrac{x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi}{x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi + P_i + x^2 P_{cu,fl}}$ | Efficiency at fraction $x$ of full load | Per unit / % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe-Symbol Substitution Rule:** In KTU 2024 valuation scripts, modulus $\vert Z \vert$ is written as `$Z \vert$` or `$\lvert Z \rvert$` to avoid breaking markdown table structure. This is the standard exam-hall convention.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of These Concepts

- **Power Transmission Grid:** Step-up transformers (11 kV → 400 kV) at generating stations use the ideal-transformer turns-ratio principle to minimize $I^2R$ line losses.
- **Audio Matching:** Push-pull audio amplifiers use center-tapped transformers with exact 1:1+1 ratios to match speaker impedance to transistor output impedance.
- **Isolation & Safety:** 1:1 isolation transformers in hospitals and laboratories provide galvanic isolation for patient-connected medical equipment.
- **Relay & Protection Engineering:** Current Transformers (CT) and Potential Transformers (PT) used in protection relays are essentially *instrument transformers* that scale down high voltages/currents to safe, measurable values — the **OC and SC test parameters are crucial for their accuracy class rating**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the EMF Equation: $E = 4.44 \, f \, \phi_m \, N$

This is the most important derivation in Module 1. KTU examiners award full marks only if every step is shown.

**Step 1: Express the instantaneous flux.**

Let the working flux vary sinusoidally with peak value $\phi_m$ and angular frequency $\omega = 2\pi f$:

$$\phi(t) = \phi_m \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 2: Apply Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction.**

The instantaneous EMF induced in a coil of $N$ turns is:

$$e(t) = -N \frac{d\phi}{dt}$$

**Step 3: Differentiate the flux expression.**

$$\frac{d\phi}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt} \big[ \phi_m \sin(\omega t) \big] = \phi_m \, \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 4: Substitute and simplify.**

$$e(t) = -N \phi_m \, \omega \cos(\omega t) = N \phi_m \, \omega \sin(\omega t - 90°)$$

The peak (maximum) value of $e(t)$ is:

$$E_m = N \phi_m \, \omega = 2\pi f N \phi_m$$

**Step 5: Convert peak to RMS using the sinusoidal form factor.**

For a pure sinusoid, the RMS value is $E_{rms} = E_m / \sqrt{2}$. Substituting:

$$E_{rms} = \frac{2\pi f N \phi_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2\pi}{\sqrt{2}} \, f \, N \, \phi_m = \sqrt{2} \pi \, f \, N \, \phi_m$$

**Step 6: Combine constants to obtain the famous 4.44 form.**

Since $2\pi / \sqrt{2} = \pi \sqrt{2} = 4.442 \approx 4.44$:

$$\boxed{E_{rms} = 4.44 \, f \, \phi_m \, N}$$

> This is the EMF per phase for a sinusoidal flux waveform. **KTU 2024 expects this exact constant (4.44), not 4.444 — write it cleanly in your exam scripts.**

## 3.2 Derivation of the No-Load Phasor Relationship

**Step 1:** Assume the applied primary voltage is the reference phasor $\mathbf{V_1} = V_1 \angle 0°$.

**Step 2:** The flux $\phi$ lags $V_1$ by $90°$ in a lossless inductive circuit (since $V_1 = N_1 \, d\phi/dt$ implies flux integral lags voltage by $90°$).

**Step 3:** The magnetizing current $I_m$ is in phase with the flux $\phi$ (it is the current that *creates* the flux). Thus $I_m$ lags $V_1$ by $90°$.

**Step 4:** The core-loss component $I_w$ is in phase with $V_1$ (it represents resistive loss).

**Step 5:** The total no-load current is the phasor sum:

$$\mathbf{I_0} = \mathbf{I_w} + j \mathbf{I_m}$$

**Step 6:** The no-load power factor angle $\phi_0$ is the angle between $V_1$ and $I_0$:

$$\phi_0 = \tan^{-1} \left( \frac{I_m}{I_w} \right)$$

## 3.3 Derivation of OC Test Formulas

The OC test is performed with the secondary **open** and instruments on the **LV side** (instruments are cheap, the LV side has low voltage).

- Voltmeter reads $V_1$ = rated primary voltage.
- Ammeter reads $I_0$ = no-load current (small, 2–10% of FL).
- Wattmeter reads $P_0$ = core loss (since $I_2 = 0$, there is no copper loss on the secondary, and the small primary copper loss $I_0^2 R_1$ is often neglected, so $P_0 \approx P_{core}$).

From the no-load phasor diagram, the active power consumed is:

$$P_0 = V_1 I_w \quad\Rightarrow\quad I_w = \frac{P_0}{V_1}$$

The magnetizing component:

$$I_m = \sqrt{I_0^2 - I_w^2} = \sqrt{I_0^2 - \left( \frac{P_0}{V_1} \right)^2}$$

Then:

$$R_0 = \frac{V_1}{I_w} = \frac{V_1^2}{P_0}$$

$$X_0 = \frac{V_1}{I_m} = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{I_0^2 - (P_0/V_1)^2}} = \frac{V_1^2}{\sqrt{(V_1 I_0)^2 - P_0^2}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of SC Test Formulas

The SC test is performed with the secondary **shorted** (through an ammeter or a thick copper link) and a **reduced voltage** applied to the **HV side** (so the HV ammeter and voltmeter handle currents that the LV side instruments might not handle safely).

At short circuit, the applied voltage $V_{sc}$ is only **5%–10%** of rated. The shunt branch current is negligibly small (since $V_{sc}$ is very low), so all current flows through the equivalent series impedance $R_{01} + jX_{01}$.

- Wattmeter reads $P_{sc} = I_{sc}^2 R_{01}$ (only copper loss at this low voltage).
- Ammeter reads $I_{sc}$ = full-load current.
- Voltmeter reads $V_{sc}$ = small applied voltage.

From the readings:

$$R_{01} = \frac{P_{sc}}{I_{sc}^2}$$

$$Z_{01} = \frac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}}$$

$$X_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^2 - R_{01}^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance of $P_{sc}$:** Since the test is conducted at full-load current $I_{sc}$, $P_{sc}$ is the **full-load copper loss** of the transformer. This is the value used in efficiency calculations.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Test

| Scenario | What is to be found | Use which test? | Why? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Core (iron) loss at rated voltage | $P_i$ | **OC Test** | LV side energized, rated $V$ → core is fully fluxed → full core loss appears |
| Full-load copper loss | $P_{cu,fl}$ | **SC Test** | HV side with reduced $V$ → current is full-load → $I^2R$ loss = full value |
| Magnetizing reactance $X_0$ | Reactive part of no-load branch | **OC Test** | Shunt-branch parameters are dominant at rated voltage |
| Leakage reactance $X_{01}$ | Series leakage parameter | **SC Test** | Series impedance dominates at low voltage |
| Equivalent resistance $R_{01}$ | Total winding resistance referred to one side | **SC Test** | $R_{01}$ is a series parameter |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why HV side for SC test?** The SC test needs full-load current. The HV side has lower rated current, so the ammeter is more compact and safer. The applied voltage is only 5–10% of rated HV, so the voltmeter and autotransformer variac handle very small voltages.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Ideal Transformer Model

This diagram shows the functional abstraction of the ideal transformer with all loss-less couplings. It replaces the unattainable physical picture with a clean engineering block.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcV["PRIMARY VOLTAGE SOURCE V1"] --> priW["PRIMARY WINDING N1 TURNS"]
    priW --> core["MAGNETIC CORE ZERO LOSS INFINITE MU"]
    core --> secW["SECONDARY WINDING N2 TURNS"]
    secW --> loadR["LOAD ZL"]
    priW -. "FARADAY INDUCTION E1" .-> core
    core -. "MUTUAL FLUX PHI" .-> secW
    secW -. "INDUCED E2" .-> loadR

    classDef elec fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef mag fill:#e6f0ff,stroke:#003399,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
    class srcV,priW,secW,loadR elec
    class core mag
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Real Transformer Equivalent Circuit (Approximate)

The T-model is the most complete; the L-model (approximate) shifts the shunt branch to the primary terminals — *this is the KTU-preferred model for OC and SC test analysis*:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    v1["V1 (PRIMARY APPLIED VOLTAGE)"] --> r1["R1 (PRIMARY WINDING RESISTANCE)"]
    r1 --> x1["jX1 (PRIMARY LEAKAGE REACTANCE)"]
    x1 --> nodeA["NODE A (IDEAL TRANSFORMER PRIMARY)"]
    nodeA --> core["R0 PARALLEL jX0 (SHUNT CORE BRANCH)"]
    core --> gndA["REFERENCE GROUND"]
    nodeA --> x2["a SQUARED TIMES jX2 (SECONDARY REACTANCE REFERRED)"]
    x2 --> r2["a SQUARED TIMES R2 (SECONDARY RESISTANCE REFERRED)"]
    r2 --> v2["V2 PRIME (SECONDARY VOLTAGE REFERRED)"]
    v2 --> loadZ["LOAD ZL PRIME"]

    classDef v fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef r fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef x fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    class v1,v2,loadZ v
    class r1,r2 r
    class x1,x2 x
    class core,gndA,nodeA core
```

## 4.3 Instrumentation Setup — Open Circuit Test

The wattmeter, voltmeter, and ammeter are placed on the **LV side**. HV side is left open.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcO["AUTOTRANSFORMER VARIAC 0 TO 250V"] --> amO["AMMETER A0"]
    amO --> wO["WATTMETER W0 CURRENT COIL"]
    wO --> lvO["LV SIDE OF TRANSFORMER"]
    wO -. "WATTMETER VOLTAGE COIL" .-> vvO["VOLTMETER V1"]
    vvO --> lvO
    lvO --> hvO["HV SIDE OPEN CIRCUIT"]
    lvO --> srcO

    classDef inst fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    classDef trans fill:#e0f2f1,stroke:#004d40,color:#000
    class srcO,amO,wO,vvO inst
    class lvO,hvO trans
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The wattmeter's *current coil* is in series with the LV winding (carries $I_0$); the wattmeter's *voltage coil* is in parallel (reads $V_1$). The variac is needed to slowly raise the LV voltage from zero to rated, avoiding inrush saturation.

## 4.4 Instrumentation Setup — Short Circuit Test

Instruments on **HV side**, LV side is shorted through a low-resistance ammeter or a thick copper link.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcS["AUTOTRANSFORMER 0 TO 50V REDUCED"] --> amS["AMMETER ASC"]
    amS --> wS["WATTMETER WSC CURRENT COIL"]
    wS --> hvS["HV SIDE OF TRANSFORMER"]
    wS -. "WATTMETER VOLTAGE COIL" .-> vvS["VOLTMETER VSC"]
    vvS --> hvS
    hvS --> lvS["LV SIDE SHORTED"]
    lvS --> srcS

    classDef inst fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    classDef trans fill:#e0f2f1,stroke:#004d40,color:#000
    classDef short fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    class srcS,amS,wS,vvS inst
    class hvS trans
    class lvS short
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU 2024 detail:** The applied voltage during SC test must be slowly increased from zero until the ammeter reads **full-load current**. The corresponding voltmeter reading is $V_{sc}$ — typically **5%–10%** of rated HV. Premature application of full rated voltage under short-circuit conditions will cause **catastrophic insulation failure and winding burn-out**.

## 4.5 Phasor Diagram — No-Load Operation (Textual Schematic)

For a complete no-load phasor diagram description:

- Reference: $\mathbf{V_1}$ along the **horizontal positive X-axis**.
- $\mathbf{I_w}$ drawn **along $\mathbf{V_1}$** (in phase, horizontal).
- $\mathbf{\phi}$ drawn **$90°$ below $\mathbf{V_1}$** (flux lags voltage by $90°$, downward).
- $\mathbf{I_m}$ drawn **along $\mathbf{\phi}$** (in phase with flux, also downward, $90°$ lagging $V_1$).
- $\mathbf{E_1}$ drawn **$180°$ opposite to $\mathbf{V_1}$** (back-EMF opposes applied voltage by Lenz's law; in phasor terms, $E_1$ lags $V_1$ by $180°$ if we ignore winding resistance, or by a tiny angle $\alpha$ for finite $R_1$).
- $\mathbf{I_0}$ is the phasor sum $\mathbf{I_w} + \mathbf{I_m}$, lying in the **fourth quadrant**, with magnitude $\sqrt{I_w^2 + I_m^2}$ and angle $\phi_0$ below the horizontal.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define an ideal transformer and state its two governing equations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An ideal transformer is a lossless, perfectly coupled static electromagnetic device in which (i) the windings have zero resistance, (ii) the core has infinite permeability, and (iii) there is zero leakage flux.

The two governing equations are:
- **Voltage Ratio:** $\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = a$
- **Current Ratio (MMF Balance):** $\dfrac{I_1}{I_2} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{1}{a}$ or equivalently $N_1 I_1 = N_2 I_2$

These follow from Faraday's Law and Ampere's Law applied to a lossless, perfectly coupled core. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for voltage equation, 1 mark for current equation.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why is the open-circuit test performed on the LV side of a transformer, and the short-circuit test on the HV side?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **OC Test on LV side (1.5 marks):** Rated voltage is applied to the LV winding, which has lower voltage and higher current. The instruments (voltmeter, ammeter, wattmeter) are designed for LV ratings — they are cheaper, safer, and more accurate. The HV side is open, so no load is required.
- **SC Test on HV side (1.5 marks):** Full-load current must flow during the test. The HV side has lower rated current, so a smaller ammeter can be used. The applied voltage is only 5%–10% of rated HV, making it safe to use a low-voltage variac. The LV side is shorted to circulate the current.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### 📌 Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** A 5 kVA, 200 V / 1000 V, 50 Hz, single-phase transformer has 80 turns on the LV side. Find:
   (i) The number of turns on the HV side.
   (ii) The peak value of the core flux.
   (iii) The full-load primary and secondary currents. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat phasor diagram, explain the no-load operation of a single-phase transformer. Derive the expressions for the core-loss component $I_w$, magnetizing component $I_m$, and no-load power factor $\cos\phi_0$. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Part (i): Number of HV turns**

Given: $V_1 = 200$ V (LV), $V_2 = 1000$ V (HV), $N_1 = 80$, $f = 50$ Hz.

$$\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{1000}{200} = 5$$

$$N_2 = 5 \times 80 = 400 \text{ turns}$$

**(a) Part (ii): Peak core flux**

Using $V_1 = 4.44 \, f \, \phi_m \, N_1$:

$$\phi_m = \frac{V_1}{4.44 \, f \, N_1} = \frac{200}{4.44 \times 50 \times 80} = \frac{200}{17760}$$

$$\phi_m = 0.01126 \text{ Wb} = 11.26 \text{ mWb}$$

**(a) Part (iii): Full-load currents**

$$I_{1,FL} = \frac{S}{V_1} = \frac{5000}{200} = 25 \text{ A (LV side, primary)}$$

$$I_{2,FL} = \frac{S}{V_2} = \frac{5000}{1000} = 5 \text{ A (HV side, secondary)}$$

**Verification:** $I_1 / I_2 = 25/5 = 5 = N_2/N_1$ ✓ (KTU cross-check requirement)

---

**(b) Phasor diagram & derivation**

The no-load phasor diagram has $V_1$ as reference, $I_0$ lagging $V_1$ by a small angle $\phi_0$, $I_w$ in phase with $V_1$, $I_m$ lagging $V_1$ by $90°$, and flux $\phi$ lagging $V_1$ by $90°$.

The no-load power input (read by the wattmeter) is:

$$P_0 = V_1 I_0 \cos\phi_0$$

Since $I_0 \cos\phi_0 = I_w$:

$$I_w = \frac{P_0}{V_1}$$

Applying Pythagoras to the orthogonal components $I_w$ and $I_m$:

$$I_m = \sqrt{I_0^2 - I_w^2} = \sqrt{I_0^2 - \left( \frac{P_0}{V_1} \right)^2}$$

The no-load power factor is by definition:

$$\cos\phi_0 = \frac{P_0}{V_1 I_0}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write $\cos\phi_0 = I_w / I_m$ (WRONG!). The correct ratio is $I_w / I_0$, where $I_0$ is the **hypotenuse** of the $I_w$–$I_m$ right triangle. Marks deducted: **1 mark** for this sign error.

---

### 📌 Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)

**(a)** Derive the EMF equation of a single-phase transformer. A 50 kVA, 6360 V / 240 V, 50 Hz transformer has 80 turns on the secondary. Find the peak flux in the core and the number of primary turns. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the procedure of the **Open-Circuit (OC) Test** and the **Short-Circuit (SC) Test** on a single-phase transformer. Clearly state:
   (i) Where the instruments are connected (HV or LV side).
   (ii) What quantities are measured in each test.
   (iii) How the equivalent-circuit parameters $R_0, X_0$ and $R_{01}, X_{01}$ are computed from the readings. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**(a) EMF Equation Derivation**

[Full derivation as in Section 3.1, yielding $E = 4.44 \, f \, \phi_m \, N$.]

**Numerical Part:**

Given: $V_2 = 240$ V (secondary), $N_2 = 80$, $V_1 = 6360$ V (primary), $f = 50$ Hz.

**Step 1: Find peak flux using the secondary winding.**

$$\phi_m = \frac{V_2}{4.44 \, f \, N_2} = \frac{240}{4.44 \times 50 \times 80} = \frac{240}{17760}$$

$$\phi_m = 0.01351 \text{ Wb} = 13.51 \text{ mWb}$$

**Step 2: Find primary turns from the turns ratio.**

$$a = \frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{6360}{240} = 26.5$$

$$N_1 = a \times N_2 = 26.5 \times 80 = 2120 \text{ turns}$$

---

**(b) OC & SC Test Procedure**

**(b)(i) Instrument Location:**

- **OC Test:** Instruments on the **LV side**; HV side kept open.
- **SC Test:** Instruments on the **HV side**; LV side shorted.

**(b)(ii) Quantities Measured:**

- **OC Test:** Voltmeter reads rated $V_1$, ammeter reads $I_0$ (2–10% of FL), wattmeter reads $P_0 = $ core loss.
- **SC Test:** Reduced $V_{sc}$ (5–10% of rated), ammeter reads $I_{sc}$ = full-load current, wattmeter reads $P_{sc} = $ full-load copper loss.

**(b)(iii) Computation of Equivalent-Circuit Parameters:**

**From OC Test:**

$$I_w = \frac{P_0}{V_1}, \quad I_m = \sqrt{I_0^2 - I_w^2}$$

$$R_0 = \frac{V_1}{I_w} = \frac{V_1^2}{P_0} \quad\quad X_0 = \frac{V_1}{I_m}$$

**From SC Test:**

$$R_{01} = \frac{P_{sc}}{I_{sc}^2} \quad\quad Z_{01} = \frac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}} \quad\quad X_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^2 - R_{01}^2}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (OC Test):**
> - Many students forget that the **wattmeter reads core loss, NOT primary copper loss.** At no-load, $I_0$ is small, so $I_0^2 R_1$ is negligible (typically < 0.1% of $P_0$). If a student includes this correction, full marks; if ignored, still acceptable in KTU 2024 Module 1 (basic level).
> - **Unit pitfall:** $R_0$ and $X_0$ come out in **Ohms referred to the LV side**. If the question asks for the HV-side referred value, divide by $a^2$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (SC Test):**
> - **Most common error:** Computing $X_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{01} - R_{01}}$ (missing the square on $R_{01}$). The correct formula is $X_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^2 - R_{01}^2}$. Marks lost: **2 marks**.
> - **Sign convention error:** Students often confuse whether $R_{01}$ is the **total equivalent resistance** or only the primary $R_1$. Always state "referred to the side where instruments are connected."

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exams):**

- ✅ **Ideal Transformer:** Zero losses, infinite $\mu$, no leakage, $\eta = 100\%$. Two equations: $V_1/V_2 = N_1/N_2$ and $I_1/I_2 = N_2/N_1$.

- ✅ **EMF Formula:** $E = 4.44 \, f \, \phi_m \, N$ — memorize the constant **4.44**, not 4.444.

- ✅ **No-Load Current Components:** $I_0 = \sqrt{I_w^2 + I_m^2}$, where $I_w$ is in phase with $V$ (core loss) and $I_m$ lags $V$ by $90°$ (magnetizing).

- ✅ **No-Load Power Factor:** Poor, typically **0.15–0.30** lagging for power transformers.

- ✅ **OC Test:** LV side instruments, HV open. Gives $R_0, X_0$ and core loss $P_0$.

- ✅ **SC Test:** HV side instruments, LV shorted, reduced voltage (5–10% rated). Gives $R_{01}, X_{01}$ and full-load $P_{cu}$.

- ✅ **Impedance Reflection:** $Z_{in} = a^2 Z_L$ — the cornerstone of audio and RF matching networks.

- ✅ **KTU Mnemonic — "OHSS":** **O**C test on **H**V side instruments on LV → **S**C test on **H**V side instruments on HV. (Wait, that was a trap! The correct mnemonic is **"OC-instruments-LV, SC-instruments-HV"**.)

- ✅ **Power conservation check:** In an ideal transformer, $V_1 I_1 = V_2 I_2$. Always verify this in numerical problems as a sanity check.

- ✅ **Units of $R_0, X_0, R_{01}, X_{01}$:** Always state "referred to LV side" or "referred to HV side" — KTU examiners award 1 extra mark for explicit mentioning.

- ✅ **Efficiency at fraction $x$ of full load:** Always include the term $x^2 P_{cu,fl}$ in the copper loss — students often forget the square and lose 2 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Losses in Transformer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT302 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES & DRIVES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Losses in Transformer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In a **transformer**, the input electrical power delivered at the primary terminals is never fully recovered as useful output power at the secondary terminals. The difference between the input power and the output power is dissipated as **losses** within the transformer. According to the **Law of Conservation of Energy**, these losses manifest as heat, sound, and minor mechanical vibrations, and they directly degrade the energy-conversion efficiency of the device.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Transformer losses are the unwanted electrical and magnetic losses that occur in a transformer during its operation, primarily categorized into Iron (Core) Losses and Copper (Ohmic) Losses. They determine the thermal rating, efficiency, and all-day energy consumption of the transformer."*

The total losses $P_{\text{total}}$ in a transformer are mathematically expressed as:

$$
P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{iron}} + P_{\text{copper}} = P_{\text{hysteresis}} + P_{\text{eddy current}} + P_{\text{stray}} + P_{\text{cu}}
$$

where the iron losses are also collectively called **constant losses** (since they are nearly independent of the load) and the copper losses are called **variable losses** (since they vary with the square of the load current).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are pumping water from a lower tank to a higher tank through a long, narrow pipe. The pump adds energy to the water, but by the time the water reaches the upper tank, you notice:

1. **Some energy is "lost" to friction** between the water and the pipe walls — this is exactly like **copper loss** ($I^2R$) in the transformer windings.
2. **Some energy is "lost" to the pipe itself** because the pipe is slightly magnetic or resistive and heats up due to alternating magnetic fields — this is exactly like **iron (core) loss** in the transformer core.
3. **A tiny amount leaks** from the pipe joints and seals — this is the **stray loss**.

Just as a plumber would ask *"Where is the energy leaking?"* to improve the pump's efficiency, an electrical engineer studies **transformer losses** to maximize the energy delivered from the primary to the secondary.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Should We Care?**
> - For a typical distribution transformer running 24×7, even a **1% efficiency drop** can mean **thousands of units (kWh)** wasted per year.
> - Losses determine the **thermal rating** (cooling system design) of the transformer.
> - Utilities are financially penalized for high **all-day efficiency** losses, making this a high-weightage topic for KTU board exams and GATE/competitive exams alike.

## 1.3 Broad Classification of Transformer Losses

| S. No. | Category | Sub-Type | Nature |
|--------|----------|----------|--------|
| 1 | **Iron / Core Losses** ($P_i$) | Hysteresis Loss ($P_h$) | Depends on $B_{\max}$ and $f$ |
| 2 | **Iron / Core Losses** ($P_i$) | Eddy Current Loss ($P_e$) | Depends on $B_{\max}$, $f$, and lamination thickness |
| 3 | **Iron / Core Losses** ($P_i$) | Stray Iron Loss | Minor leakage flux in structural parts |
| 4 | **Copper / Ohmic Losses** ($P_{\text{cu}}$) | Primary $I_1^2 R_1$ | Varies with load |
| 5 | **Copper / Ohmic Losses** ($P_{\text{cu}}$) | Secondary $I_2^2 R_2$ | Varies with load |
| 6 | **Stray Load Loss** | Leakage flux losses | Varies with load |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** *"HEICS"* → **H**ysteresis, **E**ddy current, **I**ron stray, **C**opper, **S**tray load. The first two are iron losses, the next is also iron, and the last two are load-dependent.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Iron (Core) Losses — The Magnetic Origin

The transformer core is made of **Cold Rolled Grain-Oriented (CRGO) silicon steel laminations** with a typical thickness of **0.35 mm** to **0.5 mm**, insulated from each other by a thin varnish or oxide coating. The core is subjected to an alternating magnetizing flux $\Phi(t) = \Phi_{\max} \sin(\omega t)$, which produces a sinusoidal flux density $B(t) = B_{\max} \sin(\omega t)$. This alternating magnetization produces two distinct losses:

### 2.1.1 Hysteresis Loss ($P_h$)

**Physical Origin:**
- When a ferromagnetic material is subjected to an alternating magnetic field, the magnetic domains continuously realign with the changing field direction.
- This realignment is not perfectly elastic — it consumes energy, and the energy consumed per cycle is equal to the **area enclosed by the B-H hysteresis loop** of the core material.
- Since the line frequency is $f$ cycles per second, the energy consumed per second (i.e., power loss) is $f$ times the loop area.

**Steinmetz Empirical Equation (KTU High-Yield):**

$$
P_h = \eta \, B_{\max}^{1.6} \, f \, V \quad \text{(Watts)}
$$

where:
- $\eta$ = **Steinmetz hysteresis coefficient** (depends on core material; for CRGO steel, $\eta \approx 0.001$ to $0.01$)
- $B_{\max}$ = Peak flux density in Tesla (typically **1.1 T to 1.4 T** for CRGO)
- $f$ = Supply frequency in **Hz** (50 Hz in India)
- $V$ = Volume of the core in **m³**
- The exponent **1.6** is the Steinmetz exponent ($x$).

**The "Why" Behind the Formula:**
- $\propto f$ → More cycles per second = more loop area traced per second.
- $\propto B_{\max}^{1.6}$ → Larger flux = wider hysteresis loop, but the relationship is non-linear.
- $\propto V$ → Bigger core = more material undergoing magnetization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to Reduce Hysteresis Loss:**
> - Use **soft magnetic materials** with narrow B-H loops (e.g., silicon steel, permalloy, amorphous alloys).
> - Operate the core at **lower flux density** (e.g., 1.2 T instead of 1.5 T).
> - For high-frequency applications (aero/space transformers), use **ferrite cores** with $\eta$ close to zero.

### 2.1.2 Eddy Current Loss ($P_e$)

**Physical Origin:**
- The alternating flux $\Phi(t)$ linking the core also induces an EMF in the core body itself (as per **Faraday's Law**), just like it does in the secondary winding.
- This induced EMF circulates **eddy currents** within the cross-section of the core, in planes perpendicular to the flux direction.
- These eddy currents flow through the finite resistance of the core material, dissipating power as $I^2R$ heat.

**Derivation-Based Equation (KTU High-Yield):**

$$
P_e = K_e \, B_{\max}^{2} \, f^{2} \, t^{2} \, V \quad \text{(Watts)}
$$

where:
- $K_e$ = Eddy current constant (depends on material resistivity and lamination stacking factor)
- $B_{\max}$ = Peak flux density in **Tesla**
- $f$ = Supply frequency in **Hz**
- $t$ = Thickness of each lamination in **metres** (typically 0.35 mm = 0.00035 m)
- $V$ = Volume of the core in **m³**

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Observation:** $P_e \propto t^2$ — this is why **thinner laminations** dramatically reduce eddy current loss. Halving the lamination thickness reduces eddy current loss to **one-fourth** of its original value. This is the single most important design technique to combat eddy currents.

**The "Why" Behind the Formula:**
- $\propto f^2$ → Doubling the frequency quadruples the induced EMF and hence the eddy current loss (since $P \propto V^2 \propto f^2$).
- $\propto B_{\max}^2$ → Larger flux = larger induced EMF = more eddy current dissipation.
- $\propto t^2$ → Thicker lamination = larger loop area for eddy currents = more loss.

> [!TIP]
> **Combined Iron Loss Equation (Most Useful in KTU Board Exams):**

$$
P_i = P_h + P_e = \eta B_{\max}^{1.6} f V + K_e B_{\max}^2 f^2 t^2 V
$$

For **sinusoidal operation at constant $V/f$ ratio**, $B_{\max}$ is constant, and we can combine constants:

$$
P_i = A f + B f^2 \quad \text{(Empirical two-constant form)}
$$

where $A = \eta B_{\max}^{1.6} V$ and $B = K_e B_{\max}^2 t^2 V$. This is directly used in the **Open-Circuit Test** analysis.

## 2.2 Copper (Ohmic) Losses — The Electrical Origin

Copper losses occur due to the **resistive heating** of the primary and secondary windings as the load current flows through them. Since the windings are typically made of high-conductivity **electrolytic-grade copper**, these losses follow the **Joule Heating Law** ($I^2R$).

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$
P_{\text{cu}} = I_1^2 R_1 + I_2^2 R_2 = I_1^2 R_{01} = I_2^2 R_{02}
$$

where:
- $I_1, I_2$ = Primary and secondary RMS currents in **Amperes**
- $R_1, R_2$ = Primary and secondary winding resistances in **Ohms** (DC resistance corrected to **75°C** as per KTU standard)
- $R_{01}, R_{02}$ = Total equivalent resistances referred to primary and secondary sides

**For a Fractional Load $x$:**

$$
P_{\text{cu,fl}} = x^2 \times P_{\text{cu,full}}
$$

This is a **square-law dependence**, which is critical for the **maximum efficiency** derivation.

## 2.3 Stray Load Losses

These are additional losses occurring in:
- The **structural parts** of the transformer (clamping plates, tank walls, bolts) due to leakage flux.
- The **windings themselves** due to non-uniform current distribution (proximity and skin effects) at higher frequencies.
- Typically assumed to be **0.5% to 1%** of the full-load copper loss in KTU problems unless stated otherwise.

$$
P_{\text{stray}} = 0.005 \, P_{\text{cu,full}} \quad \text{(typical assumed value)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Many students forget to include stray load losses. If the problem states *"total copper loss at full load"*, it usually means $P_{\text{cu,total}} = P_{I^2R} + P_{\text{stray}}$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Loss Type | Affected Subsystem | Mitigation in Real Designs |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------------------------|
| Hysteresis | Core lamination steel | Use CRGO, amorphous metal ribbons (e.g., Metglas) |
| Eddy current | Core lamination body | Use 0.23 mm / 0.27 mm thin laminations, oxide coating |
| Copper | Windings | Use rectangular copper strips, transposed conductors |
| Stray | Tank, frame | Use **magnetic shields**, non-magnetic tank in large transformers |
| Dielectric | Insulation | Use high-grade **epoxy-impregnated paper**, oil purification |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Gold)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | $P_h = \eta B_{\max}^{1.6} f V$ | Hysteresis loss | Watts |
| 2 | $P_e = K_e B_{\max}^2 f^2 t^2 V$ | Eddy current loss | Watts |
| 3 | $P_i = A f + B f^2$ | Total iron loss (two-constant model) | Watts |
| 4 | $P_{\text{cu,fl}} = I^2 R$ | Full-load copper loss | Watts |
| 5 | $P_{\text{cu,fl at } x} = x^2 P_{\text{cu,full}}$ | Fractional-load copper loss | Watts |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{x \, S \cos\phi}{x \, S \cos\phi + P_i + x^2 P_{\text{cu,full}}}$ | Conventional efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $x_{\text{max eff}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_i}{P_{\text{cu,full}}}}$ | Load fraction for max efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{all-day}} = \dfrac{\text{Energy output in 24 hrs}}{\text{Energy input in 24 hrs}}$ | All-day (energy) efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $P_{\text{stray}} \approx 0.5\% \text{ to } 1\% \text{ of } P_{\text{cu}}$ | Stray load loss | Watts |
| 10 | $P_{\text{total}} = P_i + P_{\text{cu}} + P_{\text{stray}}$ | Total transformer loss | Watts |

> [!TIP]
> **Units to Remember for Board Exams:**
> - $B_{\max}$ in **Tesla (T)** or **Wb/m²** (1 T = 1 Wb/m²)
> - $V$ (volume) in **m³** (NOT cm³ — convert carefully!)
> - $t$ (lamination thickness) in **metres** (NOT mm — this is the #1 unit mistake)
> - $f$ in **Hz** (50 Hz in India)
> - Output power in **Watts (W)** or **kW** — be consistent.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Hysteresis Loss Formula

**Step 1 — Start with the Energy Definition:**
The energy dissipated per unit volume of a ferromagnetic material per cycle of magnetization is equal to the area of the B-H hysteresis loop. Let this energy be denoted as $W_h$ (Joules per m³ per cycle).

**Step 2 — Relate to Steinmetz's Empirical Law:**
Steinmetz (1892) experimentally found that for a given material at typical operating flux densities:

$$
W_h = \eta \, B_{\max}^{1.6} \quad \text{[Joules/m³/cycle]}
$$

where $\eta$ is the Steinmetz coefficient. This is an empirical, not a first-principles result.

**Step 3 — Account for Frequency:**
If the supply frequency is $f$ cycles per second, the energy dissipated per second per unit volume is $f \times W_h$.

$$
\frac{P_h}{V} = f \cdot W_h = \eta \, B_{\max}^{1.6} f \quad \text{[Watts/m³]}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by Core Volume:**
Multiplying both sides by the total core volume $V$ (in m³) gives the total hysteresis loss:

$$
P_h = \eta \, B_{\max}^{1.6} f V \quad \blacksquare
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $B^{1.6}$ and not $B^2$?** The exponent 1.6 (Steinmetz exponent $x$) is empirically determined. For modern CRGO steels, the exponent is closer to **1.5 to 1.7**, while for older hot-rolled steels it can be **1.6 to 2.0**. KTU board problems almost always use **1.6** unless otherwise stated.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Eddy Current Loss Formula

**Step 1 — Consider a Single Lamination:**
Consider a single thin lamination of thickness $t$, width $w$, and height $h$ (the dimension parallel to flux). The flux $\Phi$ flows along the height $h$. The lamination can be idealized as a rectangular strip.

**Step 2 — Induced EMF in a Hypothetical Eddy Path:**
Consider a small square eddy current path of side $x$ within the lamination, lying in the plane perpendicular to the flux. The flux linking this path varies sinusoidally:

$$
\phi(t) = B(t) \cdot x \cdot h = B_{\max} \sin(\omega t) \cdot x \cdot h
$$

By Faraday's Law, the EMF induced around this path is:

$$
e(t) = -\frac{d\phi}{dt} = -B_{\max} \cdot x \cdot h \cdot \omega \cos(\omega t)
$$

The RMS value of this EMF is:

$$
E = \frac{B_{\max} \, \omega \, x \, h}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2} \, \pi \, f \, B_{\max} \, x \, h
$$

**Step 3 — Resistance of the Eddy Current Path:**
The path has length $4x$ and cross-sectional area $h \cdot dy$ (where $dy$ is an infinitesimal thickness into the page). Using the resistivity $\rho$ of the lamination material:

$$
dR = \frac{\rho \cdot 4x}{h \cdot dy}
$$

**Step 4 — Power Dissipated in the Path:**

$$
dP = \frac{E^2}{dR} = \frac{(\sqrt{2} \pi f B_{\max} x h)^2 \cdot h \, dy}{4 \rho x}
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate Across the Lamination Thickness:**
The path side $x$ varies from 0 to $t/2$ (half-thickness). Integrating across all such paths in one lamination (and doubling for both halves):

$$
P_{\text{one lamination}} = 2 \int_{0}^{t/2} \int_{0}^{t/2} \frac{2 \pi^2 f^2 B_{\max}^2 x^2 h^3}{4 \rho x} \, dx \, dy
$$

After performing the double integration (and accounting for the total volume $V$ of the core with stacking factor), the final compact form is:

$$
\boxed{P_e = K_e \, B_{\max}^{2} \, f^{2} \, t^{2} \, V} \quad \blacksquare
$$

where $K_e = \dfrac{\pi^2}{6 \rho}$ is the eddy current constant for a sinusoidal flux.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The $t^2$ Dependence is the Most Important Engineering Result of This Derivation.** It is the fundamental reason why transformer cores are **laminated**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Condition for Maximum Efficiency

**Step 1 — Write the Efficiency Expression:**
For a transformer delivering a fractional load $x$ of its full-load rating $S$ (in VA) at power factor $\cos\phi$:

$$
\eta = \frac{\text{Output Power}}{\text{Output Power + Total Losses}} = \frac{x \, S \cos\phi}{x \, S \cos\phi + P_i + x^2 \, P_{\text{cu,full}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with Respect to $x$ and Set to Zero:**

$$
\frac{d\eta}{dx} = \frac{(S \cos\phi)(x S \cos\phi + P_i + x^2 P_{\text{cu,full}}) - (x S \cos\phi)(S \cos\phi + 2x P_{\text{cu,full}})}{(x S \cos\phi + P_i + x^2 P_{\text{cu,full}})^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the Numerator to Zero Condition:**
Setting the numerator to zero:

$$
S \cos\phi \cdot (P_i + x^2 P_{\text{cu,full}}) = x S \cos\phi \cdot (2x P_{\text{cu,full}})
$$

The $S \cos\phi$ terms cancel:

$$
P_i + x^2 P_{\text{cu,full}} = 2 x^2 P_{\text{cu,full}}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $x$:**

$$
P_i = 2 x^2 P_{\text{cu,full}} - x^2 P_{\text{cu,full}} = x^2 P_{\text{cu,full}}
$$

$$
\boxed{x_{\text{max eff}} = \sqrt{\frac{P_i}{P_{\text{cu,full}}}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Maximum efficiency occurs at the load where the **variable (copper) loss equals the constant (iron) loss**. This is a fundamental result in transformer design and is the basis for the **bisection of losses principle** in optimum transformer sizing.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A 50 kVA, 2400/240 V, 50 Hz distribution transformer has the following test data:
- **Open-Circuit Test (OC):** $V_0 = 240$ V, $I_0 = 5$ A, $P_0 = 180$ W (performed on LV side)
- **Short-Circuit Test (SC):** $V_{\text{sc}} = 60$ V, $I_{\text{sc}} = 20.83$ A, $P_{\text{sc}} = 600$ W (performed on HV side)
- Stray losses are 10% of $I^2R$ losses.

**Find:** (a) Iron loss and copper loss at full load, (b) Maximum efficiency and the load at which it occurs at unity pf, (c) All-day efficiency if the load cycle is: full load for 6 hrs, half load for 12 hrs, and no load for 6 hrs (assume unity pf throughout).

---

### **Solution (a) — Iron Loss and Full-Load Copper Loss**

From the **Open-Circuit Test** (LV side, no-load condition):

$$
P_i = P_0 = 180 \text{ W}
$$

This is the iron loss, measured at rated voltage and frequency.

From the **Short-Circuit Test** (HV side, full-load current circulated):

$$
P_{\text{cu,full}} = P_{\text{sc}} = 600 \text{ W}
$$

**Separating Stray Losses:**
Given stray losses = 10% of $I^2R$ losses. Let $P_{I^2R} = x$ and $P_{\text{stray}} = 0.1x$.

$$
x + 0.1x = 600 \implies 1.1x = 600 \implies x = 545.45 \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{I^2R} = 545.45 \text{ W}, \quad P_{\text{stray}} = 54.55 \text{ W}
$$

---

### **Solution (b) — Maximum Efficiency at Unity PF**

$$
x_{\text{max eff}} = \sqrt{\frac{P_i}{P_{\text{cu,full}}}} = \sqrt{\frac{180}{600}} = \sqrt{0.3} = 0.5477
$$

Load at max efficiency = $0.5477 \times 50$ kVA = **27.39 kVA** at unity pf.

Output power at max efficiency = $27.39 \times 1$ = **27.39 kW**

Total loss at $x = 0.5477$:
- $P_i = 180$ W
- $P_{\text{cu}} = x^2 \cdot P_{\text{cu,full}} = 0.3 \times 600 = 180$ W
- $P_{\text{total}} = 180 + 180 = 360$ W

$$
\eta_{\max} = \frac{27.39 \times 10^3}{27.39 \times 10^3 + 360} = 0.9870 = \mathbf{98.70\%}
$$

---

### **Solution (c) — All-Day Efficiency**

**Energy Output in 24 hours:**

$$
E_{\text{out}} = (50 \times 6 + 25 \times 12 + 0 \times 6) \text{ kWh} = 300 + 300 + 0 = 600 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Energy Consumed by Iron Loss (24 hours, constant):**

$$
E_{\text{iron}} = 180 \text{ W} \times 24 \text{ h} = 4320 \text{ Wh} = 4.32 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Energy Consumed by Copper Loss (only during loaded hours):**

$$
E_{\text{cu}} = \underbrace{(1)^2 \times 600 \times 6}_{6\text{ hrs full load}} + \underbrace{(0.5)^2 \times 600 \times 12}_{12\text{ hrs half load}} + \underbrace{0}_{6\text{ hrs no load}
$$

$$
E_{\text{cu}} = 3600 + 1800 + 0 = 5400 \text{ Wh} = 5.40 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Total Energy Losses:**

$$
E_{\text{losses}} = 4.32 + 5.40 = 9.72 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Energy Input:**

$$
E_{\text{in}} = 600 + 9.72 = 609.72 \text{ kWh}
$$

**All-Day Efficiency:**

$$
\boxed{\eta_{\text{all-day}} = \frac{600}{609.72} = 0.9841 = \mathbf{98.41\%}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The all-day efficiency (98.41%) is **lower** than the maximum efficiency (98.70%) because the iron loss runs 24 hours even when there is no load — this is why **distribution transformers** (which stay energized all day) are designed with **lower iron loss** (i.e., smaller $P_i$), while **power transformers** (loaded most of the time) are designed with **lower copper loss**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Taxonomy of Transformer Losses (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Transformer Losses Pt] --> B[Iron Core Losses Pi]
    A --> C[Copper Ohmic Losses Pcu]
    A --> D[Stray Load Losses]

    B --> B1[Hysteresis Loss Ph]
    B --> B2[Eddy Current Loss Pe]
    B --> B3[Stray Iron Loss]

    B1 --> B1a[Steinmetz Eq: Ph = eta x Bmax^1.6 x f x V]
    B1 --> B1b[Reduced by: CRGO steel, lower Bmax]

    B2 --> B2a[Faraday-induced: Pe = Ke x Bmax^2 x f^2 x t^2 x V]
    B2 --> B2b[Reduced by: lamination, t less than 0.35mm]

    C --> C1[Primary I1 squared R1]
    C --> C2[Secondary I2 squared R2]
    C --> C3[Proximity and skin effects]

    D --> D1[Leakage flux in tank walls]
    D --> D2[Typically 0.5 to 1 percent of full load copper loss]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Energy Flow and Loss Allocation in a Transformer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Input Power Pin] --> CORE[Iron Core Pi]
    IN --> CU[Copper Winding Pcu]
    IN --> STRAY[Stray Load Pstray]
    OUT[Output Power Pout]

    CORE --> HEAT1[Heat in Core]
    CU --> HEAT2[Heat in Windings]
    STRAY --> HEAT3[Heat in Structural Parts]
    HEAT1 --> SINK[Surroundings]
    HEAT2 --> SINK
    HEAT3 --> SINK

    IN --> OUT
    OUT -.->|Pin minus Ploss| IN

    style IN fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style OUT fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CORE fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style CU fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style STRAY fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Loss Determination via Standard Tests

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Loss Determination Strategy] --> Q1{Where to find Iron Loss?}
    START --> Q2{Where to find Copper Loss?}

    Q1 --> OCT[Open Circuit Test on LV side]
    OCT --> OCT1[Apply rated voltage V0]
    OCT1 --> OCT2[Measure no load current I0]
    OCT2 --> OCT3[Measure input power P0]
    OCT3 --> OCT4[Pi = P0 = 180 W in our example]

    Q2 --> SCT[Short Circuit Test on HV side]
    SCT --> SCT1[Short LV terminals]
    SCT1 --> SCT2[Apply reduced voltage Vsc]
    SCT2 --> SCT3[Measure short circuit current Isc]
    SCT3 --> SCT4[Measure input power Psc]
    SCT4 --> SCT5[Pcu full = Psc = 600 W]

    OCT4 --> EFF[Efficiency and Max Load Calculation]
    SCT5 --> EFF
    EFF --> EFF1[x max eff = sqrt of Pi over Pcu full]
    EFF1 --> EFF2[Eta max at unity pf]
```

## 4.4 Visual Summary Table: When Each Loss Dominates

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TIME[Operating Duration] --> L1[Transformer Energized 24 hours: Distribution Type]
    TIME --> L2[Transformer Loaded Most of Day: Power Type]

    L1 --> DEC1[Design Priority: MINIMIZE IRON LOSS Pi]
    L1 --> DEC1a[Use high-grade CRGO, low Bmax]
    L1 --> DEC1b[All-day efficiency matters most]

    L2 --> DEC2[Design Priority: MINIMIZE COPPER LOSS Pcu]
    L2 --> DEC2a[Use larger conductor cross-section]
    L2 --> DEC2b[Conventional efficiency matters most]
```

# 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Explain the origin of hysteresis loss and eddy current loss in a transformer core. How are they minimized in practice?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Hysteresis Loss:** Originates from the continuous reversal of magnetic domains in the core material as it is subjected to the alternating flux. Energy equal to the area of the B-H loop is dissipated as heat per cycle. Mathematically, $P_h = \eta B_{\max}^{1.6} f V$. **(1 mark)**

**Eddy Current Loss:** Originates from the EMF induced in the core body itself by the alternating flux, per Faraday's law. This EMF drives circulating currents in the lamination cross-section, which dissipate $I^2R$ heat. Mathematically, $P_e = K_e B_{\max}^{2} f^{2} t^{2} V$. **(1 mark)**

**Minimization Techniques:**
- Hysteresis: Use **CRGO silicon steel** (narrow B-H loop), operate at lower flux density (~1.2 T).
- Eddy Current: Use **thin laminations** (0.23–0.35 mm) insulated from each other; for high-frequency applications, use **ferrite cores** or **amorphous metal ribbons**. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between conventional efficiency and all-day efficiency of a transformer. Under what condition is all-day efficiency more meaningful?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Conventional Efficiency | All-Day Efficiency |
|---------|-------------------------|---------------------|
| Definition | Output power / Input power at any instant | Total kWh output / Total kWh input over 24 hrs |
| Iron loss weighted | At that load only | For full **24 hours** (even at no load) |
| Copper loss weighted | At that load only | Weighted over actual load cycle hours |
| Most useful for | Power transformers | Distribution transformers **(1 mark)** |

**Mathematical Forms:**
$$
\eta_{\text{conv}} = \frac{x S \cos\phi}{x S \cos\phi + P_i + x^2 P_{\text{cu,full}}}
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{all-day}} = \frac{\sum (kVA_i \cdot \cos\phi_i \cdot t_i)}{\sum (kVA_i \cdot \cos\phi_i \cdot t_i) + P_i \cdot 24 + \sum (x_i^2 \cdot P_{\text{cu,full}} \cdot t_i)}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Condition for relevance:** All-day efficiency is more meaningful for **distribution transformers** that remain energized for 24 hours but are loaded only for a part of the day (e.g., 6–10 hours). For these transformers, iron loss occurs 24×7 and dominates the energy cost. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Rule:** In Part B Module questions, the student is given a **choice** between **Question A** and **Question B**. Both are 14 marks, typically with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

A 100 kVA, 2000/200 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer gave the following test results:

- **OC Test (LV side):** 200 V, 4 A, 120 W
- **SC Test (HV side):** 60 V, 50 A, 400 W

**Calculate:**
**(a)** The efficiency at **full load and 0.8 power factor lagging**, and the load at which maximum efficiency occurs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The **all-day efficiency** for the following load cycle: full load at unity pf for 10 hours, half load at 0.8 pf for 8 hours, and no load for 6 hours. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to (a) — Conventional Efficiency and Maximum Efficiency Point**

**Step 1:** Identify losses from tests.
- From OC test: **Iron loss** $P_i = 120$ W
- From SC test: **Full-load copper loss** $P_{\text{cu,full}} = 400$ W

**Step 2:** Output power at full load, 0.8 pf lagging.

$$
P_{\text{out,fl}} = S \cdot \cos\phi = 100 \times 10^3 \times 0.8 = 80{,}000 \text{ W} = 80 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3:** Total losses at full load.

$$
P_{\text{losses,fl}} = P_i + P_{\text{cu,full}} = 120 + 400 = 520 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 4:** Conventional efficiency at full load.

$$
\eta_{\text{fl}} = \frac{80{,}000}{80{,}000 + 520} = \frac{80{,}000}{80{,}520} = 0.99355 = \mathbf{99.36\%} \quad \textbf{(4 marks)}
$$

**Step 5:** Load fraction for maximum efficiency.

$$
x_{\text{max}} = \sqrt{\frac{P_i}{P_{\text{cu,full}}}} = \sqrt{\frac{120}{400}} = \sqrt{0.3} = 0.5477
$$

**Step 6:** Load in kVA at max efficiency.

$$
S_{\text{max eff}} = 0.5477 \times 100 = \mathbf{54.77 \text{ kVA}} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}
$$

---

#### **Solution to (b) — All-Day Efficiency**

**Step 1:** Compute energy output over 24 hours.

| Period | Load | pf | Duration | Energy Output (kWh) |
|--------|------|-----|----------|---------------------|
| 1 | 100 kVA (fl) | 1.0 | 10 h | $100 \times 1.0 \times 10 = 1000$ |
| 2 | 50 kVA (½ fl) | 0.8 | 8 h | $50 \times 0.8 \times 8 = 320$ |
| 3 | 0 kVA (no load) | — | 6 h | $0$ |
| | | | **Total** | **$E_{\text{out}} = 1320$ kWh** **(2 marks)** |

**Step 2:** Compute energy lost as iron loss (24 hours, constant).

$$
E_{\text{iron}} = 120 \text{ W} \times 24 \text{ h} = 2880 \text{ Wh} = 2.88 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute energy lost as copper loss (only during loaded hours, $P_{\text{cu}} = x^2 P_{\text{cu,full}}$).

$$
E_{\text{cu}} = \underbrace{(1.0)^2 \times 400 \times 10}_{\text{Period 1}} + \underbrace{(0.5)^2 \times 400 \times 8}_{\text{Period 2}} + 0
$$

$$
E_{\text{cu}} = 4000 + 800 + 0 = 4800 \text{ Wh} = 4.80 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 4:** Total energy lost.

$$
E_{\text{losses}} = 2.88 + 4.80 = 7.68 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 5:** All-day efficiency.

$$
\eta_{\text{all-day}} = \frac{1320}{1320 + 7.68} = \frac{1320}{1327.68} = 0.9942 = \mathbf{99.42\%} \quad \textbf{(5 marks)}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the help of Steinmetz's empirical law, derive the expression for **hysteresis loss** in a transformer core. Briefly explain the technique used to **minimize it** in modern transformer design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 250 kVA transformer has iron loss of 1.8 kW and full-load copper loss of 3.2 kW.
- **(i)** Find the load at which **maximum efficiency** occurs.
- **(ii)** Find the **maximum efficiency** at 0.85 power factor lagging.
- **(iii)** If this transformer supplies a load cycle of: 200 kVA at 0.8 pf for 8 hours, 150 kVA at unity pf for 10 hours, and no load for 6 hours, find the **all-day efficiency**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to (a) — Derivation of Hysteresis Loss**

**Step 1:** When a ferromagnetic core is subjected to an alternating magnetizing field, the **magnetic domains** continuously align and realign with the applied field direction. The energy expended per cycle per unit volume of the material is equal to the **area enclosed by the B-H hysteresis loop**. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Let the energy dissipated per unit volume per cycle be denoted $W_h$. Steinmetz (1892) empirically established that:

$$
W_h = \eta \, B_{\max}^{1.6} \quad \text{[Joules/m³/cycle]}
$$

where $\eta$ is the Steinmetz coefficient (depends on the core material) and $B_{\max}$ is the peak flux density in Tesla. **(2 marks)**

**Step 3:** If the supply frequency is $f$ Hz, the number of cycles per second is $f$, so the power loss per unit volume is:

$$
\frac{P_h}{V} = W_h \cdot f = \eta \, B_{\max}^{1.6} \, f \quad \text{[Watts/m³]}
$$

**Step 4:** Multiplying by the core volume $V$ (in m³):

$$
\boxed{P_h = \eta \, B_{\max}^{1.6} \, f \, V} \quad \textbf{Watts} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}
$$

**Step 5 — Minimization Techniques:**
- Use **soft magnetic materials** like **CRGO silicon steel** with very narrow hysteresis loops (small area enclosed).
- Operate the core at a **lower flux density** (around 1.1–1.3 T instead of 1.5 T).
- For high-frequency applications, use **ferrite cores** or **amorphous metal alloys** (e.g., Metglas 2605) with $\eta \to 0$. **(1 mark)**

---

#### **Solution to (b) — Numerical Application**

**Given:** $S = 250$ kVA, $P_i = 1.8$ kW, $P_{\text{cu,full}} = 3.2$ kW.

**(i) Load at Maximum Efficiency:**

$$
x_{\text{max}} = \sqrt{\frac{P_i}{P_{\text{cu,full}}}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.8}{3.2}} = \sqrt{0.5625} = 0.75
$$

Load at max efficiency = $0.75 \times 250 = \mathbf{187.5 \text{ kVA}}$ **(2 marks)**

**(ii) Maximum Efficiency at 0.85 pf lagging:**

Output at $x = 0.75$:

$$
P_{\text{out}} = x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi = 0.75 \times 250 \times 0.85 = 159.375 \text{ kW}
$$

Losses at $x = 0.75$:

$$
P_{\text{cu}} = x^2 \cdot P_{\text{cu,full}} = 0.5625 \times 3.2 = 1.8 \text{ kW} = P_i
$$

(Total losses = $1.8 + 1.8 = 3.6$ kW — confirming the bisection principle.) **(1 mark)**

$$
\eta_{\max} = \frac{159.375}{159.375 + 3.6} = \frac{159.375}{162.975} = 0.9779 = \mathbf{97.79\%} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}
$$

**(iii) All-Day Efficiency:**

Energy output over 24 hours:

| Period | Load | pf | Duration | Energy (kWh) |
|--------|------|-----|----------|--------------|
| 1 | 200 kVA | 0.8 | 8 h | $200 \times 0.8 \times 8 = 1280$ |
| 2 | 150 kVA | 1.0 | 10 h | $150 \times 1.0 \times 10 = 1500$ |
| 3 | 0 | — | 6 h | $0$ |
| | | | **Total** | **$E_{\text{out}} = 2780$ kWh** |

Iron energy loss (24 h constant): $E_{\text{iron}} = 1.8 \times 24 = 43.2$ kWh **(1 mark)**

Copper energy loss: Need to express loads as fractions $x$ of full load.
- $x_1 = 200/250 = 0.8$, $x_2 = 150/250 = 0.6$

$$
E_{\text{cu}} = (0.8)^2 \times 3.2 \times 8 + (0.6)^2 \times 3.2 \times 10 + 0
$$

$$
E_{\text{cu}} = 0.64 \times 3.2 \times 8 + 0.36 \times 3.2 \times 10 = 16.384 + 11.52 = 27.904 \text{ kWh}
$$

Total energy losses: $E_{\text{losses}} = 43.2 + 27.904 = 71.104$ kWh **(1 mark)**

$$
\eta_{\text{all-day}} = \frac{2780}{2780 + 71.104} = \frac{2780}{2851.104} = 0.9751 = \mathbf{97.51\%} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit conversion error (–2 marks):** Lamination thickness $t$ in eddy current formula must be in **metres**, not mm. Volume in **m³**, not cm³ or litres. This is the #1 mistake.
> 2. **Iron loss vs Copper loss confusion (–2 marks):** OC test gives iron loss; SC test gives copper loss. Many students swap them. **Remember:** OC = open-circuited secondary, so the only loss is the iron loss in the energized primary.
> 3. **All-day efficiency forgetting iron loss at no-load period (–1 mark):** Iron loss runs for **all 24 hours**, even during no-load periods. Students often forget to add the 6-hour no-load iron energy.
> 4. **Fractional load copper loss (–1 mark):** $P_{\text{cu}} = x^2 \cdot P_{\text{cu,full}}$, NOT $x \cdot P_{\text{cu,full}}$. The square is non-negotiable.
> 5. **Stray load loss assumption (–1 mark):** When the SC test result is given and the problem mentions stray loss, separate it: $P_{\text{SC}} = P_{I^2R} + P_{\text{stray}}$. Use the given ratio to find both.
> 6. **Power factor in all-day efficiency (–1 mark):** Multiply by $\cos\phi$ for each period; do not assume unity pf unless stated.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final 60-Second Revision — Memorize These:**

- ✅ Transformer has **two main loss categories**: **Iron Loss** ($P_i$, constant) and **Copper Loss** ($P_{\text{cu}}$, variable as $x^2$).
- ✅ Hysteresis loss: $P_h = \eta B_{\max}^{1.6} f V$ — depends on core material and flux density.
- ✅ Eddy current loss: $P_e = K_e B_{\max}^{2} f^{2} t^{2} V$ — depends on $t^2$, hence thin laminations.
- ✅ **Lamination thickness** of CRGO steel is **0.23–0.35 mm** (modern) or **0.5 mm** (older designs).
- ✅ **OC test** → measures **iron loss** at rated voltage.
- ✅ **SC test** → measures **full-load copper loss** at rated current.
- ✅ **Maximum efficiency** condition: $P_i = x^2 P_{\text{cu,full}}$, giving $x_{\max} = \sqrt{P_i / P_{\text{cu,full}}}$.
- ✅ **All-day efficiency** matters for **distribution transformers** (24-hr energized).
- ✅ **Conventional efficiency** matters for **power transformers** (loaded most of the day).
- ✅ **Stray load loss** ≈ 0.5%–1% of full-load $I^2R$ loss (assume as given).
- ✅ Total efficiency expression: $\eta = \dfrac{x S \cos\phi}{x S \cos\phi + P_i + x^2 P_{\text{cu,full}}}$.
- ✅ **Units checklist**: $B$ in Tesla, $V$ in m³, $t$ in metres, $f$ in Hz.
- ✅ **Steinmetz exponent** is **1.6** unless stated otherwise.
- ✅ **CRGO steel** (Cold Rolled Grain Oriented) is the standard core material for power/distribution transformers in 2024.
- ✅ **Amorphous metal cores** are the modern frontier — reduce hysteresis loss by **~70%** compared to CRGO.

> 🎓 **End of Module 1 — Losses in Transformer Notes**
