# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constructional Details of a DC Machine:**
> A DC machine consists of two main mechanical assemblies: the **stator** (stationary part) housing the field system and the **rotor** (rotating part) known as the **armature**, separated by an **air gap**. The field system produces the main working flux, while the armature carries the induced EMF/current. Energy conversion between electrical and mechanical domains occurs via the commutator–brush assembly, which acts as a mechanical rectifier/inverter.

The principal physical components are:
1. **Yoke (Frame)** — outer cylindrical body
2. **Pole Core and Pole Shoe** — flux-carrying magnetic poles
3. **Field Windings (Shunt/Series/Compound)** — flux-producing coils
4. **Armature Core** — slotted cylindrical iron body
5. **Armature Winding** — host of conductors embedded in slots
6. **Commutator** — segmented copper cylinder
7. **Brushes and Brush Gear** — current collectors
8. **Shaft, Bearings, End Plates** — mechanical support

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Water Wheel & Dam System**

Think of a DC machine like a **hydroelectric dam with a controlled water wheel**:

- The **Yoke** is the *concrete dam wall* — it provides the structure and completes the magnetic circuit back to the other side.
- The **Pole Core & Pole Shoes** are the *magnetic "lips"* of the dam that shape and direct the water flow (magnetic flux) downward.
- The **Field Windings** are the *electromagnets controlling the dam gates* — turn them on, and flux flows.
- The **Armature Core** is the *water wheel housing* — laminated to reduce turbulence (eddy current losses).
- The **Armature Winding** is the *set of buckets on the wheel* that catch the energy.
- The **Commutator** is the *rotary valve* — it ensures that the buckets always receive water in the correct direction relative to the wheel's rotation (this is what makes the output DC, not AC).
- The **Brushes** are the *stationary pipes tapping water from the spinning valve* — they deliver the energy to the outside world.

Without the **commutator**, the induced EMF in each coil would naturally alternate (AC), but the commutator mechanically "flips" the connection to make it appear as DC at the output terminals.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Materials and Parameters (Highlighted for Board Exams):**
> - **Yoke Material:** Cast iron (small machines) or cast steel / silicon steel (large machines). **Permeability: high; must carry stray flux without saturation.**
> - **Pole Core Material:** **Annealed laminated silicon steel** (to reduce eddy current losses).
> - **Armature Core Material:** **High-grade silicon steel laminations** (0.35 mm to 0.5 mm thick), individually insulated by **varnish or paper**, to drastically reduce hysteresis and eddy current losses.
> - **Conductor Material:** **Copper** (high conductivity ≈ 5.8 × 10⁷ S/m) for armature and field windings; aluminium is occasionally used in small machines.
> - **Commutator Material:** **Hard-drawn copper segments** insulated from each other by **mica** (typically 0.5 mm to 1 mm thick).
> - **Brush Material:** **Carbon, graphite, or electrographite** — chosen for low contact resistance and self-lubricating properties.
> - **Air Gap Length:** Typically **1 mm to 3 mm** for small machines; up to **10 mm** for large machines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Lamination?** The armature core experiences an **alternating flux** at high frequency (proportional to speed × number of poles). Solid iron would dissipate enormous energy as heat. By using thin insulated sheets (laminations), eddy current paths are broken up and losses are reduced by orders of magnitude.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of Each Component

### 2.1.1 Yoke (Magnetic Frame)
- **Function:** (i) Supports the pole cores, (ii) Carries the **return flux** back to the opposite pole, completing the magnetic circuit, (iii) Provides mechanical protection.
- **Construction:** 
  - For **small machines:** Cast iron (cheap, brittle, good magnetic properties).
  - For **large machines:** Cast steel or rolled steel (high permeability, mechanically robust).
- **Design Point:** Cross-section must be large enough to avoid saturation by stray flux (typically 1.5 to 2 times the pole core area).

### 2.1.2 Pole Core and Pole Shoe
- **Pole Core:** The narrower part near the yoke that carries the field winding.
- **Pole Shoe:** The wider, curved face facing the armature. **Functions:**
  1. Spreads the flux uniformly over the armature surface.
  2. Provides mechanical support to the field winding.
  3. Reduces reluctance of the air gap by increasing cross-sectional area.
- **Material:** **Laminated silicon steel** sheets bolted together (lamination thickness ≈ 1 mm) to reduce eddy currents.

### 2.1.3 Field Windings
There are two major field winding types based on the connection to the armature:

| Type | Wire Gauge | Turns | Resistance | Connection | Flux Behavior |
|------|------------|-------|------------|------------|---------------|
| **Shunt Field** | Thin, many turns | High | High (≈ 50–200 Ω) | Parallel with armature | Nearly constant flux (field current ≈ 1–5% of rated current) |
| **Series Field** | Thick, few turns | Low | Very low (≈ 0.01–0.5 Ω) | Series with armature | Flux depends on armature current |
| **Compound** | Both | Both | Both | Shunt + Series | Combines characteristics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus:** The **Shunt Field** carries a small current and produces a near-constant flux. The **Series Field** is wound with few turns of heavy gauge to carry the full armature current.

### 2.1.4 Armature Core
- **Cylindrical structure** mounted on the shaft, with **slots** on the outer periphery to house the armature winding.
- **Slotted type** is most common (gives better mechanical strength); **slotless type** is used in very small machines.
- **Lamination thickness:** 0.35 mm to 0.5 mm, with each lamination coated with **varnish or oxide layer**.
- **Ventilating ducts** (radial channels) are provided for cooling.

### 2.1.5 Armature Winding
Two main categories:

1. **Lap Winding**
   - Number of parallel paths: $A = P$ (where $P$ is the number of poles)
   - Used for **high-current, low-voltage** machines
   - Suitable for currents up to several hundred amperes

2. **Wave Winding**
   - Number of parallel paths: $A = 2$ (always, irrespective of poles)
   - Used for **high-voltage, low-current** machines
   - Cannot handle very high currents

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Two Types?** Lap winding provides more parallel paths (more "lanes" for current to flow), reducing current per path. Wave winding has only 2 paths, so it is ideal when voltage is the priority.

### 2.1.6 Commutator
- **Cylindrical structure** made of **hard-drawn copper segments** insulated from one another and from the shaft by **mica strips** (called **commutator mica** or **segment mica**).
- **Functions:**
  1. Acts as a **mechanical rectifier** — converts the internal AC (in the armature) to DC at the brushes.
  2. Collects current from the armature winding and delivers it to the external circuit (in generator mode).
  3. In motor mode, it feeds DC into the appropriate armature conductors as they move from one polarity region to another.
- **Undercutting:** The mica between segments is **undercut** (cut below the copper level) by 0.5–1.5 mm to prevent the mica from wearing faster than copper (since mica is harder).

### 2.1.7 Brushes and Brush Gear
- **Brushes** are typically made of **carbon, graphite, or a carbon-graphite composite** (sometimes with copper dust for high-current applications).
- Held against the commutator by **brush holders** with **springs** providing constant pressure (typically 1.5–2.5 N/cm²).
- Multiple brushes are used per arm, arranged in a **brush yoke** to ensure equal current distribution.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for Constructional Details (Module 2):**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Number of parallel paths (Lap) | $A = P$ | paths | $P$ = number of poles |
| 2 | Number of parallel paths (Wave) | $A = 2$ | paths | Independent of $P$ |
| 3 | Pole Pitch | $\tau_{p} = \dfrac{\pi D}{P}$ | slots or conductors | Distance between two adjacent poles |
| 4 | Coil Pitch (Back Pitch) | $Y_{b}$ | slots | Should be $\approx \tau_{p}$ |
| 5 | Front Pitch | $Y_{f}$ | slots | For Lap: $Y_{b} - Y_{f} = \pm 2$ |
| 6 | Commutator Pitch | $Y_{c} = \pm 1$ | segments | $\pm$ sign for progressive/retrogressive |
| 7 | Average EMF Generated | $E_{g} = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ | Volts | $\Phi$ in Wb, $Z$ = total conductors, $N$ = rpm |
| 8 | Total Conductors | $Z = 2 \times \text{coils} \times \text{turns}$ | conductors | Each coil has 2 sides; each side = 1 conductor |
| 9 | Slot Angle (electrical) | $\gamma = \dfrac{180° \times P}{\text{Total slots}}$ | degrees (electrical) | Used for coil pitch calculations |
| 10 | Number of Commutator Segments | $C = \text{Number of coils} = Z/2$ | segments | For simplex winding |

> [!WARNING]
> **Formula Notation Trap:** In KTU board exams, students often confuse $\tau_{p}$ (pole pitch) with the coil pitch $Y_{b}$. Remember: **Pole pitch is a function of geometry** (depends on D and P), while **coil pitch is a winding design choice** (should be close to $\tau_{p}$).

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The choice of **lap vs wave winding** directly impacts machine design for specific applications:

- **Traction motors (locomotives, EVs)** → **Lap winding** → high current for high torque
- **High-voltage generators in power plants** → **Wave winding** → high voltage, low current
- **Industrial DC shunt motors** → **Lap with shunt field** → nearly constant speed
- **Cranes, hoists, elevators** → **Series or compound motors** → high starting torque

The **commutator** is the heart of any DC machine and is the primary reason DC machines are **more expensive, bulkier, and maintenance-intensive** than AC machines. Modern power electronics (using IGBT/MOSFET inverters) increasingly replace mechanical commutators — a concept central to the broader field of **Electrical Drives**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Parallel Paths in Lap and Wave Windings

This is a **fundamental KTU exam derivation** that examiners frequently test.

### 3.1.1 Lap Winding — Derivation of $A = P$

**Step 1: Define the lap winding rule**
In a simplex lap winding, the commutator pitch is $Y_c = \pm 1$, meaning the winding **advances by one commutator segment** with each coil.

**Step 2: Define the back pitch and front pitch**
Let $Y_b$ = back pitch (number of slots between the two sides of a coil, counted in the direction of rotation) and $Y_f$ = front pitch (distance from the end of one coil to the start of the next coil).

For a simplex lap winding, the standard relation is:

$$Y_b - Y_f = \pm 2$$

**Step 3: Trace the number of parallel paths**
If the machine has $P$ poles, the winding will close on itself only after completing $P$ laps (one for each pole). This means the number of distinct closed paths formed by the entire winding is exactly equal to the number of poles.

**Step 4: Conclude**
Therefore, the number of parallel paths is:

$$A = P \quad \text{(Lap Winding)}$$

---

### 3.1.2 Wave Winding — Derivation of $A = 2$

**Step 1: Define the wave winding rule**
In a simplex wave winding, the commutator pitch is calculated such that the winding visits the brushes of the **same polarity** before closing:

$$Y_c = \frac{C \pm 1}{\frac{P}{2}}$$

where $C$ = number of commutator segments and $P$ = number of poles.

**Step 2: Trace the winding path**
The winding "waves" through the armature, touching every commutator segment in **two distinct routes** — one starting from the positive brush and one from the negative brush. After $C$ segments, it returns to the starting segment via the other polarity.

**Step 3: Identify the parallel paths**
This creates exactly **two independent closed paths** in the armature, regardless of the number of poles.

**Step 4: Conclude**
Therefore, the number of parallel paths is:

$$A = 2 \quad \text{(Wave Winding)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Generated EMF in a DC Machine

This is the **most tested derivation** in KTU Module 2.

### Step 1: Flux cut per revolution by one conductor
The flux per pole is $\Phi$ Webers. In one revolution, a single conductor cuts the flux of all $P$ poles. Therefore, flux cut per revolution by one conductor:

$$\Phi_{\text{cut}} = \Phi P \quad \text{(Webers per revolution)}$$

### Step 2: Time for one revolution
If the speed is $N$ revolutions per minute (rpm), the time per revolution is:

$$T = \frac{60}{N} \quad \text{seconds}$$

### Step 3: Average EMF per conductor
By Faraday's Law, average EMF per conductor is flux cut per second:

$$e_{\text{avg per conductor}} = \frac{\Phi P}{60/N} = \frac{\Phi P N}{60} \quad \text{Volts}$$

### Step 4: Total EMF of the machine
Let $Z$ = total number of armature conductors. These conductors are distributed in $A$ parallel paths. Conductors per path = $Z/A$. Conductors connected in series per path = $Z/A$. All paths are in parallel, so the EMF of one path equals the total machine EMF.

Adding EMFs of all conductors in series in one path:

$$E_g = e_{\text{avg per conductor}} \times \frac{Z}{A} = \frac{\Phi P N}{60} \times \frac{Z}{A}$$

### Step 5: Final equation (with sign convention — KTU standard)

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A} \quad \text{Volts}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-verification (sanity check):** For a 4-pole machine with $Z = 480$, $N = 1000$ rpm, $\Phi = 0.02$ Wb, lap winding ($A = 4$):

$$E_g = \frac{0.02 \times 480 \times 1000 \times 4}{60 \times 4} = \frac{38400}{240} = 160 \text{ V}$$

This is a realistic DC generator output, confirming the formula's validity.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table for Board Exams

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Comparison Table (Frequently asked in 2-mark questions):**

| Feature | Lap Winding | Wave Winding |
|---------|-------------|--------------|
| Parallel paths ($A$) | $A = P$ | $A = 2$ |
| Commutator pitch | $Y_c = \pm 1$ | $Y_c = (C \pm 1) / (P/2)$ |
| Current capacity | High (current splits) | Low (only 2 paths) |
| Voltage capacity | Low (less series turns per path) | High (more series turns per path) |
| Coil identification | Equalizer rings needed | Not required |
| Simplex form | $A = P$ | $A = 2$ |
| Duplex form | $A = 2P$ | $A = 4$ |
| Application | DC traction, large motors | Small motors, high-voltage generators |
| Number of brush sets | Equal to $P$ | Only 2 (irrespective of $P$) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students write "Wave winding has $A = P$" — this is wrong. The wave winding **always** has $A = 2$ for simplex. Only lap winding gives $A = P$.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A 4-pole DC machine has 600 armature conductors. The flux per pole is 25 mWb. The armature is lap wound. Calculate the EMF generated when the machine runs at 1500 rpm.

**Given:**
- $P = 4$
- $Z = 600$
- $\Phi = 25 \text{ mWb} = 0.025 \text{ Wb}$
- $N = 1500 \text{ rpm}$
- Lap winding → $A = P = 4$

**Solution:**

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$$

Substitute the values:

$$E_g = \frac{0.025 \times 600 \times 1500 \times 4}{60 \times 4}$$

Cancel $P$ and $A$ (both = 4):

$$E_g = \frac{0.025 \times 600 \times 1500}{60}$$

$$E_g = \frac{0.025 \times 600 \times 25}{1}$$

$$E_g = 0.025 \times 15000$$

$$E_g = 375 \text{ V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** $E_g = 375$ V. This is a typical voltage level for a medium-sized industrial DC generator.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — DC Machine Architecture (Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[DC Supply or Load] -->|Terminals| A2[Brushes Carbon Graphite]
    A2 -->|Sliding Contact| A3[Commutator Segmented Copper]
    A3 -->|Connections| A4[Armature Winding Lap or Wave]
    A4 -->|Embedded in Slots| A5[Armature Core Laminated Silicon Steel]
    A5 -->|Rotates on| A6[Shaft]
    A6 -->|Mechanical Coupling| A7[Load or Prime Mover]

    A8[Battery or Exciter DC Source] -->|Field Current| A9[Field Windings Shunt or Series]
    A9 -->|Magnetizes| A10[Pole Core and Pole Shoe Laminated Steel]
    A10 -->|Directs Flux| A11[Air Gap 1 to 3 mm]
    A11 -->|Through| A5
    A5 -->|Return Path| A12[Yoke Cast Iron or Cast Steel]
    A12 -->|Completes Circuit Back To| A10

    A6 -->|Bearing Support| A13[End Plates and Bearings]
```

**Caption:** Functional architecture of a DC machine showing the **electrical circuit path** (top) and the **magnetic circuit path** (bottom) interacting through the air gap at the armature.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Lap vs Wave Winding Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LAP["LAP WINDING: A equals P"]
        L1[Brush Positive] --- L2[Path 1]
        L1 --- L3[Path 2]
        L1 --- L4[Path 3]
        L1 --- L5[Path 4]
        L2 --- L6[Brush Negative]
        L3 --- L6
        L4 --- L6
        L5 --- L6
    end

    subgraph WAVE["WAVE WINDING: A equals 2"]
        W1[Brush Positive] --- W2[Path 1 Left Half]
        W1 --- W3[Path 2 Right Half]
        W2 --- W4[Brush Negative]
        W3 --- W4
    end
```

**Caption:** Visual contrast — Lap winding forms **$P$ parallel paths** between brushes, while Wave winding always forms **exactly 2 paths** irrespective of the number of poles.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Material Flow in Construction

```mermaid

    P3 --> P4[Armature Core]

    R2[Cast Iron or Cast Steel] --> Q1[Pouring and Casting]

    R3[Copper Wire Enamel Coated] --> S1[Winding on Bobbin]

    R4[Hard Drawn Copper Bars] --> T1[Cutting to Wedge Shape]
    T1 --> T2[Mica Insulation Between Segments]
    T2 --> T3[Commutator Assembly]
```

**Caption:** Constructional material flow showing how raw materials (silicon steel, cast iron, copper wire, hard-drawn copper) are processed into the four major subassemblies of a DC machine.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** List the main parts of a DC machine. Mention the function of the yoke.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Yoke** — outer frame; provides mechanical support and carries the return magnetic flux.
> 2. **Pole core and pole shoe** — supports field winding and directs flux across air gap.
> 3. **Field windings** — produce the working flux when energized.
> 4. **Armature core** — laminated slotted structure housing the armature winding.
> 5. **Armature winding** — induces EMF when flux is cut.
> 6. **Commutator** — converts internal AC to DC at the terminals.
> 7. **Brushes** — current collectors from the rotating commutator.
> 8. **Bearings, shaft, end covers** — mechanical support.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** Distinguish between lap winding and wave winding in a DC machine (any 3 points).
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Parallel paths:** Lap: $A = P$; Wave: $A = 2$.
> 2. **Commutator pitch:** Lap: $Y_c = \pm 1$; Wave: $Y_c = (C \pm 1)/(P/2)$.
> 3. **Application:** Lap used for high-current low-voltage machines; wave used for high-voltage low-current machines.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**
> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the constructional details of a DC machine. Label all major parts. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 4-pole, lap-wound DC machine has 600 armature conductors. The flux per pole is 30 mWb. Find the EMF generated when running at 1200 rpm. If the same machine is rewound as a wave winding, what will be the new EMF at the same speed and flux? **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks**

The student should describe:

1. **Yoke:** Made of cast iron/cast steel; carries return flux; provides mechanical support. (1 mark)
2. **Pole core and pole shoe:** Made of laminated silicon steel; supports field winding; pole shoe spreads flux uniformly. (1 mark)
3. **Field winding:** Shunt (many turns, thin wire) and/or series (few turns, thick wire). Wound on the pole core. (1 mark)
4. **Armature core:** Cylindrical, made of 0.35–0.5 mm thick laminated silicon steel; slots on the periphery hold the winding. (1 mark)
5. **Armature winding:** Conductors placed in slots; insulated with varnish/composite; can be lap or wave. (1 mark)
6. **Commutator:** Hard-drawn copper segments separated by mica; acts as a mechanical rectifier. (1 mark)
7. **Brushes and brush gear:** Carbon/graphite brushes held by springs; current transfer to external circuit. (1 mark)

A neat labelled diagram is **mandatory** and carries 1 of the 7 marks.

### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $P = 4$, $Z = 600$, $\Phi = 30 \text{ mWb} = 0.030 \text{ Wb}$, $N = 1200$ rpm.

**Case 1: Lap winding**

$$A = P = 4$$

$$E_{g,\text{lap}} = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A} = \frac{0.030 \times 600 \times 1200 \times 4}{60 \times 4}$$

Cancel $P$ and $A$:

$$E_{g,\text{lap}} = \frac{0.030 \times 600 \times 1200}{60} = \frac{21600}{60} = 360 \text{ V}$$

**Case 2: Wave winding**

$$A = 2$$

$$E_{g,\text{wave}} = \frac{0.030 \times 600 \times 1200 \times 4}{60 \times 2}$$

$$E_{g,\text{wave}} = \frac{86400}{120} = 720 \text{ V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (1 mark):** Wave winding produces **twice the EMF** of lap winding for the same conductor count because the conductors per series path double. This is why wave windings are used in high-voltage machines.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative)**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**
> **(a)** Explain the construction and function of the commutator in a DC machine. What is commutator segmentation and undercutting? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 6-pole, wave-wound DC generator has 720 conductors and runs at 1000 rpm. The flux per pole is 25 mWb. Calculate the generated EMF. If the load draws 200 A, find the current per path and the EMF drop per path (assume brush contact drop = 1 V per brush). **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks**

1. **Construction of Commutator (3 marks):**
   - Cylindrical assembly of **hard-drawn copper segments**.
   - Each segment is insulated from the next by a **mica strip** (called segment mica).
   - Segments are clamped together using **V-groove insulating rings** and a **sleeve**.
   - The entire assembly is mounted on the **shaft** and rotates with the armature.

2. **Functions of Commutator (2 marks):**
   - Acts as a **mechanical rectifier** — converts the internally induced AC to DC at the brushes.
   - Enables **current collection** from a moving (rotating) conductor to a stationary external circuit.

3. **Segmentation and Undercutting (2 marks):**
   - **Segmentation:** The commutator is divided into many segments (one per armature coil) so that the current in external brushes remains unidirectional. The number of segments = number of coils.
   - **Undercutting:** Mica between segments is cut 0.5–1.5 mm **below** the copper surface. This is necessary because mica is harder than copper; otherwise mica would protrude, cause sparking, and wear the brushes.

### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $P = 6$, Wave wound ($A = 2$), $Z = 720$, $N = 1000$ rpm, $\Phi = 0.025$ Wb, $I_L = 200$ A, brush drop = 1 V per brush.

**Step 1: Generated EMF (2 marks)**

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A} = \frac{0.025 \times 720 \times 1000 \times 6}{60 \times 2}$$

$$E_g = \frac{0.025 \times 720 \times 6000}{120} = \frac{108000}{120} = 900 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Current per path (2 marks)**

$$I_{\text{path}} = \frac{I_L}{A} = \frac{200}{2} = 100 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Total brush contact drop (2 marks)**

In a wave-wound machine, there are 2 brush sets (positive and negative). Each brush has 1 V drop:

$$V_{\text{brush}} = 2 \times 1 = 2 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Terminal voltage (1 mark)**

$$V = E_g - I_a R_a - V_{\text{brush}}$$

Without armature resistance given, the relevant quantities are: $E_g = 900$ V, $I_{\text{path}} = 100$ A, total brush drop = 2 V.

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer summary:** $E_g = 900$ V; $I_{\text{path}} = 100$ A; brush contact drop = 2 V total.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in flux:** Students write $\Phi = 30$ instead of $0.030$ Wb → 10× error. Always convert mWb to Wb.
> 2. **Confusing $A$ and $P$ in lap winding:** Since $A = P$ in lap winding, they cancel out, but students may still write $A = 2$ in error.
> 3. **Brush drop direction:** Brush drop **subtracts** from $E_g$ in a generator (just like $I_a R_a$).
> 4. **Total vs per-path quantities:** Wave winding has only 2 paths but the **total current is still the load current** — only the current per path changes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 2 — Constructional Details:**

- **Yoke** = frame; carries return flux; cast iron (small) or cast steel (large).
- **Pole core + pole shoe** = laminated silicon steel; supports field coil; spreads flux.
- **Field winding** = either **shunt** (many turns, thin, high R) or **series** (few turns, thick, low R) or **compound** (both).
- **Armature core** = cylindrical; **0.35–0.5 mm laminated silicon steel**; slotted; reduces eddy current losses.
- **Armature winding** = embedded in slots; can be **lap** ($A=P$) or **wave** ($A=2$).
- **Commutator** = hard-drawn copper segments separated by **mica**; converts AC↔DC mechanically; **undercutting** required.
- **Brushes** = carbon/graphite; sliding contact on commutator; held by springs.
- **EMF equation:** $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ — memorize and know all variables.
- **Lap winding** = high current, low voltage; **Wave winding** = high voltage, low current.
- **Air gap** is critical: too large → low flux; too small → mechanical friction.
- **Lamination purpose:** Reduce **eddy current losses** (and partially hysteresis).
- **Wave winding** requires only **2 brush sets**, regardless of $P$ (one positive, one negative).
- **Lap winding** requires **$P$ brush sets** (one set per pole).
- **Undercutting** of commutator mica: depth 0.5–1.5 mm below copper surface.
- **Material choices:** Yoke = cast iron/steel; Pole = laminated Si-steel; Armature = laminated Si-steel; Conductors = copper; Commutator = hard-drawn copper; Brushes = carbon-graphite.
- **Duplex winding** in lap: $A = 2P$; in wave: $A = 4$.
- **Total slots = total coils = total commutator segments** (for simplex windings).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT302 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES & DRIVES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three-Phase Induction Motor (Operation):** A self-starting, brushless, robust AC rotating machine that operates on the principle of *electromagnetic induction* discovered by **Nikola Tesla (1888)**. It converts balanced three-phase electrical input into mechanical output through the interaction between a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** produced by the stator and the induced currents in the short-circuited rotor cage/winding.

**Operational Block (KTU 2024 Module 2 focus):** The motor runs at a speed **N_r** that is always **less than synchronous speed N_s**. The relative difference, normalized, is called **slip (s)** — the single most critical operational parameter governing torque, current, and efficiency.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Swinging Door Effect**
> Imagine a rotating merry-go-round (the **stator RMF**) and a freely rolling metal ball placed inside it (the **rotor cage**). The ball never spins as fast as the carousel — it constantly **chases** it. The "lag" between carousel speed and ball speed is exactly what we call *slip*. Without this lag, the magnetic field lines would not *cut* the rotor bars, **no current** would be induced (by Faraday's Law), and **no torque** would be produced. So paradoxically: **the rotor must slip to work**.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Supply frequency | f | **50 Hz** (India, KTU standard) |
| Number of poles | P | 2, 4, 6, 8 (always even) |
| Synchronous speed | N_s | **750 / 1000 / 1500 / 3000 rpm** |
| Slip | s | **0.02 to 0.06** (normal running) |
| Power factor | cos φ | 0.8 – 0.9 lagging at full load |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** Always state the supply frequency as **f = 50 Hz** unless explicitly given. A slip value of **s ≈ 0.04** is the most commonly assumed full-load value in numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**Step 1 — Generation of Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF):**
A balanced **3-phase, 2-pole** winding excited by a 3-phase AC supply produces a magnetic field whose **north pole rotates in space** at synchronous speed N_s. The field completes one mechanical revolution per electrical cycle.

**Step 2 — Faraday's Law Activates the Rotor:**
As RMF flux Φ sweeps past the stationary rotor bars, the relative velocity `(N_s − N_r)` induces an EMF in each rotor bar:
$$e = B \cdot l \cdot v_{rel}$$
This EMF drives circulating currents through the shorted end-rings (cage rotor) or through the external resistors (wound rotor).

**Step 3 — Lenz's Law Produces Torque:**
The induced rotor current, placed in the stator's magnetic field, experiences a **Lorentz force** `F = BIl`. Summing forces over all bars yields a unidirectional electromagnetic torque that *drags the rotor in the same direction* as the RMF.

**Step 4 — Steady-State Equilibrium:**
Rotor accelerates until developed torque equals load torque (plus rotational losses). At this mechanical equilibrium, slip settles to a stable operating value (typically 3–6%).

**Step 5 — Why Speed Cannot Reach Synchronous:**
If N_r = N_s, there is no relative motion → no induced EMF → no rotor current → no torque. Hence the rotor *must* run below synchronous speed. This is the defining operational behavior of the **asynchronous (induction) motor**.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 8 equations — they cover ~85% of Module 2 numerical problems.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Synchronous speed (rad/s) | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi f}{P/2} = \dfrac{4\pi f}{P}$ | rad/s |
| 2 | Synchronous speed (rpm) | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | rpm |
| 3 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | dimensionless, 0 < s < 1 |
| 4 | Rotor frequency | $f_r = s f$ | Hz |
| 5 | Rotor EMF (per phase, running) | $E_{2r} = s \, E_2$ | E₂ is standstill EMF |
| 6 | Rotor reactance (running) | $X_{2r} = s \, X_2$ | X₂ is standstill reactance |
| 7 | Mechanical synchronous speed (rad/s) | $\omega_m = \dfrac{2 \pi N_s}{60}$ | rad/s |
| 8 | Electromagnetic Torque | $T_e = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}$ | N·m |

| # | Special Operating Point | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| 9 | Slip at maximum torque | $s_m = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2}$ |
| 10 | Maximum torque | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{E_2^2}{2 X_2}$ |
| 11 | Starting torque (s=1) | $T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}$ |
| 12 | Starting current ratio | $\dfrac{I_{st}}{I_{fl}} \approx 5 \text{ to } 7$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Engineering Insight:** $T_{max}$ is **independent of rotor resistance R₂** — only $s_m$ shifts! This single fact unlocks the entire *speed-control-by-rotor-resistance* method (used in slip-ring / wound-rotor induction motors and crane/hoist drives).

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Pumps, Compressors, Conveyors**: Operate at near-constant speed in the *stable region* (low slip region, s < s_m).
- **Cranes, Hoists, Elevators**: Use the *unstable region* (high slip) where the wound rotor allows resistance control for soft starting and high starting torque.
- **Electric Vehicles (EVs)**: Modern EV traction uses induction motors paired with variable-frequency drives (VFDs) to vary $N_s \propto f$.
- **Wind Turbine Generators**: Operate as **DFIG (Doubly-Fed Induction Generator)** above synchronous speed (negative slip).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Synchronous Speed from Rotating Magnetic Field Geometry

**Statement:** Prove that $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ for a balanced 3-phase, P-pole winding.

**Proof:**

A P-pole machine requires `P/2` electrical cycles (i.e., `P/2` complete flux reversals through one mechanical revolution).

The supply frequency f implies that **one electrical cycle** = $\dfrac{1}{f}$ seconds.

In one second, the RMF completes **f electrical cycles**, which corresponds to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mechanical revolutions per second} &= \frac{\text{Electrical cycles per second}}{\text{Poles}/2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{f}{P/2} = \frac{2f}{P} \quad \text{[rev/s]}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to revolutions per minute (multiply by 60):

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_s &= 60 \times \frac{2f}{P} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{120 f}{P} \quad \text{rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic Step:** The division by `P/2` arises because each mechanical revolution needs the field to sweep through `P/2` North-South pole pairs.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Rotating Magnetic Field (RMMF Vector Sum)

**Statement:** Show that three stationary coils displaced by 120° (spatial) and excited by three currents displaced by 120° (temporal) produce a resultant MMF of constant magnitude rotating at $\omega_s = 2\pi f$ rad/s.

Let the three phase currents be:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_R &= I_m \cos(\omega t) \\
i_Y &= I_m \cos(\omega t - 120°) \\
i_B &= I_m \cos(\omega t - 240°)
\end{aligned}
$$

Each coil's MMF is a spatial sinusoid displaced along the air-gap. Taking the air-gap angle $\theta$ (mechanical) as reference:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_R(\theta, t) &= N I_m \cos(\omega t) \cos(\theta) \\
F_Y(\theta, t) &= N I_m \cos(\omega t - 120°) \cos(\theta - 120°) \\
F_B(\theta, t) &= N I_m \cos(\omega t - 240°) \cos(\theta - 240°)
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the trigonometric identity $2 \cos A \cos B = \cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_R &= \frac{N I_m}{2}\left[\cos(\omega t - \theta) + \cos(\omega t + \theta)\right] \\
F_Y &= \frac{N I_m}{2}\left[\cos(\omega t - \theta) + \cos(\omega t + \theta - 240°)\right] \\
F_B &= \frac{N I_m}{2}\left[\cos(\omega t - \theta) + \cos(\omega t + \theta - 480°)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing the *second terms* (they form a balanced 3-phase set summing to zero):

$$
F_R + F_Y + F_B = \frac{3 N I_m}{2} \cos(\omega t - \theta)
$$

This is a **constant-magnitude** wave ($\frac{3NI_m}{2}$) that is stationary in space when $\omega t - \theta = 0$, i.e., $\theta = \omega t$.

Hence **the field rotates at angular velocity $\omega_s = \omega = 2\pi f$ rad/s** in the electrical sense. 

$$
\boxed{\,N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} \text{ rpm}\,}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Rotor EMF Equation (Running Condition)

**Given:** Standstill rotor EMF per phase = $E_2 = 4.44 \, f \, N_2 \, \Phi \, K_{w2}$

**At slip s**, rotor frequency $f_r = s f$, and flux per pole Φ (approximately constant, since rotor flux ≈ stator flux):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{2r} &= 4.44 \, f_r \, N_2 \, \Phi \, K_{w2} \\
E_{2r} &= 4.44 \, (s f) \, N_2 \, \Phi \, K_{w2} \\
E_{2r} &= s \cdot E_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Similarly, standstill rotor reactance $X_2 = 2\pi f L_2$ becomes:

$$
X_{2r} = 2\pi f_r L_2 = 2\pi (sf) L_2 = s X_2
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Electromagnetic Torque Equation (KTU Most-Frequently Asked)

**Starting from the power flow per phase in the rotor circuit** (resistance referred to stator):

Rotor copper loss per phase = $I_2^2 R_2$
Air-gap power per phase = $I_2^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{s}$  (since rotor resistance effectively becomes $R_2/s$)

Total air-gap power (3 phases):

$$
P_g = 3 \cdot I_2^2 \cdot \frac{R_2}{s}
$$

Rotor copper loss (3 phases):

$$
P_{cu} = 3 \cdot I_2^2 \cdot R_2 = s \cdot P_g
$$

Mechanical power developed (air-gap power − rotor copper loss):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_m &= P_g - P_{cu} \\
&= 3 I_2^2 \cdot \frac{R_2}{s} - 3 I_2^2 R_2 \\
&= 3 I_2^2 R_2 \left(\frac{1 - s}{s}\right) \\
&= (1 - s) \cdot P_g
\end{aligned}
$$

Electromagnetic torque (dividing mechanical power by synchronous mechanical speed):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_e &= \frac{P_m}{\omega_m} = \frac{P_m}{\omega_s / \text{?}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi N_s}{60}$ in mechanical rad/s (this IS already the mechanical synchronous speed for a 2-pole machine; for P-pole, we use the electrical-to-mechanical ratio correctly), we obtain:

$$
T_e = \frac{P_g}{\omega_s} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{I_2^2 R_2}{s}
$$

Substituting $I_2 = \dfrac{s E_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}}$ from the rotor equivalent circuit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_e &= \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{R_2}{s} \cdot \frac{s^2 E_2^2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2} \\
&= \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2} \text{ N·m}\,}
$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Slip at Maximum Torque

To find $s_m$, differentiate $T_e$ with respect to `s` and equate to zero. Let $K = \dfrac{3 E_2^2}{\omega_s}$ (constant). Then:

$$
T_e = K \cdot \frac{s R_2}{R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2}
$$

$$
\frac{dT_e}{ds} = K \cdot \frac{(R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2)(R_2) - (s R_2)(2s X_2^2)}{(\cdot)^2} = 0
$$

Numerator simplifies to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_2 (R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2) - 2 s^2 R_2 X_2^2 &= 0 \\
R_2^3 + s^2 R_2 X_2^2 - 2 s^2 R_2 X_2^2 &= 0 \\
R_2^3 - s^2 R_2 X_2^2 &= 0 \\
R_2^2 &= s^2 X_2^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,s_m = \frac{R_2}{X_2}\,}
$$

> **Substituting back:** $T_{max} = \dfrac{3 E_2^2}{2 \omega_s X_2}$ — independent of $R_2$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Three-Phase Stator Winding → Rotating Magnetic Field

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["3-Phase AC Supply\nf = 50 Hz"] --> B["Stator Phase R\nI_R = I_m cos wt"]
    A --> C["Stator Phase Y\nI_Y = I_m cos wt-120"]
    A --> D["Stator Phase B\nI_B = I_m cos wt-240"]
    B --> E["Spatial Winding R\nat theta = 0"]
    C --> F["Spatial Winding Y\nat theta = 120"]
    D --> G["Spatial Winding B\nat theta = 240"]
    E --> H["Resultant MMF\nF = 1.5 N I_m cos wt - theta"]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I["Rotating Magnetic Field\nomega_s = 2 pi f rad per s"]
    I --> J["Cuts Rotor Bars\nInduces EMF E2r = s E2"]
    J --> K["Rotor Currents\nCirculate in Cage"]
    K --> L["Lorentz Force\nF = B I l"]
    L --> M["Torque Te\ndrags Rotor at N_r"]
```

### 4.2 Equivalent Circuit Per-Phase (Rotor Referred to Stator Frequency)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1["V1\nStator Voltage"] --> R1["R1\nStator Resistance"]
    R1 --> X1["X1\nStator Leakage Reactance"]
    X1 --> N["Node: Air-Gap"]
    N --> Rc["Rc\nCore Loss Branch"]
    N --> Xm["jXm\nMagnetising Reactance"]
    N --> R2s["R2 / s\nRotor Resistance / slip"]
    R2s --> X2["j X2\nRotor Leakage Reactance"]
    X2 --> END["Short\nCage"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The clever transformation `R2 → R2/s` converts the **rotating** machine into a **stationary transformer equivalent** — this is the foundational trick used in **all** KTU induction motor problems.

### 4.3 Torque–Slip Characteristic Curve (Operating Regions)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Slip s = 0\nN_r = N_s\nNo torque"] --> S1["Low-slip Region\nStable Operation\ns = 0.02 to 0.06\nNormal Running"]
    S1 --> SM["s = s_m = R2/X2\nMaximum Torque\nTmax independent of R2"]
    SM --> S2["High-slip Region\nUnstable / Starting\ns = 1 at start"]
    S2 --> S3["s = 1\nStarting Torque T_st"]
    S0 --> ST["Stator Flux\nphi constant"]
    ST --> S1
    ST --> SM
    ST --> S2
```

### 4.4 Power Flow Stage Diagram (KTU Standard Sankey-Style)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["P_in\nInput Electrical Power"] --> P2["Stator Cu Loss\n3 I1^2 R1"]
    P2 --> P3["P_g\nAir-Gap Power"]
    P3 --> P4["Rotor Cu Loss\ns times P_g"]
    P3 --> P5["P_m = 1-s times P_g\nMechanical Power Developed"]
    P5 --> P6["Rotational Losses\nFriction + Windage"]
    P6 --> P7["P_out\nShaft Output Power"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, Dec 2024 Pattern]**

**Q1. [3 Marks] Define slip of an induction motor. Why cannot an induction motor run at synchronous speed?**

**Model Answer:**
Slip is defined as the relative difference between synchronous speed and actual rotor speed, expressed as a fraction (or percentage) of synchronous speed:
$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

An induction motor cannot run at synchronous speed because at $N_r = N_s$, the relative velocity between the rotating magnetic field and rotor conductors becomes zero. By Faraday's law, no EMF is induced in the rotor, no rotor current flows, and consequently no electromagnetic torque is developed. Hence the rotor must always slip below $N_s$ to produce torque — this is the fundamental reason it is called an **asynchronous** machine.

---

**Q2. [3 Marks] State the conditions for maximum torque in a 3-phase induction motor. What is the value of maximum torque?**

**Model Answer:**
Maximum torque occurs when the rotor reactance equals rotor resistance (in standstill terms) under load:
$$s_m = \frac{R_2}{X_2}$$

The maximum torque value is given by:
$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^2}{2 X_2} \quad \text{N·m}$$

**Key observation:** $T_{max}$ is independent of rotor resistance $R_2$ — varying $R_2$ only shifts the slip $s_m$ at which $T_{max}$ occurs.

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never confuse `ω_s` (synchronous in rad/s) with `2πN_s/60`** — for a P-pole motor, ensure the correct mechanical rad/s is used. Marks are routinely lost here.
> 2. **Always convert % slip to decimal** (e.g., 4% = 0.04) before substituting into equations.
> 3. **In equivalent circuit problems, show the `R₂/s` transformation explicitly** — skipping this step costs 2 marks.
> 4. **Units must accompany every numerical answer.** A naked "12.5" loses marks; write "12.5 N·m".
> 5. **Forgot the factor of 3** in 3-phase torque formula? This is the #1 KTU valuation trap — every year, ~30% of students lose 3–4 marks for missing it.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]

**A.(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch and phasor diagram, explain the production of a rotating magnetic field by a 3-phase, 2-pole winding. Derive the expression $N_s = 120f/P$.

**Model Answer:**

A balanced 3-phase, 2-pole stator winding consists of three identical coils R, Y, B displaced by **120° in space**. When energized by balanced 3-phase currents $i_R, i_Y, i_B$ displaced by 120° in time, each coil produces a sinusoidal MMF pulse along its axis. The vector sum of the three MMFs at any instant gives a resultant MMF of constant magnitude (1.5 N I_m) whose axis rotates at synchronous speed.

The mathematical derivation is shown in **Section 3.2** of these notes. The key result:

$$F_{resultant}(\theta, t) = \frac{3NI_m}{2}\cos(\omega t - \theta)$$

For the field to remain at a fixed point in space, $\omega t - \theta = 0$, i.e., $\theta = \omega t$. The angular velocity is therefore $\omega_s = \omega = 2\pi f$ rad/s in electrical terms. For a P-pole machine, each mechanical revolution requires P/2 electrical cycles, giving:

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} \quad \text{rpm}$$

*Diagrams: Sketch showing three coils 120° apart, MMF phasors at ωt = 0°, 60°, 120°, and the resultant vector rotating — full marks (3 for sketch, 3 for derivation, 1 for final equation).*

---

**A.(b) [7 Marks]** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has a rotor resistance and standstill reactance per phase of 0.02 Ω and 0.1 Ω respectively. If the ratio of standstill rotor EMF to standstill rotor reactance is 8, find:
(i) Synchronous speed
(ii) Slip at maximum torque
(iii) Maximum torque
(iv) Starting torque

Take $\omega_s = 157 \text{ rad/s}$.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- P = 4, f = 50 Hz
- $R_2 = 0.02 \, \Omega$
- $X_2 = 0.1 \, \Omega$
- $\dfrac{E_2}{X_2} = 8 \Rightarrow E_2 = 8 \times 0.1 = 0.8 \text{ V}$
- $\omega_s = 157 \text{ rad/s}$

**(i) Synchronous speed:**
$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$

**(ii) Slip at maximum torque:**
$$s_m = \frac{R_2}{X_2} = \frac{0.02}{0.1} = 0.2 \quad (20\%)$$

**(iii) Maximum torque:**
$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^2}{2 X_2} = \frac{3}{157} \cdot \frac{(0.8)^2}{2 \times 0.1}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{max} &= \frac{3}{157} \cdot \frac{0.64}{0.2} \\
&= \frac{3}{157} \cdot 3.2 \\
&= 0.0611 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Starting torque (s = 1):**
$$T_{st} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{st} &= \frac{3}{157} \cdot \frac{0.64 \times 0.02}{(0.02)^2 + (0.1)^2} \\
&= \frac{3}{157} \cdot \frac{0.0128}{0.0104} \\
&= \frac{3}{157} \cdot 1.2308 \\
&= 0.0235 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — Internal Option]

**B.(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for electromagnetic torque of a 3-phase induction motor. Show that maximum torque is independent of rotor resistance.

**Model Answer:**

Refer to **Derivation 4 (Section 3.4)** for the complete step-by-step algebraic derivation. The final result:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}$$

To find the slip for maximum torque, differentiate $T_e$ w.r.t. s and set to zero. After algebraic manipulation (shown in **Section 3.5**):

$$s_m = \frac{R_2}{X_2}$$

Substituting $s_m$ back into $T_e$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{max} &= \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{(R_2/X_2) \cdot E_2^2 \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + (R_2/X_2)^2 \cdot X_2^2} \\
&= \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^2 R_2^2 / X_2}{R_2^2 + R_2^2} \\
&= \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^2}{2 X_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $R_2$ cancels out, **maximum torque is independent of rotor resistance**. This is a foundational result used in slip-ring motor speed control and soft-start designs.

---

**B.(b) [7 Marks]** A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz induction motor running at 960 rpm draws 40 A from a 400 V supply at a power factor of 0.85 lagging. Compute:
(i) Slip
(ii) Rotor copper loss (assuming stator Cu loss equals rotor Cu loss)
(iii) Developed mechanical power

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- P = 6, f = 50 Hz, V_L = 400 V
- N_r = 960 rpm
- I_L = 40 A, cos φ = 0.85

**(i) Slip:**
$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ rpm}$$
$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \frac{1000 - 960}{1000} = 0.04$$

**(ii) Input Power:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{in} &= \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi \\
&= \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 40 \times 0.85 \\
&= 23,556 \text{ W} \approx 23.56 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

Stator Cu loss (assumed equal to rotor Cu loss, so each = P_in/3? Actually, the standard KTU convention is given — let us interpret: total stator Cu loss equals rotor Cu loss → so $P_{cu1} = P_{cu2}$).

Total electrical losses = stator Cu + rotor Cu + core loss. Using the relation:
$$\frac{P_{cu2}}{P_g} = s$$

Air-gap power: $P_g = \dfrac{P_{cu2}}{s}$

Rotor Cu loss: 
$$P_{cu2} = s \cdot P_g = s \cdot \frac{P_m}{1-s} = s \cdot \frac{P_{cu2}}{s} \cdot \frac{1}{...}$$

*Simpler standard approach:*

If stator Cu loss = rotor Cu loss, and we assume core loss is negligible:
$$P_{in} = 2 P_{cu2} + P_m$$
$$P_m = (1-s) P_g$$
$$P_{cu2} = s P_g \implies P_g = \frac{P_{cu2}}{s}$$

Also, $P_{in} - P_{cu1} = P_g$, so $P_{in} - P_{cu2} = P_g = \dfrac{P_{cu2}}{s}$.

$$
P_{in} = P_{cu2} + \frac{P_{cu2}}{s} = P_{cu2} \cdot \frac{1+s}{s}
$$

$$
P_{cu2} = \frac{s \cdot P_{in}}{1+s} = \frac{0.04 \times 23{,}556}{1.04} = 906 \text{ W}
$$

**(iii) Developed Mechanical Power:**
$$
P_m = P_{in} - 2 P_{cu2} = 23{,}556 - 2 \times 906 = 21{,}744 \text{ W} \approx 21.74 \text{ kW}
$$

(Alternative check: $P_g = P_{cu2}/s = 906/0.04 = 22{,}650$ W; $P_m = (1-s) P_g = 0.96 \times 22{,}650 = 21{,}744$ W ✓)

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save for last-night KTU prep)**

- ✅ **Synchronous speed:** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ — memorize the 50 Hz pole-speed table: 2P→3000, 4P→1500, 6P→1000, 8P→750 rpm.
- ✅ **Slip:** $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$ — never negative in motoring; becomes negative in **generating** mode.
- ✅ **RMF speed = Synchronous speed** — produced by 3-phase spatially-shifted windings + temporally-shifted currents.
- ✅ **Rotor EMF at slip s:** $E_{2r} = s E_2$ — at standstill (s=1), full EMF appears.
- ✅ **Rotor frequency:** $f_r = s f$ — only 2–3 Hz at full load (this is why rotor laminations can be thinner than stator).
- ✅ **Torque equation:** $T_e = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}$ — **always** the factor of 3 for 3-phase.
- ✅ **Maximum torque slip:** $s_m = R_2 / X_2$ — denominator dominates in normal running.
- ✅ **$T_{max}$ independent of $R_2$** — this is the KEY insight for rotor-resistance speed control.
- ✅ **Starting torque depends on $R_2$** — high $R_2$ → high starting torque (used in wound-rotor / slip-ring motors).
- ✅ **Stable operating region:** $0 < s < s_m$ (low-slip side); **Unstable region:** $s_m < s < 1$.
- ✅ **Power flow stages:** $P_{in} \to P_{cu1} \to P_g \to P_{cu2} + P_m \to P_{out}$ (after rotational losses).
- ✅ **Rotor Cu loss ratio:** $\dfrac{P_{cu2}}{P_g} = s$ — extremely useful shortcut for KTU numericals.
- ✅ **Mechanical power ratio:** $\dfrac{P_m}{P_g} = (1-s)$.
- ✅ **Cannot self-start:** No — induction motors ARE self-starting. (This is a common *trick* in KTU MCQs!)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Rotating Magnetic Field
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT302 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES & DRIVES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concept of Rotating Magnetic Field

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
A **Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF)** is defined as a magnetic flux distribution whose **magnitude remains constant in time**, but whose **axis of symmetry continuously rotates in space** at a uniform angular velocity, when produced by a balanced polyphase (typically **three-phase**) AC supply fed into spatially displaced stator windings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The concept of the rotating magnetic field is the foundational pillar for understanding the operation of **all AC machines** — induction motors, synchronous motors, and synchronous generators. Without mastering this concept, the working principle of induction motors (covered immediately after in this module) cannot be understood.

### 1.2 Mathematical Boundary of the Concept
- **Number of poles (P):** Any even integer (2, 4, 6, 8 …).
- **Supply frequency (f):** The frequency of the AC supply in **Hertz (Hz)**.
- **Spatial displacement of coils:** Each phase winding is displaced by **120 electrical degrees** in space for a standard 3-phase, 2-pole machine.
- **Time displacement of currents:** Each phase current is displaced by **120 electrical degrees** in time.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)
Imagine a **giant bar magnet** placed inside a circular ring. If you switch ON this magnet, the magnetic field lines go from North to South. Now imagine that instead of a stationary magnet, the bar magnet is mounted on the shaft of an electric motor and **spins continuously at a constant speed**. The magnetic field pattern in the air gap now appears to "rotate" — its strength stays the same, but its orientation sweeps around the air gap.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Explanation:** When you pass three separate alternating currents (each separated in time by 1/3rd of a cycle) through three separate coils (each separated in space by 120°) arranged around a circular stator, the *combined effect* is a single magnetic field that behaves exactly like that spinning bar magnet. This is the rotating magnetic field that drags the rotor along in an induction motor.

### 1.4 Why is the Rotating Magnetic Field Critical?
The stator of an AC machine **cannot physically rotate** (it is stationary). Yet, we need a moving magnetic field to induce EMF in the rotor (in a generator) or to produce torque (in a motor). The genius of Nikola Tesla's polyphase AC system is that it **fakes rotation** of a magnetic field using *stationary coils and time-varying currents*. This is the single most important idea in AC machinery.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Construction of the Stator Winding (Conceptual Setup)
- The stator has **three identical windings**: Phase-A, Phase-B, and Phase-C.
- Each winding is a concentrated or distributed coil spanning **180 electrical degrees** (for the simplest 2-pole case).
- The three winding axes are physically placed **120 electrical degrees apart** in space around the inner circumference of the stator.

Let the magnetic axis of:
- Phase-A be along $\theta = 0°$ (reference)
- Phase-B be along $\theta = 120°$
- Phase-C be along $\theta = 240°$ (or equivalently $-120°$)

### 2.2 The Three-Phase Balanced Supply
A balanced 3-phase AC supply consists of three sinusoidal currents of equal RMS value and frequency, mutually displaced in time by $120°$ (i.e., $\frac{2\pi}{3}$ radians).

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of the Phenomenon

**Step 1 — Independent MMF Generation:** Each phase winding, when energized, produces a **sinusoidally distributed pulsating MMF** along its own magnetic axis. The MMF does not rotate — it simply *pulses* (rises and falls) at the supply frequency $f$.

**Step 2 — Spatial Displacement:** Because the three coil axes are at $0°$, $120°$, and $240°$, the three pulsating MMFs are **locked in space** at these three fixed angular positions.

**Step 3 — Time Displacement:** The currents flowing in the three coils are also displaced by $120°$ in *time*. Therefore, when one winding's MMF is at its positive maximum, the other two are at half-maximum (and of opposite sign to each other).

**Step 4 — Vector Superposition:** At any instant $t$, the resultant MMF in the air gap is the **phasor sum** of the three instantaneous MMFs. Because the spatial and temporal displacements are perfectly matched (both equal to $120°$), the resultant vector has a *constant magnitude* and an *angle that changes linearly with time*.

**Step 5 — Synchronous Rotation:** The rate at which the resultant MMF vector sweeps around the air gap is called the **synchronous speed** ($N_s$). The mechanical rotation matches the electrical angular frequency $\omega$ (for a 2-pole machine).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | RPM | Mechanical rotational speed of the magnetic field |
| 2 | Synchronous angular velocity (electrical) | $\omega_s = 2 \pi f$ | rad/s | Electrical angular frequency of supply |
| 3 | Synchronous angular velocity (mechanical) | $\omega_m = \dfrac{2 \pi f}{P/2} = \dfrac{4 \pi f}{P}$ | rad/s | Mechanical shaft equivalent of electrical speed |
| 4 | Resultant MMF magnitude | $F_{max} = \dfrac{3}{2} F_{ph,max}$ | Ampere-turns (AT) | Peak value of the rotating MMF wave |
| 5 | Phase-A current | $i_a = I_m \cos(\omega t)$ | A | Instantaneous current in Phase-A |
| 6 | Phase-B current | $i_b = I_m \cos\!\left(\omega t - \dfrac{2\pi}{3}\right)$ | A | Instantaneous current in Phase-B |
| 7 | Phase-C current | $i_c = I_m \cos\!\left(\omega t - \dfrac{4\pi}{3}\right)$ | A | Instantaneous current in Phase-C |
| 8 | Space angle convention | $\theta$ measured from Phase-A axis | rad / electrical deg | Spatial position in air gap |
| 9 | Resultant MMF (compact form) | $F(\theta,t) = \dfrac{3}{2} F_{ph,max} \cos(\theta - \omega t)$ | AT | Proves rotating field |
| 10 | Slip (foundation for next topics) | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | dimensionless (or %) | Used in induction motor analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notational Warning for KTU Valuation:** Examiners *will* penalize students who confuse **electrical degrees** with **mechanical degrees**. For a $P$-pole machine, $\theta_{mechanical} = \dfrac{2}{P} \theta_{electrical}$. Always clarify the units in your answer.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Induction Motors:** The stator produces an RMF; this rotating flux cuts the stationary rotor conductors, inducing EMF and current (Faraday's law). The interaction of this induced rotor current with the stator's RMF produces torque (Lorentz force).
- **Synchronous Machines:** The rotor's DC-excited field *locks onto* the stator's RMF and rotates at exactly $N_s$.
- **AC Generators (Alternators):** The rotor's DC field, when driven by a prime mover, produces an RMF in the stator that links with stator coils and induces 3-phase EMF.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Resultant Rotating MMF from a Balanced 3-Phase Supply

#### Step 1 — Define the Three-Phase Currents
Let the three-phase balanced currents be expressed as instantaneous time functions:

$$
i_a = I_m \cos(\omega t)
$$

$$
i_b = I_m \cos\!\left(\omega t - \frac{2\pi}{3}\right)
$$

$$
i_c = I_m \cos\!\left(\omega t - \frac{4\pi}{3}\right)
$$

where:
- $I_m$ is the peak (maximum) value of the phase current,
- $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency of the supply in rad/s,
- $t$ is the time in seconds.

*Logic:* A balanced 3-phase system is defined by three sinusoids of identical magnitude and frequency, mutually separated by $\frac{2\pi}{3}$ radians in time. We adopt the standard **Phase-A reference** convention.

#### Step 2 — Express the Space-Dependent MMF of Each Phase
Each stator winding, when carrying a current $i$, produces a **sinusoidally distributed MMF** along its magnetic axis. Let $F_{ph,max}$ be the peak MMF produced by a single winding when its current is at its maximum value $I_m$.

The space-distributed MMF contributed by each phase (using $\cos\theta$ distribution and assuming identical windings) is:

$$
F_a(\theta, t) = F_{ph,max} \cos(\theta) \cdot \cos(\omega t)
$$

$$
F_b(\theta, t) = F_{ph,max} \cos\!\left(\theta - \frac{2\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \cos\!\left(\omega t - \frac{2\pi}{3}\right)
$$

$$
F_c(\theta, t) = F_{ph,max} \cos\!\left(\theta - \frac{4\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \cos\!\left(\omega t - \frac{4\pi}{3}\right)
$$

*Logic:* The factor $\cos(\theta - \alpha)$ represents the *spatial distribution* (centered on the axis of that phase coil which is at angular position $\alpha$). The factor $\cos(\omega t - \alpha)$ represents the *temporal pulsation* of the current in that coil. Both have the same displacement $\alpha = 0, \frac{2\pi}{3}, \frac{4\pi}{3}$.

#### Step 3 — Apply the Trigonometric Product-to-Sum Identity
Recall the identity:

$$
\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}\left[\cos(A - B) + \cos(A + B)\right]
$$

Apply this to each phase:

$$
F_a = \frac{F_{ph,max}}{2}\left[\cos(\theta - \omega t) + \cos(\theta + \omega t)\right]
$$

$$
F_b = \frac{F_{ph,max}}{2}\left[\cos(\theta - \omega t) + \cos\!\left(\theta + \omega t - \frac{4\pi}{3}\right)\right]
$$

$$
F_c = \frac{F_{ph,max}}{2}\left[\cos(\theta - \omega t) + \cos\!\left(\theta + \omega t - \frac{8\pi}{3}\right)\right]
$$

*Logic:* The first term in each bracket is identical across all three phases — this is the **forward-traveling (rotating) component**. The second terms differ in their phase angle — these are the **stationary / pulsating components**.

#### Step 4 — Sum the Three MMFs
The resultant air-gap MMF is:

$$
F(\theta, t) = F_a + F_b + F_c
$$

Substituting:

$$
F(\theta, t) = \frac{F_{ph,max}}{2}\Big[3\cos(\theta - \omega t) + \cos(\theta + \omega t) + \cos\!\left(\theta + \omega t - \frac{4\pi}{3}\right) + \cos\!\left(\theta + \omega t - \frac{8\pi}{3}\right)\Big]
$$

#### Step 5 — Evaluate the Sum of the Stationary Components
Let $X = \theta + \omega t$. The stationary term group is:

$$
\cos(X) + \cos\!\left(X - \frac{4\pi}{3}\right) + \cos\!\left(X - \frac{8\pi}{3}\right)
$$

Using the identity $\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\cos(\phi + k \cdot \frac{2\pi}{n}) = 0$ for $n \geq 2$, with $n = 3$, the sum equals **zero**. (This is the *Phasor Sum of Three Balanced Phasors = 0* theorem.)

Therefore:

$$
\cos(\theta + \omega t) + \cos\!\left(\theta + \omega t - \frac{4\pi}{3}\right) + \cos\!\left(\theta + \omega t - \frac{8\pi}{3}\right) = 0
$$

*Logic:* Three phasors of equal magnitude separated by $\frac{2\pi}{3}$ in angle sum to zero — the same principle behind why the neutral current of a balanced 3-phase system is zero.

#### Step 6 — Obtain the Final Result
After cancellation, only the rotating components survive:

$$
F(\theta, t) = \frac{3 F_{ph,max}}{2}\cos(\theta - \omega t)
$$

*Logic:* This is the **pinnacle result**. It shows that:
- The **magnitude** of the MMF is constant and equal to $\frac{3}{2} F_{ph,max}$ (1.5 times the peak MMF of one phase).
- The **argument** $(\theta - \omega t)$ represents a cosine wave whose peak position advances linearly with time. The peak occurs when $\theta = \omega t$, meaning the peak of the MMF wave moves to angular position $\theta = \omega t$ at time $t$.

#### Step 7 — Identify the Speed and Direction of Rotation
- **Angular velocity:** $\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega = 2\pi f$ rad/s (electrical).
- **Mechanical speed:** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ RPM.
- **Direction:** If the phase sequence is $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C$ (positive sequence), the field rotates from the Phase-A axis towards the Phase-B axis (i.e., from $0°$ towards $120°$) in space — this is the **counter-clockwise direction** by convention.
- **Reversal:** Interchanging any two of the three supply leads reverses the phase sequence (to $A \rightarrow C \rightarrow B$), which reverses the direction of rotation. This is the principle behind the **direction reversal** of a 3-phase induction motor.

### 3.2 Verification by Direct Vector Method (Quick Check)

At any specific instant, we can compute the resultant MMF vector to confirm constant magnitude.

**At $\omega t = 0$:** Only Phase-A current is at its positive peak. The MMF vector points along $\theta = 0°$ with magnitude $F_{ph,max}$.

$$
\vec{F}(0) = F_{ph,max} \angle 0°
$$

**At $\omega t = 120°$:** Only Phase-B current is at its peak. The MMF vector now points along $\theta = 120°$.

$$
\vec{F}\!\left(\frac{2\pi}{3}\right) = F_{ph,max} \angle 120°
$$

**At $\omega t = 240°$:** Only Phase-C current is at its peak.

$$
\vec{F}\!\left(\frac{4\pi}{3}\right) = F_{ph,max} \angle 240°
$$

*Logic:* In $\frac{1}{3}$ of a cycle, the MMF vector has rotated by $120°$ in space. Its magnitude is **constant** ($F_{ph,max}$ at each instant, while the analytic formula gives the rotating wave amplitude as $\frac{3}{2}F_{ph,max}$ due to superposition — both representations are consistent because the analytic form captures the *full distributed wave* including all space harmonics).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** KTU answers often confuse $F_{ph,max}$ (one-phase peak MMF) with $\frac{3}{2} F_{ph,max}$ (resultant peak MMF). Write both clearly to earn full marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of RMF Production

The following diagram maps the logical flow from a 3-phase AC source to the generation of a rotating magnetic field inside the air gap of an AC machine.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC[THREE PHASE AC SOURCE]
        A1[Phase A Supply]
        B1[Phase B Supply]
        C1[Phase C Supply]
    end

    subgraph WND[STATOR WINDING ASSEMBLY]
        WA[Phase A Winding\nAxis at 0 degrees]
        WB[Phase B Winding\nAxis at 120 degrees]
        WC[Phase C Winding\nAxis at 240 degrees]
    end

    subgraph PROD[MMF GENERATION IN EACH COIL]
        MA[Pulsating MMF from Phase A]
        MB[Pulsating MMF from Phase B]
        MC[Pulsating MMF from Phase C]
    end

    SUP[Vector Superposition\nPrinciple of Balanced Phasors]
    RES[Resultant Rotating MMF\nMagnitude = 1.5 times Fph max\nSpeed = Ns = 120f by P RPM]
    AIRG[Air Gap Flux Distribution\nConstant Magnitude\nRotating Axis]
    ROT[Rotor Interaction\nTorque Production]

    A1 --> WA
    B1 --> WB
    C1 --> WC
    WA --> MA
    WB --> MB
    WC --> MC
    MA --> SUP
    MB --> SUP
    MC --> SUP
    SUP --> RES
    RES --> AIRG
    AIRG --> ROT
```

### 4.2 Schematic Representation of Flux Axes at a Frozen Instant

The following diagram shows the spatial orientation of each phase's instantaneous MMF vector at $\omega t = 0$, illustrating how the resultant is obtained by vector addition.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph POLAR[INSTANTANEOUS PHASOR DIAGRAM AT omega t = 0]
        V1[F_a = Fph max AT 0 degrees]
        V2[F_b = -0.5 Fph max AT 120 degrees]
        V3[F_c = -0.5 Fph max AT 240 degrees]
        VR[Resultant F = 1.5 Fph max AT 0 degrees]
    end

    V1 --> SUM[Vector Sum]
    V2 --> SUM
    V3 --> SUM
    SUM --> VR
```

### 4.3 Logical Flow of Direction-Reversal Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SEQ1[Phase Sequence A B C\nPositive Sequence] --> ROT1[Field Rotates\nAnti Clockwise]
    SEQ2[Phase Sequence A C B\nNegative Sequence] --> ROT2[Field Rotates\nClockwise]

    ACT1[Swap Any Two Supply Leads] --> SEQ2

    ROT1 --> NOTE[Both Sequences Produce\nConstant Magnitude RMF\nOnly Direction Changes]
    ROT2 --> NOTE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid for KTU Students:** The block diagrams above map the *causal chain* of RMF generation. Memorize this chain: *3-phase source → 3 displaced windings → 3 pulsating MMFs → vector superposition → constant-magnitude rotating MMF → rotor interaction → torque*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define rotating magnetic field. State the two essential conditions required to produce a rotating magnetic field.**

**Model Answer:**
A **rotating magnetic field** is a magnetic flux distribution whose magnitude is constant in time but whose axis rotates continuously in space at synchronous speed.

The two essential conditions are:

1. **Spatial displacement:** The windings must be physically displaced in space by $\frac{360°}{m}$ electrical degrees, where $m$ is the number of phases (so $120°$ for 3-phase).
2. **Temporal displacement:** The currents flowing through the windings must be time-displaced by $\frac{360°}{m}$ electrical degrees (i.e., $\frac{T}{3}$ seconds for a 3-phase supply of period $T$).

Both conditions together — *equal magnitudes, $120°$ spatial shift, $120°$ temporal shift* — yield a constant-magnitude, uniformly rotating magnetic field.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sessional II]**
**What is synchronous speed? Write its formula and compute the synchronous speed of a 4-pole machine operating at 50 Hz supply.**

**Model Answer:**
**Synchronous speed ($N_s$)** is the speed at which the rotating magnetic field produced by the stator of an AC machine rotates. It is the speed at which a *synchronous motor's rotor* must rotate in the steady state.

The formula is:

$$
N_s = \frac{120 f}{P}
$$

**Given:** $f = 50$ Hz, $P = 4$

$$
N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = \frac{6000}{4} = 1500 \text{ RPM}
$$

Therefore, the synchronous speed is **1500 RPM**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation:** In the End Semester Examination (ESE), Module 2 carries questions of 14 marks each with **internal choice** — students answer either Choice A or Choice B. Below, two full choices are provided with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of a rotating magnetic field. Show analytically that a balanced 3-phase supply fed to a 3-phase winding produces a constant-magnitude rotating MMF. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 6-pole induction motor is supplied from a 50 Hz, 440 V balanced 3-phase AC source. Determine the synchronous speed, and comment on what happens to the rotating magnetic field if the supply frequency is changed to 60 Hz. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a)

We must prove that $F(\theta, t) = \frac{3}{2} F_{ph,max} \cos(\theta - \omega t)$.

The full derivation is presented in **Section 3.1** above. Students must reproduce the following key steps:

1. Write the three-phase currents: $i_a = I_m \cos(\omega t)$, $i_b = I_m \cos(\omega t - \frac{2\pi}{3})$, $i_c = I_m \cos(\omega t - \frac{4\pi}{3})$.

2. Write the three phase MMF distributions:
   - $F_a = F_{ph,max}\cos\theta \cdot \cos\omega t$
   - $F_b = F_{ph,max}\cos(\theta - \frac{2\pi}{3}) \cdot \cos(\omega t - \frac{2\pi}{3})$
   - $F_c = F_{ph,max}\cos(\theta - \frac{4\pi}{3}) \cdot \cos(\omega t - \frac{4\pi}{3})$

3. Apply product-to-sum identity:

$$
\cos A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]
$$

4. Sum and use the identity $\cos X + \cos(X - \frac{4\pi}{3}) + \cos(X - \frac{8\pi}{3}) = 0$.

5. Conclude:

$$
F(\theta, t) = \frac{3 F_{ph,max}}{2} \cos(\theta - \omega t)
$$

The **physical interpretation** of this result must be stated explicitly:
- The magnitude $\frac{3}{2} F_{ph,max}$ is **constant** in time.
- The argument $(\theta - \omega t)$ indicates the field pattern **rotates** in space at angular velocity $\omega = 2\pi f$.

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b)

**Given Data:**
- Number of poles, $P = 6$
- Supply frequency, $f = 50$ Hz
- Line voltage, $V_L = 440$ V (informational only)

**Step 1:** Compute synchronous speed at 50 Hz:

$$
N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = \frac{6000}{6} = 1000 \text{ RPM}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the corresponding electrical angular velocity:

$$
\omega_s = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 50 = 314.159 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the mechanical angular velocity:

$$
\omega_m = \frac{2\omega_s}{P} = \frac{2 \times 314.159}{6} = 104.72 \text{ rad/s}
$$

(Note: This equals $\frac{2\pi N_s}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.72$ rad/s. ✓ Consistent.)

**Step 4:** If the supply frequency is changed to 60 Hz (assume same pole count $P = 6$):

$$
N_s^{new} = \frac{120 \times 60}{6} = \frac{7200}{6} = 1200 \text{ RPM}
$$

**Step 5:** Comment — The synchronous speed increases **proportionally** with frequency. The new synchronous speed is 1200 RPM. The magnitude of the rotating MMF (assuming constant $V/f$ ratio) would remain roughly the same; otherwise, at constant voltage, the flux would decrease with increasing frequency, weakening the torque capability of the motor. This is why **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)** always maintain a constant **$V/f$ ratio**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper, Module 2 Alternative]**

**(a)** State and explain the two essential conditions for the production of a rotating magnetic field. With neat phasor diagrams at three different instants, illustrate how the resultant MMF vector maintains constant magnitude while changing direction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2-pole, 3-phase AC machine is supplied from a balanced 50 Hz source. Determine:
  (i) The synchronous speed in RPM.
  (ii) The synchronous speed in mechanical rad/s.
  (iii) The mechanical speed if the machine is rewound for 8 poles and supplied from the same source. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a)

**Two Essential Conditions:**

1. **Spatial Displacement:** The three-phase windings must be physically placed $\frac{360°}{\text{number of phases}} = \frac{360°}{3} = 120°$ apart in space around the stator periphery.

2. **Temporal Displacement:** The three-phase AC currents must be displaced in time by $\frac{1}{3}$ of one time period ($T/3$ seconds), which is equivalent to $\frac{360°}{3} = 120°$ electrical in time.

**Phasor Diagrams at Three Instants:**

Using the **direct vector method** (described in Section 3.2), at three different time instants the MMF vectors are:

**At $\omega t = 0°$:**
- $\vec{F_a} = F_{ph,max} \angle 0°$ (positive peak)
- $\vec{F_b} = F_{ph,max} \angle 120° \cdot \cos(-120°) = F_{ph,max} \angle 120° \cdot (-0.5)$
- $\vec{F_c} = F_{ph,max} \angle 240° \cdot \cos(-240°) = F_{ph,max} \angle 240° \cdot (-0.5)$

Resultant: $\vec{F} = 1.5\, F_{ph,max} \angle 0°$

**At $\omega t = 120°$:**
- $\vec{F_a} = F_{ph,max} \angle 0° \cdot \cos(120°) = -0.5\, F_{ph,max} \angle 0°$
- $\vec{F_b} = F_{ph,max} \angle 120°$ (positive peak)
- $\vec{F_c} = F_{ph,max} \angle 240° \cdot \cos(120°) = -0.5\, F_{ph,max} \angle 240°$

Resultant: $\vec{F} = 1.5\, F_{ph,max} \angle 120°$

**At $\omega t = 240°$:**
- $\vec{F_a} = F_{ph,max} \angle 0° \cdot \cos(240°) = -0.5\, F_{ph,max} \angle 0°$
- $\vec{F_b} = F_{ph,max} \angle 120° \cdot \cos(120°) = -0.5\, F_{ph,max} \angle 120°$
- $\vec{F_c} = F_{ph,max} \angle 240°$ (positive peak)

Resultant: $\vec{F} = 1.5\, F_{ph,max} \angle 240°$

**Conclusion:** In one-third of a cycle, the resultant MMF vector rotates by $120°$ in space, and its magnitude remains constant at $1.5\, F_{ph,max}$. This visually confirms the rotating magnetic field concept.

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b)

**Given:** $P = 2$, $f = 50$ Hz.

**(i) Synchronous speed in RPM:**

$$
N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{2} = 3000 \text{ RPM}
$$

**(ii) Synchronous speed in mechanical rad/s:**

$$
\omega_m = \frac{2\pi N_s}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 3000}{60} = 100\pi \approx 314.159 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Alternatively**, using $\omega_m = \frac{4\pi f}{P}$:

$$
\omega_m = \frac{4\pi \times 50}{2} = 100\pi \approx 314.159 \text{ rad/s} \quad \checkmark
$$

**(iii) Rewound for $P = 8$, same $f = 50$ Hz:**

$$
N_s^{new} = \frac{120 \times 50}{8} = \frac{6000}{8} = 750 \text{ RPM}
$$

The mechanical synchronous speed drops to 750 RPM, which is exactly $\frac{1}{4}$ of the original 3000 RPM (consistent with the inverse proportionality $N_s \propto \frac{1}{P}$).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks (Compiled from Examiner Reports):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of $\frac{3}{2}$:** Students often write the resultant MMF as simply $F_{ph,max}\cos(\theta - \omega t)$. The correct expression is $\frac{3}{2}F_{ph,max}\cos(\theta - \omega t)$. Missing this factor loses **2 marks** in derivation-type questions.
>
> 2. **Confusing electrical and mechanical degrees:** For a $P$-pole machine, one full mechanical revolution corresponds to $\frac{P}{2}$ electrical revolutions. The synchronous speed formula $N_s = \frac{120 f}{P}$ gives **mechanical RPM**, not electrical RPM.
>
> 3. **Writing phasor additions without showing the cancellation:** The stationary terms (the $\cos(A+B)$ components) sum to zero only because they are balanced. Examiners explicitly look for the **statement of the identity** $\sum \cos(X - \frac{2k\pi}{3}) = 0$.
>
> 4. **Direction of rotation errors:** Saying "the field rotates clockwise" without specifying the assumed reference direction (and the phase sequence) is incomplete. Always state: *"For phase sequence A-B-C, the field rotates from the Phase-A axis toward the Phase-B axis."*
>
> 5. **Unit inconsistency:** Writing $\omega_s = 2\pi f$ without stating units (rad/s) is a **0.5 mark deduction** per KTU valuation norms.
>
> 6. **Confusing Phase-A axis reference:** Always define the spatial reference ($\theta = 0$ at Phase-A axis) explicitly. Examiners deduct marks for ambiguity.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read Before Entering the Exam Hall:**
>
> - **Definition:** A rotating magnetic field has *constant magnitude* and a *rotating axis* in space.
> - **Origin:** Produced by a *balanced polyphase* supply feeding *spatially displaced* windings.
> - **Two Essential Conditions:** (1) Spatial $120°$ displacement between coils, (2) Temporal $120°$ time-phase shift between currents.
> - **Synchronous Speed Formula:** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ RPM. **Memorize this.**
> - **Resultant MMF:** $F(\theta, t) = \dfrac{3}{2} F_{ph,max} \cos(\theta - \omega t)$. **The factor $\frac{3}{2}$ is mandatory.**
> - **Direction of Rotation:** Determined by the phase sequence (A-B-C vs A-C-B). Swap any two leads to reverse.
> - **Speed of the Field in rad/s:** $\omega_s = 2\pi f$ (electrical), $\omega_m = \dfrac{4\pi f}{P}$ (mechanical).
> - **Three Balanced Phasors Sum to Zero:** Used in the cancellation step of the derivation — examiners *will* test this identity.
> - **Why is flux held constant?** The $\frac{3}{2}$ factor is *constant in time* (depends only on $F_{ph,max}$, which is fixed by the construction). The argument $(\theta - \omega t)$ is what makes it rotate.
> - **Application in Induction Motors:** RMF cuts rotor bars → induces current → produces torque via Lorentz force. (Detailed in the next topic of Module 2.)
> - **Application in Synchronous Machines:** Rotor DC field locks onto stator RMF at $N_s$.
> - **Foundation for Slip:** Slip $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ appears in *every* induction motor problem from this point onward.
> - **Sign Convention Tip:** Always adopt $\omega t = 0$ aligned with Phase-A current positive peak for cleanest derivations.
> - **Diagrams to Practice:** Draw the three MMF phasors at $\omega t = 0°$, $120°$, $240°$ and show vector addition by hand — examiners award 1–2 marks for correct phasor diagrams in 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Slip
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT302 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES & DRIVES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Slip

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the context of **three-phase induction motors**, **Slip (s)** is formally defined as the **relative speed** between the **rotating magnetic field** produced by the stator (synchronous speed, $N_s$) and the **actual mechanical speed** of the rotor ($N_r$), expressed as a **per-unit (decimal) fraction** or **percentage** of the synchronous speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim style):**
