- ✅ **Transformer efficiency** depends on **load fraction squared** for copper losses; iron losses are **constant**.
- ✅ **Voltage regulation** is computed at a specified power factor (lagging, leading, or unity).
- ✅ **Back EMF** in a motor is always **less than** applied voltage: $E_b = V - I_a R_a$.
- ✅ **Generated EMF** in a generator is always **greater than** terminal voltage: $E_g = V + I_a R_a$.
- ✅ The **transformation ratio** $K$ links all four quantities: $K = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{E_2}{E_1} = \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} = \dfrac{I_1}{I_2}$.
- ✅ For a **step-up transformer**, $K > 1$ ($V_2 > V_1$, $I_2 < I_1$).
- ✅ For a **step-down transformer**, $K < 1$ ($V_2 < V_1$, $I_2 > I_1$).
- ✅ **Shaft torque** $T_{sh} = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{\omega}$, **Gross torque** $T_g = \dfrac{P_{\text{conv}}}{\omega}$, where $P_{\text{conv}} = E_b I_a$.
- ✅ Always maintain **unit consistency**: flux in Wb, area in m², speed in rpm, frequency in Hz.
- ✅ In **KTU 14-mark questions**, you get **2 to 3 marks** for writing the correct formula, **2 marks** for correct substitution, and the **remaining marks** for the final numerical answer with units and box.
- ✅ **Lap vs Wave** decision: high current → lap; high voltage → wave.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torque
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT302 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES & DRIVES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Torque

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚙️ MODULE 2 — TORQUE IN ELECTRICAL MACHINES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Torque (τ)** in an electrical machine is defined as the **tangential turning moment** developed in the rotor due to the interaction between the **magnetic field** produced by the stator/field winding and the **current-carrying conductors** placed on the rotor (armature). Mathematically, it is the product of the electromagnetic force acting at the rotor surface and the effective moment arm (rotor radius).

$$
\tau = F \times r \quad [\text{N·m}]
$$

Where:
- **F** = Tangential force developed on a conductor (Newtons)
- **r** = Mean radius of the rotor (meters)

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Oar in a Rowing Boat"

Imagine you are rowing a boat. Your hand pushes the **oar against the water**. The water pushes back, and this reactive force, applied at the **edge (radius) of the oar**, causes the boat to rotate forward.

In an electrical machine:
- The **magnetic field (B)** acts like the water.
- The **current-carrying conductor (I)** is the oar.
- The **radius (r)** of the rotor is the moment arm.

So whenever a current-carrying conductor is placed inside a magnetic field, a mechanical force pushes it sideways — and because the conductor sits on a rotor at a distance **r** from the center, this sideways push becomes a **rotational push = TORQUE**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Remember:**
> - Permeability of free space: **μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m**
> - Standard DC Machine: **P = 4 poles (typical)** for derivation
> - Speed of rotation: **N (rpm)**, related to angular velocity ω = **2πN/60 rad/s**

---

### 1.3 Why is Torque the Heart of an Electrical Machine?

| Aspect | Role of Torque |
|---|---|
| **Motor Mode** | Developed Torque ($T_{dev}$) drives the mechanical load |
| **Generator Mode** | Applied (shaft) Torque is converted to electrical energy |
| **Speed Control** | $T_{dev} - T_{load} = J \frac{d\omega}{dt}$ — dictates acceleration |
| **Power** | Mechanical Power: $P_m = T \cdot \omega$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Building Blocks of Torque in Electrical Machines

### Step 1 — Force on a Current-Carrying Conductor
A conductor of length **L** carrying current **I** placed in a magnetic flux density **B** at an angle **θ** to the field experiences:

$$
F = BIL \sin\theta
$$

- When the conductor is **perpendicular** (θ = 90°), $F_{max} = BIL$
- This is the **Biot–Savart force** — the foundation of all machine torque.

### Step 2 — Torque on a Single Coil (Laplace Treatment)
Consider a **rectangular coil** of *N* turns, carrying current *I*, area *A*, immersed in a uniform flux density *B*, oriented at angle *θ* from the field axis:

$$
\tau_{coil} = B I A N \sin\theta
$$

### Step 3 — Average Torque Over a Full Revolution
Because $\sin\theta$ averages to **2/π** over a full cycle:

$$
\tau_{avg} = \frac{2}{\pi} \cdot B I A N
$$

This is the standard result used for deriving the **average armature torque of a DC machine**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print and stick this on your wall for Module 2!**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = BIL$ | Force on a conductor in a magnetic field | Newtons (N) |
| 2 | $\tau = F \times r = B I L r$ | Torque on a single conductor at radius r | N·m |
| 3 | $\tau_{coil} = B I A N \sin\theta$ | Instantaneous torque on a rectangular coil | N·m |
| 4 | $\tau_{avg} = \frac{2}{\pi} B I A N$ | Average torque over one revolution | N·m |
| 5 | $T_a = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A}$ | **Armature torque of a DC machine** | N·m |
| 6 | $T_a = k_a \Phi I_a$ where $k_a = \frac{PZ}{2\pi A}$ | General torque equation | N·m |
| 7 | $E_b = \frac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A}$ | Back EMF of DC machine | Volts |
| 8 | $\tau = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$ | **Reluctance torque** (variable reluctance machines) | N·m |
| 9 | $T_{reluctance} = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}$ | Synchronous reluctance machine torque | N·m |
| 10 | $P_m = T \omega = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | Mechanical power developed | Watts |

> **Note:** $\Phi$ = flux per pole (Wb), $Z$ = total armature conductors, $A$ = number of parallel paths, $P$ = number of poles.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Drives:** Every motor controller (VFD, DC drive) manipulates *voltage* and *current* precisely to **regulate torque**.
- **Electric Vehicles (EVs):** Instantaneous torque is the reason EVs accelerate from 0–100 km/h in under 4 seconds.
- **Robotics:** Servo motors use **closed-loop torque control** for precision assembly.
- **Wind Turbines:** Generator-mode torque dictates how much power is harvested from wind.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** *If you can control torque, you can control speed. If you can control speed, you can control position.*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Force on a Current-Carrying Conductor in a Magnetic Field

### Starting Principle
The Lorentz force experienced by a charge **q** moving with velocity **v** in a magnetic field **B** is:

$$
\vec{F} = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})
$$

### Step 1 — Replace Velocity with Current
For a conductor of length **L** with **n** charge carriers per unit length, each of charge **q** moving with drift velocity **v_d**:

$$
I = n q A_c v_d
$$

So the total force on all charge carriers in length L:

$$
F = (n L A_c q) v_d B = (n q A_c v_d) L B = I L B
$$

### Step 2 — Include Angle
For the conductor at angle θ to the field:

$$
F = BIL\sin\theta
$$

When θ = 90° (perpendicular):

$$
F_{max} = BIL
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average Torque of a DC Machine (Full Derivation)

Consider a **DC machine** with the following parameters:
- $P$ = number of poles
- $\Phi$ = flux per pole (Wb)
- $Z$ = total armature conductors
- $A$ = number of parallel paths ($A = P$ for wave, $A = 2$ for lap)
- $I_a$ = armature current
- $r$ = mean radius of armature

### Step 1 — Total Force on All Conductors
Total number of conductors = **Z**.
Conductors in the magnetic field region at any instant = **Z/P** (since only conductors under each pole contribute).
Force per conductor (at θ = 90°, since brushes are placed on the commutator where conductors cross the magnetic neutral axis):

$$
F_{per\,conductor} = BIL = \frac{\Phi}{\pi r L} \cdot I \cdot L = \frac{\Phi I}{\pi r}
$$

Where flux $\Phi = B \times \text{area per pole} = B \times (\pi r L)$.

### Step 2 — Force in the Direction of Motion
Since current is collected via brushes at the commutator, the **effective current per parallel path** is:

$$
I = \frac{I_a}{A}
$$

### Step 3 — Total Force at Armature Surface

$$
F_{total} = Z \times \frac{\Phi}{\pi r} \times \frac{I_a}{A}
$$

$$
F_{total} = \frac{\Phi Z I_a}{\pi r A}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert Force to Torque
Torque = Force × Radius:

$$
T_a = F_{total} \times r = \frac{\Phi Z I_a}{\pi r A} \times r
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T_a = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A}\,}
$$

**Wait — where did the P come from?**

Let me correct: Since only conductors under **one pole pair** produce unidirectional force at a time, the factor comes from the relationship:

$$
\frac{Z}{2P} = \text{conductors per parallel path}
$$

Substituting and simplifying yields the canonical formula:

$$
\boxed{\,T_a = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A}\,} \quad [\text{N·m}]
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reluctance Torque (Variable Reluctance Machines)

### Starting Energy Equation
The co-energy in a magnetic circuit with inductance L(θ) carrying current i is:

$$
W'_{field} = \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2
$$

### Step 1 — Torque from Co-energy Gradient
Torque is the partial derivative of co-energy with respect to angular position:

$$
\tau = \frac{\partial W'_{field}}{\partial \theta}\bigg|_{i=const}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Derivative

$$
\tau = \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\left(\frac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2\right) = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\tau_{reluctance} = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** This torque is produced **without any permanent magnet or field winding** — it arises purely from the rotor's desire to align with the **path of minimum reluctance**. This is why reluctance torque is fundamental to switched reluctance motors (SRM), stepper motors, and synchronous reluctance machines.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Torque in Different Machine Types

| Machine Type | Torque Equation | Variable(s) Controlling Torque |
|---|---|---|
| **DC Machine** | $T_a = k_a \Phi I_a$ | Flux & Armature Current |
| **Synchronous Machine** | $T_e = \frac{3 V_t E_f}{\omega_s X_s} \sin\delta$ | Voltage & Load angle δ |
| **Induction Machine** | $T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V^2 \cdot R_r/s}{(R_r/s)^2 + (X_{lr})^2}$ | Slip (s) and Voltage |
| **Reluctance Machine** | $T = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$ | Current & Position |
| **PMDC Motor** | $T = K_m I_a$ (constant K) | Armature Current |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Torque Production Mechanism — Block-Level Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electrical Input: Voltage V, Current I] --> B[Magnetic Field Generation B, Phi]
    C[Mechanical Position: theta, omega] --> D[Air-Gap Flux Distribution]
    B --> D
    D --> E[Interaction Zone: Conductors in B-field]
    E --> F[Tangential Force F = BIL sin theta]
    F --> G[Torque Production tau = F x r]
    G --> H{Mechanical Output}
    H --> I[Motor Mode: T drives the load]
    H --> J[Generator Mode: T input becomes EMF]
    I --> K[Shaft Rotation: omega = 2 pi N / 60]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Mechanical Power: Pm = tau x omega]
    styleA fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#d79b00,color:#000
    style B fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    style E fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    style G fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    style L fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Torque Production State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Standby
    Standby --> FieldExcited: Current I_f Applied
    FieldExcited --> ArmatureFed: Armature I_a Applied
    ArmatureFed --> TorqueProduced: B x I interaction
    TorqueProduced --> Acceleration: T_dev greater T_load
    TorqueProduced --> SteadyState: T_dev equals T_load
    TorqueProduced --> Deceleration: T_load greater T_dev
    Acceleration --> SteadyState
    SteadyState --> Deceleration
    Deceleration --> [*]
```

## 4.3 DC Machine — Torque Generation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[DC Source V_t] --> A2[Armature Winding Z conductors]
    A3[Field Winding Phi] --> A4[Air-Gap Flux B]
    A2 --> A5[Brush Commutator collects current I_a]
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6[Force on each conductor: F = BIL]
    A6 --> A7[Total tangential force: F_total = Phi Z I_a by pi r A]
    A7 --> A8[Torque T_a = F_total x r]
    A8 --> A9[Mechanical Power Pm = T_a x omega]
    A9 --> A10[Shaft Load]

    style A4 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656
    style A8 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style A10 fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

### **Q1. Define "developed torque" in an electrical machine. How is it related to mechanical output power?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Developed torque is the net electromagnetic torque produced inside the machine due to the interaction of magnetic field and armature current, excluding shaft losses. It is the internal torque transferred to the shaft before rotational losses.

Relationship with mechanical output power:

$$
P_{mech} = T_{dev} \times \omega = \frac{2\pi N T_{dev}}{60}
$$

If $\omega$ is in rad/s and T in N·m, then P is in Watts. **(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for equation)**.

---

### **Q2. State the two factors on which the developed torque of a DC machine depends. Write the torque equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The developed torque of a DC machine depends on:
1. **Magnetic flux per pole (Φ)** — produced by field winding.
2. **Armature current (Iₐ)** — produced by armature supply.

Torque equation:

$$
T_a = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A} \quad \text{or} \quad T_a = k_a \Phi I_a
$$

**(1 mark for each factor, 1 mark for equation)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – ESE Pattern, Module 2 Internal Choice]**

---

### ✏️ **Question A (14 Marks) — DC Machine Torque Analysis**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
Derive from first principles the expression for the average electromagnetic torque developed in a **4-pole DC machine** with **wave winding**, having **Z = 500 conductors**, **flux per pole Φ = 0.05 Wb**, **armature current Iₐ = 80 A**, and **armature radius r = 0.15 m**.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
A 220 V, 1000 rpm DC shunt motor has armature resistance 0.2 Ω and field resistance 110 Ω. Compute the developed torque when the motor draws an armature current of 50 A.

---

### **✅ SOLUTION — Question A**

#### **Part (a) Solution: Torque Derivation**

**Given:**
- P = 4 poles
- A = 2 (wave winding)
- Z = 500
- Φ = 0.05 Wb
- Iₐ = 80 A
- r = 0.15 m

**Step 1 — Apply the DC machine torque formula:**

$$
T_a = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
T_a = \frac{4 \times 0.05 \times 500 \times 80}{2 \times \pi \times 2}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator:**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 4 \times 0.05 \times 500 \times 80 = 4 \times 0.05 \times 40000 = 4 \times 2000 = 8000
$$

**Step 4 — Compute denominator:**

$$
\text{Denominator} = 2 \times \pi \times 2 = 4\pi \approx 12.566
$$

**Step 5 — Final torque:**

$$
\boxed{T_a = \frac{8000}{12.566} \approx 636.6 \, \text{N·m}}
$$

---

#### **Part (b) Solution: Developed Torque of Shunt Motor**

**Given:**
- V = 220 V
- N = 1000 rpm
- $R_a = 0.2 \, \Omega$
- $R_{sh} = 110 \, \Omega$
- $I_a = 50 \, A$

**Step 1 — Find back EMF $E_b$:**

$$
E_b = V - I_a R_a = 220 - (50 \times 0.2)
$$

$$
E_b = 220 - 10 = 210 \, \text{V}
$$

**Step 2 — Find angular velocity ω:**

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1000}{60} = \frac{2000\pi}{60}
$$

$$
\omega \approx 104.72 \, \text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply power balance equation:**

Mechanical power developed = $E_b \times I_a$:

$$
P_{dev} = 210 \times 50 = 10500 \, \text{W}
$$

**Step 4 — Find developed torque:**

$$
T_{dev} = \frac{P_{dev}}{\omega} = \frac{10500}{104.72}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{dev} \approx 100.27 \, \text{N·m}}
$$

---

### ✏️ **Question B (14 Marks) — Reluctance Torque & Energy Method**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
Explain the concept of **reluctance torque**. Derive the expression for reluctance torque from the **magnetic co-energy** principle.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
The inductance of a variable reluctance motor varies with rotor position as $L(\theta) = 0.02 + 0.015 \cos(2\theta)$ H, where θ is in radians. If the motor is supplied with a constant current of 5 A, find the **maximum reluctance torque** developed.

---

### **✅ SOLUTION — Question B**

#### **Part (a) Solution: Reluctance Torque Derivation**

The reluctance torque arises in machines where the **rotor has salient poles** (varying air-gap reluctance) but **no field winding or permanent magnets**. The torque tends to move the rotor to the position where the magnetic circuit reluctance is **minimum**.

**Step 1 — Magnetic co-energy stored:**

$$
W'_{field} = \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2
$$

**Step 2 — Torque is the partial derivative of co-energy w.r.t. angle at constant current:**

$$
\tau = \frac{\partial W'_{field}}{\partial \theta}\bigg|_{i = const}
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate:**

$$
\tau = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_{reluctance} = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}}
$$

---

#### **Part (b) Solution: Maximum Reluctance Torque**

**Given:**

$$
L(\theta) = 0.02 + 0.015 \cos(2\theta) \, \text{H}, \quad i = 5 \, \text{A}
$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate L(θ) w.r.t. θ:**

$$
\frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta} = -0.015 \times 2 \sin(2\theta) = -0.03 \sin(2\theta)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into torque equation:**

$$
\tau = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta} = \frac{1}{2} \times 25 \times (-0.03 \sin(2\theta))
$$

$$
\tau = -0.375 \sin(2\theta) \, \text{N·m}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum magnitude occurs when $\vert \sin(2\theta) \vert = 1$:**

$$
\boxed{\tau_{max} = 0.375 \, \text{N·m} \approx 0.38 \, \text{N·m}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting Units:** Always quote torque in **N·m**, not Nm, not Newton.
> 2. **Confusing A = P with A = 2:** Wave winding → A = 2; Lap winding → A = P. Getting this wrong will cost **2 marks** easily.
> 3. **Sign Convention in Reluctance Torque:** Negative sign indicates *opposing motion*. KTU examiners want **magnitude + direction** stated.
> 4. **Confusing $\omega$ vs N:** Angular velocity in rad/s is **NOT** RPM. Always use $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$.
> 5. **Not Substituting Back-EMF Formula:** In DC motor problems, students often forget $E_b = V - I_aR_a$. This step is **mandatory** for 14-mark problems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for Torque (Module 2):**

- ☐ **Force on conductor**: $F = BIL \sin\theta$ — base equation.
- ☐ **Torque on coil**: $\tau = B I A N \sin\theta$ — instantaneous.
- ☐ **Average torque**: $\tau_{avg} = \frac{2}{\pi} B I A N$.
- ☐ **DC Machine Torque**: $T_a = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A} = k_a \Phi I_a$.
- ☐ **Reluctance Torque**: $T = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}$ — energy method.
- ☐ **Mechanical Power**: $P_m = T \omega = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$.
- ☐ **Relation with Back EMF**: $E_b \cdot I_a = T_{dev} \cdot \omega$ (energy balance).
- ☐ **Wave vs Lap winding**: Wave → A = 2; Lap → A = P.
- ☐ **Sign Convention**: Developed torque opposes applied torque in motor mode.
- ☐ **Units Mastery**: Flux in Wb, Length in m, Current in A → Torque in N·m.
- ☐ **KTU Trick Question Pattern**: They often give flux density B instead of flux Φ — convert using $\Phi = B \times \text{Area}$.
- ☐ **EMF Equation pairing**: $E_b = \frac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A}$ and $T_a = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A}$ — memorize the symmetry.

> **🎯 Pro Tip for KTU ESE:** In a 14-mark problem, **always write down the governing equation first, then substitute, then simplify**. Examiners in KTU specifically reward this structured presentation — even if your final numerical answer has a small slip, you earn step marks!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Slip Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT302 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES & DRIVES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Slip Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Slip (s)** in a three-phase induction motor is defined as the **relative speed** between the rotating magnetic field of the stator (synchronous speed, $N_s$) and the actual mechanical speed of the rotor ($N_r$), expressed as a **per-unit (fraction)** of the synchronous speed.

The mathematical expression is:

$$
s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}
$$

Where:
- $N_s$ = Synchronous speed in **r.p.m** $= \dfrac{120 f}{P}$
- $N_r$ = Actual rotor speed in **r.p.m**
- $f$ = Supply frequency in **Hz** (= **50 Hz** in India)
- $P$ = Number of poles
- $s$ = Per-unit slip (dimensionless, ranges from **0 to 1**)

> [!NOTE]
> Sometimes slip is also expressed in **percentage** as $s\% = s \times 100$. In KTU board examinations, always mention the condition explicitly (e.g., "s = 0.04" or "s = 4%").

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **walking inside a moving escalator** that is going **upwards**:

- The escalator steps move at a constant speed (this is the **stator's rotating magnetic field**, $N_s$).
- You, walking on it, represent the **rotor** trying to follow the steps.
- If you stand still on the escalator, you move up at the escalator's speed — there is **zero slip** ($s = 0$).
- If you walk **down** against the escalator's direction, the escalator must drag you up — you fall behind at maximum rate, this is the **locked rotor / starting condition** ($s = 1$).
- Normally, you walk *upward* (in the same direction as the escalator) but slightly slower — this small "lag" is the **slip**.

> [!NOTE]
> The induction motor rotor can **never reach synchronous speed** because if it did, there would be **no relative motion** between the rotor and the rotating field, meaning **no flux cutting**, **no induced EMF**, and therefore **no rotor current** — and hence **no torque**. Torque-producing slip is therefore always $> 0$.

---

## 1.3 Boundary Conditions of Slip

| Physical Condition | Rotor Speed $N_r$ | Slip $s$ | Rotor Frequency $f_r$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Synchronous speed (theoretical, no torque) | $N_s$ | **0** | **0 Hz** |
| Normal full-load operation | $\approx 0.96\,N_s$ to $0.98\,N_s$ | **0.02 to 0.04** | **1 – 2 Hz** |
| High slip / heavy load | $\approx 0.5\,N_s$ | **0.5** | **25 Hz** |
| Standstill / Locked rotor / Starting | **0** | **1.0** | **50 Hz** (equal to supply) |
| Reverse rotation (plugging) | Negative ($-N_s \le N_r \le 0$) | **1 < s ≤ 2** | **50 to 100 Hz** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The rotor circuit frequency $f_r$ is **directly proportional** to slip: $f_r = s \cdot f$. This single relationship drives **every** slip-dependent quantity in the machine (rotor EMF, rotor reactance, rotor current, torque, power flow).

---

## 1.4 Slip Characteristics — Topic Scope

The **"slip characteristics"** of an induction motor collectively refer to the family of curves that describe how various electrical and mechanical quantities vary as a function of slip $s$:

1. **Torque–Slip Characteristic**: $T_e = f(s)$
2. **Rotor Current–Slip Characteristic**: $I_2 = f(s)$
3. **Power Factor–Slip Characteristic**: $\cos\phi_2 = f(s)$
4. **Rotor Copper Loss–Slip Characteristic**: $P_{cu2} = f(s)$
5. **Mechanical Output Power–Slip Characteristic**: $P_m = f(s)$

These curves form the **analytical heart** of induction motor performance analysis under the KTU 2024 syllabus for Module 2.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Rotor Equivalent Circuit and the Origin of Slip-Dependence

The induction motor rotor can be modelled (referred to the stator) as a **short-circuited transformer secondary** with one critical modification: the rotor frequency is **not** the supply frequency but $f_r = s \cdot f$.

The **per-phase rotor equivalent circuit** referred to the stator at slip $s$ is:

```
      E_2' (rotor induced EMF at slip s)
   o────[ R_2' ]────●────[ s·X_2' ]────┐
   │                 │                  │
   │                 │               (short)
   │                 │                  │
   └────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Where:
- $R_2'$ = Rotor resistance per phase referred to stator
- $X_2'$ = Rotor leakage reactance per phase at **standstill** (i.e., at $s=1$, full supply frequency $f$)
- The reactance at slip $s$ is **$sX_2'$** (because $X \propto f$, and $f_r = s f$)
- The rotor EMF at slip $s$ is **$sE_2$** (where $E_2$ is the standstill rotor EMF)

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation of Slip-Dependent Quantities

### Step 1 — Rotor EMF at Slip
By Faraday's law, EMF is proportional to relative speed between flux and rotor conductors:
$$
E_{2s} = s \cdot E_2
$$
At $s=1$ (standstill): $E_{2s} = E_2$ (maximum). At $s=0$ (synchronous): $E_{2s} = 0$.

### Step 2 — Rotor Reactance at Slip
Reactance depends on rotor frequency $f_r = s f$:
$$
X_{2s} = 2\pi f_r L_2 = 2\pi (sf) L_2 = s \cdot X_2
$$
At $s=1$: $X_{2s} = X_2$. At $s=0$: $X_{2s} = 0$ (rotor conductors see DC, no reactive drop).

### Step 3 — Rotor Current Magnitude
Applying Ohm's law to the rotor loop:
$$
I_2 = \frac{s E_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}}
$$
- At $s = 1$: $I_2$ is large (starting current, 5–7 times full-load).
- At $s = 0$: $I_2 = 0$.

### Step 4 — Rotor Power Factor
$$
\cos\phi_2 = \frac{R_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}}
$$
- At $s = 1$: $\cos\phi_2$ is poor (high reactance).
- At $s \to 0$: $\cos\phi_2 \to 1$ (purely resistive behaviour).

### Step 5 — Electromagnetic Torque
The torque is the in-phase component of rotor power divided by synchronous speed:
$$
T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s E_2^2 \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}
$$
where $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi N_s}{60} = \dfrac{4\pi f}{P}$ rad/s.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Slip Dependency | Boundary: $s \to 0$ | Boundary: $s = 1$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Per-unit Slip | $s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | — | 0 | 1 |
| 2 | Rotor speed | $N_r = N_s(1-s)$ | Linear | $N_s$ | 0 |
| 3 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \frac{120f}{P}$ | Independent | — | — |
| 4 | Rotor frequency | $f_r = s f$ | Linear | 0 Hz | 50 Hz |
| 5 | Rotor EMF | $E_{2s} = s E_2$ | Linear | 0 | $E_2$ |
| 6 | Rotor reactance | $X_{2s} = s X_2$ | Linear | 0 | $X_2$ |
| 7 | Rotor impedance | $Z_2 = \sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$ | Nonlinear | $R_2$ | $\sqrt{R_2^2 + X_2^2}$ |
| 8 | Rotor current | $I_2 = \frac{s E_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}}$ | Nonlinear | 0 | $\frac{E_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + X_2^2}}$ |
| 9 | Rotor power factor | $\cos\phi_2 = \frac{R_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}}$ | Nonlinear | 1 | $\frac{R_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + X_2^2}}$ |
| 10 | Electromagnetic torque | $T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s}\frac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$ | Nonlinear | 0 | $\frac{3 E_2^2 R_2}{\omega_s (R_2^2 + X_2^2)}$ |
| 11 | Air-gap power | $P_g = \frac{3 s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$ | Nonlinear | 0 | $\frac{3 E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}$ |
| 12 | Rotor copper loss | $P_{cu2} = s \cdot P_g$ | Linear in $s$ | 0 | $P_g$ |
| 13 | Mechanical power | $P_m = (1-s) P_g$ | Linear in $(1-s)$ | $P_g$ | 0 |
| 14 | Slip at max torque | $s_{mT} = \frac{R_2}{X_2}$ | — | — | — |
| 15 | Maximum torque | $T_{max} = \frac{3 E_2^2}{2 \omega_s X_2}$ | **Independent of $R_2$** | — | — |
| 16 | Slip at full load | $s_{fl} = \frac{R_2}{X_2} \left( \frac{s_{mT}}{\sqrt{a^2 - 1}} \right)$ where $a = T_{max}/T_{fl}$ | — | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize Equation 14 and 15** — they are the most-tested results in KTU exams. Notice that $T_{max}$ is **independent of rotor resistance $R_2$**, but the **slip at which maximum torque occurs** is **directly proportional to $R_2$**. This is the foundation of the **variable-resistance starter** (rheostatic control) of slip-ring induction motors.

---

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Dependency

| Phenomenon | Physical Reason |
|---|---|
| $E_{2s} \propto s$ | Relative speed between flux and rotor = $sN_s$; induced EMF ∝ relative speed (Faraday's law). |
| $X_{2s} \propto s$ | Rotor frequency $f_r = sf$; inductive reactance $X = 2\pi f_r L$. |
| $I_2$ high at $s=1$ | Maximum relative motion → maximum EMF → maximum current (worst case: starting inrush). |
| $I_2 \to 0$ as $s \to 0$ | No relative motion → no EMF → no current. |
| $\cos\phi_2$ improves as $s$ decreases | Reactance $sX_2$ shrinks faster than resistance; rotor becomes effectively resistive. |
| $P_{cu2} = sP_g$ | Rotor copper loss ∝ $I_2^2 R_2$ and rotor current² scales with $s^2$, impedance² scales with $s^2$ (in the dominant term) → net $P_{cu2} \propto s P_g$. |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The slip characteristics are the **primary design and control tool** for induction motors:

- **Motor selection**: From a torque–slip curve, an engineer determines whether the motor can start the connected load, accelerate it, and run it at the required speed under load.
- **Soft starters & VFDs**: Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs) deliberately alter the V/Hz ratio to shift the slip characteristic and control starting current.
- **Crane & elevator drives**: High starting torque is needed; this is achieved by **adding external rotor resistance** in slip-ring motors, which shifts the peak of the torque-slip curve toward $s = 1$.
- **Energy efficiency auditing**: Knowing the slip at full load tells the engineer the **rotor copper loss** and hence the efficiency.
- **Protection relays**: Speed sensors reading slip detect stalled-rotor conditions and trip the breaker.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Expression for Rotor Current $I_2(s)$

**Starting Point**: The per-phase rotor EMF at slip $s$ is $E_{2s} = s E_2$ and the rotor impedance is $Z_2 = R_2 + j(sX_2)$.

**Step 1 — Write the phasor EMF**:
$$
E_{2s} = s E_2 \angle 0^\circ
$$

**Step 2 — Write the phasor impedance**:
$$
Z_2 = R_2 + j(s X_2)
$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude of impedance**:
$$
\vert Z_2 \vert = \sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Ohm's law for magnitude of current**:
$$
I_2 = \frac{\vert E_{2s} \vert}{\vert Z_2 \vert} = \frac{s E_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}}
$$

**Verification at limits**:
- $s \to 0$: $I_2 \to \dfrac{0}{R_2} = 0$ ✓
- $s = 1$: $I_2 = \dfrac{E_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + X_2^2}}$ ✓ (locked rotor current)

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Expression for Electromagnetic Torque $T_e(s)$

**Step 1 — Air-gap power per phase**:
$$
P_{g,\text{per phase}} = E_{2s} I_2 \cos\phi_2 = (sE_2) \cdot \frac{sE_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}} \cdot \frac{R_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify using common denominator**:
$$
P_{g,\text{per phase}} = \frac{s^2 E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Total air-gap power (3 phases)**:
$$
P_g = 3 \cdot \frac{s^2 E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Relate air-gap power to torque via synchronous speed in rad/s**:
$$
P_g = T_e \cdot \omega_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_e = \frac{P_g}{\omega_s}
$$

**Step 5 — Final expression**:
$$
T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s^2 E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $s^2$ in the numerator can be extracted to expose the classical "$\dfrac{s E_2^2 R_2/s}{(\cdot)}$" form. The textbook standard form is:
> $$
> T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}
> $$
> This is obtained by multiplying numerator and denominator by $1/s^2$ and rearranging, but **both forms are mathematically equivalent and accepted in KTU valuation**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Slip at Maximum Torque

We differentiate $T_e$ with respect to $s$ and set it to zero.

**Step 1 — Write torque in compact form**. Let $K = \frac{3 E_2^2}{\omega_s}$ (constant). Then:
$$
T_e = K \cdot \frac{s R_2}{R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $s$** using the quotient rule $\left(\dfrac{u}{v}\right)' = \dfrac{u'v - uv'}{v^2}$:

Let $u = s R_2$, so $u' = R_2$.
Let $v = R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2$, so $v' = 2 s X_2^2$.

$$
\frac{dT_e}{ds} = K \cdot \frac{(R_2)(R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2) - (s R_2)(2 s X_2^2)}{(R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2)^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Set numerator to zero** (the denominator is always positive):
$$
R_2(R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2) - 2 s^2 R_2 X_2^2 = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify**:
$$
R_2^3 + s^2 R_2 X_2^2 - 2 s^2 R_2 X_2^2 = 0
$$
$$
R_2^3 - s^2 R_2 X_2^2 = 0
$$
$$
R_2^2 = s^2 X_2^2
$$

**Step 5 — Take square root** (slip is non-negative):
$$
s_{mT} = \frac{R_2}{X_2}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Maximum Torque Magnitude (Independent of $R_2$)

**Step 1 — Substitute $s_{mT} = R_2 / X_2$ into the torque expression**:
$$
T_{max} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{(R_2/X_2) \cdot E_2^2 \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + (R_2/X_2)^2 \cdot X_2^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the denominator**:
$$
R_2^2 + \frac{R_2^2}{X_2^2} \cdot X_2^2 = R_2^2 + R_2^2 = 2 R_2^2
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back**:
$$
T_{max} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{(R_2^2 / X_2) \cdot E_2^2}{2 R_2^2} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^2}{2 X_2}
$$

**Step 4 — Final form**:
$$
T_{max} = \frac{3 E_2^2}{2 \omega_s X_2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This result is **loadstone** for KTU problems. $T_{max}$ depends **only on $E_2$, $X_2$ and supply frequency** — it is **completely independent of rotor resistance $R_2$**. Therefore, **changing $R_2$ shifts the location of maximum torque along the slip axis, but does not change the height of the maximum torque peak**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Ratio of Full-Load Torque to Maximum Torque

This is a **highly repeated KTU problem type**.

**Step 1 — Form the ratio** $a = \dfrac{T_{max}}{T_{fl}}$:
$$
a = \frac{T_{max}}{T_{fl}} = \frac{\dfrac{3 E_2^2}{2 \omega_s X_2}}{\dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s_{fl} E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (s_{fl} X_2)^2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Cancel common terms**:
$$
a = \frac{R_2^2 + (s_{fl} X_2)^2}{2 s_{fl} R_2 X_2}
$$

**Step 3 — Use the result $s_{mT} = R_2 / X_2$**, so $R_2 = s_{mT} X_2$. Substituting:
$$
a = \frac{s_{mT}^2 X_2^2 + s_{fl}^2 X_2^2}{2 s_{fl} \cdot s_{mT} X_2 \cdot X_2} = \frac{s_{mT}^2 + s_{fl}^2}{2 s_{fl} s_{mT}}
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange into a quadratic in $s_{fl}$**:
$$
2 a \, s_{fl} s_{mT} = s_{mT}^2 + s_{fl}^2
$$
$$
s_{fl}^2 - 2 a \, s_{mT} s_{fl} + s_{mT}^2 = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the quadratic formula**:
$$
s_{fl} = s_{mT} \left( a \pm \sqrt{a^2 - 1} \right)
$$

**Step 6 — Select the correct sign**: Since $s_{fl} \ll s_{mT}$ in normal operation (full-load slip is small), we take the **negative** sign:
$$
\boxed{\,s_{fl} = s_{mT} \left( a - \sqrt{a^2 - 1} \right)\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In many textbooks the same expression is written as $s_{fl} = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2}\left(a - \sqrt{a^2 - 1}\right)$, which is identical because $s_{mT} = R_2 / X_2$.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem**: A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has $R_2 = 0.2\,\Omega$ and $X_2 = 1.0\,\Omega$ per phase (referred to stator). The standstill rotor EMF per phase is $E_2 = 100\,V$. Find:
(a) Slip at maximum torque
(b) Maximum torque
(c) Full-load slip if $T_{fl} = 0.5 \cdot T_{max}$

**Solution**:

**Step (a)**:
$$
s_{mT} = \frac{R_2}{X_2} = \frac{0.2}{1.0} = 0.2 \text{ p.u.}
$$

**Step (b)** — First compute $\omega_s$:
$$
N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ r.p.m}
$$
$$
\omega_s = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi \text{ rad/s} \approx 157.08 \text{ rad/s}
$$
$$
T_{max} = \frac{3 \times 100^2}{2 \times 50\pi \times 1.0} = \frac{30000}{100\pi} = \frac{300}{\pi} \approx 95.49 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step (c)** — Here $a = T_{max} / T_{fl} = 1/0.5 = 2$:
$$
s_{fl} = 0.2 \left( 2 - \sqrt{2^2 - 1} \right) = 0.2 \left( 2 - \sqrt{3} \right) = 0.2 (2 - 1.732) = 0.2 \times 0.268 \approx 0.0536
$$

So full-load rotor speed:
$$
N_{r,fl} = N_s(1 - s_{fl}) = 1500 (1 - 0.0536) \approx 1419.6 \text{ r.p.m}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Slip Characteristics — Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block diagrams the **functional flow of slip-dependent quantities**, which is the analytical backbone examiners expect for Module 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Supply f and Poles P] --> B[Ns equals 120f over P]
    B --> C[Rotor Speed Nr]
    C --> D{Compute Slip}
    D --> E[s equals Ns minus Nr over Ns]
    E --> F[fr equals s times f]
    E --> G[E2s equals s times E2]
    E --> H[X2s equals s times X2]
    F --> I[Z2 equals R2 squared plus sX2 squared]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[I2 equals sE2 over Z2]
    I --> K[cos phi2 equals R2 over Z2]
    J --> L[Te equals 3 over omega-s times sE2squared R2 over Z2 squared]
    K --> L
    L --> M{Maximize Te}
    M --> N[smT equals R2 over X2]
    M --> O[Tmax equals 3E2 squared over 2 omega-s X2]
    O --> P[Tmax is INDEPENDENT of R2]
    N --> Q[Shift slip axis by varying R2]
    P --> R[Engineering Use: VFD and soft starter design]
    Q --> R
```

## 4.2 Torque–Slip Curve — Operational Region Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Slip Axis] --> B[Region A: Low Slip 0 to 0.05]
    A --> C[Region B: Normal Operation 0.05 to 0.3]
    A --> D[Region C: Pullout Region 0.3 to 0.5]
    A --> E[Region D: Starting s equals 1]

    B --> F[Stable Operation Linear Torque Slip]
    C --> G[Nonlinear Increasing Torque]
    D --> H[Near Tmax Unstable]
    E --> I[Starting Torque Minimum Speed]
```

## 4.3 Effect of Rotor Resistance on Slip Characteristics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Rotor R2] --> B[Tmax Peak at smT equals R2 over X2]
    C[Increased R2 prime equals 3 times R2] --> D[Same Tmax but shifted to smT prime]
    E[Decreased R2 doubleprime equals R2 over 2] --> F[Same Tmax but shifted to smT doubleprime]
    D --> G[Starting Torque Increases]
    F --> H[Starting Torque Decreases]
    G --> I[Useful for Slip Ring Motor Starting]
    H --> J[Useful for High Speed Squirrel Cage Motors]
```

## 4.4 Power Flow Stages in the Rotor (Slip-Based Partition)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Air Gap Power Pg] --> B[Rotor Copper Loss Pcu2 equals s times Pg]
    A --> C[Mechanical Power Developed Pm equals 1 minus s times Pg]
    B --> D[Total Loss Stage 1]
    C --> E[Shaft Output After Friction and Windage]
    D --> F[Efficiency equals Pout over Pin]
    E --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the slip-based partition matters in KTU problems**: The expression $P_{cu2} = sP_g$ is one of the most elegant and frequently tested identities. It tells the engineer that **slip is the fraction of air-gap power lost as heat in the rotor**. Hence, an efficient induction motor must have **very small slip** at full load (typically 2–5%).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define slip of an induction motor. Why cannot the rotor run at synchronous speed?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
Slip is the relative speed between the rotating magnetic field and the rotor, expressed as a fraction of synchronous speed:
$$
s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}
$$
The rotor cannot reach synchronous speed because at $N_r = N_s$, the relative speed between the rotor conductors and the rotating flux is **zero**, leading to **zero induced EMF**, **zero rotor current**, and therefore **zero torque**. The motor would have no driving torque to sustain rotation, so $N_r$ always remains slightly less than $N_s$ in normal operation. **(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for explanation with formula support)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the relationship between rotor frequency and slip. What is the rotor frequency at starting and at synchronous speed?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
$$
f_r = s \cdot f
$$
- At starting, $s = 1$, so $f_r = 50$ Hz (equal to supply frequency). **(1 mark)**
- At synchronous speed (theoretical), $s = 0$, so $f_r = 0$ Hz. **(1 mark)**
- The rotor frequency is **directly proportional to slip**, which means rotor EMF, rotor reactance, rotor copper loss — all scale with $s$. **(1 mark for explanation)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a) Derive the expression for electromagnetic torque of a three-phase induction motor as a function of slip, starting from the rotor equivalent circuit.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1 — State the rotor equivalent circuit at slip $s$**:
Per phase, the rotor EMF is $E_{2s} = sE_2$, rotor resistance $R_2$, and rotor reactance at slip is $sX_2$ (since rotor frequency $f_r = sf$).

**Step 2 — Rotor current per phase**:
$$
I_2 = \frac{s E_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Rotor power factor**:
$$
\cos\phi_2 = \frac{R_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Air-gap power per phase** = (rotor EMF)(rotor current)(cos φ₂):
$$
P_{g,\text{phase}} = sE_2 \cdot \frac{sE_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}} \cdot \frac{R_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}} = \frac{s^2 E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Total air-gap power (3 phases)**:
$$
P_g = \frac{3 s^2 E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}
$$

**Step 6 — Synchronous angular speed**:
$$
\omega_s = \frac{2\pi N_s}{60} = \frac{4\pi f}{P}
$$

**Step 7 — Electromagnetic torque**:
$$
T_e = \frac{P_g}{\omega_s} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s^2 E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2/s + X_2^2}
$$

Or in the **standard textbook form**:
$$
\boxed{\,T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}\,}
$$

**Mark split**: Equivalent circuit diagram (1), EMF & reactance with slip (1), current expression (1), power factor (1), air-gap power (1), final torque (2).

---

**(b) Derive the condition for maximum torque and show that maximum torque is independent of rotor resistance.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1 — Rewrite torque compactly**:
Let $K = 3E_2^2/\omega_s$. Then
$$
T_e = K \cdot \frac{s R_2}{R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. $s$**:
$$
\frac{dT_e}{ds} = K \cdot \frac{R_2(R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2) - sR_2 \cdot 2sX_2^2}{(R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2)^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Set numerator to zero**:
$$
R_2^3 + s^2 R_2 X_2^2 - 2 s^2 R_2 X_2^2 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; R_2^2 = s^2 X_2^2
$$

**Step 4 — Slip at maximum torque**:
$$
\boxed{\,s_{mT} = \frac{R_2}{X_2}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $s_{mT}$ into torque expression**:
$$
T_{max} = K \cdot \frac{(R_2/X_2) R_2}{R_2^2 + (R_2^2/X_2^2) X_2^2} = K \cdot \frac{R_2^2 / X_2}{2 R_2^2} = \frac{K}{2X_2}
$$

**Step 6 — Final maximum torque**:
$$
T_{max} = \frac{3 E_2^2}{2 \omega_s X_2}
$$

**Step 7 — Conclusion**: Since $R_2$ does not appear in the final expression, $T_{max}$ is **independent of rotor resistance**. However, the **slip $s_{mT}$ at which this maximum occurs is directly proportional to $R_2$**, so changing rotor resistance shifts the position of the peak torque along the slip axis. **(1 mark for concluding statement)**

**Mark split**: Compact form (1), differentiation (2), slip at max torque (1), substitution (1), final expression (1), independence conclusion (1).

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]`

**(a) With the help of a neat sketch, explain the torque–slip characteristic of a three-phase induction motor. Discuss the stable and unstable regions of operation.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Sketch description** (to be drawn by student):
- X-axis: Slip $s$ (0 to 1)
- Y-axis: Torque $T_e$
- Curve rises from origin, reaches a peak ($T_{max}$ at $s_{mT} = R_2/X_2$), then dips down to a lower value at $s = 1$ called **starting torque** $T_{st}$.

**Step 1 — Shape of the curve**:
At small slips ($s \to 0$), $T_e \approx KsR_2/X_2^2$, i.e., torque is **approximately linear** with slip — this is the **stable operating region**.

**Step 2 — Maximum torque point**:
The curve reaches its peak at $s_{mT} = R_2/X_2$. The peak height is $T_{max} = \frac{3E_2^2}{2\omega_s X_2}$ (independent of $R_2$).

**Step 3 — Beyond peak (unstable region)**:
For $s > s_{mT}$, torque **decreases** with increasing slip. If a load disturbance increases the slip, the motor torque drops **below** the load torque, accelerating the disturbance — this is the **unstable region**.

**Step 4 — Starting torque at $s = 1$**:
$$
T_{st} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}
$$
The starting torque is **less than** the maximum torque.

**Step 5 — Stable vs. Unstable regions**:
- **Stable region**: $0 < s < s_{mT}$ (positive slope of torque-slip curve).
- **Unstable region**: $s_{mT} < s \le 1$ (negative slope).
- Normal operation is in the stable region close to $s \approx 0.02$–$0.05$.

**Step 6 — Effect of rotor resistance on the curve**:
Increasing $R_2$ shifts the peak to the right (higher $s_{mT}$) but does not change $T_{max}$ height. This improves starting torque at the cost of full-load efficiency.

**Mark split**: Sketch (1), shape analysis (1), $T_{max}$ point (1), starting torque (1), stable/unstable regions (2), effect of $R_2$ (1).

---

**(b) A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has rotor resistance and standstill reactance per phase (referred to stator) of $0.3\,\Omega$ and $1.5\,\Omega$ respectively. The standstill rotor EMF per phase is $200\,V$. Compute: (i) slip at which maximum torque occurs, (ii) maximum torque, (iii) full-load slip if the ratio of maximum to full-load torque is 2, and (iv) rotor speed at full load.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Given**: $P = 6$, $f = 50$ Hz, $R_2 = 0.3\,\Omega$, $X_2 = 1.5\,\Omega$, $E_2 = 200$ V, $a = T_{max}/T_{fl} = 2$.

**Step (i) — Slip at maximum torque**:
$$
s_{mT} = \frac{R_2}{X_2} = \frac{0.3}{1.5} = 0.2 \text{ p.u. (or 20%)}
$$

**Step (ii) — Synchronous speed and $\omega_s$**:
$$
N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ r.p.m.}
$$
$$
\omega_s = \frac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} = \frac{100\pi}{3} \approx 104.72 \text{ rad/s}
$$
$$
T_{max} = \frac{3 E_2^2}{2 \omega_s X_2} = \frac{3 \times 200^2}{2 \times 104.72 \times 1.5} = \frac{120000}{314.16} \approx 381.97 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step (iii) — Full-load slip with $a = 2$**:
$$
s_{fl} = s_{mT}\left(a - \sqrt{a^2 - 1}\right) = 0.2 \left(2 - \sqrt{3}\right) = 0.2 \times 0.268 = 0.0536 \text{ p.u. (or 5.36%)}
$$

**Step (iv) — Rotor speed at full load**:
$$
N_{r,fl} = N_s (1 - s_{fl}) = 1000 (1 - 0.0536) = 946.4 \text{ r.p.m.}
$$

**Mark split**: Each sub-part for (i)–(iv) gets ~1.5–2 marks. Common error trap: students forget the $2\pi/60$ conversion for $\omega_s$. Always show the unit cancellation explicitly.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls where KTU students lose marks in Slip Characteristics questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 3** in the torque formula. The expression $T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{sE_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$ is for a **3-phase** motor. For a single-phase, use coefficient 1 with appropriate modifications. Examiners specifically check this.
> 2. **Using $N_s$ in r.p.m. directly** in the torque equation. You **must** convert to $\omega_s$ in **rad/s** by multiplying by $2\pi/60$. This is the most common calculation error (–2 marks typical penalty).
> 3. **Confusing "rotor copper loss = $I_2^2 R_2$" with "$sP_g$"** in derivations. The identity $P_{cu2} = sP_g$ comes from the air-gap power partition, **not** from a direct current-squared-resistance calculation. Derivation must show the path clearly.
> 4. **Writing $s_{mT} = R_2/X_2$ but skipping the differentiation step** — partial marks are awarded for the calculus; skipping it loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Selecting the wrong root** of the quadratic for $s_{fl}$: always take the **negative sign** ($a - \sqrt{a^2-1}$) because $s_{fl} < s_{mT}$. The positive sign corresponds to the unstable branch.
> 6. **Omitting the equivalent circuit diagram** in derivations. KTU examiners specifically allot **1 mark** for the diagram; missing it costs an easy mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Slip Characteristics**
>
> - **Slip definition**: $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$; always $0 < s < 1$ in motoring mode.
> - **Rotor frequency**: $f_r = s f$ — drives every other slip-dependent quantity.
> - **Rotor EMF**: $E_{2s} = sE_2$; **Rotor reactance**: $X_{2s} = sX_2$ — both linear in $s$.
> - **Rotor current**: $I_2 = sE_2 / \sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$ — increases with $s$, peaks at $s = 1$.
> - **Power factor**: $\cos\phi_2 = R_2 / \sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$ — improves as $s \to 0$.
> - **Torque formula (must memorize)**: $T_e = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$
> - **Slip at max torque**: $s_{mT} = R_2 / X_2$ — **directly proportional to $R_2$**.
> - **Maximum torque**: $T_{max} = 3E_2^2 / (2 \omega_s X_2)$ — **independent of $R_2$**.
> - **Power flow partition**: $P_{cu2} = sP_g$ and $P_m = (1-s)P_g$ — the **"s" represents the heat-loss fraction**.
> - **Full-load slip formula**: $s_{fl} = s_{mT}\left(a - \sqrt{a^2-1}\right)$ where $a = T_{max}/T_{fl}$ (use **negative** sign).
> - **Stable region**: $0 < s < s_{mT}$ (positive slope); **Unstable region**: $s_{mT} < s \le 1$.
> - **Starting torque**: $T_{st} = \dfrac{3E_2^2 R_2}{\omega_s(R_2^2 + X_2^2)}$ at $s = 1$.
> - **Plugging (reverse braking)**: $s > 1$ — rotor rotates opposite to field, used for rapid stopping.
> - **Dynamic braking**: DC excitation of stator, rotor dissipates kinetic energy as $I_2^2 R_2$.
> - **Engineering insight**: To increase starting torque in slip-ring motors, **add external rotor resistance** — this shifts $s_{mT}$ towards 1 without changing $T_{max}$.
> - **KTU standard units**: $N_s$ in **r.p.m.**, $\omega_s$ in **rad/s**, $T_e$ in **N·m**, $E_2$ in **volts**, $R_2$ & $X_2$ in **ohms**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Starting Methods of 3-Phase Induction Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT302 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES & DRIVES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Starting Methods of 3-Phase Induction Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Starting of a 3-Phase Induction Motor (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> The process of bringing a stationary three-phase squirrel-cage or wound-rotor (slip-ring) induction motor from its **rest condition (slip = 1)** up to its **rated operating speed** by applying a controlled three-phase AC supply to the stator winding, while simultaneously limiting the **inrush (starting) current** and ensuring the development of a **satisfactory starting torque** sufficient to overcome the load's static friction and load torque at standstill.

In an induction motor, at the moment of switching ON the supply, the rotor is at rest and the relative speed between the rotating magnetic field (synchronous speed, $N_s$) and the rotor is maximum. By Faraday's law, this maximum slip frequency induces the **maximum possible rotor EMF**, which in turn produces an enormous current in the short-circuited rotor bars (typically **5 to 7 times the full-load current** in squirrel-cage motors). This calls for the use of external **starters**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Pushing a Heavy Cart" Analogy)

Imagine you are trying to push a heavy, fully-loaded railway wagon that has been sitting still for hours:

1. **The Static Friction Problem:** When the wagon is at rest, the static friction is enormous. You need to apply a massive push to even *budge* it. This is the **High Starting Current** that flows into the induction motor at slip = 1.
2. **Once it Starts Rolling:** The moment the wagon moves a few centimeters, the friction drops drastically, and you only need a small push to keep it accelerating. This represents the motor accelerating toward rated speed, where the slip drops, EMF reduces, and current falls to normal full-load value.
3. **The "Damper" Principle:** If you had a hydraulic damper on the wagon, you could apply a *controlled, smooth push* over a longer period instead of one sudden shock. This damper represents the **Starter**, which limits the initial surge of power from the supply.
4. **Gear Reduction Analogy:** A starter that reduces voltage is like using a lower gear to start the wagon — easier on your muscles, but slower. A starter that adds rotor resistance is like wearing gloves for better grip — better traction (torque) at lower effort (current).

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Numerical Benchmarks (Industry Standard):**
> - **DOL Starting Current** for a standard squirrel-cage IM: **$I_{st} = 5\text{ to } 7 \times I_{FL}$**
> - **DOL Starting Torque** for a standard squirrel-cage IM: **$T_{st} = 1.5\text{ to } 2.5 \times T_{FL}$**
> - **Acceptable Supply Voltage Dip** during starting: Typically **≤ 10%** (as per most electricity board regulations)
> - **Synchronous Speed Formula:** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ where $f$ is supply frequency and $P$ is the number of poles
> - **Slip Formula:** $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$

### 1.4 Why Starting Methods are Necessary

> [!WARNING]
> **Consequences of Starting a Large Induction Motor Directly (Without a Starter):**
> 1. **Severe Voltage Dip** in the entire supply network, affecting other connected loads (lights dim, computers restart).
> 2. **Overheating of the stator winding** due to $I^2R$ losses (copper loss becomes 25–49 times normal).
> 3. **Mechanical shock** to the shaft, coupling, bearings, and the driven load (belts slip, gears break).
> 4. **Tripping of protective devices** (MCBs, MCCBs, relays) due to high inrush current.
> 5. **Violation of electricity supply utility regulations** in industrial estates.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Theory Behind High Starting Current

At standstill, slip $s = 1$. The rotor EMF per phase is:

$$E_{2r(s=1)} = sE_{2r} = E_{2r}$$

This is the **maximum** induced EMF. The rotor impedance at standstill is:

$$Z_{2r(s=1)} = \sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2} = \sqrt{R_2^2 + X_2^2}$$

The rotor current at standstill:

$$I_{2r(s=1)} = \dfrac{sE_{2r}}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}} = \dfrac{E_{2r}}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + X_2^2}}$$

The reflected rotor current to the stator side **adds to the magnetising current**, resulting in a **stator current 5–7 times** the full-load value.

### 2.2 Classification of Starting Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification (Based on KTU 2024 Module 2 Syllabus):**

#### A. Squirrel-Cage Induction Motor Starters
1. **Direct-On-Line (DOL) Starter**
2. **Star-Delta (Y-Δ) Starter**
3. **Autotransformer Starter**
4. **Soft Starter (Solid-State Electronic Starter)**

#### B. Slip-Ring (Wound-Rotor) Induction Motor Starters
5. **Rotor Resistance Starter**

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Starting Method | Voltage Applied at Start | Starting Current (vs. DOL) | Starting Torque (vs. DOL) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Direct-On-Line (DOL)** | $V$ (full) | $I_{st}$ (Reference) | $T_{st}$ (Reference) | Small motors $\leq 5\text{ HP}$ |
| 2 | **Star-Delta (Y-Δ)** | $V / \sqrt{3}$ | $I_{st(Y)} = I_{st} / 3$ | $T_{st(Y)} = T_{st} / 3$ | Pumps, fans, compressors up to $20\text{ HP}$ |
| 3 | **Autotransformer (Tap $x$)** | $xV$ | $I_{st(auto)} = x^2 I_{st}$ | $T_{st(auto)} = x^2 T_{st}$ | Heavy industrial loads |
| 4 | **Soft Starter (Voltage Ramp)** | Ramp from $V_{initial}$ to $V$ | User adjustable | User adjustable | Modern industrial drives |
| 5 | **Rotor Resistance Starter** | $V$ (full) | Reduced by $R_{ext}$ | **Increased** (up to max torque) | Cranes, elevators, hoists |

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Relationship (Voltage-Based Starters):**
> For any reduced-voltage starter, if the voltage ratio is $x$ (i.e., applied voltage = $xV$), then:
> - Starting current scales as: $I \propto x^2$
> - Starting torque scales as: $T \propto x^2$
> (Because both current and torque are proportional to voltage squared in the linear operating region of the torque-slip curve.)

### 2.4 Detailed Theory of Each Starting Method

#### 2.4.1 Direct-On-Line (DOL) Starter
- **Principle:** Full rated 3-phase voltage is applied directly to the stator terminals through a contactor.
- **Components:** TP MCB/MCCB + 3-Pole Contactor (with NO/NC auxiliary contacts) + Thermal Overload Relay (OLR) + Start/Stop pushbuttons.
- **Operation:** Pressing the START button energizes the contactor coil; the main contacts close, connecting the motor to the line. The OLR monitors the current; if sustained overload occurs, the bimetallic strip trips and de-energizes the contactor.
- **Advantages:** Simple, cheapest, highest starting torque, no transition surge.
- **Disadvantages:** Huge inrush current, severe voltage dip, not suitable for large motors or weak supply networks.
- **Permissible Use (KTU/ISI Standard):** Motors with ratings such that the starting current does not cause a supply voltage dip exceeding **5–10%**. Practically limited to **$\leq 7.5\text{ kW (10 HP)}$** in industrial practice, although technically DOL is applicable to all ratings.

#### 2.4.2 Star-Delta (Y-Δ) Starter
- **Principle:** The stator winding is initially connected in **STAR (Y)** configuration. This reduces the **per-phase voltage** to $V_L / \sqrt{3}$. After the motor accelerates to ~75–80% of rated speed, the winding is reconfigured to **DELTA (Δ)**, applying full line voltage.
- **Key Insight (Why this works):** In star connection, the **phase voltage** is $V_{ph} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$. Since torque $\propto V^2$, starting torque drops to **$1/3$ of DOL value**.
- **Components:** 3-Pole Main Contactor (K1) + Star Contactor (K2) + Delta Contactor (K3) + Timer Relay + OLR + Mechanical/Electrical Interlocks.
- **Switching Sequence:**
  1. **K1 closed, K2 closed:** Motor in Star → runs at $1/3$ torque, $1/3$ current.
  2. **Timer triggers (typically 5–15 s):** K2 opens first (with a brief delay).
  3. **K3 closes:** Motor in Delta → runs at full voltage and rated torque.
- **Disadvantage:** **Open-transition switching** causes a current surge and torque dip during star-to-delta transition. (Modern closed-transition star-delta starters use an additional resistor to eliminate this surge.)
- **Restriction:** The motor must be **designed for normal Delta running** (i.e., its rated voltage must equal the **line-to-line** supply voltage, e.g., a 415 V motor must be Δ-rated for 415 V).

#### 2.4.3 Autotransformer Starter
- **Principle:** A **3-phase autotransformer** with multiple **taps** (typically 50%, 65%, 80% of line voltage) is connected between the supply and the motor. During starting, the motor receives the **reduced tap voltage** through the autotransformer. After acceleration, the motor is transferred directly to the full supply via a changeover switch, and the autotransformer is disconnected.
- **Operation Modes:** There are two configurations:
  - **Open-Circuit Transition (Korndorfer):** The motor is momentarily disconnected from both supply and autotransformer during transition. Less expensive but causes a small current surge.
  - **Closed-Circuit Transition (More common in KTU industrial practice):** The motor is transferred smoothly with a transition resistor or reactor, eliminating the surge.
- **Tapping:** A tap at $x$ (where $0 < x < 1$) provides:
  - Motor terminal voltage = $xV_L$
  - Starting current drawn from motor = $x I_{st(DOL)}$
  - Starting current drawn from supply = $x^2 I_{st(DOL)}$ (this is the **key advantage** — even greater reduction than star-delta at low taps)
  - Starting torque = $x^2 T_{st(DOL)}$

#### 2.4.4 Rotor Resistance Starter (For Slip-Ring IM only)
- **Principle:** External resistors are inserted **in series with the rotor winding** through slip rings and brushes. This **increases the total rotor resistance** $R_2 + R_{ext}$, which:
  1. **Reduces the starting current** drawn from the supply.
  2. **Increases the starting torque** (the maximum torque point shifts to higher slip).
- **Why this is the BEST for high-torque starting:** Unlike reduced-voltage starters (which reduce *both* current and torque), rotor resistance starter **reduces current but increases torque**. This is a unique and critical advantage.
- **Operation:** A 3-phase liquid or wire-wound rheostat is used with a controller. Resistors are **cut out in steps** as the motor accelerates, until at full speed the rotor is short-circuited directly.
- **Mathematical Condition for Maximum Starting Torque at Start:**

$$R_2 + R_{ext} = \dfrac{R_2}{s_{maxT}}$$

> where $s_{maxT}$ is the slip at which maximum torque occurs in the natural torque-slip curve (typically 0.1 to 0.2 for standard motors).

#### 2.4.5 Soft Starter (Modern Electronic Starter)
- **Principle:** Uses **back-to-back connected thyristors (SCRs)** in each phase to control the **firing angle ($\alpha$)** of the AC voltage, effectively controlling the **RMS voltage applied to the motor**.
- **Features:** Soft start (voltage ramp), soft stop, current limit, energy saving mode, built-in motor protection.
- **Advantage:** Smooth, stepless voltage control; no moving parts; programmable.
- **Disadvantage:** Only controls voltage (not frequency), so limited to fixed-frequency applications; not suitable where variable speed is needed (use VFD for that).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pumps and Fans (Centrifugal Loads):** Star-Delta or Soft Starters are widely used. The load torque is proportional to $N^2$, so starting requires relatively low torque.
- **Conveyors, Crushers, Compressors (Constant Torque Loads):** Autotransformer or DOL with high-torque motor design.
- **Cranes, Hoists, Elevators:** Slip-Ring IM with Rotor Resistance Starter — because these loads require **HIGH STARTING TORQUE** at LOW STARTING CURRENT**, which only the rotor resistance method can provide.
- **HVAC Systems:** Soft Starters or Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs) — modern, energy-efficient, with reduced mechanical stress.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Star-Delta Reduces Starting Current to 1/3

Consider a 3-phase induction motor rated for **415 V, Delta connected**. In normal operation, the per-phase voltage is $V_{ph,\Delta} = V_L = 415\text{ V}$.

**Step 1: Motor Phase Impedance (Constant)**
The per-phase impedance of the stator winding (assumed constant since $s$ varies but $R$ and $X$ are fixed) is:

$$Z_{ph} = R_1 + jX_1 + \dfrac{(R_2/s)^2 + X_2^2 \text{ reflected}}{\text{...}}$$

For simplicity, let us represent the **equivalent starting impedance per phase** as $Z_{eq}$ (a complex but fixed quantity at standstill).

**Step 2: DOL Starting Current Per Phase**

In DOL operation, with the winding in Delta, the **phase voltage** equals the **line voltage** ($V_L = 415\text{ V}$). The **phase current** is:

$$I_{ph,\Delta,DOL} = \dfrac{V_L}{Z_{eq}}$$

The **line current** in Delta is $I_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{ph}$:

$$I_{L,DOL} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \dfrac{V_L}{Z_{eq}}$$

**Step 3: Star Starting Current Per Phase**

In Star connection, the **phase voltage** is $V_{ph,Y} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$. The **phase current** is:

$$I_{ph,Y} = \dfrac{V_L / \sqrt{3}}{Z_{eq}} = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_{eq}}$$

The **line current in Star** equals the **phase current in Star** (since line = phase in Y):

$$I_{L,Y} = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_{eq}}$$

**Step 4: Ratio of Starting Currents (Line Currents)**

$$I_{L,Y} = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_{eq}}$$

$$I_{L,DOL} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L}{Z_{eq}}$$

Taking the ratio:

$$\dfrac{I_{L,Y}}{I_{L,DOL}} = \dfrac{V_L / (\sqrt{3} Z_{eq})}{\sqrt{3} V_L / Z_{eq}} = \dfrac{1}{(\sqrt{3})(\sqrt{3})} = \dfrac{1}{3}$$

$$\therefore \boxed{I_{st,Y} = \dfrac{1}{3} I_{st,DOL}}$$

**Step 5: Ratio of Starting Torques**

Starting torque is proportional to the square of the applied phase voltage:

$$\dfrac{T_{st,Y}}{T_{st,DOL}} = \left(\dfrac{V_{ph,Y}}{V_{ph,\Delta}}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{V_L/\sqrt{3}}{V_L}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2 = \dfrac{1}{3}$$

$$\therefore \boxed{T_{st,Y} = \dfrac{1}{3} T_{st,DOL}}$$

**Conclusion:** Both starting current and starting torque are reduced to **one-third** of their DOL values.

### 3.2 Derivation: Autotransformer Starting Current Reduction

Let the autotransformer tap ratio be $x$ (so the motor receives a voltage of $x V_L$).

**Step 1: Motor-side current at start**

The current drawn by the motor (which is on the secondary side of the autotransformer) is:

$$I_{motor} = \dfrac{x V_L}{Z_{eq}}$$

**Step 2: Transformer Current Transformation**

For an ideal autotransformer, the relationship between primary (line) current and secondary (motor) current is:

$$\dfrac{I_{line}}{I_{motor}} = \dfrac{N_{secondary}}{N_{primary}} = x$$

Therefore, the **line current** drawn from the supply is:

$$I_{line} = x \cdot I_{motor} = x \cdot \dfrac{x V_L}{Z_{eq}} = \dfrac{x^2 V_L}{Z_{eq}}$$

**Step 3: Ratio to DOL Starting Current**

$$I_{DOL} = \dfrac{V_L}{Z_{eq}}$$

$$\dfrac{I_{line(auto)}}{I_{DOL}} = \dfrac{x^2 V_L / Z_{eq}}{V_L / Z_{eq}} = x^2$$

$$\therefore \boxed{I_{st,auto} = x^2 \cdot I_{st,DOL}}$$

**Step 4: Starting Torque with Autotransformer**

$$\dfrac{T_{st,auto}}{T_{st,DOL}} = \left(\dfrac{x V_L}{V_L}\right)^2 = x^2$$

$$\therefore \boxed{T_{st,auto} = x^2 \cdot T_{st,DOL}}$$

**Numerical Example:** If the tap is set at $x = 0.6$ (i.e., 60% tap):
- Starting current = $0.36 \times I_{st,DOL}$ (i.e., **64% reduction**)
- Starting torque = $0.36 \times T_{st,DOL}$ (i.e., **64% reduction**)

### 3.3 Derivation: Rotor Resistance Starting Torque Maximization

The torque equation of an induction motor is:

$$T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_1^2 \cdot (R_2/s)}{(R_1 + R_2/s)^2 + (X_1 + X_2)^2}$$

The condition for **maximum torque** is found by differentiating $T$ with respect to slip $s$ and setting $\dfrac{dT}{ds} = 0$.

**Step 1: Condition for Maximum Torque (with external rotor resistance $R_{ext}$)**

The slip at which maximum torque occurs is:

$$s_{mT} = \dfrac{R_2'}{(X_1 + X_2)}$$

where $R_2' = R_2 + R_{ext}$ (total rotor resistance referred to stator).

**Step 2: For the motor to develop MAXIMUM torque AT START (i.e., at $s = 1$):**

We require $s_{mT} = 1$, which gives:

$$1 = \dfrac{R_2 + R_{ext}}{X_1 + X_2}$$

$$\therefore \boxed{R_{ext} = (X_1 + X_2) - R_2}$$

**Step 3: Numerical Example**

Let $R_2 = 0.5\ \Omega$, $X_1 = 1.0\ \Omega$, $X_2 = 1.0\ \Omega$.

$$R_{ext} = (1.0 + 1.0) - 0.5 = 1.5\ \Omega$$

With this external resistance, the motor delivers its **maximum possible torque right at starting**, while the starting current is simultaneously **limited to a safe value** — a feature no other starter can match.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Board Examination Ready)

| Parameter | DOL | Star-Delta | Autotransformer | Rotor Resistance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Initial Voltage | $100\%$ | $58\%$ ($\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$) | $50\%/65\%/80\%$ (tap) | $100\%$ |
| Starting Current | $5\text{–}7 I_{FL}$ | $\frac{1}{3}$ of DOL | $x^2$ of DOL | Reduced (set by $R_{ext}$) |
| Starting Torque | $1.5\text{–}2.5 T_{FL}$ | $\frac{1}{3}$ of DOL | $x^2$ of DOL | **Can be set to MAX torque** |
| Cost | Lowest | Low | Medium | Highest (for slip-ring IM) |
| Maintenance | Very Low | Low | Medium | High (slip rings, brushes) |
| Transition Surge | None | **Large** (open transition) | Small (closed transition) | None (stepped) |
| Motor Type | Any | Must be Δ-rated | Any | Slip-ring only |
| Typical Use | $\leq 5\text{ HP}$ | $\leq 20\text{ HP}$ | $20\text{–}200\text{ HP}$ | Cranes, elevators |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification of Induction Motor Starting Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Starting Methods of 3-Phase IM] --> branch1[Squirrel-Cage IM]
    startA --> branch2[Slip-Ring Wound Rotor IM]
    branch1 --> sub1[DOL Starter]
    branch1 --> sub2[Star-Delta Starter]
    branch1 --> sub3[Autotransformer Starter]
    branch1 --> sub4[Soft Starter]
    branch1 --> sub5[Variable Frequency Drive VFD]
    branch2 --> sub6[Rotor Resistance Starter]
    sub1 --> detail1[Full voltage applied directly through contactor]
    sub2 --> detail2[Starts in Y reconnects to Delta at 75 to 80 percent speed]
    sub3 --> detail3[Reduced voltage via autotransformer tap ratio x]
    sub4 --> detail4[SCR based stepless voltage ramp]
    sub5 --> detail5[Variable voltage and variable frequency]
    sub6 --> detail6[External resistance added to rotor circuit via slip rings]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Operating Sequence of a Star-Delta Starter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1[Press START] --> step2[K1 and K2 close, Motor in STAR]
    step2 --> step3[Motor accelerates on reduced voltage]
    step3 --> step4{Timer reaches preset value}
    step4 -- Yes --> step5[K2 opens, brief open transition]
    step5 --> step6[K3 closes, Motor in DELTA]
    step6 --> step7[Motor runs at full voltage and rated torque]
    step4 -- No --> step3
    step7 --> step8[Press STOP, K1 and K3 open, motor coasts to stop]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Rotor Resistance Starting Stages

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stageA[Stage 1: All Rotor Resistance in Circuit] --> stageB[High starting torque, low starting current]
    stageB --> stageC{Motor accelerates to 50 percent speed}
    stageC -- Yes --> stageD[Stage 2: Cut out first resistance step]
    stageD --> stageE[Motor accelerates further]
    stageE --> stageF{Motor reaches 75 percent speed}
    stageF -- Yes --> stageG[Stage 3: Cut out next resistance step]
    stageG --> stageH[Continue cutting out resistors in steps]
    stageH --> stageI[Full speed: Rotor short circuited, no external resistance]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Soft Starter Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[3 Phase AC Supply 415 V 50 Hz] --> block1[Anti parallel SCR pair per phase]
    block1 --> block2[Motor Stator Terminals]
    block2 --> outputA[Controlled Reduced RMS Voltage]
    subgraph ctrl[Control Unit]
        c1[Firing Angle Alpha Control] --> c2[Voltage Ramp Generator]
        c3[Current Limit Circuit] --> c2
        c4[Feedback from CT] --> c1
    end
    ctrl --> block1
    block2 --> feedback[Current and Voltage Sensing CT and PT]
    feedback --> c4
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{Motor rating less than 5 HP}

    q1 -- No --> q2{Load is high inertia and requires high starting torque}
    q2 -- Yes --> rec2[Use Slip Ring IM with Rotor Resistance Starter]
    q2 -- No --> q3{Motor can be reconfigured from Y to Delta at start}
    q3 -- Yes --> rec3[Use Star Delta Starter]
    q3 -- No --> q4{Variable speed control also required}
    q4 -- Yes --> rec4[Use VFD Variable Frequency Drive]
    q4 -- No --> rec5{Soft smooth starting only required}
    rec5 -- Yes --> rec6[Use Soft Starter]
    rec5 -- No --> rec7[Use Autotransformer Starter with proper tap selection]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model)]**
*State why a 3-phase induction motor cannot be started directly on load when its rating exceeds a few horsepower. Mention any two standard starting methods for squirrel-cage induction motors.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A 3-phase induction motor draws a very high starting current (typically **5 to 7 times** the full-load current) when started Direct-On-Line (DOL), because at standstill the slip $s = 1$ and the induced rotor EMF is maximum. For large motors, this inrush causes:
1. **Severe voltage dip** in the supply network, disturbing other connected loads.
2. **Overheating** of the stator winding due to $I^2R$ losses.
3. **Tripping** of protective devices.

Hence, large motors are started using reduced-voltage starters. **Two standard starting methods for squirrel-cage induction motors are:**
- Star-Delta Starter
- Autotransformer Starter

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model)]**
*Compare the starting torque of a squirrel-cage induction motor when started by (i) Direct-On-Line, (ii) Star-Delta, and (iii) Autotransformer with 60% tap. Assume DOL starting torque = $T_{st}$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Starting Method | Starting Torque |
|---|---|
| (i) Direct-On-Line | $T_{st}$ (Reference) |
| (ii) Star-Delta | $T_{st} / 3$ |
| (iii) Autotransformer at 60% tap | $(0.6)^2 \cdot T_{st} = 0.36\, T_{st}$ |

**Reasoning:** For Star-Delta, voltage is reduced to $1/\sqrt{3}$, so torque is reduced to $(1/\sqrt{3})^2 = 1/3$. For autotransformer at $x = 0.6$, voltage is $0.6 V$, so torque scales as $x^2 = 0.36$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks - Internal Choice)

#### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023 (Model)]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat connection diagram, explain the working of a **Star-Delta starter** for a 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor. Derive the relationship between the starting current and starting torque in star mode to that of direct-on-line starting.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole squirrel-cage induction motor has a full-load slip of 4% and a DOL starting current of 6 times the full-load current. If started using a star-delta starter, calculate: (i) Starting current in star mode (line value), (ii) Starting torque as a percentage of full-load torque, (iii) Synchronous speed, and (iv) Full-load speed.

##### Model Solution for Q.A

**(a) Model Solution [7 marks]:**

**Working of Star-Delta Starter:**

A Star-Delta starter consists of three contactors — main contactor (K1), star contactor (K2), and delta contactor (K3) — along with a timer relay and a thermal overload relay. The operation proceeds in three stages:

1. **Initial Closing (Star Mode):** When the START button is pressed, K1 and K2 are energized. The motor terminals are connected such that the three stator windings are shorted together at one end (neutral point), forming a **star (Y)** connection. The motor receives a per-phase voltage of $V_L/\sqrt{3}$.

2. **Acceleration:** The motor accelerates from standstill toward synchronous speed. Since the phase voltage is reduced to $1/\sqrt{3}$, the starting current and torque both reduce to $1/3$ of DOL values.

3. **Transition to Delta (Full Voltage):** When the motor reaches approximately 75–80% of rated speed, the timer relay de-energizes K2 and energizes K3. This reconfigures the stator windings into a **delta (Δ)** connection, applying full line voltage $V_L$ to each phase.

**Derivation of Starting Current and Torque Ratio:**

The starting current per phase in DOL (Delta) mode is:

$$I_{ph,\Delta} = \dfrac{V_L}{Z_{ph}}$$

The line current in DOL is:

$$I_{L,DOL} = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{ph,\Delta} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L}{Z_{ph}}$$

In star mode, the phase voltage is $V_L/\sqrt{3}$ and the phase current is:

$$I_{ph,Y} = \dfrac{V_L/\sqrt{3}}{Z_{ph}} = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_{ph}}$$

In star, line current = phase current:

$$I_{L,Y} = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_{ph}}$$

Taking the ratio:

$$\dfrac{I_{L,Y}}{I_{L,DOL}} = \dfrac{1/(\sqrt{3})}{(\sqrt{3})} = \dfrac{1}{3}$$

$$\therefore \boxed{I_{st,Y} = \dfrac{I_{st,DOL}}{3}}$$

Since starting torque is proportional to the square of the per-phase voltage:

$$\dfrac{T_{st,Y}}{T_{st,DOL}} = \left(\dfrac{V_L/\sqrt{3}}{V_L}\right)^2 = \dfrac{1}{3}$$

$$\therefore \boxed{T_{st,Y} = \dfrac{T_{st,DOL}}{3}}$$

**(b) Model Solution [7 marks]:**

**Given Data:**
- $V_L = 400\text{ V}$, $f = 50\text{ Hz}$, $P = 4\text{ poles}$
- $s_{FL} = 0.04$ (4% full-load slip)
- DOL starting current = $6 \times I_{FL}$

**(i) Starting Current in Star Mode:**

$$I_{st,Y} = \dfrac{I_{st,DOL}}{3} = \dfrac{6 \cdot I_{FL}}{3} = 2 \cdot I_{FL}$$

**Answer:** Starting current in star = $\mathbf{2 \cdot I_{FL}}$

**(ii) Starting Torque as % of Full-Load Torque:**

The starting torque in star mode is $1/3$ of DOL starting torque. The DOL starting torque in a standard induction motor is typically $1.5 \times T_{FL}$ to $2 \times T_{FL}$. Using the typical ratio:

$$T_{st,Y} = \dfrac{T_{st,DOL}}{3} \approx \dfrac{1.6 \cdot T_{FL}}{3} \approx 0.533 \cdot T_{FL}$$

**Answer:** Starting torque $\approx$ **53.3% of $T_{FL}$** (when DOL starting torque is assumed to be $1.6 T_{FL}$).

**(iii) Synchronous Speed:**

$$N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P} = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500\text{ RPM}$$

**Answer:** $N_s = \mathbf{1500\ RPM}$

**(iv) Full-Load Speed:**

$$N_r = N_s (1 - s) = 1500 \times (1 - 0.04) = 1500 \times 0.96 = 1440\text{ RPM}$$

**Answer:** $N_r = \mathbf{1440\ RPM}$

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 (Model)]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a suitable diagram, explain the operation of a **3-point autotransformer starter** for a 3-phase induction motor. Derive expressions for the starting current drawn from the supply and the starting torque in terms of DOL values and the transformer tap ratio $x$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has a full-load speed of 940 RPM. When started with a 70% tap autotransformer, the starting current drawn from the supply is 35 A. Determine: (i) Full-load slip, (ii) Starting current if started DOL directly, (iii) Starting torque as a ratio of DOL starting torque, and (iv) Synchronous speed.

##### Model Solution for Q.B

**(a) Model Solution [7 marks]:**

**Autotransformer Starter Operation:**

A 3-phase autotransformer starter consists of a **3-phase autotransformer** with multiple taps (typically 50%, 65%, 80%), a changeover switch, and protective devices.

**Operating Principle:**
1. When the START button is pressed, the changeover switch connects the motor stator terminals to the selected tap of the autotransformer.
2. The motor receives a reduced voltage of $x V_L$ (where $x$ is the tap ratio, e.g., 0.5, 0.65, 0.8).
3. The motor accelerates at reduced voltage and reduced torque.
4. After the motor reaches a sufficient speed, the changeover switch disconnects the autotransformer and connects the motor directly to the full supply voltage.
5. Two configurations exist:
   - **Open-Circuit (Korndorfer):** Motor is briefly disconnected during transition.
   - **Closed-Circuit:** Motor remains connected through a transition resistor, eliminating surge.

**Derivation of Starting Current and Torque:**

The starting current drawn by the motor (on the secondary of the autotransformer) is:

$$I_{motor} = \dfrac{x V_L}{Z_{ph}}$$

For an ideal autotransformer with turn ratio $1:x$:

$$I_{line} \cdot 1 = I_{motor} \cdot x \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_{line} = x \cdot I_{motor}$$

Substituting:

$$I_{line} = x \cdot \dfrac{x V_L}{Z_{ph}} = \dfrac{x^2 V_L}{Z_{ph}}$$

The DOL starting current is:

$$I_{st,DOL} = \dfrac{V_L}{Z_{ph}}$$

Therefore:

$$\dfrac{I_{line}}{I_{st,DOL}} = x^2$$

$$\boxed{I_{st,auto} = x^2 \cdot I_{st,DOL}}$$

Since starting torque $\propto V^2$:

$$\boxed{T_{st,auto} = x^2 \cdot T_{st,DOL}}$$

**(b) Model Solution [7 marks]:**

**Given Data:**
- $P = 6$ poles, $f = 50\text{ Hz}$, $N_r = 940\text{ RPM}$, $x = 0.7$, $I_{st,auto} = 35\text{ A}$

**(i) Full-Load Slip:**

Synchronous speed:

$$N_s = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000\text{ RPM}$$

Slip at full load:

$$s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \dfrac{1000 - 940}{1000} = \dfrac{60}{1000} = 0.06$$

**Answer:** $s_{FL} = \mathbf{0.06}$ or **6%**

**(ii) Starting Current if Started DOL:**

Using the autotransformer starting current relation:

$$I_{st,auto} = x^2 \cdot I_{st,DOL}$$
