$$35 = (0.7)^2 \cdot I_{st,DOL} = 0.49 \cdot I_{st,DOL}$$

$$I_{st,DOL} = \dfrac{35}{0.49} = 71.43\text{ A}$$

**Answer:** $I_{st,DOL} \approx \mathbf{71.43\ A}$

**(iii) Starting Torque Ratio:**

$$\dfrac{T_{st,auto}}{T_{st,DOL}} = x^2 = (0.7)^2 = 0.49$$

**Answer:** $T_{st,auto} = \mathbf{0.49 \cdot T_{st,DOL}}$ or **49% of DOL starting torque**

**(iv) Synchronous Speed:**

$$N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P} = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000\text{ RPM}$$

**Answer:** $N_s = \mathbf{1000\ RPM}$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up "line current" and "phase current"** in Star-Delta derivations. In Star, line = phase. In Delta, line = $\sqrt{3} \times$ phase. Many students forget this and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the $x^2$ factor** in autotransformer problems. Students often write $I_{st,auto} = x \cdot I_{st,DOL}$, missing that the current is transformed by the autotransformer as well.
> 3. **Assuming DOL starting torque equals full-load torque.** They are DIFFERENT. DOL starting torque is typically $1.5$ to $2.5 \times T_{FL}$, not equal to $T_{FL}$.
> 4. **Not specifying that star-delta is for Delta-rated motors only.** A motor rated 415 V/Star cannot be started with a star-delta starter.
> 5. **Confusing Rotor Resistance starter with Rotor Resistance starter — students often think it INCREASES starting current, when in fact it DECREASES starting current while INCREASING starting torque.** This unique advantage must be clearly stated.
> 6. **Unit mistakes** in the formula $N_s = 120f/P$. Students sometimes write the answer in rad/s or forget to convert units.
> 7. **No diagram or wrong diagram** in 7-mark questions. KTU examiners explicitly allocate 1–2 marks for neat, labeled diagrams.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **⚡ ULTIMATE REVISION CHEAT SHEET — Starting Methods of 3-Phase IM**

- ✅ **Need for Starter:** At $s = 1$, rotor induced EMF is maximum → rotor current is 5–7 times → stator draws high current → voltage dip and heating.
- ✅ **DOL Starter:** Simplest, cheapest, full voltage, full torque, full current. Used for $\leq 5\text{–}7.5\text{ kW}$ motors.
- ✅ **Star-Delta:** Reduces $V_{ph}$ to $1/\sqrt{3}$, current to $1/3$, torque to $1/3$. Needs delta-rated motor. Open transition causes a surge.
- ✅ **Autotransformer:** Both current and torque scale as $x^2$ where $x$ is the tap ratio. Common taps: 50%, 65%, 80%.
- ✅ **Rotor Resistance:** ONLY method that REDUCES current AND INCREASES torque. Uses slip-ring IM. $R_{ext}$ can be chosen so that maximum torque occurs at $s = 1$.
- ✅ **Soft Starter:** SCR-based, stepless voltage control, no moving parts, smooth start/stop.
- ✅ **VFD:** Used when both voltage AND frequency control are needed (variable speed).
- ✅ **Key Formulas:**
  - $I_{st,Y} = I_{st,DOL} / 3$
  - $T_{st,Y} = T_{st,DOL} / 3$
  - $I_{st,auto} = x^2 \cdot I_{st,DOL}$
  - $T_{st,auto} = x^2 \cdot T_{st,DOL}$
  - $N_s = 120f / P$
  - $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$
- ✅ **Selection Rule of Thumb:** Centrifugal pump/fan → Star-Delta; High-inertia constant-torque load → Autotransformer or DOL; Crane/hoist → Slip-ring with rotor resistance; Modern variable-speed → VFD.
- ✅ **Korndorfer Connection:** Refers to closed-circuit autotransformer transition (with reactor/resistor) to avoid transition surge.
- ✅ **Motor must be Delta-rated** to use Star-Delta starter (i.e., nameplate must show Δ connection at line voltage).
- ✅ **Slip rings and brushes are a maintenance overhead** in slip-ring IM but are necessary for the highest-torque applications.
- ✅ **Number of poles** is always an EVEN integer; common values are 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12.
- ✅ **At synchronous speed**, $s = 0$, rotor current = 0, and no torque is developed (motor cannot run at exactly $N_s$).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT302 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES & DRIVES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Working Principle # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Working Principle of a DC Machine**: A Direct Current (DC) machine is a rotating electromechanical energy conversion device that operates on the fundamental principles of **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** and the **Lorentz Force Law on Current-Carrying Conductors**. It functions bidirectionally — as a **DC Generator** (mechanical energy → electrical energy) and as a **DC Motor** (electrical energy → mechanical energy) — based on the interaction between a stationary magnetic field and current-carrying armature conductors, with commutation achieved via a **commutator-brush assembly**. The machine consists of two principal magnetic systems: 1. **Stator (Field System)** — provides the main working flux. 2. **Rotor (Armature)** — hosts the conductors where EMF is induced (generator) or force is developed (motor). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Water Wheel with a Twist** Imagine a **water wheel** (representing the rotor) placed inside a river with a steady sideways current of water (representing the magnetic field). - If you **push the wheel** (mechanical input) and let it rotate against the water current, water gets deflected backward, and an equal & opposite reaction drives the wheel — this is **motor action in reverse** (i.e., **generator action**). - If instead, water is pumped onto the wheel blades (electrical input as current), the wheel turns and can lift a bucket (mechanical output) — this is **motor action**. The "twist" in a DC machine is the **commutator** — a mechanical rectifier/inverter — which ensures that the current in a given armature conductor always flows in a direction that produces unidirectional torque (motor) or unidirectional EMF (generator). ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Physical Constants Used in DC Machine Analysis:** > - **Magnetic flux density of air** ≈ $4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$ (permeability of free space, $\mu_0$) > - **Standard field flux range** in small DC machines: **5 mWb to 50 mWb** > - **Standard armature voltage ratings**: 220 V DC, 440 V DC > - **Armature reaction coefficient (typical)** = 0.6 to 0.7 (for compensation design) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Generator Action — Faraday's Law in Action The working principle of a **DC Generator** is rooted in the dynamic interaction between a conductor and a magnetic field: 1. The **field winding** (on the stator poles) is energized by a DC excitation current, establishing a **stationary, uniform magnetic flux $\Phi$** in the air gap. 2. The **armature winding** (on the rotor) is mechanically rotated by a **prime mover** (e.g., steam turbine, diesel engine) at angular velocity $\omega$ in rad/s or speed $N$ in rpm. 3. Each armature conductor **cuts the magnetic flux lines** continuously. 4. By **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, an EMF is induced in each conductor: $$e = B \cdot l \cdot v$$ where $B$ = flux density (T), $l$ = active length of conductor (m), $v$ = relative velocity (m/s). 5. **Fleming's Right-Hand Rule** (the "Generator Rule") determines the direction of induced EMF: - **Thumb** → Motion of conductor - **Forefinger** → Magnetic field direction - **Middle finger** → Induced EMF / Current direction 6. The **commutator** (a split-ring of copper segments) rectifies the alternating EMF induced in each coil into a **unidirectional (DC) output** at the brushes. ## 2.2 Motor Action — Lorentz Force in Action The working principle of a **DC Motor** relies on the **force experienced by a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field**: 1. The field winding establishes the same working flux $\Phi$ as before. 2. A **DC supply** is fed to the armature winding through the brushes and commutator. 3. The commutator ensures that the current direction in **every armature conductor under a given pole** remains **constant** (it reverses the connection just as the conductor crosses the brush axis). 4. By the **Lorentz Force Law**, each conductor experiences a tangential force: $$F = B \cdot I \cdot l$$ where $I$ = conductor current (A), $l$ = active length (m), $B$ = flux density (T). 5. The aggregate of these forces produces a **unidirectional torque** $T$ on the armature, causing it to rotate. 6. **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule** (the "Motor Rule") determines the direction of motion: - **Forefinger** → Magnetic field - **Middle finger** → Current - **Thumb** → Direction of force/motion ## 2.3 Why Two Operations in One Machine? > [!NOTE] > **Concept of Reversibility**: A DC machine is fundamentally **reversible**. The same physical construction supports both generator and motor operation, governed by the **energy conservation principle** (Lenz's Law). This reversibility is a direct consequence of the **reciprocity theorem of electromechanical energy conversion**. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | EMF per conductor | $e = B \cdot l \cdot v$ | Volts (V) | Instantaneous EMF | | 2 | EMF per parallel path (A) | $E = \dfrac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A}$ | V (Volts) | Fundamental EMF equation | | 3 | Armature current | $I_a = A \cdot I$ | A | $A$ = parallel paths | | 4 | Torque developed | $T_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a \cdot \left(\dfrac{P}{A}\right)$ | N·m | Developed (internal) torque | | 5 | Torque (simplified) | $T_a = 0.159 \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a \cdot \left(\dfrac{P}{A}\right)$ | N·m | Numerical constant $0.159 \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi}$ | | 6 | Power developed | $P_d = E \cdot I_a$ | W (Watts) | Internal electromagnetic power | | 7 | Mechanical power | $P_m = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | W | At shaft | | 8 | Per-phase speed (for wave) | $A = 2$ | — | Wave winding | | 9 | Per-phase speed (for lap) | $A = P$ | — | Lap winding | | 10 | Frequency of induced EMF | $f = \dfrac{P \cdot N}{120}$ | Hz | In any AC machine | **Boundary Conditions / Conventions:** - For **Lap winding**: $A = P$ (always). - For **Wave winding**: $A = 2$ (always). - EMF equation valid for **both generator and motor** — sign convention handled externally. - All formulas assume **sinusoidal flux distribution**; in practice, the average flux per pole is used. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Industrial Applications of DC Machines:** > - **DC Motors**: Electric vehicles (trolleys, forklifts), rolling mills, paper mills, cranes, hoists, machine tools, servo systems, traction. > - **DC Generators**: Arc welding (differentially compounded), battery charging, electroplating, excitation supply for synchronous machines (now mostly replaced by static exciters). > - **Modern relevance**: Although largely replaced by AC drives with VFDs, DC machines remain pedagogically vital and are used where **precise speed-torque control** is needed (e.g., robotics actuators, laboratory DC supplies). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the EMF Equation of a DC Generator ### Step 1 — Flux Cut Per Revolution Per Conductor Let: - $\Phi$ = flux per pole (Wb) - $P$ = number of poles - $Z$ = total number of armature conductors - $A$ = number of parallel paths in the armature - $N$ = speed of armature (rpm) In **one complete revolution**, each conductor cuts the flux from **all $P$ poles**. $$\text{Flux cut per conductor per revolution} = \Phi \cdot P \quad \text{(Weber)}$$ > **Logic**: As the armature rotates once, every conductor sweeps past $P$ north and $P$ south poles in alternation, so it cuts a total flux equal to $P \cdot \Phi$. ### Step 2 — Flux Cut Per Conductor Per Second The number of revolutions per second is $\dfrac{N}{60}$. $$\text{Flux cut per conductor per second} = \Phi \cdot P \cdot \frac{N}{60} \quad \text{(Wb/s)}$$ > **Logic**: We multiply by frequency of revolution to convert "per revolution" to "per second." ### Step 3 — Average EMF Induced in One Conductor By Faraday's Law, the average EMF per conductor (in Volts) is numerically equal to the rate of change of flux linkage (in Wb/s): $$e_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\Phi \cdot P \cdot N}{60} \quad \text{(Volts)}$$ > **Logic**: 1 Wb/s = 1 V, by definition of the Volt. ### Step 4 — Number of Conductors in Series Per Parallel Path The total number of conductors is $Z$, and they are distributed among $A$ parallel paths. Hence, the number of conductors **in series per path** is: $$\text{Conductors in series per path} = \frac{Z}{A}$$ > **Logic**: Conductors in the same parallel path add their EMFs in series. Since the total $Z$ is split into $A$ equal paths, each path has $Z/A$ conductors. ### Step 5 — Total EMF Generated (Armature EMF, $E_g$) The total EMF between the brushes is the EMF per conductor multiplied by the number of conductors in series per path: $$ \begin{aligned} E_g &= e_{\text{avg}} \times \left(\frac{Z}{A}\right) \\ E_g &= \frac{\Phi \cdot P \cdot N}{60} \cdot \frac{Z}{A} \\ E_g &= \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic**: This is the final, textbook-standard EMF equation. It is valid for both lap and wave windings by substituting $A = P$ or $A = 2$ respectively. **Final Form:** $$\boxed{E_g = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A} \text{ Volts}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Torque Equation of a DC Motor ### Step 1 — Work Done Per Revolution The power developed in the armature (gross electromagnetic power) is: $$P_d = E_b \cdot I_a \quad \text{(Watts)}$$ The work done in one revolution (at $N$ rpm, so 1 revolution takes $60/N$ seconds): $$W = P_d \cdot \left(\frac{60}{N}\right) = E_b \cdot I_a \cdot \left(\frac{60}{N}\right) \quad \text{(Joules)}$$ > **Logic**: Power × Time = Energy. ### Step 2 — Substitute $E_b$ From the EMF Equation Using the EMF equation $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A}$: $$ \begin{aligned} W &= \left(\frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A}\right) \cdot I_a \cdot \left(\frac{60}{N}\right) \\ W &= \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a \cdot P}{A} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic**: The $N$ in the numerator and the $60/N$ cancel out — torque becomes **speed-independent** in its basic form, which is why DC motors have such stable torque characteristics. ### Step 3 — Relate Work to Torque Work done per revolution equals torque $T$ times angular displacement per revolution ($2\pi$ radians): $$W = T \cdot 2\pi$$ ### Step 4 — Solve for Torque $T$ $$ \begin{aligned} T \cdot 2\pi &= \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a \cdot P}{A} \\ T &= \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a \cdot P}{A} \\ \boxed{T_a} &= \frac{0.159 \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a \cdot P}{A} \quad \text{N·m} \end{aligned} $$ where $0.159 \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi}$. > **Logic**: This is the **armature torque** (gross developed torque). Shaft torque = Armature torque − Friction & windage torque. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Key Distinctions | Aspect | DC Generator | DC Motor | |---|---|---| | Energy conversion | Mechanical → Electrical | Electrical → Mechanical | | Governing law | Faraday's Law | Lorentz Force Law | | Hand rule | Fleming's **Right** | Fleming's **Left** | | Induced EMF | $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ (driving) | $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ (back-EMF, opposing) | | Power equation | $E_g \cdot I_a = T \cdot \omega$ | $E_b \cdot I_a = T \cdot \omega$ | | Sign of EMF in KVL | $+E_g$ (source) | $-E_b$ (load/back) | | Key current relation | $I_L = I_a$ (shunt) | $I_a = I_L$ (shunt) | | Commutator role | Mechanical **rectifier** | Mechanical **inverter** | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — DC Machine Energy Conversion ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Prime Mover
Steam / Diesel Engine] -->|Mechanical Input T_m| B[DC Machine Rotor
Armature + Commutator] C[DC Supply Source] -->|Electrical Input V, I_a| B B -->|Working Flux Phi| D[Stator Field System
Field Windings + Poles] D -->|Magnetic Coupling Air Gap| B B -->|Mode 1: Generator Action| E[Faradays Law
EMF Induced E_g] B -->|Mode 2: Motor Action| F[Lorentz Force
Torque T_a Developed] E -->|DC Output to Load| G[Electrical Load
Resistive / Battery] F -->|Shaft Torque| H[Mechanical Load
Pump / Fan / Vehicle] I[Brush Assembly] -->|Sliding Contact| B J[Commutator Segments] -->|Rectify or Switch| B style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd style C fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style D fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a style B fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828 style E fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32 style F fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32 style G fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd style H fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 ``` ## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Energy Flow Pathways ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT[INPUT] MECH[Mechanical Energy
P_mech = T_m x omega] ELEC[Electrical Energy
P_elec = V x I_a] end subgraph DC_MACHINE[DC MACHINE CORE] ARM[Armature Conductors Z] FLD[Field Poles providing Phi] COM[Commutator Brush Assembly] ARM --- FLD ARM --- COM end subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT] OUT_ELEC[Electrical Output
E_g x I_a or P_out] OUT_MECH[Mechanical Output
T_a x omega or P_shaft] end subgraph LOSSES[LOSSES] L1[Copper Loss I^2 R] L2[Iron Loss Core Hysteresis Eddy] L3[Mechanical Loss Friction Windage] L4[Brush Contact Loss] end MECH --> DC_MACHINE ELEC --> DC_MACHINE DC_MACHINE --> OUT_ELEC DC_MACHINE --> OUT_MECH DC_MACHINE --> LOSSES style DC_MACHINE fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:3px style LOSSES fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c style INPUT fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style OUTPUT fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1 ``` ## 4.3 Schematic — Cross-Sectional View of a 2-Pole DC Machine ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph STATOR[STATOR STATIONARY PART] Y1[North Pole N - Main Flux Out] Y2[South Pole S - Main Flux In] Y1 --- Y2 FW[Field Windings Carry DC Excitation Current] Y1 -.- FW Y2 -.- FW end subgraph AIRGAP[AIR GAP] AG[Uniform Radial Magnetic Field B] end subgraph ROTOR[ROTOR ROTATING PART] SH[Steel Laminated Shaft] C1[Armature Conductor A - Top of Coil] C2[Armature Conductor B - Bottom of Coil] COIL[Single Turn Coil A connected to B] C1 --- COIL COIL --- C2 end subgraph COMM[COMMUTATOR ASSEMBLY] SEG1[Copper Segment 1 connected to A] SEG2[Copper Segment 2 connected to B] BR1[Positive Brush + on Segment 1] BR2[Negative Brush - on Segment 2] SEG1 --- BR1 SEG2 --- BR2 end STATOR --> AIRGAP AIRGAP --> ROTOR ROTOR --> COMM style STATOR fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0 style ROTOR fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#ad1457 style AIRGAP fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff8f00 style COMM fill:#e8eaf6,stroke:#283593 ``` ## 4.4 Process Flow — Direction of Induced EMF via Fleming's Right-Hand Rule ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Step 1 Identify Field Direction B - North to South] P2[Step 2 Identify Motion Direction v - Direction of Conductor] P3[Step 3 Apply Flemings Right Hand Rule] P4[Step 4 Thumb equals v] P5[Step 5 Forefinger equals B] P6[Step 6 Middle Finger gives Direction of Induced EMF] P7[Step 7 Result Induced EMF Drives Current through External Load] P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7 style P1 fill:#bbdefb style P7 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q: State and explain Faraday's Laws of Electromagnetic Induction. How do these laws govern the working of a DC generator?** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > **Faraday's First Law:** Whenever a conductor cuts magnetic flux (or equivalently, the flux linking a coil changes), an EMF is induced in the conductor. As long as the relative motion between the conductor and the magnetic field continues, the EMF persists. > > **Faraday's Second Law:** The magnitude of the induced EMF is equal to the rate of change of flux linkage. > > $$\text{Mathematical form: } e = -N\frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$ > > **Application to DC Generator:** When the armature of a DC generator is rotated by a prime mover, the conductors on the armature cut the magnetic flux produced by the field poles. By Faraday's first law, an EMF is induced in each conductor, and by the second law, its magnitude is proportional to the speed of rotation and the flux per pole. The commutator rectifies this alternating EMF to a unidirectional (DC) output. (3 marks) ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Q: Distinguish between the role of the commutator in a DC generator and a DC motor.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > | Aspect | DC Generator | DC Motor | > |---|---|---| > | Function | Acts as a **mechanical rectifier** | Acts as a **mechanical inverter / switch** | > | Output | Converts internally alternating EMF into unidirectional DC at the brushes | Reverses the direction of current in the armature coil as it passes from one pole region to another, ensuring **unidirectional torque** | > | Energy Flow | Mechanical → Electrical | Electrical → Mechanical | > > In both cases, the commutator physically reverses the brush-to-coil connection at the precise instant the coil sides cross the magnetic neutral axis, so the EMF (or current) in a given external circuit is always in the same direction. (3 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]** **(a) Derive the EMF equation of a DC generator. A 4-pole, wave-wound DC generator has 50 slots with 12 conductors per slot. The flux per pole is 25 mWb and the speed is 1000 rpm. Find the EMF generated. (7 marks)** > **Model Solution (7 marks):** > > **Derivation (3 marks):** > > 1. Flux cut per conductor per revolution = $\Phi \cdot P$ > 2. Flux cut per conductor per second = $\Phi \cdot P \cdot \dfrac{N}{60}$ > 3. EMF per conductor (by Faraday's law) = $\dfrac{\Phi P N}{60}$ V > 4. Conductors in series per parallel path = $\dfrac{Z}{A}$ > 5. Total EMF: > > $$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A} \text{ V}$$ > > **Numerical Calculation (4 marks):** > > Given: > - $P = 4$, Wave winding $\Rightarrow A = 2$ > - Total conductors: $Z = 50 \times 12 = 600$ > - $\Phi = 25 \text{ mWb} = 25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Wb}$ > - $N = 1000 \text{ rpm}$ > > $$ > \begin{aligned} > E_g &= \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A} \\ > &= \frac{(25 \times 10^{-3}) \times 600 \times 1000 \times 4}{60 \times 2} \\ > &= \frac{25 \times 10^{-3} \times 600 \times 1000 \times 4}{120} \\ > &= \frac{60{,}000}{120} \\ > \boxed{E_g &= 500 \text{ V}} > \end{aligned} > $$ **(b) Derive the torque equation of a DC motor. For the same machine used as a motor, the armature current drawn is 80 A. Calculate the torque developed and the gross mechanical power developed at the shaft. (7 marks)** > **Model Solution (7 marks):** > > **Derivation (3 marks):** > > 1. Power developed in armature: $P_d = E_b \cdot I_a$ > 2. Work done per revolution: $W = P_d \cdot \dfrac{60}{N} = E_b I_a \cdot \dfrac{60}{N}$ > 3. Substituting $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$: > > $$W = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A} \cdot I_a \cdot \frac{60}{N} = \frac{\Phi Z I_a P}{A}$$ > > 4. Equating to $T \cdot 2\pi$: > > $$T = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{\Phi Z I_a P}{A} = 0.159 \cdot \frac{\Phi Z I_a P}{A} \text{ N·m}$$ > > **Numerical Calculation (4 marks):** > > Given (same machine as generator above): > - $\Phi = 25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Wb}$, $Z = 600$, $P = 4$, $A = 2$ > - $I_a = 80 \text{ A}$, $N = 1000 \text{ rpm}$ > > $$ > \begin{aligned} > T_a &= 0.159 \cdot \frac{\Phi Z I_a P}{A} \\ > &= 0.159 \cdot \frac{(25 \times 10^{-3}) \times 600 \times 80 \times 4}{2} \\ > &= 0.159 \cdot \frac{4800}{2} \\ > &= 0.159 \times 2400 \\ > \boxed{T_a &\approx 381.6 \text{ N·m}} > \end{aligned} > $$ > > Gross mechanical power: > > $$ > \begin{aligned} > P_m &= \frac{2\pi N T_a}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1000 \times 381.6}{60} \\ > &= \frac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 1000 \times 381.6}{60} \\ > &\approx 40{,}000 \text{ W} \\ > \boxed{P_m &= 40 \text{ kW}} > \end{aligned} > $$ > > **Cross-check:** $P_d = E_b \cdot I_a = 500 \times 80 = 40{,}000 \text{ W} = 40 \text{ kW}$ ✓ ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper]** **(a) Explain the working principle of a DC machine as both a generator and a motor with the help of Faraday's law and Lorentz force. State Fleming's right-hand and left-hand rules. (7 marks)** > **Model Solution (7 marks):** > > **1. Working as a Generator — Faraday's Law (3 marks):** > > When the armature of a DC machine is driven by an external prime mover, the conductors on the rotor cut the stationary magnetic flux produced by the field winding. By Faraday's First Law of Electromagnetic Induction, an EMF is induced in each conductor. The magnitude of this EMF is given by Faraday's Second Law: > > $$e = B \cdot l \cdot v = \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$ > > The commutator segments reverse the connection to the external circuit such that the brushes always collect current of the same polarity, resulting in a DC output voltage $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$. > > **2. Working as a Motor — Lorentz Force (2 marks):** > > When a DC supply is connected to the armature terminals, current flows through the armature conductors placed in the magnetic field of the stator poles. By the Lorentz force law, each conductor experiences a force: > > $$F = B \cdot I \cdot l$$ > > The collective effect of these forces on all the conductors produces a unidirectional torque, rotating the armature. The commutator ensures the current in any conductor under a given pole is in a fixed direction, yielding continuous unidirectional torque. > > **3. Fleming's Rules (2 marks):** > > - **Fleming's Right-Hand Rule** (Generator): > - **Thumb** — Direction of motion of conductor > - **Forefinger** — Direction of magnetic field (N → S) > - **Middle finger** — Direction of induced EMF / current > > - **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule** (Motor): > - **Thumb** — Direction of force / motion of conductor > - **Forefinger** — Direction of magnetic field > - **Middle finger** — Direction of current in conductor **(b) A 6-pole, lap-wound DC shunt motor draws 40 A from a 230 V supply. The field current is 2 A, armature resistance is 0.4 Ω, and the flux per pole is 0.02 Wb. The armature has 720 conductors. Calculate: (i) the back-EMF, (ii) the speed of the motor, and (iii) the torque developed. (7 marks)** > **Model Solution (7 marks):** > > **Given:** > - $P = 6$, Lap winding $\Rightarrow A = P = 6$ > - Line voltage $V_L = 230 \text{ V}$ > - Line current $I_L = 40 \text{ A}$, Field current $I_{sh} = 2 \text{ A}$ > - $R_a = 0.4 \text{ }\Omega$, $\Phi = 0.02 \text{ Wb}$, $Z = 720$ > > **Step 1 — Armature current:** > > $$I_a = I_L - I_{sh} = 40 - 2 = 38 \text{ A}$$ > > **(i) Back-EMF (2 marks):** > > $$ > \begin{aligned} > E_b &= V - I_a R_a \\ > &= 230 - (38 \times 0.4) \\ > &= 230 - 15.2 \\ > \boxed{E_b &= 214.8 \text{ V}} > \end{aligned} > $$ > > **(ii) Speed (3 marks):** > > Using $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$: > > $$ > \begin{aligned} > N &= \frac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot P} \\ > &= \frac{214.8 \times 60 \times 6}{0.02 \times 720 \times 6} \\ > &= \frac{214.8 \times 360}{0.02 \times 4320} \\ > &= \frac{77{,}328}{86.4} \\ > \boxed{N &\approx 894.6 \text{ rpm}} > \end{aligned} > $$ > > **(iii) Torque developed (2 marks):** > > $$ > \begin{aligned} > T_a &= 0.159 \cdot \frac{\Phi Z I_a P}{A} \\ > &= 0.159 \cdot \frac{0.02 \times 720 \times 38 \times 6}{6} \\ > &= 0.159 \times 0.02 \times 720 \times 38 \\ > &= 0.159 \times 547.2 \\ > \boxed{T_a &\approx 87.0 \text{ N·m}} > \end{aligned} > $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting to subtract field current in shunt machines** ($I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$, not $I_L$). **Penalty: Up to 1 mark.** > 2. **Mixing up $A$ for lap vs wave**: Lap → $A = P$; Wave → $A = 2$. A wrong choice of $A$ inverts the answer completely. **Penalty: 1–2 marks.** > 3. **Sign convention for back-EMF**: In motor problems, $E_b$ is **subtracted** from supply voltage; in generator problems, $E_g$ is the source EMF. **Penalty: 1 mark.** > 4. **Unit conversion of flux**: $\Phi$ must be in **Weber** (not mWb) when substituted into the EMF equation. **Penalty: 1 mark.** > 5. **Forgetting the $\dfrac{1}{2\pi}$ factor** in the torque equation. **Penalty: 1 mark.** > 6. **Using $I_L$ instead of $I_a$ in the torque equation** for shunt motors. **Penalty: 1 mark.** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Working Principle of DC Machines** - ✅ **Two Fundamental Laws** govern DC machine operation: **Faraday's Law** (generator) and **Lorentz Force Law** (motor). - ✅ **Reversibility Principle**: The same physical machine works as both generator and motor. - ✅ **Commutator Role**: Acts as a mechanical rectifier (generator) and a frequency-converter/switch (motor). - ✅ **Fleming's Right-Hand Rule** = Generator; **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule** = Motor. - ✅ **EMF Equation** (universal for both): $E = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ V - ✅ **Torque Equation** (universal for both): $T = 0.159 \cdot \dfrac{\Phi Z I_a P}{A}$ N·m - ✅ **Parallel paths**: Lap winding → $A = P$; Wave winding → $A = 2$. - ✅ **Back-EMF in motor**: $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ (always less than supply voltage). - ✅ **Generated EMF in generator**: $E_g = V + I_a R_a$ (always greater than terminal voltage). - ✅ **Power developed**: $P_d = E_b I_a = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ (gross electromagnetic power). - ✅ **Armature reaction** demagnetizes the main field; it must be compensated using **interpoles** or **compensating windings**. - ✅ **Lenz's Law** is the energy-conservation aspect — back-EMF opposes the cause of motion. - ✅ For **shunt DC machines**, $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$ (motor) or $I_L = I_a + I_{sh}$ (generator). - ✅ **SI Units**: $\Phi$ in Wb, $Z$ dimensionless count, $N$ in rpm, $P$ dimensionless, $A$ dimensionless, $E$ in V, $T$ in N·m. - ✅ The **mechanical commutator** is the only moving electrical contact in a DC machine, which historically limited its reliability and maintenance — a major reason for the migration to AC drives in modern industry.

topics.title: Double Revolving Field Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT302 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES & DRIVES > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Double Revolving Field Theory # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Double Revolving Field Theory (DRFT)** states that an **alternating magnetic flux**, which is *stationary in space but varies sinusoidally in time*, can be mathematically resolved into **two equal and opposite rotating magnetic fields**, each having *half the magnitude* of the original pulsating flux and rotating at **synchronous speed** ($\omega_s$) in **opposite directions**. This theory, originally proposed by **M. Leblanc in 1899**, forms the analytical foundation for understanding the operating principle of **single-phase induction motors**, which would otherwise appear physically non-self-starting despite possessing a sinusoidal air-gap flux. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** This theory is a *direct prerequisite* for analyzing single-phase induction motors (split-phase, capacitor-start, shaded-pole) and forms the analytical basis for the *Cross-Field Theory* alternative explanation. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine you are holding a **glowing rod** that lights up brightly and dims rhythmically — like a *flashing lighthouse beam*, but the rod itself is not rotating; it only pulses in brightness. To a stationary observer, the brightness varies sinusoidally. Now, picture this: **two identical rods**, each glowing at **half the original brightness**, are placed on opposite ends of a rotating turntable, spinning in **opposite directions** at exactly the same speed. At any instant, when both rods align along the same axis (the original rod's direction), their combined brightness equals the peak of the original flashing rod. When they are perpendicular, they cancel out along the original axis direction. > [!NOTE] > **The Analogy in Plain English:** > - **Pulsating field** = A stationary flashlight whose intensity oscillates between zero and maximum. > - **Two revolving fields** = Two dimmer flashlights on opposite ends of a spinning carousel. > - **Net effect at the original axis** = Identical to the pulsating flashlight at every instant. The mechanical / electrical intuition is that a *time-varying field* and a *rotating field* are mathematically equivalent when decomposed properly — a powerful insight that converts a seemingly non-rotational phenomenon into a superposition of two clean rotations. --- ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Synchronous angular velocity:** $\omega_s = 2\pi f$ rad/s, where $f$ is the supply frequency (**50 Hz** in India per IS standards). - **Synchronous speed:** $N_s = \dfrac{120f}{P}$ RPM, where $P$ = number of poles. - **Peak air-gap flux:** $\Phi_m$ (in **Weber, Wb**). - **Each revolving component flux amplitude:** $\dfrac{\Phi_m}{2}$ (**half of the peak pulsating flux**). - **Standard supply frequency for KTU problem-solving:** **$f = 50 \text{ Hz}$**, unless explicitly stated otherwise. > [!TIP] > **Memory Anchor for Exam:** "Pulsating flux = Half Flux + Half Flux, rotating in opposite directions at synchronous speed." This one-liner alone can fetch 2–3 marks in a Part-A question. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Structured Logic Decomposition The Double Revolving Field Theory is built on the principle of **mathematical superposition** of two counter-rotating phasors. Let us break down the concept step-by-step: ### Step 1: Define the Pulsating Magnetic Field A single-phase winding energized by an AC source produces an alternating flux that is: - **Stationary in space** (aligned with the magnetic axis of the winding). - **Varying in time** sinusoidally. Mathematically: $$\phi(t) = \Phi_m \cos(\omega t)$$ where $\Phi_m$ is the **maximum (peak) flux** in Weber and $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency. ### Step 2: Decompose the Pulsating Field According to the theory, this pulsating flux can be expressed as the **vector sum of two rotating flux phasors**: $$\phi(t) = \phi_f(t) + \phi_b(t)$$ where: - $\phi_f$ = forward (positive sequence) revolving flux, rotating **counter-clockwise** at $\omega$. - $\phi_b$ = backward (negative sequence) revolving flux, rotating **clockwise** at $\omega$. ### Step 3: Express Each Revolving Flux The two fluxes are: $$\phi_f(t) = \frac{\Phi_m}{2} \cdot e^{j\omega t} = \frac{\Phi_m}{2} [\cos(\omega t) + j\sin(\omega t)]$$ $$\phi_b(t) = \frac{\Phi_m}{2} \cdot e^{-j\omega t} = \frac{\Phi_m}{2} [\cos(\omega t) - j\sin(\omega t)]$$ ### Step 4: Superposition Yields the Original Pulsating Flux Adding the two phasors, the imaginary parts (perpendicular components) **cancel**, while the real parts (along the winding axis) **add**, recovering the original pulsating flux. ### Step 5: Why This Matters in Engineering Each revolving component induces its own rotor EMF and torque: - At **standstill** ($N = 0$): Both components induce equal and **opposite torques** → net torque = **0** → motor **does not self-start**. - Once **rotated by external means** in one direction, **asymmetry arises**: one component (co-rotating) dominates, producing **net torque** in that direction. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** This is precisely why every single-phase induction motor requires an *auxiliary starting mechanism* (split-phase winding, capacitor, shaded pole, etc.) — the theory proves that a single pulsating field alone cannot produce a *self-starting* rotating torque. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units / Boundary | |---|---------|-------------|------------------| | 1 | $\phi(t) = \Phi_m \cos(\omega t)$ | Pulsating flux at any instant $t$ | Wb; $\omega = 2\pi f$ | | 2 | $\phi_f(t) = \dfrac{\Phi_m}{2} e^{j\omega t}$ | Forward (CCW) revolving flux phasor | Wb | | 3 | $\phi_b(t) = \dfrac{\Phi_m}{2} e^{-j\omega t}$ | Backward (CW) revolving flux phasor | Wb | | 4 | $\phi(t) = \phi_f(t) + \phi_b(t)$ | Superposition principle (real components add) | Wb | | 5 | $\Phi_f = \Phi_b = \dfrac{\Phi_m}{2}$ | Magnitude of each revolving component | Wb | | 6 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed of each revolving field | RPM | | 7 | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi f}{P/2} = \dfrac{4\pi f}{P}$ | Mechanical synchronous angular speed | rad/s | | 8 | $s_f = \dfrac{N_s - N}{N_s}$ | Slip w.r.t. forward field | dimensionless | | 9 | $s_b = \dfrac{N_s + N}{N_s} = 2 - s_f$ | Slip w.r.t. backward field | dimensionless | | 10 | $T_f \propto \dfrac{s_f \cdot R_r}{R_r^2 + (s_f X_r)^2}$ | Forward torque (favors rotation) | N·m | | 11 | $T_b \propto \dfrac{-s_b \cdot R_r}{R_r^2 + (s_b X_r)^2}$ | Backward torque (opposes rotation) | N·m | | 12 | $T_{net} = T_f - T_b$ | Net torque on rotor | N·m | > [!WARNING] > **Critical Pitfall:** At standstill, $N = 0$, so $s_f = s_b = 1$. Both torques are equal in magnitude but opposite in sign → **net torque is exactly zero**. This mathematically proves the *non-self-starting* nature of single-phase induction motors. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Single-Phase Induction Motor Design:** The theory directly informs the design of starting windings (capacitor-start, capacitor-run, shaded-pole). 2. **Symmetrical Component Analysis:** The decomposition into forward/backward components is the spatial-domain analog of *Fortescue's symmetrical components* used in unbalanced three-phase systems. 3. **Fault Analysis in Power Systems:** Single-phase-to-ground faults on three-phase systems use similar positive/negative sequence decomposition. 4. **Servo & Control Motors:** AC servomotors use DRFT-based analysis to predict torque-speed asymmetry. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Mathematical Derivation of the Double Revolving Field Theory ### **Step 1: Statement of the Pulsating Flux** Consider a single-phase AC winding placed along the **horizontal x-axis** (the magnetic axis of the coil). When energized by a sinusoidal supply $v(t) = V_m \cos(\omega t)$, the resulting air-gap flux is: $$\phi_x(t) = \Phi_m \cos(\omega t)$$ This flux is **stationary in space** — its spatial axis is fixed along the x-direction — but its **magnitude oscillates** sinusoidally with time. --- ### **Step 2: Trigonometric Identity Setup (Core of the Proof)** We apply the following *trigonometric identity*: $$\cos(\omega t) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2\cos(\omega t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[\cos(\omega t) + \cos(\omega t)\right]$$ We further recognize that $\cos(\omega t) = \cos(-\omega t)$ (since cosine is an *even function*). Therefore: $$\phi_x(t) = \Phi_m \cos(\omega t) = \frac{\Phi_m}{2}\cos(\omega t) + \frac{\Phi_m}{2}\cos(\omega t)$$ Substituting $\cos(\omega t) = \cos(-\omega t)$ into the second term: $$\phi_x(t) = \frac{\Phi_m}{2}\cos(\omega t) + \frac{\Phi_m}{2}\cos(-\omega t)$$ --- ### **Step 3: Introduction of Two Counter-Rotating Vectors** Define two space vectors: - **Forward vector** $\vec{\phi_f}$: A phasor of magnitude $\dfrac{\Phi_m}{2}$ rotating **counter-clockwise** at angular velocity $\omega$ rad/s. At time $t$, its angular position is $+\omega t$. - **Backward vector** $\vec{\phi_b}$: A phasor of magnitude $\dfrac{\Phi_m}{2}$ rotating **clockwise** at angular velocity $\omega$ rad/s. At time $t$, its angular position is $-\omega t$. The instantaneous projections of these vectors on the **x-axis** are: $$x\text{-component of } \vec{\phi_f} = \frac{\Phi_m}{2}\cos(+\omega t)$$ $$x\text{-component of } \vec{\phi_b} = \frac{\Phi_m}{2}\cos(-\omega t)$$ The instantaneous projections on the **y-axis** (perpendicular to winding axis) are: $$y\text{-component of } \vec{\phi_f} = \frac{\Phi_m}{2}\sin(+\omega t) = +\frac{\Phi_m}{2}\sin(\omega t)$$ $$y\text{-component of } \vec{\phi_b} = \frac{\Phi_m}{2}\sin(-\omega t) = -\frac{\Phi_m}{2}\sin(\omega t)$$ --- ### **Step 4: Superposition — Verification of the Theory** **Sum of x-components:** $$\phi_{x,\text{total}} = \frac{\Phi_m}{2}\cos(\omega t) + \frac{\Phi_m}{2}\cos(\omega t) = \Phi_m \cos(\omega t) = \phi_x(t) \quad \checkmark$$ **Sum of y-components:** $$\phi_{y,\text{total}} = +\frac{\Phi_m}{2}\sin(\omega t) - \frac{\Phi_m}{2}\sin(\omega t) = 0 \quad \checkmark$$ Thus, at every instant, the **vector sum** of the two counter-rotating phasors reproduces the *original pulsating flux* along the x-axis with **zero net perpendicular component**. --- ### **Step 5: Complex Exponential (Phasor) Formulation** A more compact representation uses Euler's identity $e^{j\theta} = \cos\theta + j\sin\theta$: $$\vec{\phi_f}(t) = \frac{\Phi_m}{2} e^{+j\omega t}$$ $$\vec{\phi_b}(t) = \frac{\Phi_m}{2} e^{-j\omega t}$$ Adding them: $$\vec{\phi_f}(t) + \vec{\phi_b}(t) = \frac{\Phi_m}{2}\left[e^{+j\omega t} + e^{-j\omega t}\right]$$ Applying Euler's formula: $$e^{j\omega t} + e^{-j\omega t} = 2\cos(\omega t)$$ Therefore: $$\vec{\phi_f}(t) + \vec{\phi_b}(t) = \frac{\Phi_m}{2} \cdot 2\cos(\omega t) = \Phi_m \cos(\omega t)$$ This is the **elegant phasor proof** of the Double Revolving Field Theory. --- ### **Step 6: Torque Derivation at Standstill (Crucial KTU Result)** The rotor experiences two torques due to the two revolving fields: $$T_f = k \cdot \frac{s_f \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + (s_f X_2)^2} \quad \text{(forward, driving)}$$ $$T_b = -k \cdot \frac{s_b \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + (s_b X_2)^2} \quad \text{(backward, opposing)}$$ At **standstill** ($N = 0$): $$s_f = \frac{N_s - 0}{N_s} = 1, \quad s_b = \frac{N_s + 0}{N_s} = 1$$ Therefore: $$T_f = k \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2} \quad \text{and} \quad T_b = -k \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}$$ **Net torque at standstill:** $$T_{\text{net}} = T_f + T_b = k \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2} - k \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2} = 0$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the most critical KTU result:** The mathematically rigorous proof that a single-phase induction motor has **zero starting torque** and is therefore **not self-starting**. Every Part-A or Part-B question on DRFT must explicitly state this conclusion. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Slip and Torque Behavior at Various Speeds | Rotor Speed $N$ | Slip (Forward) $s_f$ | Slip (Backward) $s_b$ | Forward Torque $T_f$ | Backward Torque $\vert T_b \vert$ | Net Torque $T_{\text{net}}$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | $0$ (standstill) | $1$ | $1$ | $+$ medium | $-$ medium | **0** (no start) | | $0 < N < N_s$ (running) | $0 < s_f < 1$ | $1 < s_b < 2$ | $+$ large | $-$ small | **Positive** (drives motor) | | $N = N_s$ (synchronous) | $0$ | $2$ | $0$ | $-$ very small | **Negative** (drag) | | $N > N_s$ (overspeed) | $s_f < 0$ | $s_b > 2$ | $-$ | $-$ | **Negative** (acts as brake) | > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** Draw this table for any "explain DRFT" question — it visually proves why the motor accelerates in the direction of an external push. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Concept Flow Diagram — Decomposition of Pulsating Flux ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Single-Phase AC Supply
v(t) = Vm cos omega t] --> B[Single-Phase Winding
placed along x-axis] B --> C[Pulsating Air-Gap Flux
phi t = Phi m cos omega t] C --> D{Apply DRFT
Mathematical Resolution} D --> E[Forward Revolving Flux
phi f = Phi m by 2
rotating CCW at omega] D --> F[Backward Revolving Flux
phi b = Phi m by 2
rotating CW at omega] E --> G[Induces Forward EMF
in Rotor Conductors] F --> H[Induces Backward EMF
in Rotor Conductors] G --> I[Produces Forward Torque T_f
drives rotor in CCW direction] H --> J[Produces Backward Torque T_b
opposes rotor motion] I --> K[Net Torque T_net = T_f minus T_b] J --> K K --> L{Is T_net zero?} L -- Yes at standstill --> M[Motor is NOT
self-starting] L -- No when rotated externally --> N[Motor accelerates
in pushed direction] ``` ## 4.2 Phasor Rotation Sequence Diagram ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Time as Time Axis t participant FW as Forward Vector phi_f participant BW as Backward Vector phi_b participant Result as Resultant phi_net Time->>FW: Rotate CCW by angle omega t Time->>BW: Rotate CW by angle omega t FW->>Result: Project onto x-axis = Phi m by 2 cos omega t BW->>Result: Project onto x-axis = Phi m by 2 cos omega t Result->>Result: Sum along x = Phi m cos omega t Result->>Result: Sum along y = 0 Cancellation ``` ## 4.3 Torque-Slip Characteristic (Conceptual Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph Standstill_Region[At N = 0] S1[Slip s_f = 1] --> T1[Forward Torque T_f] S2[Slip s_b = 1] --> T2[Backward Torque T_b] T1 --> N1[Net Torque = 0] T2 --> N1 end subgraph Running_Region[At 0 less than N less than N_s] R1[Slip s_f between 0 and 1] --> R3[Forward Torque T_f increases] R2[Slip s_b between 1 and 2] --> R4[Backward Torque T_b decreases] R3 --> R5[Net Torque positive] R4 --> R5 end ``` ## 4.4 Physical Coordinate Representation ``` +Y Axis | | phi_f (CCW, rotating up) | / | / angle = +omega t | / _____________|/_____________ +X Axis |\ | \ | \ angle = -omega t | \ | phi_b (CW, rotating down) | -Y Axis Each phasor has magnitude Phi_m / 2 Resultant along X-axis: Phi_m * cos(omega t) [pulsating] Resultant along Y-axis: 0 [cancellation] ``` > [!NOTE] > The diagram above illustrates the **geometrical meaning** of DRFT: two equal-length phasors rotating in opposite directions have a *resultant that always lies along the x-axis*, oscillating in magnitude sinusoidally — exactly mimicking the original pulsating flux. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **State and explain the Double Revolving Field Theory.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The Double Revolving Field Theory states that an **alternating magnetic flux** which is stationary in space but varies sinusoidally with time can be **resolved into two equal rotating magnetic fields** of *half the peak magnitude*, rotating in **opposite directions** at **synchronous speed** $\omega_s = 2\pi f$ rad/s. Mathematically, if the pulsating flux is: $$\phi(t) = \Phi_m \cos(\omega t)$$ then it can be expressed as the superposition of two counter-rotating phasors: $$\phi(t) = \frac{\Phi_m}{2} e^{j\omega t} + \frac{\Phi_m}{2} e^{-j\omega t}$$ Each component has **magnitude $\dfrac{\Phi_m}{2}$** and rotates at **synchronous speed**. This theory explains why single-phase induction motors are **not self-starting** — at standstill, both components produce equal and opposite torques, resulting in **zero net starting torque**. --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Why is a single-phase induction motor not self-starting? Justify using the Double Revolving Field Theory.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A single-phase induction motor is not self-starting because the single-phase winding produces only a **pulsating flux** (not a rotating flux). According to the Double Revolving Field Theory, this pulsating flux can be resolved into **two equal revolving fluxes** rotating in *opposite directions* at synchronous speed. At standstill, the rotor speed $N = 0$, which means: - Slip with respect to forward field: $s_f = 1$ - Slip with respect to backward field: $s_b = 1$ Since both slips are equal, the **forward torque** $T_f$ and **backward torque** $T_b$ are equal in magnitude but opposite in sign. Hence: $$T_{\text{net}} = T_f - T_b = 0$$ The zero net torque at standstill confirms the motor cannot start on its own and requires an **auxiliary starting mechanism** (capacitor, split-phase winding, etc.). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **(a)** Derive the expression for an alternating flux in terms of two counter-rotating flux vectors using the Double Revolving Field Theory. Explain how each component has magnitude equal to half the peak flux. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A single-phase induction motor has the following parameters: $f = 50$ Hz, $P = 4$ poles, rotor resistance $R_2 = 0.5 \;\Omega$, standstill rotor reactance $X_2 = 1.0 \;\Omega$. Calculate: **(i)** Synchronous speed, **(ii)** Slip with respect to forward and backward fields at a rotor speed of 1400 RPM, **(iii)** Forward and backward torques in per-unit form. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A:** ### Part (a) — Derivation **Step 1:** Consider a single-phase winding placed along the x-axis, carrying current $i(t) = I_m \cos(\omega t)$. The resulting air-gap flux is: $$\phi(t) = \Phi_m \cos(\omega t)$$ **Step 2:** Apply the Euler identity $2\cos(\omega t) = e^{j\omega t} + e^{-j\omega t}$: $$\phi(t) = \frac{\Phi_m}{2}\left[e^{j\omega t} + e^{-j\omega t}\right]$$ **Step 3:** Define: - $\vec{\phi_f}(t) = \dfrac{\Phi_m}{2} e^{j\omega t}$ — phasor rotating **counter-clockwise** at angular velocity $\omega$. - $\vec{\phi_b}(t) = \dfrac{\Phi_m}{2} e^{-j\omega t}$ — phasor rotating **clockwise** at angular velocity $\omega$. **Step 4:** Both phasors have magnitude $\dfrac{\Phi_m}{2}$, which is exactly **half the peak** of the original pulsating flux. **Step 5:** Projecting both vectors on the x-axis and adding gives back $\Phi_m \cos(\omega t)$; projections on the y-axis cancel. Hence the decomposition is mathematically exact. **Conclusion:** The alternating flux is equivalent to two equal, opposite, half-magnitude rotating fluxes. --- ### Part (b) — Numerical Solution **(i) Synchronous Speed:** $$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ RPM}$$ **(ii) Slips at $N = 1400$ RPM:** $$s_f = \frac{N_s - N}{N_s} = \frac{1500 - 1400}{1500} = \frac{100}{1500} = 0.0667$$ $$s_b = \frac{N_s + N}{N_s} = \frac{1500 + 1400}{1500} = \frac{2900}{1500} = 1.9333$$ **(iii) Forward and Backward Torques (Per-Unit Form):** The torque expression for an induction motor (referred to stator) is: $$T = \frac{k \cdot s \cdot R_2 / s}{R_2^2 / s^2 + X_2^2} \cdot \Phi^2$$ Equivalently, in simpler form: $$T \propto \frac{s \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}$$ **Forward torque (per-unit constant $k = 1$):** $$T_f = \frac{s_f \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + (s_f X_2)^2} = \frac{0.0667 \times 0.5}{(0.5)^2 + (0.0667 \times 1.0)^2}$$ $$T_f = \frac{0.03333}{0.25 + 0.00444} = \frac{0.03333}{0.25444} = 0.1310 \text{ p.u.}$$ **Backward torque (magnitude):** $$|T_b| = \frac{s_b \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + (s_b X_2)^2} = \frac{1.9333 \times 0.5}{(0.5)^2 + (1.9333 \times 1.0)^2}$$ $$|T_b| = \frac{0.9667}{0.25 + 3.7377} = \frac{0.9667}{3.9877} = 0.2424 \text{ p.u.}$$ **Net torque:** $$T_{\text{net}} = T_f - |T_b| = 0.1310 - 0.2424 = -0.1114 \text{ p.u.}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The negative net torque indicates the rotor would *decelerate*. For sustained acceleration, the rotor must be operating at a slip closer to the design point (typically $s_f \approx 0.05$–$0.1$) where $T_f$ dominates over $|T_b|$. --- ### **Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** Explain the Double Revolving Field Theory with the help of neat vector diagrams at four different instants: $\omega t = 0°$, $\omega t = 45°$, $\omega t = 90°$, and $\omega t = 135°$. Show that the resultant flux is always along the x-axis and varies sinusoidally. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive the expressions for forward and backward slips in a single-phase induction motor. Prove mathematically that the net starting torque is zero. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B:** ### Part (a) — Vector Diagram Analysis Let $\Phi_f$ and $\Phi_b$ each have magnitude $\dfrac{\Phi_m}{2}$ and rotate in opposite directions. | Instant $\omega t$ | $\Phi_f$ angle (CCW) | $\Phi_b$ angle (CW) | x-component sum | y-component sum | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | $0°$ | $0°$ | $0°$ | $\dfrac{\Phi_m}{2} + \dfrac{\Phi_m}{2} = \Phi_m$ | $0$ | | $45°$ | $+45°$ | $-45°$ | $\dfrac{\Phi_m}{2}(\cos 45° + \cos 45°) = \dfrac{\Phi_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ | $\dfrac{\Phi_m}{2}(\sin 45° - \sin 45°) = 0$ | | $90°$ | $+90°$ | $-90°$ | $\dfrac{\Phi_m}{2}(\cos 90° + \cos 90°) = 0$ | $0$ | | $135°$ | $+135°$ | $-135°$ | $\dfrac{\Phi_m}{2}(\cos 135° + \cos 135°) = -\dfrac{\Phi_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ | $0$ | The resultant **always lies along the x-axis** and its magnitude traces $\Phi_m \cos(\omega t)$, confirming the pulsating nature. ### Part (b) — Slip Derivations and Zero Starting Torque Let $N_s$ = synchronous speed, $N$ = actual rotor speed. **Forward slip:** Relative speed of rotor with respect to forward field = $N_s - N$. $$s_f = \frac{N_s - N}{N_s}$$ **Backward slip:** The backward field rotates at $-N_s$. Relative speed = $N_s - (-N) = N_s + N$. $$s_b = \frac{N_s + N}{N_s} = 1 + \frac{N}{N_s} = 2 - s_f$$ **At standstill** ($N = 0$): $$s_f = 1, \quad s_b = 1$$ Torque produced by each revolving field: $$T_f = k \cdot \frac{s_f R_2}{R_2^2 + (s_f X_2)^2}, \quad T_b = -k \cdot \frac{s_b R_2}{R_2^2 + (s_b X_2)^2}$$ Substituting $s_f = s_b = 1$: $$T_f = k \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}, \quad T_b = -k \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}$$ **Net torque:** $$T_{\text{net}} = T_f + T_b = k \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2} - k \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2} = 0 \quad \blacksquare$$ This rigorously proves that a single-phase induction motor has **zero starting torque**. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Read Carefully):** > > 1. **Forgetting to state "half the magnitude":** Many students write only that the two fields are equal and opposite but forget to specify that each has magnitude $\Phi_m / 2$. **Cost: 1 mark lost.** > > 2. **Missing the standstill slip equality:** When justifying non-self-starting, you MUST explicitly write $s_f = s_b = 1$ and equate the torques. A vague "they cancel out" answer gets only partial credit. > > 3. **Sign error in backward torque:** Backward torque is **negative** (opposes rotation). Forgetting the negative sign in $T_b$ will flip the sign of the net torque and lead to logically wrong conclusions. **Cost: 2 marks lost.** > > 4. **Mixing up rotating vs. pulsating:** The pulsating flux is *stationary in space*; only its *magnitude* varies. Students often wrongly say "the flux rotates" — that describes a *polyphase* field, not single-phase. > > 5. **Skipping the vector diagram:** A 7-mark derivation question without a vector/space diagram typically loses 2 marks in KTU valuation. > > 6. **Unit omission in numericals:** Always quote $N_s$ in RPM, $f$ in Hz, $\Phi$ in Wb. Missing units → up to 1 mark deduction. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Core Statement:** Pulsating flux $\equiv$ Two equal, opposite, half-magnitude rotating fluxes at synchronous speed. - **Mathematical Identity Used:** $\cos(\omega t) = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[e^{j\omega t} + e^{-j\omega t}\right]$. - **Magnitude of each revolving flux:** $\dfrac{\Phi_m}{2}$ (half of peak). - **Direction:** One CCW (forward), one CW (backward), both at $\omega_s = 2\pi f$ rad/s. - **Slip Relations:** $s_f = \dfrac{N_s - N}{N_s}$ and $s_b = \dfrac{N_s + N}{N_s} = 2 - s_f$. - **Standstill Condition:** $s_f = s_b = 1$ → Equal and opposite torques → Net starting torque = **0**. - **Conclusion:** Single-phase induction motor is **NOT self-starting** — requires auxiliary starting winding. - **Y-axis Cancellation:** Always verify that perpendicular components sum to zero; this is the geometric sanity check. - **Real Components Add, Imaginary Components Cancel:** Critical Euler-form insight. - **Real-World Link:** DRFT is the spatial-domain twin of *Symmetrical Components* in unbalanced three-phase systems. - **Standard Indian Frequency:** Use **$f = 50$ Hz** unless otherwise stated in KTU problems. - **Examiner-Friendly Additions:** Always include a vector diagram; always state magnitudes explicitly; always show the standstill slip equality. > [!TIP] > **One-Line Exam Punchline (Memorize):** > *"An alternating flux stationary in space is equivalent to two equal half-magnitude fluxes rotating at synchronous speed in opposite directions — making single-phase motors inherently non-self-starting."*

topics.title: Different Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT302 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES & DRIVES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Different Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to Different Types of DC Machines

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
A **DC Machine** is an electromechanical energy conversion device that operates on the principle of Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction. It can function either as a **DC Generator** (converting mechanical energy into DC electrical energy) or as a **DC Motor** (converting DC electrical energy into mechanical energy). DC Machines are broadly classified into different types based on the **method of field excitation** and the **interconnection between the armature and field windings**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** The classification of DC Machines is one of the **most frequently asked 3-mark and 14-mark questions** in KTU University Examinations. Mastery of field winding connections and their performance characteristics is mandatory for Module 2.

### 1.2 The Master Classification Tree

DC Machines are classified into the following major categories:

**A. Based on the Source of Field Excitation:**

1. **Separately Excited DC Machine** – Field winding is energized by an *external* independent DC source.
2. **Self-Excited DC Machine** – Field winding is energized by the machine's *own* armature output.
   - **Shunt Wound (Shunt Generator / Shunt Motor)**
   - **Series Wound (Series Generator / Series Motor)**
   - **Compound Wound**
     - **Cumulatively Compound** (Long Shunt & Short Shunt)
     - **Differentially Compound** (Long Shunt & Short Shunt)

**B. Based on Output Mode:**
- **DC Generator** (Producer)
- **DC Motor** (Consumer)

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Water Tap System**
> Imagine a water tap (field winding) controlling a tank (armature).
>
> - **Separately Excited:** A *separate pump* controls the tap — independent control. (Used in precision labs, Ward-Leonard systems.)
> - **Shunt Connection:** The tap is connected *in parallel* to the main pipe — full pressure always available, stable flow. (Used in constant-speed applications like lathes.)
> - **Series Connection:** The tap is *in series* with the main pipe — flow depends entirely on demand. (Used in traction, cranes — high starting torque.)
> - **Compound Connection:** A *combination* of both — like having two taps, one parallel and one series, giving the best of both worlds. (Used in rolling mills, elevators.)

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Flux per pole (Φ):** measured in **Weber (Wb)**
- **Number of poles (P):** always an **even integer** (2, 4, 6, 8…)
- **Speed (N):** measured in **RPM (Revolutions Per Minute)**
- **Number of parallel paths (A):**
  - **Wave winding:** A = **2** (always, regardless of poles)
  - **Lap winding:** A = **P** (equal to number of poles)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Each Type

#### **Type 1: Separately Excited DC Machine**

- **Construction:** The field winding is **physically separate** from the armature circuit. It is supplied by an independent external DC source (such as a battery or another DC generator called an *exciter*).
- **Current Relationship:** $I_a$ (armature current) and $I_f$ (field current) are **independent** of each other.
- **Characteristics:** Provides excellent **independent control** of flux and armature current. Used in **Ward-Leonard speed control systems**, servo motors, and laboratory experiments requiring wide speed ranges.
- **Disadvantage:** Requires an **additional DC source**, increasing cost and complexity.

#### **Type 2: Self-Excited Shunt DC Machine**

- **Construction:** The field winding has a **large number of turns of thin wire** with high resistance (typically 100–1000 Ω). It is connected in **parallel (shunt)** with the armature.
- **Current Relationship:**
$$I_L = I_a + I_f$$
- **Voltage Relationship:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ (motor) or $V = E_g - I_a R_a$ (generator)
- **Field Current:** $I_f = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}}$ — remains **approximately constant** since V is nearly constant.
- **Characteristics:** **Constant speed** operation. Poor starting torque. Used for **lathes, fans, centrifugal pumps, blowers, machine tools**.
- **Important Property:** Shunt field winding **cannot be opened** while the machine is running (dangerously high EMF due to residual magnetism collapse).

#### **Type 3: Self-Excited Series DC Machine**

- **Construction:** The field winding has a **few turns of thick wire** with very low resistance (typically less than 1 Ω). It is connected in **series** with the armature, so the entire armature current flows through it.
- **Current Relationship:** $I_L = I_a = I_{se}$ (series field current = armature current)
- **Flux Relationship:** Before magnetic saturation, $\Phi \propto I_a$
- **Voltage Relationship:** $V = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ (motor)
- **Characteristics:** **Very high starting torque** (proportional to $I_a^2$ before saturation). Cannot be started under **no-load** condition (will dangerously accelerate). Used for **traction systems, cranes, hoists, electric trains, trolleybuses**.
- **Important Property:** Series motor **must always be loaded**; a no-load run causes **runaway speed** → mechanical destruction.

#### **Type 4: Self-Excited Compound DC Machine**

A combination of both shunt and series field windings on the same pole core.

**Sub-type 4A: Cumulatively Compound**
- Series field **aids** the shunt field: $\Phi_{total} = \Phi_{sh} + \Phi_{se}$
- Series flux is in the **same direction** as shunt flux.
- Provides **high starting torque** (due to series field) AND **good speed regulation** (due to shunt field).
- **Applications:** Rolling mills, elevators, punches, shears, heavy industrial drives.

**Sub-type 4B: Differentially Compound**
- Series field **opposes** the shunt field: $\Phi_{total} = \Phi_{sh} - \Phi_{se}$
- Series flux is in the **opposite direction** to shunt flux.
- Rarely used as motor (poor starting torque, unstable).
- Used as **arc welding generators** (to maintain constant current) and as **boosters** in DC distribution systems.

**Long Shunt vs. Short Shunt Compound:**
- **Long Shunt:** Shunt field is connected across the **load + series field combination**.
- **Short Shunt:** Shunt field is connected across the **armature only**.

### 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Type | Real-World Application |
|------|------------------------|
| Separately Excited | Ward-Leonard drives, paper mills, rolling mills (precision) |
| Shunt Motor | Lathes, fans, centrifugal pumps, line shafts |
| Series Motor | Electric locomotives, trolleys, cranes, winches |
| Cumulatively Compound | Presses, elevators, rolling mills, punches |
| Differentially Compound | Arc welding generators, DC boosters |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Master Formula Reference Table**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi N Z P}{60 A}$ | EMF generated in a DC Generator | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi N Z P}{60 A}$ | Back EMF in a DC Motor | Volts (V) |
| 3 | $V = E_g - I_a R_a$ | Generator voltage equation (separately excited) | Volts |
| 4 | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Motor voltage equation (separately excited) | Volts |
| 5 | $V = E_g - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ | Series Generator voltage equation | Volts |
| 6 | $V = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ | Series Motor voltage equation | Volts |
| 7 | $I_L = I_a + I_f$ | Shunt machine current (Long Shunt compound) | Amperes |
| 8 | $I_f = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}}$ | Shunt field current | Amperes |
| 9 | $A = P$ (Lap winding) | Parallel paths in lap winding | — |
| 10 | $A = 2$ (Wave winding) | Parallel paths in wave winding | — |
| 11 | $P = \dfrac{60 f}{N}$ (AC machine) | Pole relation (reference) | — |
| 12 | $T_a = \dfrac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A}$ | Armature torque developed | N·m |
| 13 | $T_a \propto \Phi I_a$ | Torque proportionality (high-yield) | — |
| 14 | $N \propto \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{\Phi}$ | Speed proportionality (high-yield) | — |

**Critical Boundary Conditions:**
- Shunt field: $R_{sh}$ is **very large** (100–1000 Ω)
- Series field: $R_{se}$ is **very small** (< 1 Ω)
- Lap winding: $A = P$
- Wave winding: $A = 2$
- Shunt motor: **constant speed** characteristic
- Series motor: **variable speed** (inverse relation with load)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: EMF Equation of a DC Machine

The EMF equation is the **fundamental equation** for all DC machines. Every type — separately excited, shunt, series, compound — follows this base derivation.

#### **Step 1: Flux Cut by One Conductor Per Revolution**

Consider a conductor of length $L$ moving with velocity $v$ perpendicular to a magnetic flux density $B$.

The flux cut by the conductor per second is given by:

$$e = B \cdot L \cdot v$$

Where:
- $B$ = Flux density in Tesla ($T$)
- $L$ = Length of conductor in meters ($m$)
- $v$ = Velocity in m/s

#### **Step 2: Relating Velocity to Speed**

The linear velocity of the armature conductor at the periphery is:

$$v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$$

Where:
- $D$ = Diameter of the armature in meters ($m$)
- $N$ = Speed of the armature in RPM

> **Logic:** The circumference of the armature is $\pi D$. In one revolution, the conductor covers a distance equal to the circumference. In $N$ revolutions per minute, the distance covered per minute is $\pi D N$, and per second it is $\dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$.

#### **Step 3: Expressing Flux Density B**

The average flux density $B$ over one pole pitch is:

$$B = \dfrac{\Phi}{A_{pole}} = \dfrac{\Phi}{\pi D L / P}$$

> **Logic:** Total flux per pole $\Phi$ is distributed over the pole area. The pole area is the circumference under one pole ($\pi D / P$) multiplied by the axial length $L$.

Rearranging:

$$B = \dfrac{\Phi \cdot P}{\pi D L}$$

#### **Step 4: EMF Induced in One Conductor**

Substituting Step 2 and Step 3 into Step 1:

$$e = B \cdot L \cdot v = \left(\dfrac{\Phi \cdot P}{\pi D L}\right) \cdot L \cdot \left(\dfrac{\pi D N}{60}\right)$$

Simplifying by canceling $L$ and $\pi D$:

$$e = \dfrac{\Phi \cdot P \cdot N}{60}$$

#### **Step 5: Total EMF for All Conductors**

Total EMF is the sum of EMFs induced in all conductors connected in series. If there are $Z$ total conductors distributed across $A$ parallel paths, the number of conductors in **series per path** is:

$$\text{Conductors per path} = \dfrac{Z}{A}$$

Therefore, the **total EMF** generated is:

$$\boxed{E_g = \dfrac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A}}$$

> **Logic:** In each parallel path, the conductors are connected in series, so their EMFs add up. The total EMF is the EMF per conductor multiplied by the number of conductors in series per path.

#### **Step 6: Verification with Special Cases**

**For a Wave Wound Generator (A = 2):**

$$E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{120}$$

**For a Lap Wound Generator (A = P):**

$$E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N}{60}$$

> **Logic Check:** This shows why lap wound machines generate lower voltage but higher current (more parallel paths), while wave wound machines generate higher voltage but lower current.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Torque Equation of a DC Motor

#### **Step 1: Mechanical Power Developed**

The mechanical power developed in the armature is:

$$P_m = \omega \cdot T_a$$

Where:
- $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ is the angular velocity in rad/s
- $T_a$ is the armature torque in N·m

#### **Step 2: Electrical Power Converted**

The electrical equivalent of the mechanical power is:

$$P_m = E_b \cdot I_a$$

> **Logic:** The back EMF $E_b$ multiplied by the armature current gives the power actually converted from electrical to mechanical form (excluding copper losses).

#### **Step 3: Equating the Two Expressions**

$$\omega \cdot T_a = E_b \cdot I_a$$

$$\left(\dfrac{2\pi N}{60}\right) \cdot T_a = \left(\dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}\right) \cdot I_a$$

#### **Step 4: Canceling N and Rearranging**

$$\dfrac{2\pi T_a}{60} = \dfrac{\Phi Z I_a P}{60 A}$$

$$T_a = \dfrac{\Phi Z I_a P}{2 \pi A}$$

$$\boxed{T_a = 0.1591 \cdot \dfrac{\Phi Z I_a P}{A}}$$

> **Logic:** This is the fundamental torque equation. Note that $T_a \propto \Phi I_a$ — torque depends on flux and armature current. For a series motor, since $\Phi \propto I_a$, we get $T_a \propto I_a^2$ (very high starting torque).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of All DC Machine Types

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandatory Comparison Table — Frequently Asked in 14-mark Questions**

| Parameter | Separately Excited | Shunt | Series | Cumulative Compound | Differential Compound |
|-----------|-------------------|-------|--------|---------------------|------------------------|
| Field connection | External source | Parallel to armature | In series with armature | Both series + shunt (aiding) | Both series + shunt (opposing) |
| Flux variation | Independent control | Nearly constant | ∝ $I_a$ | Increases with load | Decreases with load |
| Speed regulation | Excellent (5–10%) | Good (10–15%) | Poor (poor) | Moderate | Poor (negative) |
| Starting torque | Moderate | Low (1.5–2× FL) | Very high (3–5× FL) | High (2.5–4× FL) | Very low |
| No-load safety | Safe | Safe | **Dangerous** (runaway) | Safe | Safe |
| Load capacity | Constant | Constant | Variable load | Variable load | Limited |
| Cost | Highest (extra source) | Moderate | Lowest | Moderate | Moderate |
| Typical use | Ward-Leonard, servo | Lathes, fans | Traction, cranes | Rolling mills, elevators | Welding generators |
| Speed vs Load | Nearly constant | Slight drop | Large drop | Moderate drop | May rise |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[DC Machine] --> nodeB{Source of Excitation}
    nodeB -->|External Source| nodeC[Separately Excited]
    nodeB -->|Self Excited| nodeD[Self Excited DC Machine]
    
    nodeD --> nodeE[Shunt Wound]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Series Wound]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Compound Wound]
    
    nodeG --> nodeH[Cumulatively Compound]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Differentially Compound]
    
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Long Shunt]
    nodeH --> nodeK[Short Shunt]
    
    nodeI --> nodeL[Long Shunt]
    nodeI --> nodeM[Short Shunt]
    
    nodeC --> nodeN[DC Motor Mode]
    nodeC --> nodeO[DC Generator Mode]
    
    nodeE --> nodeP[Constant Speed App]
    nodeF --> nodeQ[High Torque App]
    nodeH --> nodeR[Industrial Heavy Duty]
    nodeI --> nodeS[Welding Gen Use]
    
    style nodeA fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style nodeB fill:#7f6000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style nodeC fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style nodeD fill:#7f6000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style nodeE fill:#385723,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style nodeF fill:#a52a2a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style nodeG fill:#7f6000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style nodeH fill:#385723,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style nodeI fill:#a52a2a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style nodeN fill:#bf9000,color:#000000,stroke:#000000
    style nodeO fill:#bf9000,color:#000000,stroke:#000000
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Field Winding Connections

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Separate[Separately Excited Setup]
        nodeExtSource[External DC Source] --> nodeF1[Field Winding]
        nodeArmature1[Armature] --> nodeLoad1[Load]
    end
    
    subgraph ShuntConn[Shunt Wound Setup]
        nodeArmature2[Armature] --> nodeLoad2[Load]
        nodeArmature2 -.Parallel.-> nodeF2[Shunt Field]
    end
    
    subgraph SeriesConn[Series Wound Setup]
        nodeArmature3[Armature] --> nodeF3[Series Field]
        nodeF3 --> nodeLoad3[Load]
    end
```

### 4.3 Field Flux Interaction Diagram (Cumulative vs Differential)

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    nodeBranch -->|Same Direction| nodeCumFlux[Cumulatively Compound]
    nodeCumFlux --> nodeCumEq[Phi total = Phi sh PLUS Phi se]
    nodeCumEq --> nodeCumResult[Flux INCREASES with load]
    nodeCumResult --> nodeCumApp[Used in Motors for High Torque]
    
    nodeBranch -->|Opposite Direction| nodeDiffFlux[Differentially Compound]
    nodeDiffFlux --> nodeDiffEq[Phi total = Phi sh MINUS Phi se]
    nodeDiffEq --> nodeDiffResult[Flux DECREASES with load]
    nodeDiffResult --> nodeDiffApp[Used as Welding Generators]
```
