## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*List any three differences between a DC shunt motor and a DC series motor.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Sl. No. | DC Shunt Motor | DC Series Motor |
|---------|----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Field winding connected in **parallel** with armature | Field winding connected in **series** with armature |
| 2 | Field winding has **high resistance** (100–1000 Ω) | Field winding has **low resistance** (< 1 Ω) |
| 3 | Flux $\Phi$ is **nearly constant** | Flux $\Phi$ varies with load ($\Phi \propto I_a$) |
| 4 | Speed is **approximately constant** | Speed **varies** inversely with load |
| 5 | **Low starting torque** (1.5–2× FL) | **Very high starting torque** (3–5× FL) |
| 6 | Safe at **no-load** | **Dangerous** at no-load (runaway speed) |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*What is meant by cumulatively compounded DC machine? Mention one application.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In a **cumulatively compounded DC machine**, the series field winding and shunt field winding are connected such that their fluxes **add up** (are in the same direction). The total flux is:

$$\Phi_{total} = \Phi_{sh} + \Phi_{se}$$

This machine combines the advantages of both shunt (good speed regulation) and series (high starting torque) machines.

**Application:** Rolling mills, elevators, punches, and heavy industrial drives where high starting torque and reasonable speed regulation are simultaneously required. **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for flux relation, 1 mark for application)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 2]**

**(a)** With neat circuit diagrams, explain the construction and working principle of a **DC shunt motor** and a **DC series motor**. Compare their characteristics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **220 V DC shunt motor** has an armature resistance of $0.3 \, \Omega$ and a field resistance of $110 \, \Omega$. At no-load, the motor draws 6 A from the supply and runs at 1000 RPM. At full-load, the motor draws 50 A and the armature reaction weakens the flux by 4%. Calculate:
   - (i) The speed at full-load
   - (ii) The starting torque as a ratio of full-load torque **(7 marks)**

#### **Solution to Q3(a):**

**Construction of DC Shunt Motor:**

A DC shunt motor consists of:
- **Armature winding:** Placed on the rotor (rotating part), wound on a laminated iron core to reduce eddy current losses. The armature has slots to hold the conductors.
- **Field winding (Shunt):** Made of many turns of **thin gauge wire** with **high resistance (110 Ω typically)**, wound on the pole cores. It is connected in parallel with the armature.
- **Commutator:** A cylindrical assembly of copper segments mounted on the armature shaft. It converts the AC induced in the armature coils into DC at the brushes.
- **Brushes:** Made of carbon/ graphite, sliding on the commutator, providing the electrical connection to the rotating armature.

**Working Principle:**
When DC supply is given to the shunt field winding, flux $\Phi$ is established in the air gap. Simultaneously, current $I_a$ flows through the armature conductors. According to **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule**, a force $F = BIL$ acts on each conductor, producing a torque that rotates the armature.

**Construction of DC Series Motor:**

Similar construction, but the **field winding** consists of a **few turns of thick wire** with **very low resistance (< 1 Ω)**. It is connected in **series** with the armature, so the entire armature current flows through it.

**Working Principle:**
The series field flux is proportional to the armature current ($\Phi \propto I_a$ before saturation). The torque $T_a \propto \Phi I_a \propto I_a^2$, which gives very high starting torque.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Shunt Motor | Series Motor |
|-----------|-------------|--------------|
| Flux | Nearly constant | ∝ $I_a$ |
| Speed regulation | Good | Poor |
| Starting torque | Low | Very high |
| No-load safety | Safe | Dangerous |
| Load ability | Constant load | Variable load |
| Application | Lathes, fans | Traction, cranes |

**(Full 7 marks: 2 marks construction, 2 marks working, 3 marks comparison)**

---

#### **Solution to Q3(b):**

**Given Data:**
- $V = 220$ V
- $R_a = 0.3 \, \Omega$
- $R_{sh} = 110 \, \Omega$
- No-load: $I_{L0} = 6$ A, $N_0 = 1000$ RPM
- Full-load: $I_{L} = 50$ A
- Flux weakening at full-load: 4% (i.e., $\Phi_1 = 0.96 \, \Phi_0$)

**Step 1: Calculate shunt field current**

$$I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}} = \dfrac{220}{110} = 2 \, \text{A}$$

**Step 2: Calculate no-load armature current**

$$I_{a0} = I_{L0} - I_{sh} = 6 - 2 = 4 \, \text{A}$$

**Step 3: Calculate no-load back EMF**

$$E_{b0} = V - I_{a0} R_a = 220 - (4)(0.3) = 220 - 1.2 = 218.8 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 4: Calculate full-load armature current**

$$I_{a1} = I_L - I_{sh} = 50 - 2 = 48 \, \text{A}$$

**Step 5: Calculate full-load back EMF**

$$E_{b1} = V - I_{a1} R_a = 220 - (48)(0.3) = 220 - 14.4 = 205.6 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 6: Use EMF equation to find new speed**

From $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$:

$$\dfrac{E_{b1}}{E_{b0}} = \dfrac{\Phi_1 N_1}{\Phi_0 N_0}$$

$$N_1 = N_0 \cdot \dfrac{E_{b1}}{E_{b0}} \cdot \dfrac{\Phi_0}{\Phi_1}$$

$$N_1 = 1000 \cdot \dfrac{205.6}{218.8} \cdot \dfrac{1}{0.96}$$

$$N_1 = 1000 \cdot 0.9397 \cdot 1.0417$$

$$\boxed{N_1 \approx 979 \, \text{RPM}}$$

**Step 7: Calculate starting torque as a ratio of full-load torque**

At starting, $N = 0$, so $E_b = 0$.

Starting armature current:

$$I_{a,start} = \dfrac{V - E_b}{R_a} = \dfrac{220 - 0}{0.3} = 733.33 \, \text{A}$$

Starting torque $\propto \Phi_0 \cdot I_{a,start}$ (assume full flux at start):

Full-load torque $\propto \Phi_1 \cdot I_{a1}$

$$\dfrac{T_{start}}{T_{FL}} = \dfrac{\Phi_0 \cdot I_{a,start}}{\Phi_1 \cdot I_{a1}} = \dfrac{1 \cdot 733.33}{0.96 \cdot 48}$$

$$\dfrac{T_{start}}{T_{FL}} = \dfrac{733.33}{46.08} \approx 15.91$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{T_{start}}{T_{FL}} \approx 15.91 : 1}$$

**(Full 7 marks: 2 marks for Part (i), 5 marks for Part (ii))**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the **EMF equation** of a DC generator. Hence, define the **voltage regulation** of a DC generator. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **long-shunt compound DC generator** delivers a load current of 100 A at 250 V. The armature, series field, and shunt field resistances are $0.05 \, \Omega$, $0.03 \, \Omega$, and $100 \, \Omega$ respectively. Calculate:
   - (i) The generated EMF
   - (ii) The armature current
   - (iii) The total power generated **(7 marks)**

#### **Solution to Q4(a):**

**EMF Equation Derivation:**

Refer to the exhaustive derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes. The final equation is:

$$\boxed{E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}}$$

**Voltage Regulation Definition:**
Voltage regulation is the **change in terminal voltage** of a generator when the full load is removed, expressed as a **percentage of full-load voltage**, while keeping the speed and field excitation constant.

$$\text{Voltage Regulation (\%)} = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100$$

Where:
- $V_{NL}$ = No-load terminal voltage
- $V_{FL}$ = Full-load terminal voltage

**(Full 7 marks: 5 marks derivation, 2 marks definition)**

---

#### **Solution to Q4(b):**

**Given Data:**
- $I_L = 100$ A
- $V = 250$ V (terminal voltage)
- $R_a = 0.05 \, \Omega$
- $R_{se} = 0.03 \, \Omega$
- $R_{sh} = 100 \, \Omega$
- **Configuration: Long Shunt**

**Step 1: Identify the Long Shunt Circuit**

In a long shunt compound generator, the shunt field is connected across the **load + series field** combination. Therefore:

$$I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}}$$

**Step 2: Calculate shunt field current**

$$I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}} = \dfrac{250}{100} = 2.5 \, \text{A}$$

**Step 3: Calculate armature current (Part ii answer)**

In a long shunt configuration, $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$:

$$I_a = 100 + 2.5 = 102.5 \, \text{A}$$

$$\boxed{I_a = 102.5 \, \text{A}}$$

**Step 4: Calculate voltage drop across armature and series field**

$$E_g = V + I_a(R_a + R_{se})$$

$$E_g = 250 + (102.5)(0.05 + 0.03)$$

$$E_g = 250 + (102.5)(0.08)$$

$$E_g = 250 + 8.2$$

$$\boxed{E_g = 258.2 \, \text{V}}$$

**Step 5: Calculate total power generated (Part iii answer)**

The total power generated in the armature is:

$$P_g = E_g \cdot I_a = 258.2 \times 102.5$$

$$\boxed{P_g = 26465.5 \, \text{W} \approx 26.47 \, \text{kW}}$$

**(Full 7 marks: 2 marks for Part (i), 2 marks for Part (ii), 3 marks for Part (iii))**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout — Frequently Lost Marks**
>
> **Pitfall #1:** Students often confuse **Long Shunt** and **Short Shunt** compound generators. Remember:
> - **Long Shunt:** Shunt field is across the **LOAD terminals** → $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$
> - **Short Shunt:** Shunt field is across the **armature only** → $I_{sh} = (V + I_{se} R_{se}) / R_{sh}$
>
> **Pitfall #2:** Forgetting to **add** the shunt field current to the load current to get the armature current ($I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$) — this is a classic error worth **2–3 marks**.
>
> **Pitfall #3:** Mixing up **$E_g$** (generator EMF) with **$E_b$** (motor back EMF). The signs in the voltage equation are different:
> - Generator: $V = E_g - I_a R_a$
> - Motor: $V = E_b + I_a R_a$
>
> **Pitfall #4:** For the EMF equation, students often write $\Phi Z N / 60$ without including **A** (parallel paths). For **lap wound** machines, forgetting $A = P$ leads to a **wrong answer of 60% magnitude**.
>
> **Pitfall #5:** In series motor problems, students forget that the **field winding carries the full armature current** — this affects all torque and EMF calculations.
>
> **Pitfall #6:** Failing to mention the **armature reaction** effect on flux (when given in problems) — leads to a 1–2 mark deduction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 — Different Types of DC Machines: Quick-Fire Revision**

- [x] DC Machines are of **two main categories**: **Separately Excited** and **Self-Excited** (Shunt, Series, Compound).
- [x] **Shunt field winding:** High turns, thin wire, **high resistance** (100–1000 Ω), connected in **parallel**.
- [x] **Series field winding:** Few turns, thick wire, **low resistance** (< 1 Ω), connected in **series**.
- [x] **Lap winding:** $A = P$ (more current, less voltage); **Wave winding:** $A = 2$ (less current, more voltage).
- [x] **EMF Equation:** $E_g = E_b = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ — applicable to **all** DC machines.
- [x] **Torque Equation:** $T_a = \dfrac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A}$ or $T_a \propto \Phi \cdot I_a$.
- [x] **Speed Equation:** $N \propto \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{\Phi}$ — increases when flux decreases.
- [x] **Cumulatively Compound:** Series flux **aids** shunt flux — high starting torque, good speed regulation.
- [x] **Differentially Compound:** Series flux **opposes** shunt flux — used in **welding generators**.
- [x] **Long Shunt:** Shunt field across load; **Short Shunt:** Shunt field across armature.
- [x] **Series motor danger:** Never run on no-load (runaway speed → mechanical destruction).
- [x] **Shunt motor:** Flux is **nearly constant** → speed is **nearly constant** with load.
- [x] **Generator vs Motor:** $V = E_g - I_a R_a$ for generators; $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ for motors.
- [x] **Ward-Leonard system:** Uses separately excited DC motor for wide-range speed control.
- [x] **Application Mapping:** Shunt → Lathes, Fans; Series → Traction, Cranes; Compound → Rolling Mills.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Split Phase
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT302 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES & DRIVES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Split Phase

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Split-Phase Induction Motor (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A *Split-Phase Motor* is a single-phase AC induction motor that produces a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** at start by employing **two physically displaced stator windings** — a *Main (Running) Winding* and an *Auxiliary (Starting) Winding* — separated in space by **90 electrical degrees** and energized in *time-phase* by a deliberate **electrical phase shift** (typically **20° to 30°**) created through differences in the resistance-to-reactance (R/X) ratios of the two windings.

The auxiliary winding is automatically disconnected by a **centrifugal switch** once the rotor reaches approximately **70% to 80% of synchronous speed**, after which the motor continues to run solely on the main winding as a standard single-phase induction motor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the name "Split-Phase"?**
> The single-phase AC supply is effectively "split" into two phases internally — not through an external polyphase source, but by exploiting the **different time constants (L/R ratios)** of two parallel-connected windings. The current in one winding leads the other in time, mimicking a two-phase supply at the instant of starting.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Person Rowing a Single Boat" Intuition

Imagine a long boat floating on still water with two rowers sitting at the ends:

- **Rower A (Main Winding):** Strong, heavy, pulls the oar slowly — represents the highly **inductive** (low R, high X) main winding. Current lags voltage by a large angle.
- **Rower B (Auxiliary Winding):** Lighter, quick reflexes, pulls the oar faster — represents the highly **resistive** (high R, low X) starting winding. Current lags voltage by a small angle.

> When both pull together but **out of sync in time**, the boat begins to **rotate (oscillate)**, producing a turning effect. Once the boat gains momentum, **Rower B stops rowing** (centrifugal switch opens), and Rower A continues alone — the boat keeps spinning.

This "out-of-sync-but-coordinated" effort is exactly how the split-phase motor creates its **starting torque**.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value (KTU Reference) |
|---|---|
| Synchronous Speed ($N_s$) | 1500 / 3000 RPM (for 50 Hz, 4-pole / 2-pole) |
| Standard Supply Frequency ($f$) | **50 Hz** (India) |
| Phase Displacement between currents | **20° to 30°** |
| Starting Torque | **1.5 to 2 times** full-load torque |
| Auxiliary Winding Cut-out Speed | **70% – 80% of $N_s$** |
| Power Rating Range | 60 W to 250 W (fractional kW) |

---

### 1.4 Syllabus Mapping (KTU 2024 — CO Alignment)

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome Mapping:**
> - **CO2:** *Understand the construction, working principle, and performance characteristics of single-phase AC machines.*
> - **Blooms Level:** *L2 – Understand / L3 – Apply*
> - **Module Weightage:** Module 2 carries **20%** of internal evaluation marks for PCMRT302.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Architecture

A Split-Phase motor consists of:

**1. Stator Assembly**
- **Main Winding (M):** Distributed, low-resistance copper winding placed deep in the slots. High inductance → high reactance ($X_m$).
- **Auxiliary Winding (A):** Concentrated, high-resistance winding (often uses finer wire or added resistance wire). Placed at 90° electrical displacement from M.
- Both windings are connected **in parallel** across the single-phase AC supply.

**2. Rotor Assembly**
- **Squirrel-cage rotor** — identical to that of a three-phase induction motor.
- Made of laminated silicon steel with skewed, cast-aluminum conductor bars shorted by end rings.

**3. Starting Switch — Centrifugal Mechanism**
- Mounted on the rotor shaft inside the motor.
- **Closed** at standstill (both windings energized).
- **Opens** at ~75% of $N_s$ (auxiliary winding disconnected).

---

### 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Energization at Standstill:**
When the single-phase AC supply is switched ON, both the main winding (M) and auxiliary winding (A) are connected in parallel.

**Step 2 — Current Phase Displacement:**
Because the windings have drastically different R/X ratios:
- Current in main winding: $\bar{I}_m$ lags voltage $V$ by angle $\phi_m = \tan^{-1}(X_m / R_m)$ (large, ~70°)
- Current in auxiliary winding: $\bar{I}_a$ lags voltage $V$ by angle $\phi_a = \tan^{-1}(X_a / R_a)$ (small, ~30°)
- **Time-phase difference:** $\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a$ (ideally 90°, practically 20°–30°)

**Step 3 — Space Displacement:**
The two windings are physically separated by **90 electrical degrees** in space on the stator.

**Step 4 — Rotating Magnetic Field Generation:**
The combination of **space quadrature (90°)** and **time quadrature (α ≈ 25°)** produces a **non-uniform (elliptical) rotating magnetic field**, which generates a **net starting torque** to accelerate the rotor.

**Step 5 — Acceleration & Cut-off:**
As rotor speed rises, the induced back-EMF in the auxiliary winding decreases. At ~75% of $N_s$, the centrifugal switch opens, disconnecting the auxiliary winding. The motor continues to run as a single-phase induction motor based on **Double Revolving Field Theory**.

---

### 2.3 Double Revolving Field Theory — Conceptual Justification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A single-phase alternating flux $\phi(t) = \phi_m \cos(\omega t)$ can be **mathematically resolved** into two counter-rotating flux components of equal magnitude ($\phi_m / 2$):
> $$\phi_f = \frac{\phi_m}{2} \cos(\omega t - \theta) \quad \text{(forward component)}$$
> $$\phi_b = \frac{\phi_m}{2} \cos(\omega t + \theta) \quad \text{(backward component)}$$

At standstill ($s = 1$), these components induce equal EMFs in the rotor, producing **equal and opposite torques** → **net starting torque = 0**.

This is precisely why a plain single-phase motor **cannot self-start**, and why the **split-phase** trick is needed to generate the initial elliptical rotating field.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All essential formulas for KTU End Semester Examination on Split-Phase Motors:**

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ | Synchronous Speed | $N_s$ in RPM; $f$ in Hz; $P$ = poles |
| 2 | $\phi_m = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_m}{R_m}\right)$ | Impedance angle of Main Winding | $X_m, R_m$ in ohms; $\phi_m$ in rad |
| 3 | $\phi_a = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_a}{R_a}\right)$ | Impedance angle of Aux. Winding | $X_a, R_a$ in ohms; $\phi_a$ in rad |
| 4 | $\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a$ | Time-phase displacement between $\bar{I}_m$ and $\bar{I}_a$ | $\alpha$ in electrical degrees |
| 5 | $I_m = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{R_m^2 + X_m^2}}$ | Main winding current magnitude | Amperes |
| 6 | $I_a = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{R_a^2 + X_a^2}}$ | Auxiliary winding current magnitude | Amperes |
| 7 | $\phi_r = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_2 \, s}{R_2}\right)$ | Rotor power factor angle | Slip $s$ is dimensionless |
| 8 | $T \propto \dfrac{s \, E_2^2 \, R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}$ | Torque-slip relation (single-phase) | $E_2, R_2, X_2$ are rotor referred values |
| 9 | $T_{start} \approx (1.5 \text{ to } 2) \, T_{FL}$ | Typical starting torque multiple | Expressed in N·m |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Split-phase motors are the **workhorses of low-power domestic and light-industrial applications**, including:
- **Domestic:** Ceiling fans, table fans, washing machines, refrigerator compressors (older models).
- **Industrial (light-duty):** Blowers, small pumps, polishing machines, office equipment.
- **Commercial:** Vending machines, ATM cooling fans, copy machines.

Their popularity stems from the absence of heavy/inexpensive capacitors, simple construction, and reliability at low cost.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Phase Displacement Angle (α)

**Given:** A single-phase supply of voltage $V$ is applied across two parallel stator windings.

**Main winding** has impedance $\bar{Z}_m = R_m + jX_m$

**Auxiliary winding** has impedance $\bar{Z}_a = R_a + jX_a$

**Step 1 — Compute the current phasors:**

Using Ohm's law for each parallel branch:

$$\bar{I}_m = \frac{V \angle 0^\circ}{R_m + j X_m}$$

$$\bar{I}_a = \frac{V \angle 0^\circ}{R_a + j X_a}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to polar form:**

The magnitude and phase of the main winding current are:

$$\bar{I}_m = \frac{V}{\sqrt{R_m^2 + X_m^2}} \angle \phi_m$$

where the **impedance angle** of the main winding is:

$$\phi_m = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{X_m}{R_m}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Similarly for the auxiliary winding:**

$$\bar{I}_a = \frac{V}{\sqrt{R_a^2 + X_a^2}} \angle \phi_a$$

with:

$$\phi_a = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{X_a}{R_a}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Phase difference between the two currents:**

Since both currents lag the same supply voltage $V$, the **time-phase difference** between them is the difference of their impedance angles:

$$\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{X_m}{R_m}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{X_a}{R_a}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** For the split-phase motor, the main winding is *highly inductive* (low $R_m$, high $X_m$), so $\phi_m$ is large (≈ 70°). The auxiliary winding is *highly resistive* (high $R_a$, low $X_a$), so $\phi_a$ is small (≈ 30°). The resultant $\alpha \approx 40°$, but in practice it is engineered to be around **20°–30°** by careful winding design.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Starting Torque (From Double Revolving Field Theory)

For a single-phase induction motor, the **net electromagnetic torque** is the difference between the forward and backward torques:

$$T_{em} = T_f - T_b$$

**Step 1 — Forward component torque:**

The forward rotating field interacts with the rotor at slip $s$:

$$T_f = k \cdot \phi_f^2 \cdot \frac{(R_2 / s)}{(R_2 / s)^2 + X_2^2}$$

**Step 2 — Backward component torque:**

The backward rotating field sees a slip of $(2 - s)$:

$$T_b = k \cdot \phi_b^2 \cdot \frac{[R_2 / (2-s)]}{[R_2 / (2-s)]^2 + X_2^2}$$

**Step 3 — Since forward and backward flux magnitudes are equal in pure single-phase operation:**

$$\phi_f = \phi_b = \frac{\phi_m}{2}$$

Substituting:

$$T_{em} = k \left(\frac{\phi_m}{2}\right)^2 \left[\frac{R_2 / s}{(R_2 / s)^2 + X_2^2} - \frac{R_2 / (2-s)}{[R_2 / (2-s)]^2 + X_2^2}\right]$$

**Step 4 — At standstill ($s = 1$):**

The two bracketed terms become equal in magnitude but opposite in sign, giving:

$$T_{em}\big|_{s=1} = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This proves mathematically that **a pure single-phase motor has zero starting torque** — which is the fundamental reason the split-phase auxiliary winding is essential.

**Step 5 — In the split-phase motor, the auxiliary winding breaks this symmetry:**

By introducing a time-phase shift $\alpha$ between the two windings, the backward component of the air-gap flux is **suppressed**, converting the alternating field into an **elliptical rotating field**. The net torque at $s = 1$ becomes:

$$T_{start} \approx k' \cdot I_m \cdot I_a \cdot \sin(\alpha)$$

where $k'$ is a machine constant. This is the origin of the starting torque in a split-phase motor.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Winding Characteristics

| Parameter | Main (Running) Winding | Auxiliary (Starting) Winding |
|---|---|---|
| Wire Gauge | Thick (low resistance) | Thin (high resistance) |
| Resistance $R$ | Low | High |
| Reactance $X$ | High | Low |
| Impedance Angle $\phi$ | Large (≈ 70°) | Small (≈ 30°) |
| Current Magnitude | High (running current) | Moderate (only during start) |
| Operating Duration | Continuous | Transient (until ~75% $N_s$) |
| Disconnection | Never | Centrifugal switch |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Split-Phase Motor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Single Phase Supply V] --> B[Stator Winding Assembly]
    B --> C1[Main Winding M]
    B --> C2[Auxiliary Winding A]
    C1 --> D1[Low R High X Inductive Branch]
    C2 --> D2[High R Low X Resistive Branch]
    D1 --> E[Common Neutral Point N]
    D2 --> E
    D1 --> F[Rotating Elliptical Field]
    D2 --> F
    F --> G[Squirrel Cage Rotor]
    G --> H{Rotor Speed}
    H -- Below 75 percent Ns --> I[Centrifugal Switch CLOSED]
    H -- Above 75 percent Ns --> J[Centrifugal Switch OPEN]
    I --> K[Aux Winding Connected]
    J --> L[Aux Winding Disconnected]
    K --> M[Net Starting Torque Generated]
    L --> N[Running on Main Winding Only]
    M --> O[Mechanical Output Shaft]
    N --> O
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Phasor Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V[Supply Voltage V] --> IM[Im Vector I sub m]
    V --> IA[Im Vector I sub a]
    IM --> PM[Phase Angle phi sub m Large]
    IA --> PA[Phase Angle phi sub a Small]
    PM --> ALPHA[Time Phase Displacement alpha]
    PA --> ALPHA
    ALPHA --> RMF[Elliptical Rotating Field]
    RMF --> TORQUE[Starting Torque T sub start]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Flow — Operational Sequence (State Machine)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Standstill State s equal 1] --> S1[Energize M and A Windings]
    S1 --> S2[Compute Phase Displacement alpha]
    S2 --> S3[Generate Elliptical RMF]
    S3 --> S4[Rotor Accelerates]
    S4 --> S5{Speed Reached 75 percent Ns}
    S5 -- NO --> S4
    S5 -- YES --> S6[Centrifugal Switch Trips]
    S6 --> S7[Aux Winding Disconnected]
    S7 --> S8[Single Phase Running Mode]
    S8 --> S9[Steady State Operation]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Torque-Speed Characteristic Sketch (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Slip s Axis 0 to 1] --> B[Torque T Axis]
    B --> C1[Starting Torque Region s close to 1]
    B --> C2[Pull Up Torque Region]
    B --> C3[Max Torque Region s close to 0.2]
    B --> C4[Full Load Torque Region]
    B --> C5[Pull Out Torque Limit]
    C1 --> D[Split Phase Motor Curve]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Note:** The Mermaid blocks above represent the **functional architecture** of the split-phase system. They map the electrical → magnetic → mechanical energy flow stages and the state transitions governed by the centrifugal switch.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"Why is a single-phase induction motor not self-starting? Explain in brief."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A single-phase induction motor has only one stator winding, which produces a **purely alternating (pulsating) magnetic field** along a fixed axis. According to the **Double Revolving Field Theory**, this pulsating field can be resolved into two equal and opposite rotating magnetic fields, each of magnitude $\phi_m / 2$, rotating at synchronous speed $N_s$ in opposite directions.

At standstill, these two components induce equal EMFs in the rotor, producing **equal and opposite torques** ($T_f = T_b$). The net torque is:

$$T_{net} = T_f - T_b = 0$$

Hence, the motor **cannot develop any starting torque** on its own. This is the fundamental reason a single-phase motor needs an auxiliary mechanism (split-phase, capacitor-start, shaded-pole, etc.) for self-starting. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"What is the function of the centrifugal switch in a split-phase motor? At what speed does it operate?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **centrifugal switch** is mounted on the rotor shaft and serves to **automatically disconnect the auxiliary (starting) winding** from the supply once the motor has accelerated to a sufficient speed.

**Operating speed:** The centrifugal switch is designed to trip (open) at approximately **70% to 80% of synchronous speed** ($N_s$). At this speed, the auxiliary winding has completed its function of generating the starting torque, and disconnecting it prevents:
- Excessive heat generation in the high-resistance auxiliary winding.
- Unnecessary power consumption.

After disconnection, the motor continues to run as a standard single-phase induction motor on the **main winding alone**. **(3 marks)**

---

### PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** with **internal choice**. Each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks** each. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working principle** of a split-phase induction motor. Discuss the role of the **double revolving field theory** in justifying its operation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The main winding of a split-phase motor has $R_m = 4 \, \Omega$ and $X_m = 12 \, \Omega$. The auxiliary winding has $R_a = 20 \, \Omega$ and $X_a = 8 \, \Omega$. Both are connected across a **230 V, 50 Hz** single-phase supply. Calculate:
- (i) The phase angle of the current in each winding.
- (ii) The time-phase displacement between the two currents.
- (iii) The magnitude of currents in each winding. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

**Part (a) — Model Solution:**

A **split-phase induction motor** consists of:

**Stator:** Two windings — the *main winding* (M) and the *auxiliary winding* (A) — placed 90 electrical degrees apart in space.
- The **main winding** is highly inductive (low $R$, high $X$), so its current lags the supply voltage by a large angle $\phi_m$.
- The **auxiliary winding** is highly resistive (high $R$, low $X$), so its current lags the voltage by a small angle $\phi_a$.

**Rotor:** Squirrel-cage rotor identical to a three-phase induction motor.

**Working Principle:**
When the motor is switched ON, the two windings draw currents $\bar{I}_m$ and $\bar{I}_a$ that are displaced in time by an angle $\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a \approx 25°$. Combined with the 90° spatial displacement, this generates an **elliptical rotating magnetic field** that produces a **net starting torque** (1.5 to 2 times full-load torque).

A **centrifugal switch** disconnects the auxiliary winding at about 75% of $N_s$, after which the motor runs on the main winding only.

**Role of Double Revolving Field Theory:**
The single-phase alternating field is mathematically equivalent to two counter-rotating fields of equal magnitude. At standstill, these produce equal opposing torques ($T_f = T_b$), giving **zero net starting torque** in a plain single-phase motor. The auxiliary winding of the split-phase motor **breaks this symmetry** by suppressing the backward field component, converting the alternating flux into a rotating elliptical field. This is why the split-phase motor **can self-start**, unlike a plain single-phase motor. **(7 marks — Construction: 2, Working: 3, DRF Theory: 2)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Main winding: $R_m = 4 \, \Omega$, $X_m = 12 \, \Omega$
- Auxiliary winding: $R_a = 20 \, \Omega$, $X_a = 8 \, \Omega$
- Supply: $V = 230$ V, $f = 50$ Hz

**Step (i) — Phase angle of each winding current:**

For the main winding:

$$\phi_m = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{X_m}{R_m}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{12}{4}\right) = \tan^{-1}(3) = 71.57^\circ$$

For the auxiliary winding:

$$\phi_a = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{X_a}{R_a}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{8}{20}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.4) = 21.80^\circ$$

**Step (ii) — Time-phase displacement:**

$$\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a = 71.57^\circ - 21.80^\circ = 49.77^\circ$$

**Step (iii) — Magnitude of currents:**

Main winding current:

$$I_m = \frac{V}{\sqrt{R_m^2 + X_m^2}} = \frac{230}{\sqrt{4^2 + 12^2}} = \frac{230}{\sqrt{160}} = \frac{230}{12.649} = 18.18 \, \text{A}$$

Auxiliary winding current:

$$I_a = \frac{V}{\sqrt{R_a^2 + X_a^2}} = \frac{230}{\sqrt{20^2 + 8^2}} = \frac{230}{\sqrt{464}} = \frac{230}{21.54} = 10.68 \, \text{A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Summary:**
> - $\phi_m = 71.57^\circ$, $\phi_a = 21.80^\circ$
> - $\alpha = 49.77^\circ$
> - $I_m = 18.18$ A, $I_a = 10.68$ A
> - The phase displacement $\alpha = 49.77^\circ$ is significantly higher than the ideal 25° — this is acceptable but not optimal; real designs target $\alpha \approx 25°$ to balance starting torque and starting current. **(7 marks — Phase angles: 2, Displacement: 2, Currents: 3)**

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **torque-slip characteristics** of a split-phase induction motor with a neat sketch. Why does the **auxiliary winding get disconnected** during running? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 4-pole, 50 Hz, single-phase split-phase induction motor has a forward field impedance of $Z_f = (4 + j2) \, \Omega$ and backward field impedance of $Z_b = (4 + j3) \, \Omega$ at standstill, with rotor impedance referred to stator of $Z_r = (2 + j1) \, \Omega$. Compute the **synchronous speed** and the **starting torque** if the supply voltage is 220 V. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

**Part (a) — Model Solution:**

**Torque-Slip Characteristic Sketch (Qualitative Description):**

The torque-slip curve of a split-phase motor is the **superposition** of two curves: one for the forward rotating field and another for the backward rotating field, displaced symmetrically about $s = 1$ on the slip axis.

- At $s = 1$ (standstill): Forward and backward torques are **not exactly equal** (because of the auxiliary winding's phase shift), giving a **net positive starting torque** of about 1.5 to 2 × full-load torque.
- As the rotor accelerates, the **forward torque increases** and the **backward torque decreases**.
- Near $s = 0.2$ (typical max torque region), the net torque reaches a maximum.
- At $s = 0$ (synchronous speed), the net torque is zero (ideal no-load condition).

**Why Auxiliary Winding is Disconnected:**

The auxiliary winding is **designed for transient duty only**. If kept connected during steady-state running:
1. It would draw **continuous current** and overheat (since it has high resistance and is wound with thin wire).
2. It would cause **phase imbalance** and reduce the running efficiency.
3. It would lower the **power factor** during normal operation.

Hence, a **centrifugal switch** (or sometimes a relay) opens at ~75% of $N_s$, isolating the auxiliary winding. The motor then operates on the main winding alone in a manner consistent with Double Revolving Field Theory. **(7 marks — Curve explanation: 3, Disconnection reasons: 4)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Number of poles: $P = 4$
- Frequency: $f = 50$ Hz
- Supply voltage: $V = 220$ V
- Forward field impedance: $Z_f = 4 + j2 \, \Omega$
- Backward field impedance: $Z_b = 4 + j3 \, \Omega$
- Rotor impedance (referred): $Z_r = 2 + j1 \, \Omega$

**Step 1 — Synchronous Speed:**

$$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \, \text{RPM}$$

**Step 2 — Synchronous Angular Velocity:**

$$\omega_s = \frac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \, \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Forward and Backward Torque at $s = 1$ (Starting Condition):**

Using the relation:

$$T = k \cdot \frac{R_2 / s}{[R_2 / s]^2 + X_2^2} \cdot \phi^2$$

At standstill, the forward torque component (using forward field impedance):

$$T_f = \frac{V^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{R_f}{R_f^2 + X_f^2}$$

with $R_f = 4 \, \Omega$, $X_f = 2 \, \Omega$:

$$T_f = \frac{220^2}{157.08} \cdot \frac{4}{4^2 + 2^2} = \frac{48400}{157.08} \cdot \frac{4}{20} = 308.12 \times 0.2 = 61.62 \, \text{N·m}$$

Similarly for the backward torque:

$$T_b = \frac{V^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{R_b}{R_b^2 + X_b^2}$$

with $R_b = 4 \, \Omega$, $X_b = 3 \, \Omega$:

$$T_b = \frac{48400}{157.08} \cdot \frac{4}{4^2 + 3^2} = 308.12 \times \frac{4}{25} = 308.12 \times 0.16 = 49.30 \, \text{N·m}$$

**Step 4 — Net Starting Torque:**

$$T_{start} = T_f - T_b = 61.62 - 49.30 = 12.32 \, \text{N·m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Summary:**
> - $N_s = 1500$ RPM
> - $T_f = 61.62$ N·m
> - $T_b = 49.30$ N·m
> - **Net starting torque $T_{start} = 12.32$ N·m**
> 
> The starting torque is positive and non-zero, confirming that the split-phase motor can self-start. **(7 marks — $N_s$: 1.5, $T_f$: 2.5, $T_b$: 2, $T_{net}$: 1)**

---

### ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Split-Phase Motor Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign on $T_b$:** Students often write $T_{net} = T_f + T_b$, giving a wrong (much larger) answer. The backward torque **opposes** the forward torque and must be **subtracted**.
> 2. **Confusing degrees with radians in $\omega_s$:** Always convert $N_s$ to rad/s using $\omega_s = 2\pi N_s / 60$ before using it in torque equations.
> 3. **Skipping the DRF theory explanation:** Even in numerical problems, a 1-2 line justification of the formula used earns easy marks. Don't jump straight to calculation.
> 4. **Not specifying the unit of $\alpha$:** Always write "electrical degrees" or "radians" — examiners deduct marks for ambiguous units.
> 5. **Misidentifying the starting torque ratio:** Split-phase motors have **LOW to MEDIUM** starting torque (1.5–2 × FL). Do NOT confuse with capacitor-start (3–4.5 × FL) or capacitor-run motors.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Split-phase motor is a **single-phase induction motor with an auxiliary starting winding**.
- ✅ Main winding is **inductive** (low R, high X); auxiliary winding is **resistive** (high R, low X).
- ✅ Windings are **spatially displaced by 90 electrical degrees** and **temporally displaced by 20°–30°**.
- ✅ Starting torque: **1.5 to 2 × full-load torque** — moderate, not high.
- ✅ Auxiliary winding is **disconnected at ~75% of $N_s$ by a centrifugal switch**.
- ✅ Rotor is always **squirrel-cage type** (no slip rings or brushes).
- ✅ Working principle rests on **Double Revolving Field Theory** — a single-phase alternating field splits into two counter-rotating fields.
- ✅ Synchronous speed: $N_s = 120f / P$.
- ✅ Net starting torque: $T_{start} \approx k' \cdot I_m \cdot I_a \cdot \sin(\alpha)$.
- ✅ Plain single-phase motor has **zero starting torque** — this is why split-phase is essential.
- ✅ Applications: **Fans, blowers, washing machines, refrigerator compressors, small pumps.**
- ✅ Phase displacement formula: $\alpha = \tan^{-1}(X_m/R_m) - \tan^{-1}(X_a/R_a)$.
- ✅ Running mode is **single-phase** on main winding only — efficiency is moderate.
- ✅ KTU board tip: Always include the **DRF theory justification** alongside any torque calculation for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacitor Start
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT302 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES & DRIVES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Capacitor Start

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal Academic Definition

A **Capacitor Start Single-Phase Induction Motor** is a modified split-phase induction motor in which a **capacitor** is connected in series with the **auxiliary (starting) winding** to produce a significantly larger phase displacement (approaching **90°**) between the currents of the main (running) winding and the auxiliary winding during the starting period. This enhanced phase split produces a much higher **starting torque** than the conventional resistance split-phase motor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – Single Phase Motors):** The capacitor start motor is classified under *split-phase starting methods* of single-phase induction motors. The starting winding is disconnected by a **centrifugal switch** once the rotor reaches approximately **75% of synchronous speed**.

**Key Constructional Features:**
- **Stator:** Laminated silicon-steel core with two windings displaced by **90° electrical** in space:
  - **Main (Running) Winding (M):** Highly inductive, low resistance, designed for continuous operation.
  - **Auxiliary (Starting) Winding (A):** Low inductive reactance, designed for intermittent duty.
- **Capacitor (C):** Connected in series with the auxiliary winding — typically an **AC electrolytic capacitor** rated for short-duration starting duty only (NOT a continuous-duty oil-filled type in capacitor-run motors).
- **Centrifugal Switch (CS):** Mounted on the rotor shaft; opens automatically when rotor speed reaches ~75% of $N_s$, disconnecting the auxiliary winding and capacitor from the supply.
- **Rotor:** Standard **squirrel-cage rotor** identical to three-phase motors — robust and maintenance-free.

### 💡 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – "The Two-Person Boat":** Imagine two people pushing a stuck boat (the rotor) into motion. If both push at exactly the *same time and angle*, the boat will barely rotate — most effort cancels out. But if **one person pushes from the front (auxiliary winding, leading current)** and **the other pushes from the side (main winding, lagging current)**, with a **90° separation between their pushes**, the boat begins to spin smoothly and powerfully. The capacitor is the "synchronizer" that makes these two pushes occur at the right time-angle. Once the boat is moving (motor reaches speed), only one person is needed to keep it going — so the other is released (centrifugal switch opens).

### 🔑 Real-World Applications

| Application Sector | Specific Use |
|---|---|
| **HVAC & Refrigeration** | Hermetic refrigerant compressors |
| **Pumps** | Centrifugal and submersible pumps |
| **Machine Tools** | Wood-working lathes, small drill presses |
| **Appliances** | Washing machines (older designs), air coolers |
| **Industrial** | Conveyor belts requiring high starting torque |

---

### 🔁 Related Single-Phase Motor Family (for context)

| Motor Type | Starting Torque (× F.L. Torque) | Starting Phase Angle |
|---|---|---|
| Split-Phase | **1.5 to 2 times** | ~25°–30° |
| **Capacitor Start** | **3 to 4.5 times** | **~80°–90°** |
| Capacitor Run | 0.5 to 1.0 times | ~90° (running) |
| Capacitor Start-Capacitor Run | 3 to 4.5 times | ~90° (both) |
| Shaded Pole | 0.5 to 1.0 times | Very small |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🔍 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Connection at Standstill (s = 1):**
When the motor is switched ON, both the main winding **M** and the auxiliary winding **A** (in series with capacitor **C**) are energized in parallel across the single-phase AC supply **V**.

**Step 2 — Why Phase Shift Occurs:**
The two windings have *different impedances*:
- **Main winding (M):** $Z_m = R_m + jX_m$ → highly **inductive** → current $I_m$ **lags** V by a large angle $\theta_m$.
- **Auxiliary winding (A) + Capacitor (C):** $Z_a = R_a + j(X_a - X_c)$ → because $X_c \gg X_a$, the circuit becomes **capacitive** → current $I_a$ **leads** V by angle $\theta_a$.

**Step 3 — Resulting Phase Angle Between Currents:**
The phase angle $\alpha$ between $I_m$ and $I_a$ is:

$$\alpha = \theta_m + \theta_a$$

By choosing a *suitable capacitor value*, $\alpha$ can be made approximately **90°** (ideally), which is the condition for **maximum starting torque**.

**Step 4 — Production of Rotating Magnetic Field:**
When two alternating fluxes, displaced by **90° in space** (windings) and **α in time-phase** (currents), interact, the resultant is a **rotating magnetic field** instead of a pure pulsating field. This rotating field cuts the squirrel-cage rotor conductors, induces currents, and produces a starting torque in the direction of rotation.

**Step 5 — Acceleration and Switch Action:**
The rotor accelerates. When it reaches about **75% of synchronous speed**, the **centrifugal switch** opens, disconnecting the auxiliary winding and capacitor. The motor then runs on the main winding alone, sustained by the rotating field established by the main winding's interaction with the rotor's induced currents (cross-field theory).

**Step 6 — Why Capacitor is Disconnected:**
- The capacitor is an **AC electrolytic** type, designed for **short-duration** starting duty only (typically < 3 seconds).
- Continuous operation would overheat and destroy the capacitor.
- The auxiliary winding has higher resistance and would overheat if left in circuit continuously.

---

### 📐 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Synchronous Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | Standard synchronous speed formula | rpm |
| 2 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Slip at any operating speed | dimensionless |
| 3 | Main winding impedance | $Z_m = \sqrt{R_m^2 + X_m^2}$ | Magnitude of main winding impedance | Ω |
| 4 | Auxiliary winding impedance (with capacitor) | $Z_a = \sqrt{R_a^2 + (X_a - X_c)^2}$ | Magnitude of auxiliary branch impedance | Ω |
| 5 | Main winding current | $I_m = \dfrac{V}{Z_m}$ | RMS current through main winding | A |
| 6 | Auxiliary winding current | $I_a = \dfrac{V}{Z_a}$ | RMS current through auxiliary winding | A |
| 7 | Phase angle of main current | $\theta_m = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{X_m}{R_m}\right)$ | Lag angle of $I_m$ w.r.t. V | rad or ° |
| 8 | Phase angle of auxiliary current | $\theta_a = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{X_c - X_a}{R_a}\right)$ | Lead angle of $I_a$ w.r.t. V (note sign reversal because capacitive) | rad or ° |
| 9 | Phase angle between currents | $\alpha = \theta_m + \theta_a$ | Critical angle for torque production | rad or ° |
| 10 | Starting Torque | $T_{st} = K \cdot I_m \cdot I_a \cdot \sin\alpha$ | Fundamental starting torque expression | N·m |
| 11 | Starting Torque Ratio (approx.) | $T_{st} \approx K \cdot V^2 \cdot \dfrac{\sin\alpha}{Z_m \cdot Z_a}$ | Voltage-squared dependence | N·m |
| 12 | For Maximum Starting Torque | $\sin\alpha = 1 \Rightarrow \alpha = 90°$ | Optimal design condition | — |
| 13 | Reversal Direction | Reverse either main or auxiliary winding, NOT both | Standard rule | — |
| 14 | Typical Starting Torque | $T_{st} = 3$ to $4.5 \times T_{FL}$ | Empirical range for capacitor-start | × full-load |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the vertical pipe `|` symbol inside any markdown table row containing mathematical absolute-value expressions to avoid table syntax corruption.

---

### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Manufacturing:** Heavy-load starting applications (compressors, conveyors) demand high starting torque that ordinary split-phase motors cannot deliver.
- **Energy Efficiency:** Once started, the capacitor is disconnected, so losses are minimized.
- **Cost-Effective Alternative:** Provides performance approaching three-phase motors at single-phase distribution infrastructure cost.
- **Reliability:** The centrifugal switch is a proven, maintenance-free mechanical sensor — used for decades in domestic and industrial equipment.

---

### 🧠 Why and How — The Underlying Physics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why":** A single-phase winding alone produces only a **pulsating (alternating) magnetic field** — it does NOT rotate. By Doubly-Split Theory, a pulsating field = two equal counter-rotating fields → net starting torque = 0. **Hence the rotor cannot self-start.** Adding a second winding with a phase-shifted current creates a **net rotating component**, which provides starting torque.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "How":** The capacitor in the auxiliary branch makes $I_a$ lead V (capacitive behavior), while $I_m$ lags V (inductive behavior). With proper capacitor sizing, the *time-phase difference* between currents approaches the *space-phase difference* between windings (90°), producing a near-circular rotating field at standstill.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🧮 Derivation 1: Phase Angle Between Main and Auxiliary Currents

**Given:**
- Supply voltage: $V$ (RMS, single-phase AC)
- Main winding parameters: $R_m$, $X_m$
- Auxiliary winding parameters: $R_a$, $X_a$
- Capacitor: $X_c = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi f C}$

**Step 1 — Write the Impedances**

The main winding is purely inductive:

$$Z_m = R_m + jX_m$$

The auxiliary branch has inductance in series with capacitance:

$$Z_a = R_a + jX_a + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C} = R_a + j(X_a - X_c)$$

Because $X_c \gg X_a$ in a properly designed capacitor-start motor, the net reactance of the auxiliary branch is **negative** (capacitive):

$$Z_a = R_a - j(X_c - X_a)$$

**Step 2 — Compute Magnitudes**

$$Z_m = \sqrt{R_m^2 + X_m^2}$$

$$Z_a = \sqrt{R_a^2 + (X_c - X_a)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Currents**

$$I_m = \dfrac{V}{Z_m} \quad \text{(lags V by } \theta_m \text{)}$$

$$I_a = \dfrac{V}{Z_a} \quad \text{(leads V by } \theta_a \text{)}$$

**Step 4 — Phase Angles**

The lag angle of $I_m$:

$$\theta_m = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{X_m}{R_m}\right)$$

The lead angle of $I_a$ (note the sign change due to net capacitive behavior):

$$\theta_a = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{X_c - X_a}{R_a}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Phase Angle Between the Two Currents**

Since $I_m$ lags V by $\theta_m$ and $I_a$ leads V by $\theta_a$, the total angular separation between $I_m$ and $I_a$ is:

$$\boxed{\alpha = \theta_m + \theta_a = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{X_m}{R_m}\right) + \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{X_c - X_a}{R_a}\right)}$$

**Step 6 — Design Condition for α = 90°**

For maximum starting torque, we require $\alpha = 90°$, i.e., $\sin\alpha = 1$:

$$\theta_m + \theta_a = 90° \Rightarrow \theta_a = 90° - \theta_m$$

This implies:

$$\tan\theta_a = \tan(90° - \theta_m) = \cot\theta_m = \dfrac{R_m}{X_m}$$

Substituting the expression for $\tan\theta_a$:

$$\dfrac{X_c - X_a}{R_a} = \dfrac{R_m}{X_m}$$

Solving for $X_c$:

$$X_c = X_a + \dfrac{R_a \cdot R_m}{X_m}$$

The required capacitor value is:

$$\boxed{C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f \cdot X_c} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f \left( X_a + \dfrac{R_a R_m}{X_m} \right)}}$$

---

### 🧮 Derivation 2: Starting Torque Using Double Revolving Field Theory

**Step 1 — Express the Two Flux Waves**

A single-phase pulsating flux $\phi(t) = \Phi_m \cos(\omega t)$ can be resolved into two equal counter-rotating fluxes each of magnitude $\dfrac{\Phi_m}{2}$:

$$\phi_f(t) = \dfrac{\Phi_m}{2} \cos(\omega t - \theta_s)$$

$$\phi_b(t) = \dfrac{\Phi_m}{2} \cos(\omega t + \theta_s)$$

where $\theta_s$ is the spatial angle and $f$ denotes *forward*, $b$ denotes *backward*.

**Step 2 — Torque Produced by Each Component**

For each rotating field, torque is proportional to the square of flux divided by rotor impedance, in the direction of rotation:

$$T_f = K \cdot \dfrac{\phi_f^2 \cdot R_2 / s}{R_2^2/s^2 + X_2^2}$$

$$T_b = K \cdot \dfrac{\phi_b^2 \cdot R_2 / (2-s)}{R_2^2/(2-s)^2 + X_2^2}$$

**Step 3 — Net Torque**

$$T_{net} = T_f - T_b$$

At standstill ($s = 1$): both torques are equal in magnitude but opposite in direction:

$$T_{st} = T_f - T_b = 0 \quad \text{(for single winding alone)}$$

This proves a single-phase motor has **zero starting torque** without auxiliary winding.

**Step 4 — Adding Auxiliary Winding**

With both windings active at standstill, the resultant forward flux $\phi_f$ is enhanced and backward flux $\phi_b$ is reduced. The net starting torque becomes:

$$T_{st} = K \cdot \phi_f \cdot \phi_b \cdot \sin\alpha$$

Where $\alpha$ is the time-phase angle between the main and auxiliary currents.

**Step 5 — Substituting Flux in Terms of Currents**

Since flux is proportional to current (with constant impedance):

$$\phi_m \propto I_m, \quad \phi_a \propto I_a$$

And the rotating flux components depend on $I_m$, $I_a$ and $\alpha$:

$$\boxed{T_{st} = K \cdot I_m \cdot I_a \cdot \sin\alpha = K \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{Z_m Z_a} \cdot \sin\alpha}$$

**Step 6 — Final Torque Expression**

$$\boxed{T_{st} \propto V^2 \cdot \dfrac{\sin\alpha}{Z_m \cdot Z_a}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Observation:** Because $T_{st} \propto \sin\alpha$, maximizing $\alpha$ to 90° **maximizes the starting torque**. This is precisely why the capacitor-start motor achieves starting torques of 3 to 4.5 times full-load — much higher than the plain split-phase motor (where $\alpha \approx 25°$ to $30°$ only).

---

### 🧮 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Summary

| Parameter | Split-Phase Motor | **Capacitor Start Motor** |
|---|---|---|
| Phase shift device | Resistance (inherent in winding) | **Capacitor (external)** |
| Phase angle α | ~25° to 30° | **~80° to 90°** |
| Starting torque | 1.5 – 2 × F.L. | **3 – 4.5 × F.L.** |
| Auxiliary winding current | Same magnitude as main | **Higher (limited by capacitor)** |
| Cost | Low | **Moderate** |
| Capacitor type | None | **AC electrolytic (starting duty)** |
| Switch | Centrifugal | **Centrifugal** |
| Applications | Fans, blowers (light loads) | **Compressors, pumps, conveyors** |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 🔌 Circuit Diagram (Mermaid Functional Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC Supply V] --> B[Main Switch]
    B --> C[Main Winding M]
    B --> D[Auxiliary Branch]
    D --> E[Capacitor C]
    E --> F[Auxiliary Winding A]
    F --> G[Centrifugal Switch CS]
    G --> H[Return to Supply]
    C --> H
    classDef supply fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef winding fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cap fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef switch fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class A,B supply
    class C,F winding
    class E cap
    class G switch
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Single-phase AC Supply V** feeds a **Main Switch** (manual/push-button starter).
- Two parallel branches form the stator circuit:
  - **Branch 1 (Main):** Main winding **M** with high inductance, low resistance.
  - **Branch 2 (Auxiliary):** Auxiliary winding **A** in series with capacitor **C** and centrifugal switch **CS**.
- Both branches return to the supply neutral/return line.
- After ~75% synchronous speed, the **centrifugal switch CS** opens, isolating the auxiliary branch.

---

### ⚙️ Constructional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Stator Core - Laminated Si Steel] --> P2[Main Winding M - 90 deg Space Shift]
    P1 --> P3[Auxiliary Winding A - 90 deg Space Shift]
    P4[AC Capacitor C - Starting Duty] --> P3
    P5[Centrifugal Switch CS on Rotor Shaft] --> P3
    P6[Squirrel Cage Rotor] --> P7[Rotating Magnetic Field Interaction]
    P2 --> P7
    P7 --> P8[Starting Torque Production]
    P5 --> P9[Switch Opens at 75 percent Ns]
    P9 --> P10[Motor Runs on Main Winding Only]
    classDef stator fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px
    classDef rotor fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef control fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef output fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px
    class P1,P2,P3 stator
    class P4,P5 control
    class P6 rotor
    class P7,P8,P9,P10 output
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **Stator Core** houses both windings displaced by 90° in space.
- The **Capacitor** and **Centrifugal Switch** form the auxiliary branch control elements.
- The **Squirrel Cage Rotor** is mechanically connected to the **Centrifugal Switch**.
- The interaction produces the **Starting Torque**, after which the centrifugal switch **opens**, and the motor continues on the main winding alone.

---

### 📈 Torque-Speed Characteristic (Conceptual Mermaid Plot)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0["s = 1 Standstill"] --> T0["Tst Maximum 3 to 4.5 x TFL"]
    T0 --> A1["Rotor Accelerates"]
    A1 --> S1["s decreases"]
    S1 --> T1["Torque Rises to Maximum Tmax near s = 0.2"]
    T1 --> S2["Torque drops to rated value"]
    S2 --> S3["75 percent Ns Switch Opens"]
    S3 --> S4["Main Winding Only - Stable Operation"]
    S4 --> SN["Rated Operating Point near Synchronous Speed"]
    classDef start fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef accel fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef run fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class S0,T0 start
    class A1,S1,T1 accel
    class S2,S3,S4,SN run
```

**Reading the Curve:**
- **At s = 1 (standstill):** $T_{st}$ is very high (3–4.5 × full-load torque).
- **As rotor accelerates:** torque follows the typical induction motor curve, peaking near $s \approx 0.2$.
- **At ~75% Ns:** Centrifugal switch opens, torque momentarily drops (since auxiliary winding disconnects), but main winding sustains the rotation.
- **Steady-state operation:** occurs near synchronous speed on the main-winding torque curve.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** Why is a capacitor connected in series with the auxiliary winding of a single-phase induction motor in a *capacitor-start* motor?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A capacitor is connected in series with the auxiliary winding to create a large phase displacement (ideally approaching **90°**) between the currents of the main and auxiliary windings. The main winding is highly inductive, so its current **lags** the supply voltage, while the auxiliary winding in series with a capacitor becomes capacitive, so its current **leads** the supply voltage. This combined time-phase separation, combined with the **90° spatial displacement** of the windings, produces a strong rotating magnetic field at standstill. The result is a **high starting torque** (3 to 4.5 times full-load torque), which the plain split-phase motor cannot achieve. The capacitor is disconnected by a centrifugal switch once the motor reaches about 75% of synchronous speed.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** List any three applications where capacitor-start motors are preferred over plain split-phase motors. Justify briefly.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Capacitor-start motors are preferred over plain split-phase motors in applications requiring **high starting torque**, because the capacitor creates a larger phase angle (~90°) between winding currents, giving 3 to 4.5 times full-load starting torque compared to only 1.5 to 2 times for split-phase motors.

**Three applications:**
1. **Refrigerant compressors** — require high starting torque to overcome refrigerant pressure head.
2. **Centrifugal water pumps** — must accelerate impeller and water column from rest.
3. **Conveyor belts and machine tool drives** — need to overcome static friction and inertial loads of heavy machinery.

---

### 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Module 2]`

**(a)** With a neat schematic diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a **capacitor-start single-phase induction motor**. Discuss the role of the capacitor and the centrifugal switch. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the starting torque of a capacitor-start motor and show that it is proportional to $\sin\alpha$, where $\alpha$ is the phase angle between the main and auxiliary winding currents. **[7 marks]**

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question A(a) — Construction and Working [7 Marks]

**Construction [3 marks]:**

The capacitor-start single-phase induction motor consists of:

1. **Stator:** A laminated silicon-steel core carrying two windings displaced by **90° electrical** in space:
   - **Main winding (M):** Designed for continuous duty; high inductance, low resistance.
   - **Auxiliary winding (A):** Designed for intermittent duty only; lower inductance.

2. **Capacitor (C):** An AC electrolytic capacitor connected in series with the auxiliary winding. It is designed for short-duration starting duty (typically a few seconds).

3. **Centrifugal Switch (CS):** Mounted on the rotor shaft, in series with the auxiliary winding. It automatically opens when rotor reaches ~75% of synchronous speed.

4. **Rotor:** A squirrel-cage rotor identical to that used in three-phase motors — robust and maintenance-free.

**Working Principle [4 marks]:**

When the motor is switched ON, both main and auxiliary windings are energized in parallel across the single-phase AC supply.

- The current $I_m$ in the highly inductive main winding **lags** the supply voltage $V$ by a large angle $\theta_m$.
- The current $I_a$ in the auxiliary branch (inductive winding + capacitor) **leads** the supply voltage $V$ by angle $\theta_a$, because the capacitive reactance $X_c$ dominates the inductive reactance $X_a$.

The resulting phase angle between the two currents is:

$$\alpha = \theta_m + \theta_a \approx 80° \text{ to } 90°$$

The two windings (spatially displaced by 90°) carrying currents with phase displacement $\alpha$ produce a **rotating magnetic field** at standstill. This rotating field cuts the rotor conductors and induces currents, producing a high **starting torque** (3 to 4.5 × full-load torque).

The rotor accelerates. When it reaches approximately **75% of synchronous speed**, the **centrifugal switch** opens, disconnecting the auxiliary winding and capacitor from the supply. The motor then runs on the main winding alone, sustained by the cross-field effect between the main winding's pulsating flux and the rotor-induced currents.

**Role of Capacitor:** Creates large phase displacement between currents, producing strong rotating magnetic field and high starting torque.

**Role of Centrifugal Switch:** Protects the auxiliary winding and capacitor from overheating by removing them from the circuit once the motor has accelerated to operating speed.

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question A(b) — Starting Torque Derivation [7 Marks]

Using the **Double Revolving Field Theory**, a single-phase pulsating flux can be decomposed into two equal counter-rotating flux components of magnitude $\dfrac{\Phi_m}{2}$ each.

When both main and auxiliary windings carry currents $I_m$ and $I_a$ with phase angle $\alpha$ between them, the resultant forward flux $\phi_f$ is enhanced and the backward flux $\phi_b$ is reduced. The starting torque is given by:

$$T_{st} = K \cdot \phi_f \cdot \phi_b \cdot \sin\alpha$$

Substituting $\phi_f \propto I_m$ and $\phi_b \propto I_a$:

$$T_{st} = K \cdot I_m \cdot I_a \cdot \sin\alpha$$

Using $I_m = \dfrac{V}{Z_m}$ and $I_a = \dfrac{V}{Z_a}$:

$$\boxed{T_{st} = K \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{Z_m \cdot Z_a} \cdot \sin\alpha}$$

This expression clearly shows:

- $T_{st} \propto V^2$ (voltage-squared dependence — typical of induction motors)
- $T_{st} \propto \sin\alpha$ (phase-angle dependence)
- $T_{st}$ is **maximum** when $\alpha = 90°$, i.e., $\sin\alpha = 1$

In the capacitor-start motor, by choosing an appropriate capacitor value, $\alpha$ can be made very close to 90°, yielding starting torques of **3 to 4.5 times** the full-load torque.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Module 2 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Explain why a single-phase induction motor is **not self-starting** by itself. How does the addition of an auxiliary winding with a series capacitor make it self-starting? **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A capacitor-start motor has the following parameters: $V = 230\text{ V}$, $f = 50\text{ Hz}$, main winding impedance $Z_m = 5 + j5\ \Omega$, auxiliary winding impedance $Z_a = 8 - j12\ \Omega$. Calculate the phase angle between the main and auxiliary winding currents. **[7 marks]**

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question B(a) — Why Single-Phase Motors are Not Self-Starting [7 Marks]

A single-phase induction motor has only one stator winding. When energized by single-phase AC, it produces a **pulsating (alternating) magnetic field**, not a rotating one.

By the **Double Revolving Field Theory**, this pulsating field can be resolved into two equal counter-rotating magnetic fields, each of magnitude $\dfrac{\Phi_m}{2}$, rotating at synchronous speed $N_s$ in opposite directions.

Each rotating field induces currents in the rotor and produces a torque in its own direction of rotation:

- Forward torque: $T_f \propto \dfrac{\phi_f^2 \cdot R_2/s}{(R_2/s)^2 + X_2^2}$
- Backward torque: $T_b \propto \dfrac{\phi_b^2 \cdot R_2/(2-s)}{(R_2/(2-s))^2 + X_2^2}$

At **standstill ($s = 1$)**, $T_f = T_b$, so the net starting torque is **zero**. Hence, the single-phase motor cannot start by itself.

**How Capacitor Makes It Self-Starting [4 marks]:**

By adding an auxiliary winding displaced 90° in space and connecting it in series with a capacitor, the currents in the two windings are made to differ in time-phase by an angle $\alpha$ approaching 90°. This produces a **rotating magnetic field** at standstill. The rotating field acts asymmetrically on the rotor — producing a **net unidirectional starting torque**. The rotor begins to rotate in the direction determined by the phase sequence (auxiliary current leads, so rotation is from auxiliary to main winding).

The capacitor thus converts a single-phase pulsating field into a quasi-rotating field, enabling self-starting.

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question B(b) — Phase Angle Calculation [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $V = 230\text{ V}$, $f = 50\text{ Hz}$
- Main winding: $Z_m = 5 + j5\ \Omega$
- Auxiliary winding (with capacitor): $Z_a = 8 - j12\ \Omega$

**Step 1 — Magnitudes of Impedances**

$$Z_m = \sqrt{5^2 + 5^2} = \sqrt{50} = 7.07\ \Omega$$

$$Z_a = \sqrt{8^2 + 12^2} = \sqrt{64 + 144} = \sqrt{208} = 14.42\ \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Phase Angle of Main Winding Current (lagging)**

$$\theta_m = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{X_m}{R_m}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{5}{5}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1) = 45°$$

So $I_m$ lags V by **45°**.

**Step 3 — Phase Angle of Auxiliary Winding Current (leading)**

Since $Z_a = 8 - j12$ is net capacitive:

$$\theta_a = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\vert X_a \vert}{R_a}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{12}{8}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1.5) = 56.31°$$

So $I_a$ leads V by **56.31°**.

**Step 4 — Phase Angle Between the Two Currents**

$$\alpha = \theta_m + \theta_a = 45° + 56.31° = 101.31°$$

**Step 5 — Practical Consideration**

Although $\alpha = 101.31° > 90°$, this still produces a high starting torque because $\sin\alpha = \sin(101.31°) \approx 0.981$, very close to unity. The slight excess over 90° indicates the capacitor value chosen is slightly larger than the optimal value for maximum torque — a small oversizing that provides safety margin in industrial design.

$$\boxed{\alpha \approx 101.31° \quad \text{with} \quad \sin\alpha \approx 0.981}$$

**Starting torque factor (relative to maximum possible):**

$$\dfrac{T_{st,\text{actual}}}{T_{st,\text{max}}} = \sin(101.31°) \approx 0.981 \approx 98.1\%$$

So the motor achieves about 98% of its maximum possible starting torque — an excellent design.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Sign error in phase angle:** Students often write $\theta_a = \tan^{-1}(X_a/R_a)$ instead of $\tan^{-1}((X_c - X_a)/R_a)$. The capacitor makes the auxiliary branch capacitive, so the sign of reactance **reverses**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the centrifugal switch:** When asked "why is the capacitor disconnected?", students write only about capacitor heating. **The auxiliary winding also overheats** — both must be mentioned for full marks.
>
> 3. **Confusing capacitor-start with capacitor-run:** Capacitor-start uses an **AC electrolytic capacitor** (short duty); capacitor-run uses an **oil-filled paper capacitor** (continuous duty). Mixing these up loses marks.
>
> 4. **Direction of rotation rule:** To reverse a single-phase induction motor, reverse the connections of **either** the main winding **or** the auxiliary winding — **not both**. Many students incorrectly state "reverse the supply."
>
> 5. **Phase angle sign:** When auxiliary impedance is net capacitive ($X_c > X_a$), $\theta_a$ is a **lead** angle (positive). When main impedance is inductive, $\theta_m$ is a **lag** angle. The total phase separation $\alpha = \theta_m + \theta_a$ is the **sum**, not the difference.
>
> 6. **Units in derivation:** Always include units (Ω, A, V, N·m, rad/s, rpm) in the final answer — KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Capacitor Start Motor**

- ✅ A capacitor-start motor is a **single-phase induction motor** with high starting torque (3 – 4.5 × F.L.).
- ✅ **Auxiliary winding** is connected in **series with a capacitor** and a **centrifugal switch**, all in series.
- ✅ The capacitor produces a **large phase angle (≈90°)** between main and auxiliary winding currents.
- ✅ **Main winding:** high inductance, low resistance — current **lags** V.
- ✅ **Auxiliary winding:** low inductance, in series with capacitor — net **capacitive** branch — current **leads** V.
- ✅ Phase angle between currents: $\alpha = \theta_m + \theta_a$ (both contribute additively).
- ✅ Starting torque formula: $T_{st} = K \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{Z_m Z_a} \cdot \sin\alpha$.
- ✅ For **maximum starting torque**, design for $\alpha = 90°$, i.e., $\sin\alpha = 1$.
- ✅ Required capacitor reactance: $X_c = X_a + \dfrac{R_a R_m}{X_m}$.
- ✅ Required capacitor value: $C = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi f X_c}$.
- ✅ **Centrifugal switch** disconnects auxiliary branch at ~**75% of $N_s$**.
- ✅ Capacitor type: **AC electrolytic** (intermittent duty only).
- ✅ Direction reversal: reverse **either** main **or** auxiliary winding — not both.
- ✅ Applications: compressors, pumps, conveyors, refrigeration units — high-starting-torque loads.
- ✅ **Cannot be reversed while running** — must come to a complete stop before reversing.
- ✅ Starting current is **high** (5–7 × full-load current).
- ✅ Power factor during starting is **improved** compared to split-phase motor.
- ✅ Common exam trap: don't confuse $\theta_a$ sign — capacitive branch → leading current → positive $\theta_a$.
