# Module 2 — Capacitor Start-Run Motor (CSCR)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Paraphrase):**
> A **Capacitor Start-Run Motor** (also called a *Two-Value Capacitor Motor* or *Capacitor Start-Capacitor Run / CSCR Motor*) is a single-phase induction motor that employs **two separate capacitors** connected in parallel with the auxiliary (starting) winding — a **high-value electrolytic start capacitor (Cₛ)** for producing large starting torque, and a **low-value oil/paper run capacitor (Cᵣ)** permanently connected in the auxiliary branch to improve running performance.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Gear Bicycle"

Imagine pushing a heavy cart that is stuck. You have two ways to push it:
- A **big kick** (sharp, short burst of effort) to overcome the static friction — this is what the **start capacitor** does.
- A **steady, smooth walk** to keep the cart rolling without straining yourself — this is the job of the **run capacitor**.

The *big kick* (Cₛ) is engaged only at the start and is then **disconnected by a centrifugal switch** once the motor reaches about **70–80 %** of synchronous speed. The *steady walk* (Cᵣ) stays connected for the entire journey, making the ride smooth, efficient, and quiet.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Identifiers for Board Examinations:**
> - Two capacitors (start + run) — distinguish it from *Capacitor Start* (one capacitor) and *Capacitor Run* (one permanent capacitor).
> - Centrifugal switch **must** be present (since Cₛ is electrolytic and cannot stay connected in the AC circuit for long).
> - Phase angle between main and auxiliary winding currents approaches **90°** at start → maximum starting torque.

### Physical Constants / Standard Design Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Start capacitor (Cₛ) | **20 – 300 µF**, Electrolytic, AC-rated |
| Run capacitor (Cᵣ) | **3 – 50 µF**, Oil-paper / Metallised polypropylene |
| Auxiliary/Main turns ratio (a) | **0.7 – 1.5** (typically ≈ 1) |
| Phase split at start | ≈ **90°** (ideal) |
| Centrifugal switch cut-out speed | **70 – 80 % of Nₛ** |
| Starting torque | **200 – 350 %** of full-load torque |
| Pull-out torque | **200 – 250 %** of full-load torque |
| Power factor (running) | **0.85 – 0.95 lagging (near unity)** |

Where:
$$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P} \text{ rpm}$$

$f$ = supply frequency (**50 Hz** in India), $P$ = number of poles.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction — Two Windings, Two Capacitors

The motor essentially has the same physical structure as a split-phase motor with one crucial upgrade:

- **Main (Running) Winding (M):** Heavy-gauge copper, low resistance, high inductance; placed in the lower part of the slots.
- **Auxiliary (Starting) Winding (A):** Fine-gauge copper, high resistance (in some designs), placed spatially **90° electrical** away from M.
- **Two Capacitors in Parallel** across the auxiliary branch:
  - **Cₛ** — Start capacitor, in series with a **centrifugal switch (CSW)**.
  - **Cᵣ** — Run capacitor, permanently in the auxiliary branch.
- **Squirrel-cage rotor** — identical to that of a three-phase induction motor.
- **Centrifugal switch** mounted on the rotor shaft — spring-loaded contacts that open when rotor exceeds cut-out speed.

> [!NOTE]
> The run capacitor is **continuous-duty, oil-filled**, while the start capacitor is **intermittent-duty, electrolytic**. Electrolytics cannot tolerate continuous AC excitation because they overheat, dry out, and eventually explode.

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Single-phase problem:** A single winding fed by single-phase AC produces a **pulsating** (alternating) magnetic field — not rotating. By itself, it produces **zero starting torque**.

2. **Two-phase emulation:** By placing two windings **90° apart in space** and feeding them with currents **displaced ≈ 90° in time**, we synthesise a true rotating magnetic field.

3. **Role of Cₛ:** At standstill, the centrifugal switch is closed, so **both Cₛ and Cᵣ** are in parallel across the auxiliary winding. The combined capacitance (Cₛ + Cᵣ) is large, making the auxiliary branch **highly capacitive**, so its current leads the supply voltage by a large angle.

4. **Phase angle achievement:** With proper choice of Cₛ, the angle between Iₘ (main current, lags V) and Iₐ (auxiliary current, leads V) is made **≈ 90°**, giving **maximum starting torque**.

5. **Switch-off of Cₛ:** As rotor reaches ~75 % of Nₛ, the centrifugal switch **opens**, disconnecting Cₛ. Only Cᵣ remains in circuit.

6. **Running operation:** Cᵣ is sized such that the running phase angle and the resulting **circular rotating field** are optimised for smooth, quiet, high-power-factor running.

## 2.3 The "Why" — Why Two Capacitors and Not One?

| Stage | If Only Cᵣ (small) | If Only Cₛ (large) | Solution |
|---|---|---|---|
| Starting | Torque too low (~50 % FLT) | Torque high but capacitor overheats | Use **both** in parallel |
| Running | Poor power factor, noisy | Capacitor bursts (electrolytic) | Keep only Cᵣ, switch off Cₛ |

> The two-capacitor topology gives the **best of both worlds**: **high starting torque** *and* **good running performance**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **board-exam ready**. Memorise the symbols and units.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | rpm |
| 2 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | dimensionless, $0 < s < 1$ |
| 3 | Auxiliary branch capacitive reactance | $X_c = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi f C}$ | ohms (Ω), $C = C_s + C_r$ at start |
| 4 | Main-winding impedance | $\bar{Z}_m = R_m + j X_m$ | Ω |
| 5 | Auxiliary-winding impedance (with capacitor) | $\bar{Z}_a = R_a + j (X_a - X_c)$ | Ω |
| 6 | Phase angle between Iₘ and Iₐ | $\theta = \theta_a - \theta_m$ | radians (ideal ≈ 90°) |
| 7 | Forward-field torque | $T_f \propto \phi^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_2' / s}{(R_2' / s)^2 + X_2'^2}$ | N·m |
| 8 | Backward-field torque | $T_b \propto \phi^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_2' / (2-s)}{(R_2' / (2-s))^2 + X_2'^2}$ | N·m |
| 9 | Net torque | $T = T_f - T_b$ | N·m |
| 10 | Starting torque (generalised) | $T_s = K \cdot I_a \cdot I_m \cdot \sin\theta$ | N·m |
| 11 | Maximum starting torque condition | $\theta = 90^\circ$ | ideal phase split |
| 12 | Capacitance for 90° phase split | $C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f\left(X_a + \dfrac{R_a R_m}{X_m}\right)}$ | Farads (F) |
| 13 | Turns ratio condition for perfect balance | $R_a \cdot X_m = R_m \cdot X_a$ | purely resistive balance |
| 14 | Effective capacitance (start) | $C_{eq} = C_s + C_r$ | Farads (F) |
| 15 | Effective capacitance (run) | $C_{eq} = C_r$ | Farads (F) |
| 16 | Auxiliary current magnitude | $I_a = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{R_a^2 + (X_a - X_c)^2}}$ | amperes (A) |
| 17 | Main current magnitude | $I_m = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{R_m^2 + X_m^2}}$ | amperes (A) |
| 18 | Slip at maximum torque (forward) | $s_{max} = \dfrac{R_2'}{X_2'}$ | dimensionless |
| 19 | Line current (vector sum) | $\bar{I}_L = \bar{I}_m + \bar{I}_a$ | amperes (A) |
| 20 | Real-world engineering utility | High-start, high-pf drive for compressors, pumps, conveyors | industrial HVAC |

> [!NOTE]
> Always use $\vert x \vert$ or "mod x" notation when describing absolute values — the KTU evaluator strictly penalises ambiguous symbols.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| HVAC / Refrigeration | Compressors of window AC, refrigerators (high starting torque, low running noise) |
| Industrial Pumps | Centrifugal pumps needing soft-start, high-torque, continuous-duty |
| Conveyors & Hoists | Heavy inertia loads where high starting torque is critical |
| Machine Tools | Lathes, drills, woodworking machines (smooth torque, near-unity PF) |
| Domestic Appliances | Washing machines, food mixers, blowers |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Capacitance Required for 90° Phase Split

**Given:**
- Main winding impedance: $\bar{Z}_m = R_m + j X_m$
- Auxiliary winding impedance (without capacitor): $\bar{Z}_a^{\,0} = R_a + j X_a$
- Capacitive reactance in auxiliary branch: $X_c = \dfrac{1}{\omega C}$
- Net auxiliary impedance: $\bar{Z}_a = R_a + j (X_a - X_c)$
- Supply voltage: $\bar{V} = V \angle 0°$ (taken as reference).

**To find:** Value of $C$ such that phase angle between $\bar{I}_m$ and $\bar{I}_a$ is **exactly 90°**, giving **maximum starting torque**.

**Step 1 — Express the currents.**

$$\bar{I}_m = \frac{\bar{V}}{\bar{Z}_m} = \frac{V}{R_m + j X_m} = \frac{V}{\sqrt{R_m^2 + X_m^2}} \angle -\phi_m$$

where

$$\phi_m = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{X_m}{R_m}\right)$$

$$\bar{I}_a = \frac{\bar{V}}{\bar{Z}_a} = \frac{V}{R_a + j (X_a - X_c)} = \frac{V}{\sqrt{R_a^2 + (X_a - X_c)^2}} \angle -\phi_a$$

where

$$\phi_a = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{X_a - X_c}{R_a}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Phase difference required for maximum torque.**

For maximum starting torque we require $\theta = \phi_m - \phi_a = 90°$, i.e.

$$\phi_m - \phi_a = 90° \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \tan(\phi_m - \phi_a) \to \infty$$

**Step 3 — Apply tangent subtraction formula.**

$$\tan(\phi_m - \phi_a) = \frac{\tan \phi_m - \tan \phi_a}{1 + \tan \phi_m \, \tan \phi_a}$$

For this ratio to be infinite, the **denominator must equal zero**:

$$1 + \tan \phi_m \, \tan \phi_a = 0$$

$$\tan \phi_m \, \tan \phi_a = -1$$

**Step 4 — Substitute tangent identities.**

$$\frac{X_m}{R_m} \cdot \frac{X_a - X_c}{R_a} = -1$$

$$X_m (X_a - X_c) = -R_m R_a$$

$$X_a - X_c = -\frac{R_m R_a}{X_m}$$

$$X_c = X_a + \frac{R_m R_a}{X_m}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for capacitance.**

$$C = \frac{1}{2\pi f \, X_c} = \frac{1}{2\pi f \left( X_a + \dfrac{R_m R_a}{X_m} \right)}$$

$$\boxed{\,C = \frac{1}{2\pi f \left( X_a + \dfrac{R_m R_a}{X_m} \right)}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Special case: equal time-constants (pure balance condition).**

If $R_a / X_a = R_m / X_m$, i.e. $R_a X_m = R_m X_a$, the auxiliary branch with capacitor behaves as a **pure resistance** while the main branch remains **inductive** — this automatically gives a 90° phase split.

## 3.2 Derivation — Starting Torque in Terms of Phase Angle

Starting torque of a single-phase induction motor with an auxiliary winding is given by the **double revolving-field theory** as:

$$T_s = \frac{2}{\omega_s} \cdot (I_a I_m / 2) \cdot R_2' \cdot \sin\theta$$

Simplifying (collecting constants into $K$):

$$\boxed{\,T_s = K \, I_a \, I_m \, \sin\theta\,}$$

where $K = \dfrac{R_2'}{\omega_s}$ is a motor constant.

**Inference table — Effect of $\theta$:**

| Phase angle $\theta$ | $\sin\theta$ | Relative $T_s$ |
|---|---|---|
| $30°$ (no capacitor, plain split-phase) | $0.500$ | Low |
| $60°$ | $0.866$ | Medium |
| $75°$ | $0.966$ | High |
| $90°$ (ideal capacitor split) | $1.000$ | **Maximum** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a plain split-phase motor, $\theta$ is only about **25°–35°**, giving $T_s$ ≈ 1.5× FLT. For a **capacitor start** motor, $\theta \approx 80°$ gives $T_s$ ≈ 2.5× FLT. For a **capacitor start-run** motor, $\theta$ reaches **90° at start**, giving $T_s$ ≈ **3× FLT or more**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Single-Phase Motors

> This comparison table is a **favourite 14-mark question** in KTU board exams. Memorise the qualitative differences.

| Feature | Split-Phase | Capacitor Start | Capacitor Run | **Capacitor Start-Run (CSCR)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Capacitors used | None | 1 (start only) | 1 (run only) | **2 (start + run)** |
| Phase split $\theta$ | ~30° | ~80° | ~80° (run), low (start) | **~90° (start), ~80° (run)** |
| Starting torque | 1.0–1.5 × FLT | 2.5–3.5 × FLT | 0.5–1.0 × FLT | **2.5–3.5 × FLT** |
| Running PF | 0.5–0.6 | 0.5–0.6 | 0.8–0.95 | **0.85–0.95** |
| Running efficiency | Low | Low | High | **High** |
| Centrifugal switch | Yes | Yes | **No** | **Yes** (for Cₛ only) |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Medium | **Highest** |
| Noise level | High | High | Low | **Lowest** |
| Typical applications | Fans, blowers | Compressors, pumps | Fans, woodworking | **Compressors, conveyors, ACs, refrigerators** |
| Reversibility | Easy | Easy | Difficult | Difficult |

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 250 W, 4-pole, 50 Hz capacitor start-run motor has the following parameters:
$R_m = 4 \, \Omega$, $X_m = 6 \, \Omega$, $R_a = 6 \, \Omega$, $X_a = 8 \, \Omega$.
Calculate the value of capacitance required for **90° phase split** between the two winding currents. Take $V = 230 \, \text{V}$ (single phase).

**Step 1 — Apply the derived formula.**

$$C = \frac{1}{2\pi f \left( X_a + \dfrac{R_m R_a}{X_m} \right)}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the bracketed term.**

$$\frac{R_m R_a}{X_m} = \frac{4 \times 6}{6} = 4 \, \Omega$$

$$X_a + \frac{R_m R_a}{X_m} = 8 + 4 = 12 \, \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C$.**

$$C = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 50 \times 12} = \frac{1}{3769.91} \, \text{F}$$

$$C \approx 265.26 \, \mu\text{F}$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation.**

A capacitance of ~265 µF is typical for an **electrolytic start capacitor** of small motors. Since the centrifugal switch removes $C_s$ at run speed, the **permanent oil-filled run capacitor** would be much smaller (~10–30 µF) sized for the running phase split.

> [!NOTE]
> Such numericals frequently appear in **Part A (3 marks)** or as a sub-part of **Part B (7 marks)** in KTU examinations.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1["Single Phase AC Supply V=230V 50Hz"] --> B1["Main Winding M - Rm and Xm"]
    A1 --> B2["Auxiliary Branch - Ra and Xa"]
    B2 --> C1["Run Capacitor Cr - Oil Filled - Permanent"]
    B2 --> C2["Start Capacitor Cs - Electrolytic"]
    C2 --> D1["Centrifugal Switch CSW - Closed at Start"]
    D1 --> E1["Auxiliary Current Ia Leads V by Large Angle"]
    B1 --> E2["Main Current Im Lags V by Small Angle"]
    E1 --> F1["Phase Angle Theta between Im and Ia approaches 90 Degrees"]
    E2 --> F1
    F1 --> G1["Rotating Magnetic Field in Stator"]
    G1 --> H1["Squirrel Cage Rotor Picks up Torque"]
    H1 --> I1["Rotor Accelerates to 75 percent of Ns"]
    I1 --> J1["Centrifugal Switch Opens - Cs Disconnected"]
    J1 --> K1["Only Cr Remains in Auxiliary Branch"]
    K1 --> L1["Smooth Running - High Power Factor - Quiet Operation"]
    L1 --> M1["Mechanical Load Output - Pump Compressor Conveyor"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Phasor Diagram — Current Relationships

```mermaid
graph LR
    V0["V along Reference Axis"] --> IM["Im Lags V by Angle phi m"]
    V0 --> IA["Ia Leads V by Angle phi a"]
    IM --> Theta["Phase Angle Theta equals phi m plus phi a equals 90 degrees"]
    IA --> Theta
    Theta --> Result["Maximum Starting Torque T s equals K Im Ia sin 90"]
    Result --> Note1["At Start Both Cs and Cr in Circuit"]
    Note1 --> Note2["Cs Disconnected by Centrifugal Switch at Run Speed"]
    Note2 --> Note3["Only Cr Provides Improved Running Phase Angle"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — Operational State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Standstill
    Standstill --> StartingPhase: Switch ON - CSW closed - Both Cs and Cr active
    StartingPhase --> Acceleration: 90 degree phase split - High starting torque 2.5 to 3.5 FLT
    Acceleration --> RunTransition: Rotor speed exceeds 75 percent of Ns
    RunTransition --> RunningPhase: CSW opens - Cs removed - Only Cr active
    RunningPhase --> SteadyRunning: Near unity PF - Smooth and Quiet
    SteadyRunning --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Physical Schematic — Equivalent Circuit Description

> [!NOTE]
> Since the actual physical schematic requires complex drawing tools, the equivalent electrical circuit is described below:

- **Main branch (left):** Supply $\rightarrow$ Main winding ($R_m$ in series with $jX_m$) $\rightarrow$ back to supply.
- **Auxiliary branch (right):** Supply $\rightarrow$ Aux. winding ($R_a$ in series with $jX_a$) $\rightarrow$ node X.
  - From node X: **Run capacitor $C_r$** in parallel with **(Start capacitor $C_s$ + Centrifugal switch CSW)**, both connected back to the supply.

```
            Supply V
               |
        +------+------+
        |             |
      [Rm+jXm]      [Ra+jXa]
        |             |
        |             +-------||Cs||---CSW---+ (start branch)
        |             |                     |
        |             +-------||Cr||--------+ (run branch, permanent)
        |             |
        +------+------+
               |
             Rotor (squirrel cage)
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1. (3 marks)** Why is a **centrifugal switch** essential in a Capacitor Start-Run motor, but absent in a Capacitor Run motor?

**Model Answer:**
A centrifugal switch is essential in a Capacitor Start-Run motor because the **start capacitor $C_s$ is an electrolytic, AC-rated capacitor designed for intermittent duty only**. If $C_s$ remained connected during running, it would overheat, dry out, and explode within minutes due to continuous AC losses. The centrifugal switch, mounted on the rotor shaft, **opens automatically when the rotor reaches 70–80 % of synchronous speed**, physically disconnecting $C_s$ from the auxiliary branch. The run capacitor $C_r$, being an oil-filled paper/metalised polypropylene type, is continuous-duty rated and remains permanently in circuit. Hence, a Capacitor Run motor (which uses only $C_r$) does not require any centrifugal switch, since $C_r$ is rated for continuous AC excitation.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. (3 marks)** Define *phase splitting* in the context of single-phase induction motors. Why is it necessary?

**Model Answer:**
*Phase splitting* is the technique of producing a **second, time-displaced current** in an auxiliary winding so that the two stator windings — separated by 90° in space — carry currents that are also displaced in time. This synthesises a **rotating magnetic field** from a single-phase supply. It is necessary because a single winding fed from a single-phase source produces only a **pulsating magnetic field**, which gives **zero net starting torque**. By splitting the phase using a capacitor (capacitor start / start-run motors) or by using a high-resistance auxiliary winding (split-phase motor), a rotating field is created and starting torque is generated. Without phase splitting, a single-phase induction motor **cannot self-start**.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

### Option A — Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A.** With the aid of a neat circuit diagram and phasor diagram, explain the **construction, working principle, and starting-characteristic behaviour** of a **Capacitor Start-Run (CSCR) motor**. Show mathematically how the **value of capacitance** is chosen for a 90° phase split. **(14 marks)**

**Sub-part (a) — Construction & Working (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Construction:** The stator carries two windings placed 90° electrical apart in space:
- **Main (running) winding (M):** Heavy copper, low resistance, high inductance ($R_m$, $X_m$).
- **Auxiliary (starting) winding (A):** Lighter copper, two capacitors connected in parallel across its terminals:
  - $C_s$ — electrolytic start capacitor (high value) in series with a **centrifugal switch (CSW)**.
  - $C_r$ — oil-filled run capacitor (low value), permanently connected.
- Rotor: standard squirrel-cage.

**Step 2 — Working Principle (Narrative Form):**
1. On energisation, the centrifugal switch is **closed**, so $C_s$ and $C_r$ are both active in the auxiliary branch. The combined capacitance makes this branch highly capacitive — the auxiliary current $I_a$ leads the supply voltage $V$ by a large angle.
2. The main winding, being inductive, draws current $I_m$ that **lags** $V$ by a smaller angle.
3. With proper sizing, $\theta$ (angle between $I_m$ and $I_a$) becomes ≈ **90°**, producing a near-perfect **rotating magnetic field** and hence **maximum starting torque (≈ 2.5–3.5 × full-load torque)**.
4. As rotor accelerates to about **75 % of $N_s$**, the centrifugal switch **opens**, removing $C_s$ from the circuit.
5. Only $C_r$ remains in the auxiliary branch. It maintains a healthy phase split (~70°–80°) during running, ensuring **high power factor (~0.9), smooth torque, low noise, and high efficiency**.

**Step 3 — Phasor Diagram Description:**
- Reference: $\bar{V}$ along horizontal axis.
- $\bar{I}_m$ lags $\bar{V}$ by angle $\phi_m = \tan^{-1}(X_m/R_m)$.
- $\bar{I}_a$ leads $\bar{V}$ by angle $\phi_a = \tan^{-1}[(X_c - X_a)/R_a]$.
- Angle between them: $\theta = \phi_m + \phi_a \to 90°$ for ideal starting.

**Sub-part (b) — Mathematical Derivation of Capacitance (7 marks)**

Starting from the condition $\tan(\phi_m - \phi_a) = \infty$, we set $1 + \tan\phi_m \tan\phi_a = 0$:

$$\frac{X_m}{R_m} \cdot \frac{X_a - X_c}{R_a} = -1$$

$$X_c = X_a + \frac{R_m R_a}{X_m}$$

$$C = \frac{1}{2\pi f \left( X_a + \dfrac{R_m R_a}{X_m} \right)}$$

A numerical illustration:
- $R_m = 4 \, \Omega$, $X_m = 6 \, \Omega$, $R_a = 6 \, \Omega$, $X_a = 8 \, \Omega$, $f = 50 \, \text{Hz}$.
- $\dfrac{R_m R_a}{X_m} = \dfrac{4 \times 6}{6} = 4 \, \Omega$
- $X_c = 8 + 4 = 12 \, \Omega$
- $C = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \times 50 \times 12} \approx 265.3 \, \mu\text{F}$.

**Conclusion:** A capacitor of about 265 µF is required at start. After $C_s$ is switched out, the smaller run capacitor $C_r$ (~10–30 µF) maintains optimum running performance.

---

### Option B — Question B (14 Marks)

**Q.B.** Compare the **starting and running performance** of **split-phase, capacitor start, capacitor run, and capacitor start-run motors** in a detailed analytical matrix. Explain why the **CSCR motor is preferred for high-inertia industrial loads** such as compressors and conveyors. **(14 marks)**

**Sub-part (a) — Comparative Analytical Matrix (7 marks)**

| Feature | Split-Phase | Capacitor Start | Capacitor Run | **Capacitor Start-Run** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Phase split $\theta$ at start | 25°–35° | 75°–85° | 25°–35° | **≈ 90°** |
| Starting torque | 1.0–1.5 × FLT | 2.5–3.5 × FLT | 0.5–1.0 × FLT | **2.5–3.5 × FLT** |
| Running power factor | 0.5–0.6 | 0.5–0.6 | 0.8–0.95 | **0.85–0.95** |
| Running efficiency | 60–65 % | 60–65 % | 70–80 % | **75–85 %** |
| Noise / vibration | High | High | Low | **Lowest** |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Medium | **Highest** |
| Centrifugal switch | Yes | Yes | No | **Yes (for $C_s$ only)** |

**Sub-part (b) — Why CSCR is Preferred for High-Inertia Industrial Loads (7 marks)**

1. **High starting torque:** Compressors and conveyors have very high static friction and inertia. The CSCR motor delivers 2.5–3.5 × FLT starting torque (because of 90° phase split at start), which is sufficient to overcome static friction and accelerate the load.

2. **Smooth running:** Once the load is moving, $C_r$ maintains a healthy circular rotating field, eliminating torque pulsations that cause vibration and wear in plain split-phase motors.

3. **High running power factor:** Industrial plants have many inductive loads; the $C_r$ capacitor partially compensates the overall plant PF, reducing line current and $I^2R$ losses.

4. **Higher efficiency and lower heating:** Continuous-duty oil-filled $C_r$ has low losses; the auxiliary winding carries less current at run speed, reducing copper losses.

5. **Quiet operation:** Important in HVAC and refrigeration applications (e.g., air-conditioner compressors used indoors).

6. **Reliability:** Squirrel-cage rotor (no brushes, no slip rings), simple starting switch — long service life with minimal maintenance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "Capacitor Start" with "Capacitor Start-Run":** In Part A, students often write "one capacitor is used" for both — this costs 1 full mark. Clearly state **two capacitors** with the **centrifugal switch** removing only $C_s$.
> 2. **Drawing $C_s$ and $C_r$ in series instead of parallel:** The two capacitors are in **parallel** across the auxiliary winding, not in series. Wrong circuit = lose 2 marks in Part B.
> 3. **Forgetting units:** Writing $C = 265$ without "$\mu$F" loses a half-mark in the numerical.
> 4. **Skipping the cut-out speed:** Always state **"centrifugal switch operates at 70–80 % of synchronous speed"** — this is a favourite 1-mark follow-up.
> 5. **Using "single-phase induction motor" loosely:** Be precise — write *"single-phase split-phase / capacitor-type induction motor"*.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 2-Minute Revision before entering the examination hall:**

- [x] **Capacitor Start-Run motor = Two-value capacitor motor = CSCR motor.**
- [x] **Two capacitors:** $C_s$ (electrolytic, intermittent) and $C_r$ (oil-filled, continuous duty).
- [x] **Both are connected in parallel** with the auxiliary winding.
- [x] **Centrifugal switch opens at ~75 % of $N_s$**, removing $C_s$.
- [x] $C_r$ remains **permanently** in the circuit.
- [x] Starting phase split: **≈ 90°** (maximum possible starting torque).
- [x] Running phase split: **~70°–80°** (good compromise for circular field).
- [x] Starting torque: **2.5–3.5 × FLT**; Running PF: **0.85–0.95**.
- [x] Capacitance for 90° split:
       $C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f \left( X_a + \dfrac{R_m R_a}{X_m} \right)}$
- [x] Equivalent capacitance at start: $C_{eq} = C_s + C_r$.
- [x] Equivalent capacitance at run: $C_{eq} = C_r$.
- [x] Net torque: $T = T_f - T_b$ (double revolving field theory).
- [x] Forward-field torque ∝ $\dfrac{R_2' / s}{(R_2'/s)^2 + X_2'^2}$.
- [x] Backward-field torque ∝ $\dfrac{R_2' / (2-s)}{(R_2'/(2-s))^2 + X_2'^2}$.
- [x] Starting torque expression: $T_s = K \, I_a \, I_m \, \sin\theta$.
- [x] Rotor: **Squirrel-cage**, identical to 3-phase IM.
- [x] **Applications:** Compressors (AC, fridge), conveyors, pumps, machine tools, blowers.
- [x] **NOT reversible easily** (unlike split-phase and capacitor-start motors) — this is a common viva question.
- [x] Most **expensive** of all single-phase motors due to two capacitors and centrifugal switch.
- [x] $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$; $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$.

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Universal Motor** is a specially designed **series-wound commutator-type DC motor** that is mechanically and electrically engineered to operate satisfactorily on **both single-phase AC supply and DC supply** with approximately the same speed and torque characteristics. It is a fractional-kilowatt (sub-fractional) commutator machine whose series field winding and armature are connected in series, and whose entire magnetic circuit is constructed using **laminated silicon steel** rather than solid cast iron to minimize eddy-current losses during AC operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Universal motors are classified as **single-phase commutator motors** under the broader category of **fractional-kilowatt (FHP) AC motors**. They belong to the family of motors that require commutation and thus cannot operate on pure three-phase supply without modification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Operating Principle:** The fundamental reason a universal motor works on both AC and DC is that the **torque produced is proportional to the product of armature current and main-field flux** ($T \propto \phi I_a$). Since both field and armature are in series, the same current flows through both windings on both DC and AC (per half-cycle on AC), reversing simultaneously and preserving the unidirectional torque.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of a universal motor like a **reversible water faucet with synchronized flaps**. Imagine two doors on a hinge that must always open in the same direction when water (current) flows through them. If the water flows forward, both doors swing one way; if the water is suddenly reversed, both doors swing the opposite way together, still pushing in the same rotational sense. 

In a universal motor:
- The **armature current** is the "water flow."
- The **field winding and armature winding** are the "two doors" — connected in series.
- When AC reverses direction 50 times per second (for 50 Hz), **both the field current and armature current reverse together**, so the force (torque) on the conductors always acts in the **same rotational direction**.

**Real-World Examples:** Universal motors are inside your **mixer-grinder**, **handheld vacuum cleaner**, **electric hair dryer**, **portable drill**, **sewing machine**, and **table fan (older types)**. They are used wherever **high speed, high starting torque, and lightweight construction** are required from a single-phase supply.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value / Unit |
|-----------|---------------------|
| Standard Supply | **220 V / 50 Hz, Single-Phase AC** (or 110 V / 60 Hz) |
| Power Range | **0.1 kW to 1.5 kW** (fractional kW class) |
| Operating Speed | **3,000 RPM to 30,000 RPM** (no-load can exceed 20,000 RPM) |
| Number of Poles | Almost always **2-pole** construction |
| Field Configuration | **Series wound** (compounded types are rare) |
| Lamination Material | **Silicon steel laminations** (0.35 mm to 0.5 mm thick) |
| Commutator Material | **Hard-drawn copper** segments with mica insulation |
| Brush Material | **Carbon / Graphite** (sometimes with copper dusting) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Two Poles?** Universal motors are almost always built with **only two poles** because at high speeds, the AC excitation frequency makes additional poles unnecessary and they reduce commutator sparking by simplifying the magnetic path.

---

## 1.4 Salient Features of a Universal Motor

- **High Power-to-Weight Ratio:** Compact and lightweight for the power delivered.
- **High Speed Operation:** Can run at speeds up to 20,000 RPM, far higher than induction motors.
- **High Starting Torque:** Excellent starting torque due to series characteristic.
- **Universal Operation:** Runs on both AC and DC with similar characteristics.
- **Easy Speed Control:** Speed can be varied using simple methods (resistance, tapping, SCR).
- **Series Speed-Torque Characteristic:** Like a DC series motor — high no-load speed (dangerous).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of a Universal Motor

The universal motor is essentially a **DC series motor adapted for AC operation**. Its construction consists of the following key components:

### 2.1.1 Stator (Field System)

The stator provides the magnetic field and houses the **field winding**.

- **Laminated Core:** The field core is built from **thin silicon steel laminations** (typically 0.35 mm thick) that are stamped and stacked together. This is **critical** because at AC operation, the alternating flux induces eddy currents; lamination drastically reduces these losses.
- **Field Windings (Main Poles):** Concentrated coils wound around the two salient poles. The windings carry the **same current as the armature** since they are in series.
- **Compensating Winding (in compensated types):** Embedded in the pole faces to neutralize the **armature reaction**, which is severe at high speeds and causes commutation problems on AC.
- **Number of Poles:** Almost universally **two (2) poles**.

### 2.1.2 Rotor (Armature)

The rotor is identical in principle to a **DC machine armature**, but refined for AC operation.

- **Laminated Armature Core:** Built from silicon steel laminations with **semi-closed slots** to reduce flux pulsations and tooth-pulsation losses.
- **Armature Winding:** Typically a **wave winding** (because it gives only two parallel paths regardless of poles, ideal for high-voltage AC).
- **Commutator:** Made of hard-drawn copper segments insulated by **mica**. The commutator surface is often made **smooth and wear-resistant**.

### 2.1.3 Brush Gear & Commutator

- **Brushes:** Usually **carbon or graphite** brushes, sometimes **electrographitic** for longer life.
- **Brush Holders:** Provide constant spring pressure on the commutator.
- **Commutator Poles (Interpoles):** Present in **compensated universal motors** to improve commutation by neutralizing the reactance voltage induced in the coils undergoing commutation.

---

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The operation can be understood in distinct logical steps:

1. **Supply Energization:** When connected to a single-phase AC supply, the same current $I$ flows through both the field winding and the armature (series connection).

2. **Flux Production:** This current produces a **magnetic flux $\phi$** in the field poles. Because the field iron is laminated, the flux can reverse rapidly without excessive eddy-current losses.

3. **Armature Reaction:** The current-carrying armature conductors placed in this flux experience a **force $F = BIL$** according to Lorentz's force law.

4. **Torque Generation:** The cumulative force produces a **torque $T$** on the armature. 

5. **AC Half-Cycle Reversal:** When the AC supply reverses direction (every half cycle), **both field current and armature current reverse simultaneously**. As a result:
   - The flux $\phi$ reverses direction.
   - The current direction in conductors reverses.
   - The torque $T \propto \phi \cdot I_a$ remains **unidirectional** (both factors flip sign together, so their product stays positive).

6. **Continuous Rotation:** This produces a **unidirectional torque** every half-cycle, giving continuous rotation at high speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Laminated Core?** On AC supply, the field flux is alternating. A solid iron core would suffer from severe **eddy-current losses** ($P_e \propto B_m^2 f^2 t^2$). Laminating the core with insulated sheets confines eddy currents to small loops, drastically reducing losses.

---

## 2.3 Why It Works on Both AC and DC

Theoretical justification:

| Condition | DC Supply | AC Supply |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| Field Current | $I_f = I_a$ (DC value) | $i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$ |
| Flux | $\phi = $ constant | $\phi(t) = \phi_m \sin(\omega t)$ |
| Armature Current | $I_a$ (DC) | $i_a(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$ |
| Instantaneous Torque | $T \propto \phi \cdot I_a$ (constant) | $T(t) \propto \phi_m \sin(\omega t) \cdot I_m \sin(\omega t) = \phi_m I_m \sin^2(\omega t)$ |
| Average Torque | $\phi \cdot I_a$ | $\frac{\phi_m I_m}{2}$ |

The torque on AC is **pulsating** but always positive (because $\sin^2 \geq 0$), producing a **unidirectional average torque**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU-high-yield and must be memorized for ESE.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $T \propto \phi \cdot I_a$ | Torque proportional to flux × armature current | Nm |
| 2 | $E_b \propto \phi \cdot N$ | Back EMF proportional to flux × speed | Volts |
| 3 | $V = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ | Voltage equation on DC | Volts |
| 4 | $V_m \sin(\omega t) = e_b + i_a(R_a + R_{se}) + L\frac{di_a}{dt}$ | Voltage equation on AC (includes reactance) | Volts |
| 5 | $T_{avg} = \frac{1}{2}\phi_m I_m$ | Average torque on AC supply | Nm |
| 6 | $T_{avg} = \frac{1}{2}\phi_{rms} I_{rms}$ | Equivalent form using RMS values | Nm |
| 7 | $N \propto \frac{V}{\phi} - \frac{I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k\phi}$ | Speed equation | RPM |
| 8 | $\phi \propto I_a$ | Flux ∝ current (series motor, unsaturated) | — |
| 9 | $T \propto I_a^2$ | Torque ∝ current² (series characteristic) | Nm |
| 10 | $N \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{T}}$ | Speed ∝ 1/√T (series characteristic, unsaturated) | RPM |

> [!NOTE]
> **Kritical Point on Reactance:** The term $L\frac{di_a}{dt}$ in AC operation represents the **self-induced EMF due to alternating flux** linking the series field. This reactance causes a **drop in power factor** (typically 0.7 to 0.9 lagging) and slightly reduces torque compared to DC operation at the same RMS voltage.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Universal motors are the **workhorse of portable single-phase AC appliances**. Their high speed-to-weight ratio makes them ideal for:

- **Domestic Appliances:** Mixers, blenders, hair dryers, vacuum cleaners, washing machines (small).
- **Power Tools:** Handheld drills, circular saws, angle grinders, jigsaws.
- **Office Equipment:** Photocopy machines, paper shredders.
- **Sewing Machines:** Where smooth high-torque operation is needed.
- **Fans:** Older table fans used universal motors for their high speed.

The **speed control** of universal motors via TRIAC/SCR phase-angle control is one of the most commercially exploited features, enabling dimmer-like speed regulation in nearly every modern appliance.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Average Torque on AC Supply

This is the most critical derivation for KTU exams.

### Starting Point: Instantaneous Torque Expression

For a DC series motor, the torque produced by the armature at any instant is given by:

$$T_{inst} = k \cdot \phi \cdot i_a$$

Where:
- $k$ = machine constant ($\frac{P}{2\pi A}$, with $P$ = poles, $A$ = parallel paths)
- $\phi$ = instantaneous flux per pole
- $i_a$ = instantaneous armature current

### Step 1: Substitute AC Quantities

On AC supply, both flux and armature current vary sinusoidally in phase (because they are in series and the field iron is unsaturated):

$$i_a(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$$

$$\phi(t) = \phi_m \sin(\omega t)$$

(Assuming field saturation is neglected — unsaturated magnetic circuit.)

### Step 2: Write Instantaneous Torque

$$T_{inst}(t) = k \cdot \phi_m \sin(\omega t) \cdot I_m \sin(\omega t)$$

$$T_{inst}(t) = k \cdot \phi_m I_m \sin^2(\omega t)$$

### Step 3: Apply Trigonometric Identity

Using $\sin^2(\theta) = \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$:

$$T_{inst}(t) = k \cdot \phi_m I_m \cdot \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2}$$

$$T_{inst}(t) = \frac{k \cdot \phi_m I_m}{2} - \frac{k \cdot \phi_m I_m}{2}\cos(2\omega t)$$

### Step 4: Calculate Average Torque

The average over one full cycle is obtained by integrating:

$$T_{avg} = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T T_{inst}(t) \, dt$$

Where $T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$ is the time period.

The second term, $\frac{k \phi_m I_m}{2}\cos(2\omega t)$, averages to **zero** over a complete cycle because the integral of cosine over a full period is zero.

Therefore:

$$T_{avg} = \frac{k \cdot \phi_m I_m}{2}$$

### Step 5: Express in Terms of RMS Values

Since $I_{rms} = \frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ and $\phi_{rms} = \frac{\phi_m}{\sqrt{2}}$:

$$\frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}} = I_{rms} \implies I_m = \sqrt{2}\,I_{rms}$$

$$\frac{\phi_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \phi_{rms} \implies \phi_m = \sqrt{2}\,\phi_{rms}$$

Substituting:

$$T_{avg} = \frac{k \cdot (\sqrt{2}\,\phi_{rms})(\sqrt{2}\,I_{rms})}{2} = \frac{k \cdot 2 \cdot \phi_{rms} I_{rms}}{2}$$

$$\boxed{T_{avg} = k \cdot \phi_{rms} \cdot I_{rms}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** The average torque of a universal motor on AC supply equals $k \cdot \phi_{rms} \cdot I_{rms}$ — exactly the same form as a DC motor's torque equation, but with RMS quantities. **This is why it gives comparable performance on both supplies.**

---

## 3.2 Speed Equation Derivation (AC Operation)

### Starting Point: AC Voltage Equation

For a series motor on AC supply, applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law:

$$v(t) = e_b(t) + i_a(t)R_a + i_a(t)R_{se} + L_{se}\frac{di_a}{dt} + L_a\frac{di_a}{dt}$$

Where:
- $e_b(t)$ = instantaneous back EMF
- $R_a$ = armature resistance
- $R_{se}$ = series field resistance
- $L_{se}$ = series field inductance
- $L_a$ = armature inductance

### Step 1: Combine Resistances and Inductances

Let $R = R_a + R_{se}$ and $L = L_a + L_{se}$ (total resistance and inductance).

$$v(t) = e_b(t) + i_a(t)R + L\frac{di_a}{dt}$$

### Step 2: Express Back EMF

The instantaneous back EMF is given by:

$$e_b(t) = k \cdot \phi(t) \cdot \omega(t)$$

In RMS form (assuming sinusoidal steady state and constant speed):

$$E_b = k \cdot \phi_{rms} \cdot \omega$$

Where $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ is the angular speed in rad/s.

### Step 3: RMS Voltage Equation

$$V = \sqrt{E_b^2 + (I_{rms}R)^2 + (I_{rms}X_L)^2}$$

Where $X_L = \omega L$ is the inductive reactance of the series field.

### Step 4: Solve for Speed

$$E_b = \sqrt{V^2 - I_{rms}^2(R^2 + X_L^2)}$$

$$k \phi_{rms} \omega = \sqrt{V^2 - I_{rms}^2 Z^2}$$

Where $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + X_L^2}$ is the total impedance.

$$\omega = \frac{\sqrt{V^2 - I_{rms}^2 Z^2}}{k \phi_{rms}}$$

Converting to RPM:

$$\boxed{N = \frac{60}{2\pi k \phi_{rms}}\sqrt{V^2 - I_{rms}^2(R^2 + X_L^2)}}$$

### Step 5: Power Factor

The overall power factor of the motor:

$$\cos\phi_{pf} = \frac{R}{Z} = \frac{R}{\sqrt{R^2 + X_L^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The reactive drop $I_{rms} X_L$ causes the universal motor's power factor to **lag** (typically 0.85 to 0.95 lagging) and slightly reduces the back EMF available for mechanical work compared to DC operation at the same RMS voltage.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: DC vs AC Operation

| Parameter | On DC Supply | On AC Supply | Effect |
|-----------|--------------|--------------|--------|
| Flux | Constant $\phi_{DC}$ | Pulsating $\phi_m \sin(\omega t)$ | AC has flux pulsation |
| Current | Constant $I_a$ | Sinusoidal $I_m \sin(\omega t)$ | AC current varies |
| Torque | Constant (no ripple) | Pulsating (frequency $2f$) | Slightly higher losses |
| Average Torque | $k\phi_{DC}I_a$ | $k\phi_{rms}I_{rms}$ | Slightly lower on AC |
| Power Output | Same for same RMS V,I | Reduced by ~5-15% due to reactance | AC has lower output |
| Power Factor | Unity | 0.85–0.95 lagging | Reactive component present |
| Core Losses | Minimal (constant flux) | Higher (hysteresis + eddy) | AC has more heat |
| Commutation | Good (no reactance voltage) | Poor (reactance voltage) | AC needs compensation |

---

## 3.4 Speed-Torque Characteristic Derivation

For a series motor in the **unsaturated region**, the flux is proportional to current:

$$\phi = c \cdot I_a$$

Where $c$ is a constant depending on magnetic circuit geometry.

### Step 1: Torque Equation

$$T = k \phi I_a = k (c I_a) I_a = k c I_a^2$$

$$\therefore I_a = \sqrt{\frac{T}{kc}}$$

### Step 2: Back EMF

$$E_b = k \phi N = k(c I_a) N = k c I_a N$$

### Step 3: Voltage Equation (DC, Neglecting Saturation)

$$V = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_{se})$$

$$V = k c I_a N + I_a R$$

$$V = I_a(kcN + R)$$

### Step 4: Solve for N

$$I_a = \frac{V}{kcN + R}$$

### Step 5: Combine with Torque Equation

$$I_a = \sqrt{\frac{T}{kc}}$$

Equating:

$$\sqrt{\frac{T}{kc}} = \frac{V}{kcN + R}$$

Squaring:

$$\frac{T}{kc} = \frac{V^2}{(kcN + R)^2}$$

$$T = \frac{kcV^2}{(kcN + R)^2}$$

### Step 6: Solve for N

$$(kcN + R)^2 = \frac{kcV^2}{T}$$

$$kcN + R = V\sqrt{\frac{kc}{T}}$$

$$kcN = V\sqrt{\frac{kc}{T}} - R$$

$$\boxed{N = \frac{V}{kc}\sqrt{\frac{kc}{T}} - \frac{R}{kc} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{kc}}\left[\frac{V}{\sqrt{T}} - \frac{R}{\sqrt{kc}}\right]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** $N \propto \frac{V}{\sqrt{T}}$ — the speed is **inversely proportional to the square root of torque**. This gives the famous **inverse-square-root drooping characteristic** of a DC series / universal motor. Doubling the torque reduces speed by a factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 220 V, 50 Hz universal motor takes 0.5 A current at no-load and 5 A at full load on AC supply. Calculate: (a) Average torque at full load, (b) Speed at full load.

Given:
- $V = 220$ V (RMS)
- $f = 50$ Hz
- $I_{FL} = 5$ A
- Assume $R_a + R_{se} = 5\ \Omega$ (typical small motor)
- Assume constant $k\phi = 0.5$ V·s/rad (assumed machine constant)

### (a) Average Torque Calculation

Back EMF at full load:

$$E_b = V - I_{FL} \cdot R = 220 - 5 \times 5 = 195 \text{ V}$$

Average torque (using DC-equivalent form with RMS values):

$$T_{avg} = k\phi \cdot I_{rms} = 0.5 \times 5 = 2.5 \text{ Nm}$$

### (b) Speed Calculation

$$E_b = k\phi \cdot \omega = 0.5 \cdot \omega$$

$$\omega = \frac{E_b}{k\phi} = \frac{195}{0.5} = 390 \text{ rad/s}$$

Converting to RPM:

$$N = \frac{\omega \times 60}{2\pi} = \frac{390 \times 60}{2\pi}$$

$$N = \frac{23{,}400}{6.2832}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 3{,}724 \text{ RPM}}$$

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
```
