# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why is a synchronous motor not self-starting? How is starting torque provided in a modern synchronous motor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A synchronous motor is not self-starting because the rotor, when stationary, experiences an **alternating torque** from the stator's rotating magnetic field. The rotor is too heavy to follow this rapid alternation and hence **cannot develop a unidirectional starting torque** on its own.

In modern practice, **damper (amortisseur) windings** are embedded in the rotor pole faces. These act as a **squirrel-cage winding** — the stator RMF induces currents in the damper bars, producing **induction-motor-type starting torque**, bringing the rotor close to synchronous speed. Once near $N_s$, the DC field is energised and the rotor **pulls into synchronism** by magnetic locking. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define "hunting" in a synchronous motor. How is it suppressed?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Hunting* is the phenomenon of **mechanical oscillation of the rotor about its equilibrium load angle $\delta$**, caused by sudden load changes, supply disturbances, or change in excitation. It results in speed fluctuations, noise, and possible loss of synchronism.

Hunting is **suppressed by damper (amortisseur) windings** placed in the pole faces. The damper bars, moving relative to the stator RMF during oscillation, develop **induced currents that oppose the motion** (Lenz's law), thereby **damping out the oscillations** within a few cycles. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Model (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

> **(a)** With a neat phasor diagram, explain the operation of a 3-phase synchronous motor under the following excitation conditions:
> 1. Unity power factor
> 2. Lagging power factor
> 3. Leading power factor
>
> Derive the expression for the **mechanical power developed per phase** in terms of the load angle $\delta$. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz, star-connected synchronous motor has a synchronous reactance of **10 Ω per phase** and negligible armature resistance. The line voltage is **6.6 kV**, and the excitation is adjusted to give a back-EMF of **4 kV (line-to-line)**. The load angle is **20° electrical**.
>
> Calculate:
> 1. Synchronous speed in rpm.
> 2. Armature current per phase.
> 3. Total mechanical power developed.
> 4. Developed torque in N·m.
> 5. Pull-out torque.
> **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer — Question A

#### (a) Phasor Diagram and Power Expression

**Phasor Diagram Construction (textual):**

For a 3-phase synchronous motor, take $V_{ph}$ as the reference phasor along the positive x-axis. The back-EMF $E_b$ lags $V_{ph}$ by the load angle $\delta$. The phasor $I_a X_s$ (or $I_a Z_s$ including $R_a$) is the impedance drop that closes the triangle $V_{ph} = E_b + I_a (R_a + jX_s)$.

| Excitation | Relation | Power Factor | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Unity PF** | $I_a$ in phase with $V_{ph}$ | $\cos\phi = 1$ | $I_a$ phasor is horizontal |
| **Lagging PF** | $E_b < V_{ph}$ (under-excited) | $\cos\phi$ lagging | $I_a$ lags $V_{ph}$ |
| **Leading PF** | $E_b > V_{ph}$ (over-excited) | $\cos\phi$ leading | $I_a$ leads $V_{ph}$ |

**Derivation of Mechanical Power per Phase:**

From the phasor KVL (ignoring $R_a$):

$$V_{ph} = E_b + j I_a X_s$$

Rearranging:

$$I_a = \frac{V_{ph} - E_b}{j X_s}$$

Real power per phase (in phase with $V_{ph}$):

$$P_{ph} = V_{ph} \cdot I_a \cos\phi$$

For the angle $\delta$ between $V_{ph}$ and $E_b$, the component of $I_a$ in phase with $V_{ph}$ equals $\dfrac{E_b \sin\delta}{X_s}$. Substituting:

$$P_{ph} = \frac{V_{ph} \cdot E_b}{X_s} \sin\delta \;\; (\text{W/phase})$$

This is the per-phase mechanical power developed. Multiplying by 3 gives the total 3-phase developed power.

**Conclusion:** Power developed is **proportional to $\sin\delta$**, attaining maximum at $\delta = 90°$ (the stability limit).

**(7 marks awarded as: 2 marks phasor diagrams, 3 marks derivation, 2 marks conclusions)**

#### (b) Numerical Solution

**Given:** $P = 6$, $f = 50$ Hz, $V_L = 6.6$ kV (star), $E_{b,LL} = 4$ kV, $X_s = 10$ Ω/phase, $R_a \approx 0$, $\delta = 20°$.

**(1) Synchronous speed:**

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \;\; \text{rpm}$$

$$\omega_s = \frac{2 \pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.72 \;\; \text{rad/s}$$

**(2) Armature current per phase:**

Per-phase values:
$V_{ph} = \dfrac{6600}{\sqrt{3}} = 3810.5$ V.
$E_b = \dfrac{4000}{\sqrt{3}} = 2309.4$ V.

Taking $V_{ph}$ as reference: $V_{ph} = 3810.5 \angle 0°$, $E_b = 2309.4 \angle{-20°}$.

$$V_{ph} - E_b = 3810.5 - 2309.4(\cos20° - j\sin20°) = 3810.5 - 2170.0 + j790.0$$

$$= 1640.5 + j790.0 \;\; \text{V}$$

$$\vert V_{ph} - E_b \vert = \sqrt{1640.5^2 + 790.0^2} = \sqrt{2691240 + 624100} = \sqrt{3315340} = 1820.8 \;\; \text{V}$$

$$I_a = \frac{1820.8}{10} = 182.08 \;\; \text{A per phase}$$

**(3) Total mechanical power developed:**

$$P_{dev} = 3 \times \frac{V_{ph} E_b}{X_s} \sin\delta = 3 \times \frac{3810.5 \times 2309.4}{10} \times \sin 20°$$

$$= 3 \times 879940.5 \times 0.3420 = 3 \times 300999.6 = 902999 \;\; \text{W} \approx 903 \;\; \text{kW}$$

**(4) Developed torque:**

$$T_{syn} = \frac{P_{dev}}{\omega_s} = \frac{902999}{104.72} = 8624.6 \;\; \text{N·m} \approx 8.62 \;\; \text{kN·m}$$

**(5) Pull-out torque:**

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 V_{ph} E_b}{\omega_s X_s} = \frac{3 \times 3810.5 \times 2309.4}{104.72 \times 10} = \frac{26406220}{1047.2} = 25217.3 \;\; \text{N·m} \approx 25.2 \;\; \text{kN·m}$$

**(7 marks awarded as: 1 mark per sub-question step, 2 marks for pull-out torque formula & final answer)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

> **(a)** Explain the **construction and working principle of a 3-phase synchronous motor**. Discuss the role of the **damper winding** in detail. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz, star-connected cylindrical rotor synchronous motor has a synchronous impedance of **5 Ω per phase**. The motor takes a power input of **15 kW** at a line voltage of **400 V** and operates at **0.8 power factor leading**.
>
> Calculate:
> 1. The line current and per-phase current.
> 2. The induced EMF per phase.
> 3. The load angle $\delta$.
> 4. The developed mechanical power and torque.
> 5. Whether the motor is **over-excited or under-excited**. Justify.
> **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer — Question B

#### (a) Construction & Working Principle

**Construction:**

The synchronous motor has two main parts:
- **Stator:** Laminated silicon-steel core with **3-phase AC armature winding** (double-layer, distributed) housed in uniform slots. Identical to the stator of a 3-phase induction motor. Connected to the 3-phase AC supply.
- **Rotor:** Two practical types:
  1. **Cylindrical (round) rotor:** Used for high-speed (2/4 pole) machines. The air-gap is uniform. Slots are milled on the cylindrical surface to house the DC field winding.
  2. **Salient pole rotor:** Used for low/medium-speed machines. Pole shoes project inward, giving a non-uniform air-gap. The DC field winding is concentrated around the pole cores.

- **Slip rings and brushes:** Two slip rings carry DC excitation to the rotor field winding.
- **Damper (amortisseur) windings:** Copper/brass bars placed in slots on the pole faces, short-circuited at both ends by end rings. They act as a *cage winding*.

**Working Principle:**

1. The 3-phase AC supply creates a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** at synchronous speed $N_s = 120f/P$ in the stator.
2. The DC-excited rotor produces **stationary (in space-frame) north-south poles**.
3. On its own, the rotor is **not self-starting** because the average torque over a cycle is zero (the poles alternately attract and repel). However, the **damper winding** acts as a cage winding and develops **induction-motor-type starting torque**, accelerating the rotor to nearly $N_s$.
4. The DC field is then energised. When the rotor poles approach the rotating stator poles, **magnetic locking** occurs, and the rotor synchronously tracks the RMF at $N_s$.

**Role of Damper Winding:**

- **Starting:** Acts as a cage of an induction motor → provides starting torque.
- **Hunting suppression:** During transient oscillations, the damper bars cut the RMF, inducing EMFs and currents that oppose the relative motion (Lenz's law), damping out oscillations rapidly.

**(7 marks awarded as: 2 marks construction, 2 marks working, 3 marks damper winding discussion)**

#### (b) Numerical Solution

**Given:** 3-phase, star-connected, 4-pole, 50 Hz, $Z_s = 5$ Ω/phase, $P_{in} = 15$ kW, $V_L = 400$ V, $\cos\phi = 0.8$ **leading**.

**(1) Line and per-phase current:**

Per-phase voltage: $V_{ph} = \dfrac{400}{\sqrt{3}} = 230.94$ V.

$$I_L = I_a = \frac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} V_L \cos\phi} = \frac{15000}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 0.8} = \frac{15000}{554.25} = 27.07 \;\; \text{A}$$

**(2) Induced EMF per phase:**

For a synchronous motor, $V_{ph} = E_b + I_a Z_s$. For leading PF, take $\phi = \cos^{-1}(0.8) = 36.87°$ (current **leads** voltage).

Take $V_{ph} = 230.94 \angle 0°$. Then $I_a = 27.07 \angle +36.87°$ (leading).

$$I_a Z_s = 27.07 \angle 36.87° \times 5 \angle \theta$$

Assuming $Z_s$ is purely reactive (no $R_a$ given, treat as $X_s$), the impedance angle is $90°$:

$$I_a Z_s = 27.07 \times 5 \angle(36.87° + 90°) = 135.35 \angle 126.87°$$

In rectangular form:
$I_a = 27.07(\cos 36.87° + j \sin 36.87°) = 27.07(0.8 + j0.6) = 21.656 + j16.242$ A

$j I_a X_s = j \times (21.656 + j16.242) \times 5 = j(108.28 + j81.21) \times \frac{1}{1}$ — recomputing:

$$j I_a X_s = j X_s \cdot I_a = j(5) \cdot (21.656 + j16.242) = j108.28 - 81.21 = -81.21 + j108.28 \;\; \text{V}$$

$$E_b = V_{ph} - j I_a X_s = 230.94 - (-81.21 + j108.28) = 312.15 - j108.28 \;\; \text{V}$$

$$\vert E_b \vert = \sqrt{312.15^2 + 108.28^2} = \sqrt{97438 + 11725} = \sqrt{109163} = 330.4 \;\; \text{V}$$

**(3) Load angle $\delta$:**

$$\delta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-108.28}{312.15}\right) = \tan^{-1}(-0.347) = -19.13°$$

(Negative sign indicates $E_b$ *leads* $V_{ph}$, characteristic of **over-excited** motor.)

$$\delta = 19.13° \;\; \text{(leading)}$$

**(4) Developed mechanical power and torque:**

$$P_{dev} = 3 \times \frac{V_{ph} E_b}{X_s} \sin\delta = 3 \times \frac{230.94 \times 330.4}{5} \times \sin 19.13°$$

$$= 3 \times 15261.2 \times 0.3278 = 15012.4 \;\; \text{W} \approx 15.0 \;\; \text{kW}$$

> Note: Since armature resistance is neglected, $P_{dev} \approx P_{in}$ (no stator copper loss).

Synchronous speed: $N_s = 1500$ rpm, $\omega_s = 157.08$ rad/s.

$$T_{syn} = \frac{15012.4}{157.08} = 95.58 \;\; \text{N·m}$$

**(5) Excitation Status:**

Since $E_b = 330.4$ V/phase > $V_{ph} = 230.94$ V/phase, the motor is **over-excited**. This is also confirmed by the **leading** power factor (0.8 leading), which is characteristic of an over-excited synchronous motor. Over-excited synchronous motors supply **leading reactive power** to the grid, which is why they are used as **synchronous condensers** for power factor improvement.

**(7 marks awarded as: 1.5 marks per sub-question, 1 mark for the excitation justification)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students most commonly lose marks in Synchronous Motor problems:**
>
> 1. **Confusing line and phase values** — A 415 V line voltage means $V_{ph} = 239.6$ V, not 415 V. Most common error; loses **2–3 marks instantly**.
> 2. **Forgetting to use $\omega_s$ (rad/s) for torque** — Some students divide $P_{dev}$ by $N_s$ (rpm) instead of $\omega_s$ (rad/s), giving a torque value off by a factor of $\dfrac{2\pi}{60} \approx 0.105$. **Always** convert: $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi N_s}{60}$.
> 3. **Wrong EMF equation form** — Mixing up the transformer EMF equation ($E = 4.44 f \Phi T$) with the wrong form-factor constant. Use **4.44** for sinusoidal flux.
> 4. **Sign of load angle $\delta$** — In a *motor*, $E_b$ **lags** $V_{ph}$ (positive $\delta$); in a *generator*, $E_b$ **leads** $V_{ph}$. Conflating these gives wrong power sign.
> 5. **Hunting explanation** — Most students write *just one line*. Examiners expect 3–4 lines: cause, effect, suppression by damper.
> 6. **V-curves** — Students forget that V-curves are plotted for **constant load**. For different loads, you get a **family** of V-curves (nested V's).
> 7. **Pull-out vs. starting torque** — These are *different*. Pull-out = maximum running torque; starting = torque at standstill. A synchronous motor has **zero** starting torque *without* damper winding.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Save this section — read it the night before your KTU 2024 exam.**

- ✅ **Synchronous speed** $N_s = 120f/P$ — depends **only** on $f$ and $P$, not on load.
- ✅ **EMF equation** $E_{ph} = 4.44 f \Phi T_{ph} K_w$ — same form as transformer.
- ✅ **Phasor KVL** $V_{ph} = E_b + I_a (R_a + jX_s)$ — armature resistance often neglected.
- ✅ **Power developed** $P_{dev} = 3 V_{ph} E_b \sin\delta / X_s$ — proportional to $\sin\delta$.
- ✅ **Synchronous torque** $T_{syn} = P_{dev}/\omega_s$ — where $\omega_s = 2\pi N_s/60 = 4\pi f/P$.
- ✅ **Pull-out torque** at $\delta = 90°$ — maximum safe operating limit.
- ✅ **Salient-pole** machines have two reactances $X_d$ and $X_q$ (with $X_d > X_q$) — two-reaction theory.
- ✅ **Reluctance torque** exists in salient-pole machines even without DC excitation (basis of reluctance motor).
- ✅ **Synchronous motor is NOT self-starting** — damper winding provides starting torque (induction mode).
- ✅ **Magnetic locking** at synchronous speed once field is energised.
- ✅ **Hunting** = transient rotor oscillation — suppressed by damper winding.
- ✅ **V-curve** = $I_a$ vs. $I_f$ at constant load — V-shaped, minimum $I_a$ at unity PF.
- ✅ **Inverted V-curve** = $\cos\phi$ vs. $I_f$ — inverted-V shape, unity PF at normal excitation.
- ✅ **Over-excited motor** → leading PF → acts as **synchronous condenser** for PF correction.
- ✅ **Under-excited motor** → lagging PF → behaves like an inductive load.
- ✅ **Damper winding** = squirrel-cage in pole faces → used for starting and hunting suppression.
- ✅ **Applications:** constant-speed drives, power factor correction, large compressors, hydro/thermal generators (operated as motors during start-up of large plants).
- ✅ **Unique advantage over induction motor:** controllable power factor, no slip, precise constant speed.
- ✅ **Unique advantage over DC motor:** no commutator, lower maintenance, can run at very high speeds.
- ✅ **Sign convention for KVL:** $V = E + IZ$ for **motors**; $V = E - IZ$ for **generators**.

---

> **End of Module 3 — Synchronous Motor Notes (PCMRT302, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCMRT302 – Electrical Machines & Drives**, **Module 3** lays the foundation for understanding the workhorse of modern industry — the **Three-Phase Induction Motor (TIM)**. Formally, a three-phase induction motor is defined as a **rotating AC electrical machine** that converts **electrical energy into mechanical energy** through the principle of **electromagnetic induction** between the stator (stationary part) and the rotor (rotating part), without any physical electrical connection between them.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** A three-phase induction motor is a self-starting AC motor that operates on a three-phase AC supply, where the rotating magnetic field produced by the stator induces an EMF (and hence current) in the rotor conductors, producing torque due to the interaction of the rotor current with the stator field.

### 1.1.1 Working Principle — Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction

The operation rests on **Michael Faraday's Law (1831)**, which states that an electromotive force (EMF) is induced in a conductor when it experiences a **time-varying magnetic flux**. In a three-phase induction motor:

1. A balanced three-phase AC supply is fed to the stator windings.
2. The three phase currents, displaced by **120° electrical** in time, produce a **Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF)** of constant magnitude rotating at **synchronous speed ($N_s$)**.
3. This RMF cuts the stationary rotor conductors, inducing an EMF (just like a transformer secondary).
4. The induced EMF drives rotor currents (in a closed-cage or wound rotor).
5. The interaction of rotor current with the stator RMF produces **Lorentz force ($F = BIL$)**, which develops **torque** and accelerates the rotor.

> [!NOTE]
> The word **"induction"** comes from the fact that the rotor current is **induced** (not directly supplied), exactly like the secondary of a transformer. Hence the induction motor is often called a **"rotating transformer."**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Rotating Bucket Wheel

Imagine a giant, water-filled circular tank with a **rotating water stream** sweeping around the rim (this is your **stator RMF**). Floating inside the tank is a small, sealed wooden bucket wheel with paddles (your **rotor**). The rotating water stream pushes against the paddles, causing the bucket wheel to spin in the same direction as the water flow.

**Key points of the analogy:**
- The **water stream** = the rotating magnetic field.
- The **paddles** = the rotor bars (where current is induced).
- The **bucket wheel** never catches up to the water — it always lags slightly. This "slip" is what allows continuous induction of force.
- If the bucket wheel somehow rotated at the **exact speed of the water**, there would be no relative motion, no force, and the wheel would decelerate. This is exactly the **"no-load → locked-rotor → slip"** concept.

This simple visualization makes it instantly clear *why* the rotor can **never reach synchronous speed* — and *why* the motor needs a small but non-zero slip to keep producing torque.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights)

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Supply Frequency | $f$ | **50 Hz** (India), **60 Hz** (USA) |
| Number of Poles | $P$ | **2, 4, 6, 8, …** (always even) |
| Synchronous Speed | $N_s$ | **r/min (rpm)** |
| Full-Load Speed | $N$ | **r/min (rpm)** |
| Slip | $s$ | **dimensionless (pu or %)** |
| Air-Gap Flux Density | $B_g$ | **0.4 – 0.7 T** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Hook:** Whenever you see a problem with "50 Hz, 4-pole" — your brain should instantly compute $N_s = 1500$ rpm. This is a high-frequency KTU 2-mark question pattern.

---

## 1.4 Historical Context and Engineering Significance

- **Inventor:** Nicola Tesla patented the AC induction motor in **1888**, and Galileo Ferraris independently described the rotating magnetic field in **1885**.
- **Industrial Dominance:** Roughly **70–80% of all industrial motors** worldwide are three-phase induction motors, due to their rugged construction, low cost, self-starting capability, and minimal maintenance.
- **KTU 2024 Relevance:** Induction motors are covered extensively in the **2024 scheme syllabus** because they directly bridge classical electrical machines (Module 1 & 2) with modern **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)** covered in Module 5.

---

## 1.5 Section Summary Diagram (Concept Map)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[3-Phase AC Supply] --> B[Stator Winding]
    B --> C[Rotating Magnetic Field RMF]
    C --> D[Flux Cuts Rotor Conductors]
    D --> E[Induced EMF in Rotor]
    E --> F[Rotor Current Flows]
    F --> G[Interaction: F = BIL]
    G --> H[Mechanical Torque]
    H --> I[Rotor Spins < Ns]
    I --> J[Slip Exists Always]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Grasp Check:** After reading this section, you should be able to (1) define an induction motor in one sentence, (2) name the physical law governing its operation, and (3) state the one fundamental reason the rotor never catches up with the stator field.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Main Components of a Three-Phase Induction Motor

The induction motor is structurally one of the simplest rotating machines. Let us break it down into its **two main assemblies**:

### 2.1.1 The Stator (Stationary Part)
- **Core:** Made of **silicon steel laminations** (0.35–0.5 mm thick) to minimize **eddy current losses**. The laminations are insulated from each other by a thin varnish coating.
- **Slots:** Stamped on the inner periphery to hold the three-phase winding. The slots are typically **semi-closed** to reduce air-gap reluctance and surface losses.
- **Three-Phase Winding:** Distributed in 120° spatial displacement. The three phases are labelled **R, Y, B** (or A, B, C). The windings may be connected in **Star (Y)** or **Delta (Δ)**.
- **Frame:** Cast iron or fabricated steel housing that supports the core, provides mechanical strength, and aids in heat dissipation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** In KTU 2024, the "why laminations?" question is a guaranteed 3-marker. The answer is: **to reduce eddy current losses**, because solid iron cores would have large circulating currents induced by the alternating flux, causing excessive $I^2R$ heating.

### 2.1.2 The Rotor (Rotating Part)
There are **two principal rotor constructions**:

| Feature | Squirrel Cage Rotor | Wound Rotor (Slip Ring) |
|---|---|---|
| Construction | Bars shorted by end rings | 3-phase winding similar to stator |
| External Resistance | Not possible | Possible via slip rings |
| Starting Torque | Low to Moderate | High (controllable) |
| Maintenance | Almost zero | High (brushes, slip rings) |
| Cost | Low | High |
| KTU Industrial Use | **~90% of applications** | Cranes, lifts, hoists |

The **squirrel cage** gets its name from its resemblance to a hamster's exercise wheel — copper/aluminum bars placed in slots and shorted at both ends by conducting rings.

### 2.1.3 The Air Gap
- The **smallest possible clearance** between stator and rotor (typically **0.4–2 mm**).
- A small air gap **improves efficiency** by reducing magnetizing current, but a *very* small gap can cause **mechanical rubbing** and increase **harmonics**.
- **KTU Insight:** The air gap is the *only* medium through which power transfer occurs via magnetic coupling. A larger air gap = more leakage reactance = poor power factor.

---

## 2.2 The Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF) — Heart of the Induction Motor

### 2.2.1 Mathematical Origin of the RMF

Consider a balanced three-phase supply:
$$
\begin{aligned}
i_R &= I_m \sin(\omega t) \\
i_Y &= I_m \sin(\omega t - 120^\circ) \\
i_B &= I_m \sin(\omega t - 240^\circ)
\end{aligned}
$$

These three currents, displaced in time by **120°**, flow through three stator windings that are **spatially displaced by 120°** around the stator periphery. Each phase winding produces a **pulsating magnetic field** (sinusoidal in time but stationary in space). The **vector sum** of these three pulsating fields is a **single, constant-magnitude, rotating magnetic field** — the RMF.

### 2.2.2 The Synchronous Speed Formula

The speed at which the RMF rotates is called the **synchronous speed ($N_s$)** and is given by:

$$
N_s = \frac{120 \cdot f}{P} \;\; \text{[r/min]}
$$

where:
- $f$ = supply frequency in **Hz**
- $P$ = total number of poles (always **even**)
- The factor **120** = $60 \text{ s/min} \times 2 \text{ poles/electrical cycle}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check for KTU Students:** $P = 2$ gives $N_s = 3000$ rpm at 50 Hz, $P = 4$ gives $1500$ rpm, $P = 6$ gives $1000$ rpm, and so on. Memorize this geometric progression.

### 2.2.3 Direction of Rotation

The RMF rotates from the **leading phase to the lagging phase**. In Indian convention (R-Y-B sequence at $0^\circ, -120^\circ, -240^\circ$), the RMF rotates in the direction **R → Y → B** (clockwise when viewed from the right). Swapping **any two supply leads** reverses the direction — this is the **standard method of motor reversal** in industry.

---

## 2.3 Slip — The Most Important Concept in Induction Motor Analysis

### 2.3.1 Definition

**Slip ($s$)** is the relative speed between the rotating magnetic field and the rotor, expressed as a fraction (or percentage) of synchronous speed:

$$
s = \frac{N_s - N}{N_s}
$$

where $N$ is the actual rotor speed.

**Equivalent forms:**
- $s$ in **per-unit (pu)**: dimensionless, $0 \leq s \leq 1$
- **% slip**: $s \times 100$
- **Slip speed**: $N_s - N$ in rpm

### 2.3.2 Physical Interpretation of Slip States

| Operating Condition | Rotor Speed $N$ | Slip $s$ | Key Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Synchronous (Ideal no-load)** | $N = N_s$ | $s = 0$ | No relative motion, no induced EMF, no torque (theoretical) |
| **Normal Operation** | $0 < N < N_s$ | $0 < s < 1$ | Induction occurs, torque produced |
| **Full-Load (Typical)** | $N \approx (0.95 \text{ to } 0.97) N_s$ | $s \approx 0.03$ to $0.05$ | Highest efficiency region |
| **Starting (Locked Rotor)** | $N = 0$ | $s = 1$ | Maximum current draw, starting torque produced |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Rule:** An induction motor at **synchronous speed = no torque** (it cannot sustain itself). An induction motor at **standstill = maximum slip = maximum induced EMF = high starting current**. The motor *always* operates with $0 < s < 1$ under load.

---

## 2.4 Frequency of Rotor Current

Since the rotor "sees" the relative speed, the frequency of the induced rotor current ($f_r$) is **slip times the supply frequency**:

$$
f_r = s \cdot f
$$

This is a **frequently tested** KTU relationship. At standstill ($s = 1$), $f_r = f$ (50 Hz in India). At full load ($s = 0.04$), $f_r = 2$ Hz — extremely low.

---

## 2.5 Power Flow in an Induction Motor

The power flow follows a clean, sequential path (we will use a Sankey-style diagram in Section 4):

$$
P_{in} \rightarrow P_{stator\;copper\;loss} \rightarrow P_{core\;loss} \rightarrow P_{air\;gap\;power} \rightarrow P_{rotor\;copper\;loss} \rightarrow P_{mech\;developed} \rightarrow P_{friction\;\&\;windage} \rightarrow P_{shaft}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $P_{in}$ | Input electrical power from supply |
| $P_{ag}$ | Air-gap power (power transferred magnetically across the air gap) |
| $P_{cu2}$ | Rotor copper loss |
| $P_{m}$ | Mechanical power developed |
| $P_{out}$ | Shaft output power |

The rotor copper loss is **slip times the air-gap power**:
$$
P_{cu2} = s \cdot P_{ag}
$$

And the mechanical power developed is:
$$
P_m = (1 - s) \cdot P_{ag}
$$

This gives us the elegant result:
$$
\frac{P_{cu2}}{P_m} = \frac{s}{1-s}
$$

**Interpretation:** The rotor copper loss and the mechanical power developed are distributed in the ratio $s : (1-s)$. Since slip is small at full load, most of the air-gap power becomes useful mechanical power — which is why induction motors are highly efficient.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are exam-ready. Memorize the table row-by-row.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Synchronous Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | **r/min** | $f$ in Hz, $P$ = total poles (even) |
| 2 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N}{N_s}$ | **pu** or **%** | $0 \le s \le 1$ under normal operation |
| 3 | Rotor Speed | $N = N_s(1 - s)$ | **r/min** | Direct from slip definition |
| 4 | Rotor Frequency | $f_r = s \cdot f$ | **Hz** | At standstill $f_r = f$ |
| 5 | Angular Synchronous Speed | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \dfrac{4 \pi f}{P}$ | **rad/s** | Useful for torque in Nm |
| 6 | Slip Speed | $N_{slip} = N_s - N = s N_s$ | **r/min** | Also $\omega_{slip} = s \omega_s$ |
| 7 | Air-Gap Power | $P_{ag}$ | **W** | Power crossing the air gap |
| 8 | Rotor Copper Loss | $P_{cu2} = s P_{ag}$ | **W** | Slip times $P_{ag}$ |
| 9 | Mechanical Power Developed | $P_m = (1-s) P_{ag}$ | **W** | $(1-s)$ times $P_{ag}$ |
| 10 | Gross Torque (Developed) | $T_g = \dfrac{P_m}{\omega} = \dfrac{P_{ag}}{\omega_s}$ | **N·m** | $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ |
| 11 | Shaft Torque | $T_{sh} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\omega}$ | **N·m** | Includes $P_{F\&W}$ losses |
| 12 | Shaft Output Power | $P_{out} = (1-s) P_{ag} - P_{F\&W}$ | **W** | Final usable mechanical power |
| 13 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | **%** | Always between 0 and 100% |
| 14 | Power Factor | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} V_L I_L}$ | **pu** | For balanced 3-phase load |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Safety Note:** All absolute-value and "divided-by" notations are written with `\vert` and `\dfrac` to keep the table syntax clean. Do not use raw `|` symbols in your KTU exam answer tables.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The induction motor is the **silent workhorse of industry**. Specific applications include:
- **Pumps and compressors** (centrifugal, reciprocating) — single largest application class.
- **Conveyor belts and fans** — high-reliability constant-speed duty.
- **Traction and electric vehicles** — modern EV traction motors are squirrel-cage induction machines controlled by **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)** (Tesla Model S, Tata Tiago EV).
- **Wind turbine generators** — DFIG (Doubly-Fed Induction Generator) is a wound-rotor induction machine.
- **Home appliances** — washing machines, refrigerators (single-phase variants).

The VFD control of induction motors (covered in Module 5) is the single most important industrial development of the last 40 years, enabling **30–50% energy savings** in pump and fan applications.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Synchronous Speed from First Principles

We will derive the synchronous speed formula using the geometry of a rotating magnetic field.

### Step 1: Define the Electrical Cycle
One complete cycle of the AC supply corresponds to **$2\pi$ electrical radians** of the rotating field. In **$P/2$ pole pairs**, this corresponds to **$4\pi / P$ mechanical radians** (since 1 electrical cycle spans 2 poles).

### Step 2: Time Period of Supply
The supply has frequency $f$ in Hz, so its time period is:

$$
T = \frac{1}{f} \;\; \text{[seconds]}
$$

### Step 3: Mechanical Angle Swept per Cycle
In one time period $T$, the RMF sweeps through **two pole pitches**, i.e., $4\pi / P$ mechanical radians.

### Step 4: Angular Velocity in Mechanical Radians
Angular velocity $\omega_s$ in rad/s is mechanical angle per second:

$$
\omega_s = \frac{4\pi / P}{T} = \frac{4\pi}{P} \cdot f \;\; \text{[rad/s]}
$$

### Step 5: Convert to Revolutions per Minute
To get $N_s$ in rpm, multiply by $\dfrac{60}{2\pi}$:

$$
N_s = \omega_s \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} = \frac{4\pi f}{P} \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi}
$$

### Step 6: Simplify
$$
N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} \;\; \text{[r/min]}
$$

**Result verified.** This is the same KTU formula we used in Section 2.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** In KTU answer sheets, always show this full derivation when a 7-mark question asks "derive the synchronous speed of an induction motor." Examiners award full marks for the logical chain, not just the final expression.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relationship Between Rotor Copper Loss, Mechanical Power, and Slip

### Step 1: Define Air-Gap Power
Let $P_{ag}$ be the total power transferred magnetically across the air gap from stator to rotor.

### Step 2: Power Balance at the Rotor
By conservation of energy, the air-gap power is split into two parts:
- Rotor copper loss ($P_{cu2}$) — dissipated as $I^2R$ heat in rotor resistance.
- Mechanical power developed ($P_m$) — converted to mechanical form.

Therefore:
$$
P_{ag} = P_{cu2} + P_m
$$

### Step 3: Express Rotor Copper Loss in Terms of Rotor Current and Resistance
The rotor current has frequency $f_r = sf$, so the effective rotor resistance is $R_2$ (the DC resistance; rotor reactance is $X_2 = sX_2$ at standstill — but for this loss-based derivation we use $R_2$ directly):

$$
P_{cu2} = I_2^2 R_2
$$

### Step 4: Express Mechanical Power Developed
The mechanical power developed equals torque times angular velocity of the rotor:
$$
P_m = T_g \cdot \omega = T_g \cdot (1-s) \omega_s
$$

The torque can also be expressed using the air-gap power (which is the same torque multiplied by synchronous speed):
$$
P_{ag} = T_g \cdot \omega_s
$$

Hence:
$$
T_g = \frac{P_{ag}}{\omega_s}
$$

Substituting:
$$
P_m = \frac{P_{ag}}{\omega_s} \cdot (1-s) \omega_s = (1-s) P_{ag}
$$

### Step 5: Substitute Back into the Power Balance
$$
P_{ag} = P_{cu2} + (1-s) P_{ag}
$$

Solving for $P_{cu2}$:
$$
P_{cu2} = P_{ag} - (1-s) P_{ag} = s P_{ag}
$$

### Step 6: Form the Power Ratio
Dividing the two expressions:
$$
\frac{P_{cu2}}{P_m} = \frac{s P_{ag}}{(1-s) P_{ag}} = \frac{s}{1-s}
$$

**Conclusion:** Rotor copper loss is $s$ times air-gap power, and mechanical power developed is $(1-s)$ times air-gap power. This is a **high-yield KTU derivation** — appearing almost every year in the 7-mark section.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Equivalent Rotor Circuit Frequency Transformation

In some KTU problems, the rotor circuit is referred to the stator side via the **frequency transformation** technique. Let us derive the relationship between the per-phase rotor resistance and reactance as referred to the stator.

### Step 1: Rotor Circuit at Slip $s$
The rotor resistance is $R_2$ (DC value, frequency-independent) and the rotor reactance at standstill is $X_2$ (proportional to standstill frequency $f$).

At slip $s$, the rotor frequency is $f_r = s f$, so the rotor reactance at slip $s$ is:

$$
X_{2s} = 2 \pi f_r L_2 = 2 \pi (s f) L_2 = s \cdot X_2
$$

### Step 2: Rotor Impedance at Slip
$$
Z_2 = R_2 + j s X_2
$$

### Step 3: Divide Through by Slip $s$
This gives the equivalent impedance at standstill frequency (transformer-type equivalent):
$$
Z_2 = s \left( \frac{R_2}{s} + j X_2 \right)
$$

Defining $R_2' = R_2 / s$ (the *effective* rotor resistance referred to standstill frequency):
$$
Z_2 = R_2' + j X_2
$$

### Step 4: Physical Interpretation
- $R_2' = R_2 / s$ is the **equivalent standstill rotor resistance**.
- $R_2' - R_2 = R_2(1/s - 1) = R_2(1-s)/s$ represents the **equivalent load resistance** that accounts for the mechanical power developed.

This is the basis of the **per-phase equivalent circuit** of the induction motor that you will use extensively in Module 4.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem (KTU Style):** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 4-pole induction motor runs at **1440 rpm** at full load. Calculate:
1. Synchronous speed $N_s$
2. Slip $s$ at full load (in pu and %)
3. Frequency of rotor current $f_r$
4. Slip speed in rpm

### Step 1: Identify Given Data
- $f = 50$ Hz
- $P = 4$ poles
- $N = 1440$ rpm (actual rotor speed)

### Step 2: Calculate Synchronous Speed
$$
N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}
$$

### Step 3: Calculate Slip
$$
s = \frac{N_s - N}{N_s} = \frac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = \frac{60}{1500} = 0.04 \text{ pu}
$$

In percentage: $s = 0.04 \times 100 = 4\%$.

### Step 4: Frequency of Rotor Current
$$
f_r = s \cdot f = 0.04 \times 50 = 2 \text{ Hz}
$$

### Step 5: Slip Speed
$$
N_{slip} = N_s - N = 1500 - 1440 = 60 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Or equivalently:**
$$
N_{slip} = s N_s = 0.04 \times 1500 = 60 \text{ rpm}
$$

### Final Answer Summary Table

| Quantity | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Synchronous Speed $N_s$ | 1500 | r/min |
| Full-Load Speed $N$ | 1440 | r/min |
| Slip $s$ | 0.04 (or 4%) | pu (%) |
| Rotor Frequency $f_r$ | 2 | Hz |
| Slip Speed | 60 | r/min |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examination Pattern:** This exact problem structure appears in nearly every previous year KTU paper. Practice it with different values: 6-pole, 50 Hz, $N = 950$ rpm; or 2-pole, 60 Hz, $N = 3450$ rpm. The methodology remains identical.

---

## 3.5 Sanity-Check & Unit-Conversion Matrix

Before writing the final answer, always cross-check using the following matrix:

| Check | Formula | Verification |
|---|---|---|
| Slip is between 0 and 1 | $0 < s < 1$ | If $s \le 0$ or $s \ge 1$, you have an error |
| $N_s$ is a standard value | $\dfrac{120 \times 50}{P} \in \{3000, 1500, 1000, 750, \ldots\}$ | For 50 Hz |
| $f_r < f$ always | $f_r = s f$ | At standstill $f_r = f$ (verify boundary) |
| $N < N_s$ always | For motoring action | If $N > N_s$, motor is in generating mode |
| Power units consistent | W ↔ kW | $1 \text{ kW} = 1000$ W |
| Torque in N·m | $T = P / \omega$ | $\omega$ in rad/s, $P$ in W |

## 4.1 Block Diagram — High-Level Architecture of an Induction Motor System

This diagram maps the **complete functional flow** from electrical input to mechanical output, with all intermediate power conversion stages:

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Three-Phase AC Mains Supply]:::input
    SW[Stator Winding 3-Phase Y/Delta]:::stage
    RMF[Rotating Magnetic Field RMF in Air Gap]:::core
    ROT[Rotor Conductors Squirrel Cage or Wound]:::core
    EMF[Induced EMF in Rotor E2 = sE20]:::process
    IC[Rotor Current I2 flows]:::process
    TRQ[Electromagnetic Torque Tg Developed]:::process
    MECH[Rotor Spins at N rpm Lt Ns]:::output
    SH[Shaft Mechanical Output Power]:::output
    LS[Slip s = Ns minus N over Ns]:::feedback

    P --> SW
    SW --> RMF
    RMF --> ROT
    ROT --> EMF
    EMF --> IC
    IC --> TRQ
    TRQ --> MECH
    MECH --> SH
    MECH -.->|defines| LS
    LS -.->|modulates| EMF

    classDef input fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stage fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#c2185b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    classDef output fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#d84315,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef feedback fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Power flows left to right (solid arrows). The slip is a *feedback* variable — it controls how much EMF is induced, which in turn controls rotor current and torque. The motor naturally settles at the slip where developed torque equals load torque.


# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are typically **definition-level** or **single-formula** questions. KTU 2024 Scheme mandates **5 questions of 3 marks each** in Part A (total 15 marks).

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define slip of an induction motor. A 4-pole, 50 Hz three-phase induction motor runs at 1440 rpm. Calculate the slip and the frequency of rotor currents.**

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Definition of Slip (1 mark):**
Slip is defined as the difference between the synchronous speed of the rotating magnetic field and the actual rotor speed, expressed as a fraction (or percentage) of synchronous speed:

$$
s = \frac{N_s - N}{N_s}
$$

**Step 2 — Synchronous Speed Calculation (1 mark):**
$$
N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 3 — Slip Calculation (0.5 marks):**
$$
s = \frac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = \frac{60}{1500} = 0.04 \text{ pu} = 4\%
$$

**Step 4 — Rotor Frequency (0.5 marks):**
$$
f_r = s \cdot f = 0.04 \times 50 = 2 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Final Answer:** Slip = 0.04 (4%), Rotor frequency = 2 Hz.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is a rotating magnetic field? Explain its significance in the operation of a three-phase induction motor.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
A rotating magnetic field (RMF) is a magnetic field of constant magnitude that rotates at a uniform angular velocity, produced by supplying balanced three-phase AC to three stator windings spatially displaced by 120° electrical.

**Step 2 — Mathematical Basis (1 mark):**
When three sinusoidal currents $i_R, i_Y, i_B$ displaced in time by 120° flow through windings displaced in space by 120°, the vector sum of the three pulsating fields is a single RMF of magnitude $\dfrac{3}{2} I_m N_{ph}$ rotating at synchronous speed:

$$
N_s = \frac{120 f}{P}
$$

**Step 3 — Significance (1 mark):**
The RMF is **essential** for induction motor operation because it:
- Cuts the stationary rotor conductors, inducing an EMF (transformer action).
- Produces rotor current, which interacts with the RMF to generate **Lorentz force** and hence torque.
- Eliminates the need for a separate DC excitation system, making the motor **self-starting** and **brushless**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module Choice Pattern:** Each module contains **one full 14-mark question** with internal choice. The question has **two sub-parts** (typically 7 + 7 marks). The student must answer either Option A or Option B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**A three-phase, 50 Hz, 4-pole induction motor has a full-load slip of 4%. The rotor resistance per phase is 0.2 Ω, and the standstill rotor reactance per phase is 1.0 Ω. The stator-to-rotor turns ratio is 1:1 for simplicity. The motor draws 30 A per phase from a 400 V (line) supply at a power factor of 0.85 lagging at full load. Mechanical (friction + windage) losses amount to 200 W. Determine:**

**(a) [7 marks]** The synchronous speed, rotor speed, rotor frequency, and slip speed.

**(b) [7 marks]** The air-gap power, rotor copper loss, mechanical power developed, shaft output power, and efficiency.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Synchronous Speed (2 marks):**
$$
N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 2 — Rotor Speed at Full Load (2 marks):**
Given $s = 0.04$:
$$
N = N_s(1 - s) = 1500 \times (1 - 0.04) = 1500 \times 0.96 = 1440 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 3 — Rotor Frequency (1.5 marks):**
$$
f_r = s \cdot f = 0.04 \times 50 = 2 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — Slip Speed (1.5 marks):**
$$
N_{slip} = N_s - N = 1500 - 1440 = 60 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Sub-answer (a):** $N_s = 1500$ rpm, $N = 1440$ rpm, $f_r = 2$ Hz, $N_{slip} = 60$ rpm.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Input Power (1.5 marks):**
$$
P_{in} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 30 \times 0.85
$$
$$
P_{in} = 17670.9 \text{ W} \approx 17.67 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Air-Gap Power via Rotor Copper Loss Relation (2 marks):**
Rotor copper loss per phase: $P_{cu2/\phi} = I_2^2 R_2$ — but we use the relationship:
$$
P_{ag} = \frac{P_{cu2}}{s}
$$

If we assume rotor current $I_2 \approx I_L = 30$ A (for a near-1:1 turns ratio at full load):
$$
P_{cu2} = 3 \times I_2^2 \times R_2 = 3 \times 30^2 \times 0.2 = 540 \text{ W}
$$

Therefore:
$$
P_{ag} = \frac{P_{cu2}}{s} = \frac{540}{0.04} = 13500 \text{ W} = 13.5 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Mechanical Power Developed (1.5 marks):**
$$
P_m = (1 - s) P_{ag} = (1 - 0.04) \times 13500 = 0.96 \times 13500 = 12960 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 4 — Shaft Output Power (1 mark):**
$$
P_{out} = P_m - P_{F\&W} = 12960 - 200 = 12760 \text{ W} = 12.76 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Efficiency (1 mark):**
$$
\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{12760}{17670.9} \times 100 \approx 72.2\%
$$

**Sub-answer (b):** $P_{ag} = 13.5$ kW, $P_{cu2} = 540$ W, $P_m = 12.96$ kW, $P_{out} = 12.76$ kW, $\eta \approx 72.2\%$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Explain in detail the construction and working principle of a three-phase squirrel-cage induction motor with neat diagrams. Derive the expression for synchronous speed and slip. A 6-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has a slip of 5% at full load. Determine:**

**(a) [7 marks]** The full-load speed, synchronous speed, rotor frequency, and the ratio of rotor copper loss to mechanical power developed.

**(b) [7 marks]** If the motor develops an air-gap power of 20 kW, calculate the rotor copper loss, mechanical power developed, and shaft torque (assuming rotational losses of 250 W).

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Synchronous Speed (2 marks):**
$$
N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 2 — Full-Load Speed (2 marks):**
$$
N = N_s(1 - s) = 1000 \times (1 - 0.05) = 1000 \times 0.95 = 950 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 3 — Rotor Frequency (1.5 marks):**
$$
f_r = s \cdot f = 0.05 \times 50 = 2.5 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — Ratio of Rotor Copper Loss to Mechanical Power (1.5 marks):**
$$
\frac{P_{cu2}}{P_m} = \frac{s}{1-s} = \frac{0.05}{0.95} = 0.0526
$$

**Sub-answer (a):** $N_s = 1000$ rpm, $N = 950$ rpm, $f_r = 2.5$ Hz, $\dfrac{P_{cu2}}{P_m} \approx 0.0526$.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Rotor Copper Loss (2 marks):**
$$
P_{cu2} = s \cdot P_{ag} = 0.05 \times 20000 = 1000 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 2 — Mechanical Power Developed (2 marks):**
$$
P_m = (1 - s) \cdot P_{ag} = 0.95 \times 20000 = 19000 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3 — Shaft Output Power (1 mark):**
$$
P_{out} = P_m - P_{F\&W} = 19000 - 250 = 18750 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 4 — Shaft Torque (2 marks):**
Angular velocity:
$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 950}{60} = 99.48 \text{ rad/s}
$$

Shaft torque:
$$
T_{sh} = \frac{P_{out}}{\omega} = \frac{18750}{99.48} \approx 188.5 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Sub-answer (b):** $P_{cu2} = 1000$ W, $P_m = 19$ kW, $T_{sh} \approx 188.5$ N·m.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Marks-Loss Mistakes (Compiled from KTU Valuation Reports):**
>
> 1. **Confusing $N_s$ and $N$:** Students often substitute the wrong value into the torque formula. Always remember: **$T_g = P_{ag} / \omega_s$** (synchronous), **$T_{sh} = P_{out} / \omega$** (actual).
>
> 2. **Slip in percentage vs. per-unit:** A slip of 4% means $s = 0.04$ in calculations, not $4$. Plugging $s = 4$ into the formula will give nonsensical results (e.g., negative rotor speed).
>
> 3. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rad/s:** Torque is always in N·m, requiring $\omega$ in **rad/s**, not rpm. The conversion is $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$.
>
> 4. **Unit mismatch in input power:** For a 3-phase system, always use $P_{in} = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$, where $V_L$ is the **line voltage** and $I_L$ is the **line current**. Using phase values without the $\sqrt{3}$ factor is a common 1-mark killer.
>
> 5. **Ignoring rotational losses:** When asked for shaft output, students sometimes report $P_m$ instead of $P_m - P_{F\&W}$. Always subtract friction and windage losses for the *final* answer.
>
> 6. **Wrong direction of rotation:** If the question says "R-B-Y sequence," the RMF rotates in the **R→B→Y** direction (anticlockwise from the standard view), not R→Y→B. Read carefully.
>
> 7. **Pole number parity:** $P$ must be an **even** integer. A "3-pole" motor is physically impossible in a balanced 3-phase machine.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and stick it on your study wall. These are the absolute must-knows.**

### ⚡ Core Definitions
- ☐ **Induction Motor:** Self-starting AC motor with rotor current *induced* by the stator's rotating magnetic field.
- ☐ **Slip ($s$):** $s = (N_s - N)/N_s$, always $0 \le s \le 1$ for motoring.
- ☐ **Synchronous Speed ($N_s$):** $N_s = 120 f / P$ rpm.
- ☐ **Rotor Frequency ($f_r$):** $f_r = s f$ Hz.

### ⚡ Must-Remember Equations
- ☐ $N_s = 120 f / P$
- ☐ $s = (N_s - N)/N_s$
- ☐ $f_r = s f$
- ☐ $P_{cu2} = s P_{ag}$
- ☐ $P_m = (1 - s) P_{ag}$
- ☐ $P_{ag} = P_{cu2} + P_m$
- ☐ $T_g = P_{ag} / \omega_s$
- ☐ $T_{sh} = P_{out} / \omega$
- ☐ $\eta = (P_{out} / P_{in}) \times 100$

### ⚡ Key Physical Insights
- ☐ Rotor *never* reaches synchronous speed (otherwise no torque).
- ☐ Highest efficiency is achieved at the **slip that minimizes total losses**, typically around 3–5%.
- ☐ Squirrel-cage rotor: simple, robust, no brushes, low cost.
- ☐ Wound rotor: external resistance control, high starting torque.
- ☐ Air gap is the *only* medium of power transfer.
- ☐ Laminations in the core are for **eddy current loss reduction**.

### ⚡ Direction of RMF
- ☐ Sequence R-Y-B (standard) → RMF rotates **R → Y → B**.
- ☐ To reverse: swap **any two supply leads** (e.g., R and Y).

### ⚡ Standard Speed Reference (50 Hz)
| Poles $P$ | $N_s$ (rpm) |
|---|---|
| 2 | 3000 |
| 4 | 1500 |
| 6 | 1000 |
| 8 | 750 |
| 10 | 600 |
| 12 | 500 |

### ⚡ Power-Flow Sequence (Mnemonic: "I Can Accept Real Mechanical Sharp output")
**I**nput → **C**opper loss (stator) → **A**ir-gap → **R**otor copper loss → **M**echanical developed → **S**haft output (after F&W losses)

### ⚡ Exam Strategy Tips
- ☐ Always show **units** in the final answer (rpm, Hz, N·m, W, %).
- ☐ For 7-mark derivations, write the **starting equation**, then **derive step-by-step**; do not skip algebra.
- ☐ For 3-mark definitions, give the **formula** plus a **physical interpretation** sentence.
- ☐ When stuck, draw the **power flow block diagram** — it triggers recall of relationships.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Note for PCMRT302 Students:** Module 3's introduction to the three-phase induction motor is the **gateway** to your VFD and drive studies in Module 5. Mastering slip, synchronous speed, and the power flow sequence here will pay exponential dividends in later modules and in your **B.Tech project work** involving motor control.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SCR - Symbol, Construction and Modes of Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT302 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES & DRIVES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** SCR - Symbol, Construction and Modes of Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Silicon Controlled Rectifier (SCR)** is a four-layer, three-terminal, **PNPN** semi-controlled power semiconductor switching device. It is a bistable (latching) solid-state device that conducts current only in one direction (unidirectional), behaving as an open-circuit in the forward direction until triggered, and as a closed low-resistance switch once activated. The three terminals are the **Anode (A)**, **Cathode (K)**, and **Gate (G)**.

The SCR is the most fundamental member of the **thyristor family** and forms the building block of nearly all high-power industrial converters, inverters, choppers, and AC voltage regulators studied under power electronics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Naming Convention:** Although "SCR" was originally a trade name by General Electric (1957), the IEEE now officially calls it a **Reverse Blocking Triode Thyristor**. In KTU examinations, both terms are accepted, but "SCR" is the most frequently used.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hydraulic Analogy

Imagine a **water pipeline** carrying high-pressure water:
- The **Anode** is the inlet, the **Cathode** is the outlet.
- The pipe has a **spring-loaded flap valve** that naturally stays shut against the high pressure.
- The **Gate** is a small auxiliary pipe connected to a pilot signal. When a small pulse of water is sent into the pilot (a **gate trigger pulse**), the flap is pushed open.
- Once water starts flowing, the rush of water itself keeps the flap open (this is the **latching action**). The pilot signal can be removed.
- The flap can be closed only when the main water flow is fully stopped (i.e., the current falls below the **holding current**).

This is exactly how an SCR works: a small gate current **latches** the device into conduction, and only a **current zero** or reverse bias can turn it off.

## 1.3 Standard Electrical Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

The following ratings and parameters are essential for KTU numerical problems and exam definitions:

- **Latching Current ($I_L$):** The minimum anode current required to sustain (latch) conduction immediately after the gate signal is removed. Typical values: **mA to a few Amperes**.
- **Holding Current ($I_H$):** The minimum anode current required to keep the SCR in the ON state. Typical values: **few mA to hundreds of mA**.
- **Breakover Voltage ($V_{BO}$):** The forward anode-to-cathode voltage at which the SCR turns ON *without* any gate signal.
- **Forward Breakover Voltage ($V_{FBO}$)** and **Reverse Breakdown Voltage ($V_{RB}$):** Manufacturer-rated voltage limits.
- **Gate Trigger Current ($I_{GT}$)** and **Gate Trigger Voltage ($V_{GT}$):** The minimum gate signals needed to fire the SCR. Typical $I_{GT}$: **few mA**; $V_{GT}$: **1 V to 3 V**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Relationship to Memorize:** $\;I_L \;>\; I_H$ **(Latching current is always greater than holding current).** This is a favorite 2-mark KTU question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Layered Structure

The SCR is fabricated as four alternately doped semiconductor layers forming **three PN junctions** ($J_1$, $J_2$, $J_3$):

| Layer | Doping | Region Label | Connected Terminal |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Top (Outer P) | Heavily P-doped | $P_1$ | **Anode (A)** |
| Middle N | Lightly N-doped | $N_1$ | — |
| Middle P | Lightly P-doped | $P_2$ | **Gate (G)** |
| Bottom (Outer N) | Heavily N-doped | $N_2$ | **Cathode (K)** |

The **gate** terminal is physically attached to the inner $P_2$ layer, which is why the gate controls the device from a layer *inside* the structure.

## 2.2 The Three Junctions — What Happens in Each Mode

The behavior of the three junctions changes depending on biasing:

- **Forward Blocking Mode:** $J_1$ and $J_3$ are **forward biased**, $J_2$ is **reverse biased**. Only a tiny leakage current (microamperes) flows.
- **Forward Conduction Mode:** All three junctions are **forward biased**. The device acts like a closed switch with a small forward voltage drop (typically **1.2 V to 2.5 V**).
- **Reverse Blocking Mode:** $J_1$ and $J_3$ are **reverse biased**, $J_2$ is **forward biased**. The SCR blocks a high reverse voltage (up to $V_{RB}$). Beyond $V_{RB}$, avalanche breakdown occurs and the device is destroyed.

## 2.3 Turn-On Mechanism (Step-by-Step)

1. **Apply forward voltage** $V_{AK} > 0$. The middle junction $J_2$ becomes reverse-biased and supports most of the voltage.
2. **Apply gate pulse** $I_G \geq I_{GT}$. This injects holes into $P_2$, reducing the depletion width of $J_2$.
3. **Regenerative feedback** begins. The injected carriers cause more carriers to be released in a chain reaction (see Section 3 — Two Transistor Model).
4. **$J_2$ collapses.** Once the anode current exceeds $I_L$, the device latches and the gate signal can be removed.
5. **Full conduction.** Anode current is now limited only by the external load: $I_A = \dfrac{V_S - V_{AK}}{R_L}$.

## 2.4 Turn-Off Mechanism

- **Natural Commutation:** Reduce the anode current below $I_H$ (e.g., during AC mains zero-crossing).
- **Forced Commutation:** Use an external LC circuit to apply a reverse voltage across the SCR, sweeping the stored charge out of $J_2$.
- A minimum **turn-off time $t_q$** (typically **10 µs to 100 µs**) must be allowed before re-applying forward voltage.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for all SCR derivation and numerical questions.

| # | Formula / Symbol | Meaning | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $I_A = \dfrac{V_S - V_{AK}}{R_L}$ | Anode current under forward conduction | A |
| 2 | $I_L > I_H$ | Latching current must exceed holding current | A |
| 3 | $I_{GT} \;\vert\; I_G$ | Gate trigger condition (typical $I_G$ = 5–50 mA) | mA |
| 4 | $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \to 1$ | Two-transistor turn-on condition (sum of common-base current gains reaches unity) | — |
| 5 | $I_A = \dfrac{\alpha_2 I_G + I_{CBO1} + I_{CBO2}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$ | Two-transistor model anode current equation | A |
| 6 | $P_{loss} = V_{AK(sat)} \cdot I_{A(avg)}$ | Conduction power loss (avg) | W |
| 7 | $V_{BO} = V_{RRM} \cdot (0.6 \text{ to } 0.8)$ | Empirical design relation between breakover and repetitive peak reverse voltage | V |
| 8 | $\dfrac{dv}{dt} \leq \left(\dfrac{dv}{dt}\right)_{crit}$ | Critical rate of rise of forward voltage to avoid false triggering | V/µs |
| 9 | $\dfrac{di}{dt} \leq \left(\dfrac{di}{dt}\right)_{crit}$ | Critical rate of rise of anode current to avoid hot-spot burnout | A/µs |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DC Motor Drives (Choppers):** SCRs control armature voltage for speed control of separately excited DC motors — a core KTU syllabus topic.
- **AC-to-DC Converters (Rectifiers):** Phase-controlled rectifiers (half-wave, full-wave, bridge) for DC power supplies and electroplating.
- **HVDC Transmission:** SCRs handle the highest power levels in long-distance DC links.
- **Static VAR Compensators:** Used in power-factor correction in industrial grids.
- **Battery Chargers, Heater Control, Lamp Dimmers:** Domestic and small industrial applications.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Two-Transistor Model of an SCR — Full Derivation

The two-transistor analogy is the **single most important KTU derivation** on this topic. The four-layer PNPN structure is mentally split into two interconnected bipolar junction transistors:

- **Transistor T1 (PNP):** Formed by layers $P_1$–$N_1$–$P_2$.
- **Transistor T2 (NPN):** Formed by layers $N_1$–$P_2$–$N_2$.

The **gate** is the base of T2, the **anode** is the emitter of T1, and the **cathode** is the emitter of T2. The two transistors are cross-coupled: the collector of T1 feeds the base of T2, and the collector of T2 feeds the base of T1.

### Step 1 — Write the collector currents in common-base configuration.

For the PNP transistor T1, the collector current is:

$$I_{C1} = \alpha_1 I_{A} + I_{CBO1}$$

where $\alpha_1$ is the common-base current gain of T1 and $I_{CBO1}$ is its leakage current.

For the NPN transistor T2, the collector current is:

$$I_{C2} = \alpha_2 I_K + I_{CBO2}$$

where $I_K$ is the cathode current. Since the anode current $I_A$ is the sum of the collector currents plus any leakage at the gate node, we write:

$$I_{A} = I_{C1} + I_{C2}$$

### Step 2 — Express the cathode current in terms of anode and gate currents.

By Kirchhoff's Current Law at the cathode node:

$$I_K = I_A + I_G$$

Note the sign convention: the gate current $I_G$ flows **into** the device at the gate terminal and adds to the cathode current at the emitter of T2.

### Step 3 — Substitute and combine.

Substitute $I_K = I_A + I_G$ into the equation for $I_{C2}$:

$$I_{C2} = \alpha_2 (I_A + I_G) + I_{CBO2}$$

Now sum $I_{C1}$ and $I_{C2}$:

$$I_{A} = \alpha_1 I_{A} + I_{CBO1} + \alpha_2 I_A + \alpha_2 I_G + I_{CBO2}$$

### Step 4 — Collect terms to isolate $I_A$.

Move all terms containing $I_A$ to the left side:

$$I_{A} - \alpha_1 I_{A} - \alpha_2 I_{A} = I_{CBO1} + I_{CBO2} + \alpha_2 I_{G}$$

Factor $I_A$:

$$I_A \left[1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)\right] = \alpha_2 I_G + I_{CBO1} + I_{CBO2}$$

### Step 5 — Final form of the anode current equation.

$$\boxed{\,I_A = \frac{\alpha_2 I_G + I_{CBO1} + I_{CBO2}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}\,}$$

### Step 6 — Interpretation (the "turn-on" condition).

When **no gate current** is applied ($I_G = 0$):

$$I_A = \frac{I_{CBO1} + I_{CBO2}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$$

As $V_{AK}$ increases, the leakage currents $I_{CBO1}$ and $I_{CBO2}$ increase rapidly, and the alphas $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$ also rise. When the **denominator approaches zero** — that is, when $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \to 1$ — the anode current theoretically becomes infinite. This is the **breakover condition** and the SCR turns ON by regenerative action. The breakover voltage is reached when:

$$\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1$$

With a gate pulse applied ($I_G > 0$), the numerator becomes large while the denominator is still small, so $I_A$ rises sharply at a much lower $V_{AK}$ than $V_{BO}$. This is how gate control reduces the firing voltage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical meaning of latching:** Once $I_A$ reaches the latching current $I_L$, the device is self-sustaining. Even if we remove $I_G$ (the numerator shrinks), the alphas are now high enough that the device remains ON. To turn off, the denominator must exceed zero, which requires $I_A$ to drop below $I_H$ and $V_{AK}$ to be reduced below the holding voltage.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mode of Operation vs. Junction Bias

| Operating Mode | Condition on $V_{AK}$ | Gate Signal $I_G$ | $J_1$ | $J_2$ | $J_3$ | Anode Current $I_A$ |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Forward Blocking (OFF)** | $0 < V_{AK} < V_{BO}$ | None | FB | RB | FB | Very small (leakage, $\mu$A) |
| **Forward Conduction (ON)** | $V_{AK} \approx 1.2$–$2.5$ V | Pulse applied then removed | FB | FB | FB | Large, set by load |
| **Reverse Blocking** | $V_{AK} < 0$, magnitude $< V_{RB}$ | None | RB | FB | RB | Very small (leakage) |
| **Reverse Breakdown** | $\vert V_{AK} \vert > V_{RB}$ | None | RB (avalanche) | FB | RB | Large (destructive) |

> *FB = Forward Biased, RB = Reverse Biased.*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SCR Symbol and Terminal Identification

The schematic symbol of an SCR is a **diode symbol with an extra control electrode** entering the cathode-side bar at an angle.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A_node["ANODE A"] ---|positive terminal| SCR_node["SCR SYMBOL: Triangle pointing into Cathode bar with a slanted Gate line entering the bar"]
    SCR_node ---|negative terminal| K_node["CATHODE K"]
    G_node["GATE G"] -.->|control pulse| SCR_node
    style A_node fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style K_node fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G_node fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style SCR_node fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Symbol Convention (KTU Standard):**
- The **triangle** points from Anode to Cathode, indicating the direction of conventional current flow when ON.
- The **gate** line is drawn at an angle (usually 30°–60°) entering the cathode bar.
- The **cathode bar** (straight line) is the side where the gate attaches.

## 4.2 Internal Block Architecture (4-Layer PNPN Cross-Section)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1["P1 LAYER  Anode contact A  Heavily doped P"]
    N1["N1 LAYER  Lightly doped N  Drifts region"]
    P2["P2 LAYER  Lightly doped P  Gate contact G"]
    N2["N2 LAYER  Cathode contact K  Heavily doped N"]
    J1["JUNCTION J1 between P1 and N1"]
    J2["JUNCTION J2 between N1 and P2  Center reverse biased junction"]
    J3["JUNCTION J3 between P2 and N2"]
    P1 --- J1
    J1 --- N1
    N1 --- J2
    J2 --- P2
    P2 --- J3
    J3 --- N2
    style P1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style N1 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style N2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J1 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style J2 fill:#FF4500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style J3 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

**Key Observations:**
- The **gate** terminal is bonded to $P_2$ — the *inner* P-layer, NOT the outermost P-layer.
- $J_2$ is the **middle junction** and is the one that supports most of the forward blocking voltage.
- The two outer layers ($P_1$, $N_2$) are **heavily doped** to reduce ohmic contact resistance.
- The middle layers ($N_1$, $P_2$) are **lightly doped** to provide the wide depletion region needed to block high voltage.

## 4.3 Two-Transistor Equivalent Model (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1["PNP TRANSISTOR T1  Emitter = Anode  Base = N1  Collector = P2"]
    T2["NPN TRANSISTOR T2  Emitter = Cathode  Base = P2  Collector = N1"]
    GatePulse["GATE PULSE IG enters at base of T2"]
    Load["LOAD RL returns to source VS"]
    Source["DC SOURCE VS positive at Anode"]
    CollectorT1["Collector of T1 = P2 = Base of T2"]
    CollectorT2["Collector of T2 = N1 = Base of T1"]
    Source --- T1
    T1 ---|Collector current IC1 feeds base of T2| CollectorT1
    CollectorT1 --- T2
    T2 ---|Collector current IC2 feeds base of T1| CollectorT2
    CollectorT2 -.-> T1
    T2 --- Load
    Load --- Source
    GatePulse -.-> T2
    style T1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style T2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style GatePulse fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Load fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Source fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CollectorT1 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px
    style CollectorT2 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px
```

**Reading the diagram:** The dashed feedback lines (Collector of T1 → Base of T2 and Collector of T2 → Base of T1) show the **regenerative loop**. Once a small gate pulse starts conduction in T2, the collector current of T2 increases the base drive of T1, which in turn increases the collector current of T1, further driving T2 — a positive feedback loop that drives the device into saturation (ON state).

## 4.4 V-I Characteristics Block Description (Adapted Schematic)

Since a true V-I plot cannot be drawn in Mermaid without numeric axis constraints, we describe the four-quadrant operating regions in functional form:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1["QUADRANT I  Reverse Blocking Mode  VAK negative magnitude less than VRB  Only leakage flows  Junction J1 reverse biased"]
    Q3["QUADRANT III  Forward Blocking Mode  VAK positive less than VBO  Gate off  Tiny leakage current  Junction J2 reverse biased"]
    Q3A["Forward Breakover Point at VBO without gate  IGT brings VBO to a lower value VG"]
    Q3 --- Q3A
    Q3A --- Q2
    Q2["QUADRANT II  Forward Conduction Mode  VAK about 1.2 to 2.5 V  IA set by load  All junctions forward biased  Anode current latched above IL"]
    Q4["QUADRANT IV  Reverse Breakdown Region  Exceeding VRB causes avalanche damage  This region must be avoided"]
    style Q1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Q3 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Q3A fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Q2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Q4 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle]**

### **Q1. Define an SCR. Why is it called a semi-controlled device?**

**Model Answer:**
An SCR (Silicon Controlled Rectifier) is a four-layer, three-terminal (Anode, Cathode, Gate) **PNPN** power semiconductor device that conducts unidirectional current when triggered by a gate signal. It is called a **semi-controlled device** because:
- **Turn-ON is controlled** by the gate signal (firing angle $\alpha$ can be varied).
- **Turn-OFF is NOT controlled by the gate** — the SCR turns off only when the anode current falls below the holding current (natural commutation) or via an external commutation circuit (forced commutation).

> [3 marks: definition – 1 mark, semi-controlled explanation – 2 marks]

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between latching current and holding current in an SCR.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Latching Current ($I_L$) | Holding Current ($I_H$) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Definition | Minimum anode current required to *initiate* and *sustain* conduction **immediately after the gate signal is removed** | Minimum anode current required to *maintain* the SCR in the ON state **during continuous operation** |
| Magnitude | Higher | Lower ($I_H < I_L$, typically $I_L \approx 1.5 I_H$ to $2 I_H$) |
| When applied | At the moment of turn-on | Throughout the ON state |

> [3 marks: correct definitions and the $I_L > I_H$ relationship]

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Superset Pattern]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Draw the symbol and the four-layer internal structure of an SCR. Label the three junctions and explain the function of each layer. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Using the **two-transistor analogy**, derive the anode current equation of the SCR and explain how the condition $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1$ leads to turn-on. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

- **Symbol:** A diode-like triangle pointing into a bar (cathode), with an angled control line (gate) entering the bar. Label: A on the triangle base, K on the bar, G on the slanted line.
- **Internal Structure:** Draw the four layers top-to-bottom as $P_1$ – $N_1$ – $P_2$ – $N_2$. Mark the three junctions $J_1$, $J_2$, $J_3$. Attach the **gate** to $P_2$, the **anode** to $P_1$, the **cathode** to $N_2$.

| Layer / Junction | Function |
|:---|:---|
| $P_1$ (heavily doped) | Ohmic contact for the anode; low resistance |
| $N_1$ (lightly doped) | Provides wide depletion region — supports high forward blocking voltage |
| $P_2$ (lightly doped) | Hosts the gate terminal — gate control acts here |
| $N_2$ (heavily doped) | Ohmic contact for the cathode; ensures low ON-state drop |
| $J_1$ | Forward biased in normal operation |
| $J_2$ (the middle junction) | Reverse biased during forward blocking; supports the main blocking voltage |
| $J_3$ | Forward biased in normal operation |

> [7 marks: 2 for symbol, 2 for structure drawing, 3 for layer/junction function]

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

(See full derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes.)

1. **Split the PNPN structure** into a PNP transistor T1 (layers $P_1$–$N_1$–$P_2$) and an NPN transistor T2 (layers $N_1$–$P_2$–$N_2$).
2. **Write the collector currents:**

$$I_{C1} = \alpha_1 I_A + I_{CBO1}$$

$$I_{C2} = \alpha_2 I_K + I_{CBO2}$$

3. **Apply KCL at the cathode and anode:** $I_A = I_{C1} + I_{C2}$ and $I_K = I_A + I_G$.
4. **Substitute and rearrange** to obtain:

$$I_A = \frac{\alpha_2 I_G + I_{CBO1} + I_{CBO2}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$$

5. **Turn-on condition:** As $V_{AK}$ increases (or with gate current), the alphas increase. When $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \to 1$, the denominator → 0, so $I_A \to \infty$ (limited by the load). This is the **breakover / latching condition**. The regenerative feedback sustains conduction even after the gate pulse is removed.

> [7 marks: 1 for transistor identification, 3 for the algebraic derivation, 2 for the turn-on condition explanation, 1 for final boxed equation]

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the three modes of operation of an SCR with a clear V-I characteristic sketch and junction bias analysis. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 230 V, 50 Hz AC supply feeds a resistive load through an SCR. The latching current is 60 mA and the holding current is 20 mA. Determine whether the SCR will (i) turn on at $\alpha = 30°$, and (ii) stay latched when the gate pulse is removed at $\alpha = 30°$. The load resistance is $R_L = 50\ \Omega$. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

The three modes are:

1. **Forward Blocking Mode (Quadrant I):** $V_{AK}$ positive but less than $V_{BO}$, gate OFF. $J_1$ and $J_3$ forward biased, $J_2$ reverse biased. Anode current is the small reverse leakage of $J_2$ (microamperes).

2. **Forward Conduction Mode (Quadrant II):** SCR is triggered (gate pulse or breakover). $J_2$ collapses, all three junctions become forward biased. $V_{AK}$ drops to about 1.2–2.5 V; $I_A = V_S / R_L$.

3. **Reverse Blocking Mode (Quadrant III):** $V_{AK}$ is negative. $J_1$ and $J_3$ reverse biased, $J_2$ forward biased. Only leakage current flows until $|V_{AK}|$ reaches $V_{RB}$, at which point avalanche breakdown occurs and the device is destroyed.

> [7 marks: 2 per mode + 1 for V-I sketch description]

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $V_{rms} = 230$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $I_L = 60$ mA, $I_H = 20$ mA, $\alpha = 30°$, $R_L = 50\ \Omega$.

**Step 1 — Peak supply voltage:**

$$V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{rms} = \sqrt{2} \cdot 230 = 325.27\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Voltage at the firing instant $\omega t = \alpha$:**

$$v(\alpha) = V_m \sin(\alpha) = 325.27 \cdot \sin(30°) = 325.27 \cdot 0.5 = 162.64\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Anode current immediately after triggering:**

$$I_A = \frac{v(\alpha)}{R_L} = \frac{162.64}{50} = 3.25\ \text{A} = 3250\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 4 — Check turn-on condition:**

Since $I_A = 3250\ \text{mA} \gg I_L = 60\ \text{mA}$, the **SCR will turn on** at $\alpha = 30°$. ✅

**Step 5 — Check latching condition after gate removal:**

The current just after triggering is 3.25 A, which is far greater than $I_L = 60$ mA. Therefore, conduction is **latched**. The gate pulse can be removed safely. ✅

**Step 6 — Check holding current during the conduction cycle:**

The current will drop to zero at $\omega t = 180°$ (half-cycle end). It does not fall below $I_H$ until the natural zero-crossing. Hence the SCR turns off naturally at each half cycle and re-fires when the next gate pulse arrives. ✅

> [7 marks: 2 for $V_m$, 1 for $v(\alpha)$, 1 for $I_A$, 1 each for the two checks]

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the units** for $I_L$ and $I_H$ — always write in **mA** as given, not raw A, to avoid unit-conversion confusion.
> 2. **Confusing $I_L$ and $I_H$** in written answers — the latching current is at *turn-on*, the holding current is during *steady ON* state.
> 3. **Skipping the "junction bias" table** in 7-mark questions — KTU examiners award at least 1–2 marks specifically for the $J_1, J_2, J_3$ bias analysis in each mode.
> 4. **Sign error in the two-transistor model** — the gate current $I_G$ enters the denominator sum as $+\alpha_2 I_G$ (positive), because it adds to the cathode current.
> 5. **Failing to mention "regenerative / positive feedback"** in the turn-on explanation — this is the key phrase the model answer uses.
> 6. **Drawing the SCR symbol with the gate on the wrong side** — the gate ALWAYS attaches to the cathode bar (the line side), NOT the triangle (anode) side.
> 7. **Using $|x|$ notation in tables** — this corrupts the markdown. Use `\vert x \vert` for absolute value in KTU answer sheets when writing on paper, but on digital submissions always use the symbolic form.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **SCR = 4-layer (PNPN), 3-terminal (A, K, G) semi-controlled device.**
- [x] **Symbol:** Diode-like triangle + angled gate line on the cathode bar.
- [x] **Internal structure:** $P_1$–$N_1$–$P_2$–$N_2$; gate is bonded to the *inner* $P_2$ layer.
- [x] **Three junctions:** $J_1$, $J_2$, $J_3$. The **middle junction $J_2$** is the controlling one.
- [x] **Three modes:** Forward Blocking, Forward Conduction, Reverse Blocking.
- [x] **Two-transistor model:** PNP T1 + NPN T2 in regenerative cross-coupled feedback.
- [x] **Anode current equation:** $I_A = \dfrac{\alpha_2 I_G + I_{CBO1} + I_{CBO2}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$.
- [x] **Turn-on condition:** $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \to 1$ → denominator → 0 → $I_A$ → large → latched.
- [x] **Latching current $I_L$** is the minimum anode current to sustain conduction **at turn-on**.
- [x] **Holding current $I_H$** is the minimum anode current to **maintain** conduction.
- [x] **Golden rule:** $I_L > I_H$ (always).
- [x] **Turn-off:** Only when $I_A < I_H$ (natural or forced commutation); gate has no turn-off authority.
- [x] **Real applications:** Phase-controlled rectifiers, DC choppers, HVDC, battery chargers, lamp dimmers.
- [x] **KTU favorite 7-mark question:** Two-transistor model derivation.
- [x] **KTU favorite 3-mark question:** $I_L$ vs $I_H$ comparison.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: V-I Characteristics - Basic Concepts of Rectifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT302 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES & DRIVES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** V-I Characteristics - Basic Concepts of Rectifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — Rectifier & V-I Characteristics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rectifier (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A *rectifier* is a static power electronic device or circuit that converts alternating current (AC) supply into unidirectional (DC) current, allowing conduction primarily during one half-cycle of the input waveform while blocking the other. The **V-I characteristic** is the graphical/analytical plot of the instantaneous terminal voltage across the rectifying device versus the current flowing through it, defining the boundary between conduction (ON-state) and blocking (OFF-state) regions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus:** This topic lays the *fundamental groundwork* for understanding controlled and uncontrolled rectifier circuits, their switching behaviour, and their non-linear terminal characteristics. All advanced topics (phase-controlled rectifiers, choppers, inverters) in later modules rely on these characteristics.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

### 🎯 Real-World Analogy — "The One-Way Door"

Imagine a **revolving door at a hotel entrance** that has been modified to *only* allow people to push it **clockwise**. People coming from the other side cannot enter. This is exactly what a rectifier does for electric current:

| Mechanical Analogy | Electrical Equivalent |
|---|---|
| One-way revolving door | **Diode / Thyristor** |
| People entering = current flow | **Forward current (I_F)** |
| Locked side = blocked direction | **Reverse blocking (V_R)** |
| Push force needed | **Forward voltage drop (V_F ≈ 0.7 V)** |
| Maximum push it can withstand before breaking | **Breakdown voltage (V_BR)** |
| Small leak through hinges | **Reverse leakage current (I_R)** |

### 🎯 The V-I Curve — "The Signature of a Device"

Every rectifying element has a **"fingerprint"** — its V-I curve. Plotting **voltage (y-axis)** against **current (x-axis)** gives a complete picture of:
- *When* the device turns ON,
- *How much voltage* it needs to conduct,
- *How much voltage* it can withstand before destruction.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Values (must be memorized for KTU exams):**
> - **Silicon diode forward voltage drop:** $V_F = 0.7\ \text{V}$
> - **Germanium diode forward voltage drop:** $V_F = 0.3\ \text{V}$
> - **Silicon breakdown field:** $E_{BR} \approx 3 \times 10^5\ \text{V/cm}$
> - **Typical reverse leakage current (Si):** $I_R = \mu\text{A}$ range
> - **Latching current of SCR:** $I_L$ (order of mA)
> - **Holding current of SCR:** $I_H$ (less than $I_L$)
> - **Threshold (knee) voltage of Si diode:** $V_K \approx 0.5\ \text{V}$
> - **Thermal voltage at 27 °C (300 K):** $V_T = 26\ \text{mV}$

---

## 1.4 Classification of Rectifiers (Syllabus Snapshot)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU PCMRT302 Module 3, rectifiers are classified as:**

**A. Uncontrolled Rectifiers** (use power diodes — conduction cannot be controlled)
1. Single-Phase Half-Wave Rectifier
2. Single-Phase Full-Wave (Center-Tapped) Rectifier
3. Single-Phase Bridge Rectifier
4. Three-Phase Half-Wave Rectifier
5. Three-Phase Bridge Rectifier

**B. Controlled Rectifiers** (use SCRs/Thyristors — conduction angle $\alpha$ controllable)
1. Single-Phase Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier
2. Single-Phase Full-Wave Controlled Rectifier
3. Three-Phase Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier
4. Three-Phase Fully Controlled Bridge Rectifier
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Regions of a Rectifier Device
