Every rectifying device (diode/SCR) operates in **two distinct regions** described by its V-I characteristic:

### 🔹 Region I — Forward Bias (Quadrant I)
- Anode is **positive** with respect to cathode.
- Once applied voltage exceeds the **threshold / cut-in voltage** ($V_K$), the device starts conducting heavily.
- Current rises sharply; voltage remains nearly constant (≈ $0.7\ \text{V}$ for Si).
- This is called the **forward conduction region** or **ON-state**.

### 🔹 Region II — Reverse Bias (Quadrant III)
- Anode is **negative** with respect to cathode.
- A tiny **reverse saturation current** ($I_0$ or $I_R$) flows — typically in **$\mu$A** for Si.
- If reverse voltage exceeds the **breakdown voltage** $V_{BR}$ (or $V_Z$ for Zener), the device enters **avalanche / Zener breakdown** region — current shoots up, voltage is clamped.
- For normal rectifier operation, this region must be **avoided**.

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Model — The Shockley Diode Equation

The exact V-I relation of a **p-n junction diode** (BJT-style rectifier) is given by the *Shockley Equation*:

$$I_D = I_0 \left( e^{\frac{V_D}{\eta V_T}} - 1 \right)$$

Where:
- $I_D$ = Diode current (A)
- $I_0$ = Reverse saturation current (A)
- $V_D$ = Voltage across the diode (V)
- $\eta$ = Ideality factor (1 to 2)
- $V_T = \frac{kT}{q}$ = Thermal voltage ≈ **26 mV** at 300 K
- $k$ = Boltzmann constant = $1.38 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$
- $q$ = Electron charge = $1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (K)

### Boundary Conditions:
| Condition | Behaviour |
|---|---|
| $V_D \gg V_T$ (forward bias) | $I_D \approx I_0 \cdot e^{V_D / \eta V_T}$ — exponential rise |
| $V_D \ll 0$ (reverse bias) | $I_D \approx -I_0$ — constant reverse saturation |
| $V_D < V_{BR}$ (reverse breakdown) | $I_D$ rises sharply, device may be damaged |

---

## 2.3 Ideal vs. Practical Rectifier — V-I Comparison

| Parameter | Ideal Rectifier | Silicon Diode | Thyristor (SCR) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forward voltage drop ($V_F$) | **0 V** | **0.7 V** | **1.0 – 1.5 V** |
| Reverse leakage current ($I_R$) | **0** | **μA range** | **mA range** |
| ON-state resistance | 0 Ω | very small | small |
| Control over conduction | None | None | Yes (via gate) |
| Turn-OFF mechanism | Automatic | Automatic (at $i = 0$) | Requires forced commutation |
| Knee / cut-in voltage | 0 V | ~0.5 V | ~0.7 V + breakover |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table for last-minute revision — these are the *most-tested* formulas for V-I characteristics & basic rectifiers:**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Shockley Diode Eqn. | $I_D = I_0 \left( e^{V_D/\eta V_T} - 1 \right)$ | A | Universal p-n junction model |
| 2 | Thermal Voltage | $V_T = kT/q$ | V | 26 mV @ 300 K |
| 3 | Dynamic Resistance | $r_d = \frac{\eta V_T}{I_D}$ | Ω | Slope of V-I at operating point |
| 4 | Diode Power Dissipation | $P_D = V_F \cdot I_F$ | W | Heat to be removed |
| 5 | Avg. DC output (Half-wave) | $V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}$ | V | $I_{dc} = V_{dc} / R_L$ |
| 6 | Avg. DC output (Full-wave) | $V_{dc} = \dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi}$ | V | Double of half-wave |
| 7 | RMS output (Half-wave) | $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_m}{2}$ | V | |
| 8 | RMS output (Full-wave) | $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ | V | Same as AC input |
| 9 | Ripple Factor (Half-wave) | $\gamma = 1.21$ | — | Dimensionless |
| 10 | Ripple Factor (Full-wave) | $\gamma = 0.482$ | — | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Rectification Efficiency (HWR) | $\eta = \dfrac{40.6\%}{1}$ | — | Maximum possible |
| 12 | Rectification Efficiency (FWR) | $\eta = 81.2\%$ | — | Maximum possible |
| 13 | Peak Inverse Voltage (HWR) | $PIV = V_m$ | V | Diode rating |
| 14 | Peak Inverse Voltage (FWR) | $PIV = 2 V_m$ | V | Each diode sees $2V_m$ |
| 15 | Form Factor | $FF = \dfrac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}}$ | — | $FF > 1$ always |
| 16 | Transformer Utilization Factor | $TUF = \dfrac{P_{dc}}{V_A I_A}$ | — | TUF $ < 1$ |
| 17 | Latching Current (SCR) | $I_L$ | A | Must exceed to turn ON |
| 18 | Holding Current (SCR) | $I_H$ | A | Must fall below to turn OFF |

> ⚠️ **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the formulas above, the `vert` symbol has been used to denote ratios (e.g. $\dfrac{V_m}{\pi}$) — when copying into your notebook, use proper fractions or parentheses.

---

## 2.5 Why V-I Characteristics Matter in Real Engineering

- **Device selection:** Choose a diode whose PIV rating exceeds the maximum reverse voltage in the circuit.
- **Thermal design:** Forward voltage drop × current = heat; this drives heatsink sizing.
- **Loss estimation:** ON-state losses affect total converter efficiency.
- **Protection design:** Reverse breakdown characteristics drive snubber and MOV design.
- **Drive circuit design:** SCR latching and holding currents dictate gate pulse design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — RMS and Average Output Voltage of a Half-Wave Rectifier

### Problem Setup
A single-phase half-wave rectifier is supplied by $v_s(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$.
The diode conducts from $\omega t = 0$ to $\omega t = \pi$ and blocks from $\pi$ to $2\pi$.

### Step 1: Instantaneous output voltage

$$v_o(t) = \begin{cases} V_m \sin(\omega t), & 0 \le \omega t \le \pi \\ 0, & \pi \le \omega t \le 2\pi \end{cases}$$

### Step 2: Average (DC) output voltage

By definition:

$$V_{dc} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^{2\pi} v_o(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

Substituting the piecewise expression:

$$V_{dc} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) + \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{\pi}^{2\pi} 0 \, d(\omega t)$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \left[ -\cos(\omega t) \right]_0^{\pi}$$

$$V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \left( -\cos\pi + \cos 0 \right)$$

$$V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \left( -(-1) + 1 \right)$$

$$\boxed{V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \approx 0.318 \, V_m}$$

### Step 3: RMS output voltage

By definition:

$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^{2\pi} v_o^2(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi} V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

Using the identity $\sin^2\theta = \dfrac{1-\cos 2\theta}{2}$:

$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2} \, d(\omega t)$$

$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{4\pi} \left[ \omega t - \frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2} \right]_0^{\pi}$$

$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{4\pi} \left( \pi - 0 - 0 + 0 \right)$$

$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{4}$$

$$\boxed{V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{2}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Rectification Efficiency

Efficiency is defined as the ratio of DC output power to AC input power:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{dc}}{P_{ac}} = \frac{V_{dc} \cdot I_{dc}}{V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms}}$$

Since the load is purely resistive $R_L$, the currents are proportional to voltages:

$$\eta = \frac{V_{dc}^2 / R_L}{V_{rms}^2 / R_L} = \left( \frac{V_{dc}}{V_{rms}} \right)^2$$

Substituting values for half-wave:

$$\eta = \left( \frac{V_m/\pi}{V_m/2} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{2}{\pi} \right)^2$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{HWR} = \frac{4}{\pi^2} = 0.4053 = 40.53\%}$$

For full-wave rectifier (both half-cycles conduct):

$$V_{dc} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi}, \quad V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$$

$$\eta = \left( \frac{2V_m/\pi}{V_m/\sqrt{2}} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \right)^2 = \frac{8}{\pi^2}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{FWR} = 0.8118 = 81.18\%}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ripple Factor (γ)

Ripple factor quantifies the amount of AC (ripple) present in the DC output:

$$\gamma = \frac{V_{ac,rms}}{V_{dc}} = \sqrt{ \left( \frac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}} \right)^2 - 1 }$$

For half-wave:

$$\gamma = \sqrt{ \left( \frac{V_m/2}{V_m/\pi} \right)^2 - 1 } = \sqrt{ \left( \frac{\pi}{2} \right)^2 - 1 }$$

$$\gamma = \sqrt{ \frac{\pi^2}{4} - 1 } = \sqrt{2.467 - 1} = \sqrt{1.467}$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_{HWR} = 1.21}$$

For full-wave:

$$\gamma = \sqrt{ \left( \frac{V_m/\sqrt{2}}{2V_m/\pi} \right)^2 - 1 } = \sqrt{ \left( \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} \right)^2 - 1 }$$

$$\gamma = \sqrt{ \frac{\pi^2}{8} - 1 } = \sqrt{1.2337 - 1} = \sqrt{0.2337}$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_{FWR} = 0.482}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Dynamic (AC) Resistance of a Diode

The dynamic resistance is the inverse slope of the V-I curve at the operating (Q-) point.

From Shockley's equation (forward bias approximation):

$$I_D = I_0 \cdot e^{V_D / \eta V_T}$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $V_D$:

$$\frac{dI_D}{dV_D} = \frac{I_0}{\eta V_T} \cdot e^{V_D / \eta V_T} = \frac{I_D}{\eta V_T}$$

The dynamic resistance is:

$$\boxed{r_d = \frac{dV_D}{dI_D} = \frac{\eta V_T}{I_D}}$$

**Numerical example:** If $I_D = 10\ \text{mA}$ and $\eta = 1$ at room temperature:

$$r_d = \frac{1 \times 26\ \text{mV}}{10\ \text{mA}} = 2.6\ \Omega$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix — Side-by-Side Comparison

| Performance Parameter | Half-Wave Rectifier | Full-Wave Center-Tapped | Full-Wave Bridge |
|---|---|---|---|
| No. of diodes | 1 | 2 | 4 |
| $V_{dc}$ | $V_m / \pi$ | $2 V_m / \pi$ | $2 V_m / \pi$ |
| $V_{rms}$ | $V_m / 2$ | $V_m / \sqrt{2}$ | $V_m / \sqrt{2}$ |
| Ripple Frequency | $f$ | $2f$ | $2f$ |
| Ripple Factor $\gamma$ | 1.21 | 0.482 | 0.482 |
| Rectification Efficiency $\eta$ | 40.53 % | 81.18 % | 81.18 % |
| Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV) | $V_m$ | $2 V_m$ | $V_m$ |
| TUF (Half-wave/FWR center-tap/Bridge) | 0.287 | 0.692 | 0.810 |
| Form Factor $FF$ | 1.57 | 1.11 | 1.11 |
| Transformer Required | No (optional) | Yes, center-tapped | No (optional) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 V-I Characteristic Curve of a p-n Junction Diode

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below is a **schematic representation** of the V-I curve. Mermaid is used to capture the *flow logic* and the key operating points (ON/OFF/breakdown) of the characteristic, rather than a literal Cartesian plot.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["START: V=0, I=0"] --> B["Forward Bias Region\nV less than 0.5 V"]
    B --> C["Knee / Cut-in Point\nV_K approximately 0.5 V\nI_K approximately 1 percent of rated I"]
    C --> D["Forward Conduction\nV approximately 0.7 V constant\nI rises sharply"]
    D --> E["Operating Point Q\nV_F rated, I_F rated\nP_D = V_F times I_F"]
    E --> F["Reverse Bias Region\nV less than V_BR\nI_R is microampere order"]
    F --> G["Breakdown Point\nV equals V_BR or V_Z\nReverse current rises sharply"]
    G --> H["Avalanche or Zener Region\nDevice clamps voltage\nDestructive if not current-limited"]
    H --> I["END: DESTRUCTION or ZENER REGULATION"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Rectification Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input["AC Supply\nV_s = V_m sin omega t"] --> tf["Step-Down Transformer\n(Optional)\nV_s reduced to required level"]
    tf --> rect["Rectifier Block\nDiode Bridge or SCRs\nConverts AC to Pulsating DC"]
    rect --> filt["Filter Block\nLC or Capacitor\nRemoves AC ripple"]
    filt --> reg["Voltage Regulator\nZener or IC\nProduces steady DC"]
    reg --> load["DC Load\nR_L or Machine"]
    load --> fb["Feedback\nSensed for control"]
    fb --> reg
```

## 4.3 Architecture of Three Rectifier Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UNCONTROLLED["Uncontrolled Rectifiers - Use Power Diodes"]
        HWR["Single-Phase Half-Wave\n1 Diode\nLowest Efficiency 40.6 percent"]
        FWR_CT["Single-Phase Full-Wave Center-Tap\n2 Diodes\nNeeds CT Transformer\nPIV equals 2V_m"]
        FWR_BR["Single-Phase Bridge\n4 Diodes\nNo CT Needed\nPIV equals V_m\nMost Common"]
    end
    subgraph CONTROLLED["Controlled Rectifiers - Use SCRs or Thyristors"]
        CHWR["Single-Phase Half-Wave Controlled\nFiring Angle alpha controllable"]
        CFWR["Single-Phase Full-Wave Controlled\nQuadrant I and IV operation"]
        C3P["Three-Phase Bridge Controlled\nIndustrial Drive Applications"]
    end
    UNCONTROLLED --> CONTROLLED
    FWR_BR -. "Generalized to" .-> CFWR
```

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Selecting a Rectifier Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1{"DC Power\nRequirement?"} -->|Low less than 100W| Q2{"Cost Critical?"}
    Q1 -->|Medium 100W to 1kW| Q3{"Transformer\nAvailable?"}
    Q1 -->|High greater than 1kW| Q4["Use Three-Phase Bridge"]
    Q2 -->|Yes| A1["Half-Wave Rectifier"]
    Q2 -->|No| A2["Bridge Rectifier"]
    Q3 -->|Center-Tapped| A3["Full-Wave CT Rectifier"]
    Q3 -->|Plain Secondary| A4["Bridge Rectifier"]
    A1 --> OUT["Select Topology"]
    A2 --> OUT
    A3 --> OUT
    A4 --> OUT
    Q4 --> OUT
```

## 4.5 Operating Quadrants of a Thyristor (SCR) Rectifier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Q1["Quadrant I: Forward Conduction Mode"]
        D1["V_AK greater than 0\nI_A greater than 0\nGate pulse applied\nRectifier Mode"]
    end
    subgraph Q2["Quadrant II: Forward Blocking Mode"]
        D2["V_AK greater than 0\nI_A equals 0\nGate OFF\nWait for firing angle alpha"]
    end
    subgraph Q3["Quadrant III: Reverse Blocking Mode"]
        D3["V_AK less than 0\nI_A equals 0\nNaturally OFF during\nnegative half cycle"]
    end
    subgraph Q4["Quadrant IV: Reverse Conduction Mode"]
        D4["V_AK less than 0\nI_A greater than 0\nInverter Mode\nalpha greater than 90 degrees"]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part A questions test *recall, definitions, and direct formula application*. Answers should be 3–5 sentences with one supporting equation or diagram.

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**Define a rectifier. With a neat V-I characteristic of a p-n junction diode, mark the following regions: (i) forward conduction, (ii) reverse saturation, (iii) breakdown.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A rectifier is a static device that converts bidirectional AC supply into unidirectional (pulsating DC) current by allowing conduction in only one direction.

**V-I Characteristic — Three marked regions:**

- **Forward Conduction (Quadrant I):** The diode conducts heavily once the forward voltage exceeds the cut-in (knee) voltage $V_K \approx 0.5\ \text{V}$ for silicon. The forward voltage drop settles at $V_F \approx 0.7\ \text{V}$ and current rises sharply.
- **Reverse Saturation (Quadrant III):** A small reverse leakage current $I_0$ (order of $\mu\text{A}$ for Si) flows when reverse bias is applied, and it remains nearly constant.
- **Breakdown Region:** When the reverse voltage exceeds the breakdown voltage $V_{BR}$, the current rises sharply due to avalanche multiplication, which may destroy the device unless current is limited.

```
   I ↑
     |        /
     |       /  ← Forward
     |      /     Conduction
     |     /
----+----/--------→ V
    |   /  V_K≈0.5V
    |  /
    | /
    |/___________
    |  I_0 (μA)  ← Reverse
    |  ___________   Saturation
    | /  |  ↑
    |/   | V_BR
    |    |  ← Breakdown
```

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Distinguish between the V-I characteristics of a power diode and a thyristor (SCR).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Power Diode | Thyristor (SCR) |
|---|---|---|
| Forward conduction begins at | $V_F \approx 0.7\ \text{V}$ | Forward breakover voltage $V_{BO}$ (gate-unged) or at firing angle $\alpha$ (gated) |
| Forward voltage drop during conduction | $\approx 0.7\ \text{V}$ | $\approx 1.0$–$1.5\ \text{V}$ |
| Reverse leakage current | Very small ($\mu$A) | Slightly higher (mA) |
| Turn-ON control | None — turns ON at $V_K$ | **Yes** — needs gate pulse once $V_AK > 0$ and $i_G > i_{GT}$ |
| Turn-OFF mechanism | Automatic when current reaches zero | Requires $i_A < I_H$ (holding current) — natural or forced commutation |
| Key parameters | $V_F, I_F, PIV$ | $V_{BO}, I_L, I_H, I_{GT}, V_{GT}$ |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part B questions carry 14 marks. They have sub-parts (typically (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks) and require *circuit diagrams, derivations, and numerical solutions*. The University allows an internal choice — students answer **either** Q-A **or** Q-B.

---

### 🔸 **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model]

**Q-A. (a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a single-phase half-wave rectifier with resistive load. Derive expressions for:
(i) Average (DC) output voltage
(ii) RMS output voltage
(iii) Rectification efficiency

**(b)** A single-phase half-wave rectifier supplies a resistive load of $R_L = 100\ \Omega$ from a 230 V, 50 Hz AC source. An ideal transformer with turns ratio 5:1 is used. Calculate:
(i) Peak secondary voltage $V_m$
(ii) DC output voltage $V_{dc}$
(iii) DC load current $I_{dc}$
(iv) RMS load current $I_{rms}$
(v) DC power delivered to load
(vi) Ripple factor

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(a):**

**Circuit Diagram:** Single-phase half-wave rectifier — one diode D in series with the secondary of a transformer and load resistor $R_L$.

**Input:** $v_s = V_m \sin \omega t$

**Diode conduction:** $0 \le \omega t \le \pi$ — diode forward biased, $v_o = v_s$.
**Blocking:** $\pi \le \omega t \le 2\pi$ — diode reverse biased, $v_o = 0$.

**(i) DC Output Voltage:**

$$V_{dc} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^{2\pi} v_o(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi} V_m \sin \omega t \, d(\omega t)$$

$$V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \big[ -\cos \omega t \big]_0^{\pi} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \cdot 2 = \boxed{\frac{V_m}{\pi}}$$

**(ii) RMS Output Voltage:**

$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi} V_m^2 \sin^2 \omega t \, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_m^2}{4\pi} \int_0^{\pi} (1 - \cos 2\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{4\pi} \cdot \pi = \frac{V_m^2}{4} \Rightarrow \boxed{V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{2}}$$

**(iii) Rectification Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{dc}}{P_{ac}} = \frac{V_{dc}^2 / R_L}{V_{rms}^2 / R_L} = \left( \frac{V_m/\pi}{V_m/2} \right)^2 = \frac{4}{\pi^2}$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 0.4053 = 40.53\%}$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(b):**

**Given:** $V_{s,primary} = 230\ \text{V (rms)},\ N_1:N_2 = 5:1,\ R_L = 100\ \Omega,\ f = 50\ \text{Hz}$

**(i) Peak secondary voltage $V_m$:**

$$V_{s,secondary} = 230 \times \frac{1}{5} = 46\ \text{V (rms)}$$

$$V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 46 = \boxed{65.05\ \text{V}}$$

**(ii) DC output voltage:**

$$V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} = \frac{65.05}{\pi} = \boxed{20.71\ \text{V}}$$

**(iii) DC load current:**

$$I_{dc} = \frac{V_{dc}}{R_L} = \frac{20.71}{100} = \boxed{0.2071\ \text{A} = 207.1\ \text{mA}}$$

**(iv) RMS load current:**

$$V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{2} = \frac{65.05}{2} = 32.53\ \text{V}$$

$$I_{rms} = \frac{V_{rms}}{R_L} = \frac{32.53}{100} = \boxed{0.3253\ \text{A}}$$

**(v) DC power to load:**

$$P_{dc} = V_{dc} \cdot I_{dc} = 20.71 \times 0.2071 = \boxed{4.29\ \text{W}}$$

**(vi) Ripple factor:**

$$\gamma = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}}\right)^2 - 1} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{32.53}{20.71}\right)^2 - 1}$$

$$\gamma = \sqrt{2.467 - 1} = \sqrt{1.467} = \boxed{1.211}$$

---

### 🔸 **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**Q-B. (a)** With a neat circuit diagram and waveforms, explain the operation of a single-phase full-wave bridge rectifier with resistive load. Derive expressions for average and RMS output voltages and rectification efficiency.

**(b)** For a single-phase bridge rectifier, the input AC voltage is $230\ \text{V},\ 50\ \text{Hz}$ and the load resistance is $50\ \Omega$. If the diode forward resistance is $r_f = 0.5\ \Omega$, calculate:
(i) Peak, average, and RMS output voltage
(ii) DC power delivered to load
(iii) AC input power
(iv) Rectification efficiency
(v) Peak inverse voltage (PIV) of each diode

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(a):**

**Circuit Diagram:** Four diodes $D_1, D_2, D_3, D_4$ arranged in bridge form; AC source applied across one diagonal, DC load across the other diagonal.

**Operation:**
- **Positive half-cycle** ($\omega t$ from $0$ to $\pi$): $D_1$ and $D_3$ conduct. Current flows through load top-to-bottom.
- **Negative half-cycle** ($\omega t$ from $\pi$ to $2\pi$): $D_2$ and $D_4$ conduct. Current still flows through load top-to-bottom (unidirectional).

**Waveform:** Output is $|V_m \sin \omega t|$ for the full cycle.

**(i) DC output voltage:**

$$V_{dc} = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_0^{\pi} V_m \sin \omega t \, d(\omega t) = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}$$

**(ii) RMS output voltage:**

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi} \int_0^{\pi} V_m^2 \sin^2 \omega t \, d(\omega t)} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**(iii) Rectification efficiency:**

$$\eta = \left( \frac{V_{dc}}{V_{rms}} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{2V_m/\pi}{V_m/\sqrt{2}} \right)^2 = \frac{8}{\pi^2} = 0.812 = 81.2\%$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(b):**

**Given:** $V_s = 230\ \text{V rms},\ f = 50\ \text{Hz},\ R_L = 50\ \Omega,\ r_f = 0.5\ \Omega$

**(i) Peak, average, RMS output voltage:**

$$V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 230 = 325.27\ \text{V (peak)}$$

$$V_{dc} = \frac{2V_m}{\pi} = \frac{2 \times 325.27}{\pi} = \boxed{207.07\ \text{V}}$$

$$V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{325.27}{\sqrt{2}} = \boxed{230\ \text{V}}$$

**(ii) DC power to load (accounting for diode drop):**

Total forward resistance per cycle = $2 r_f$ (two diodes conduct in series with load).

$$I_{dc} = \frac{V_{dc}}{R_L + 2 r_f} = \frac{207.07}{50 + 1} = \boxed{4.06\ \text{A}}$$

$$P_{dc} = V_{dc} \cdot I_{dc} = 207.07 \times 4.06 = \boxed{840.7\ \text{W}}$$

**(iii) AC input power:**

$$I_{rms} = \frac{V_{rms}}{R_L + 2 r_f} = \frac{230}{51} = 4.51\ \text{A}$$

$$P_{ac} = V_s \cdot I_s = 230 \times 4.51 = \boxed{1037.3\ \text{W}}$$

(Or equivalently, $P_{ac} = I_{rms}^2 (R_L + 2 r_f) = 4.51^2 \times 51 \approx 1037.3\ \text{W}$.)

**(iv) Rectification efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{dc}}{P_{ac}} = \frac{840.7}{1037.3} = \boxed{0.8104 = 81.04\%}$$

(Slightly less than ideal 81.2% due to diode forward resistance.)

**(v) Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV):**

In a bridge rectifier, when $D_1$ and $D_3$ conduct, $D_2$ and $D_4$ are reverse biased. The voltage across each blocking diode is $V_m$ (the full secondary peak).

$$\boxed{PIV = V_m = 325.27\ \text{V}}$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks — V-I Characteristics & Rectifier Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert RMS to peak:** Most numerical problems give the *RMS* value of AC supply. Students use $V_{s,rms}$ directly in place of $V_m$ in $V_{dc} = V_m/\pi$, leading to **2 of 7 marks lost** instantly. **Always** compute $V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{rms}$ **first**.
>
> 2. **Mixing Half-Wave and Full-Wave formulas:** The factor of 2 in $V_{dc} = 2V_m/\pi$ for full-wave is frequently missed. The same for PIV: $V_m$ for half-wave, $2V_m$ for full-wave center-tap, $V_m$ for bridge.
>
> 3. **Using ideal formulas with non-ideal data:** When the problem gives a diode forward resistance $r_f$, students *still* use $V_{dc} = 2V_m/\pi$ **without** accounting for the drop. Result: ~5% efficiency error → lose 2 marks.
>
> 4. **Confusing PIV and Breakdown Voltage:** PIV is the *maximum reverse voltage the diode experiences in the circuit*, not the diode's intrinsic $V_{BR}$. $V_{BR}$ must be > PIV.
>
> 5. **Drawing incomplete V-I curves:** V-I must show *all three regions* (forward, reverse saturation, breakdown) with proper quadrant labelling. Half the marks for this question are for the *diagram*.
>
> 6. **Wrong ripple factor formula:** Use $\gamma = \sqrt{(V_{rms}/V_{dc})^2 - 1}$, not $\gamma = (V_{rms} - V_{dc})/V_{dc}$. The latter is a *common wrong shortcut*.
>
> 7. **Ignoring the load resistance unit:** A common slip is to keep $R_L$ in k$\Omega$ but compute current in A. Always write units explicitly.

---

## 5.4 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **Pin this list before the exam — every point is KTU-tested at some point.**

- [ ] Rectifier = AC → DC converter; **non-linear device**, characterized by V-I curve.
- [ ] **V-I characteristic** has THREE regions: **forward bias (quadrant I), reverse saturation (quadrant III), breakdown (extreme quadrant III).**
- [ ] **Silicon diode:** $V_F \approx 0.7\ \text{V}$, $V_K \approx 0.5\ \text{V}$. **Germanium:** $V_F \approx 0.3\ \text{V}$.
- [ ] **Shockley equation:** $I_D = I_0 \left( e^{V_D/\eta V_T} - 1 \right)$; thermal voltage $V_T = 26\ \text{mV}$ at 300 K.
- [ ] **Dynamic resistance:** $r_d = \eta V_T / I_D$ (small-signal slope of V-I curve).
- [ ] **Half-wave:** $V_{dc} = V_m/\pi$, $V_{rms} = V_m/2$, $\eta = 40.6\%$, $\gamma = 1.21$, PIV $= V_m$, ripple freq $= f$.
- [ ] **Full-wave (CT or bridge):** $V_{dc} = 2V_m/\pi$, $V_{rms} = V_m/\sqrt{2}$, $\eta = 81.2\%$, $\gamma = 0.482$, ripple freq $= 2f$.
- [ ] **Bridge vs CT:** Bridge uses 4 diodes, no center-tapped transformer, PIV $= V_m$. CT uses 2 diodes, needs center-tap, PIV $= 2V_m$.
- [ ] **Ripple factor** = measure of residual AC in DC; **lower is better**.
- [ ] **TUF (Transformer Utilization Factor):** HWR = 0.287, CT-FWR = 0.692, Bridge = 0.810. **Bridge is most efficient utilization of transformer.**
- [ ] **Form Factor** $= V_{rms}/V_{dc}$; for pure DC, $FF = 1$.
- [ ] **SCR / Thyristor:** Conduction controlled by gate pulse; must exceed **latching current $I_L$** to turn ON, must drop below **holding current $I_H$** to turn OFF. $I_H < I_L$ always.
- [ ] **Firing angle $\alpha$:** delay angle of gate pulse; $\alpha = 0$ → diode-like behaviour; $\alpha = 90°$ → half-power; $\alpha = 180°$ → no output.
- [ ] **Uncontrolled rectifiers** = diodes (no firing control); **Controlled rectifiers** = SCRs (firing angle $\alpha$ settable).
- [ ] **Diode selection criteria:** $V_{BR} > PIV$, $I_F(\text{rated}) > I_{dc}$, and thermal dissipation $P_D = V_F I_F$ within heatsink capacity.
- [ ] **Phase relation:** Output frequency of HWR = input frequency $f$. Output frequency of FWR = $2f$.
- [ ] **Peak current rating:** $I_{peak} = V_m / R_L$ for resistive load (the diode sees this peak for an instant at $\omega t = \pi/2$).
- [ ] **For KTU numericals:** **always draw the circuit diagram first** — examiners award 1–2 marks purely for the diagram.

> ✅ **One-line takeaway for V-I characteristics & basic rectifiers:** *"A rectifier's V-I curve is its identity — it tells you when it conducts, how much it drops, and when it dies. The DC output of an ideal rectifier should be a flat line; everything else (ripple, efficiency, PIV) tells you how far your real rectifier is from that ideal."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Phase Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier with R Load
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT302 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES & DRIVES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Single Phase Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier with R Load # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Single-Phase Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier with R Load** is a phase-controlled power-electronic converter that uses a single **Silicon Controlled Rectifier (SCR / Thyristor)** to convert a sinusoidal AC input voltage $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ into a unidirectional, variable-magnitude DC output voltage across a purely **resistive load $R$**. The magnitude of the DC output is regulated by the **firing (trigger) angle $\alpha$** at which the thyristor is gated into conduction during each positive half-cycle. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic is the foundational building block for all converter topologies (semi-converters, full converters, dual converters) covered in Module 3 of PCMRT302. The expressions derived here for $V_{dc}$, $V_{rms}$, FF, RF, $\eta$ and TUF are directly reused in Module 4 (choppers) and Module 5 (inverters) with sign/structural modifications. ## 1.2 The Four Defining Components | # | Component | Symbol | Function | |---|-----------|--------|----------| | 1 | AC Source | $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ | Supplies sinusoidal input at 50 Hz, 230 V RMS (India). | | 2 | Thyristor | $T_1$ (SCR) | Unidirectional controlled switch; latches ON once gated. | | 3 | Gate Pulse Source | $i_g$ at $\omega t = \alpha$ | Initiates conduction only when $v_s > 0$. | | 4 | Resistive Load | $R$ | Purely dissipative; $v_o = i_o R$, instantaneous power always $\geq 0$. | ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Smart Water Tap" Imagine a pressurized water pipe (the AC source) that pushes water first forward, then sucks backward in alternating cycles. Mount a **one-way valve** (the thyristor) on the pipe: - The valve **only opens forward** — so the "sucking" (negative half-cycle) is automatically blocked. - Even during the forward push, the valve stays **closed until you press a button** (apply the gate pulse). - The **delay between the push starting and your button press** = the **firing angle $\alpha$**. - **Press early** ($\alpha \to 0$) → most of the water gets through → **high average flow (DC)**. - **Press late** ($\alpha \to 180^\circ$) → almost no water gets through → **low average flow (DC)**. This is precisely how a half-wave controlled rectifier behaves: the **earlier** you fire the SCR during the positive half-cycle, the **larger** the average DC output voltage. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Consequence for R Load:** The thyristor turns OFF naturally at $\omega t = \pi$ because the load current falls to zero exactly when the AC voltage crosses zero. There is **no negative load voltage** and **no forced commutation** is required — this makes the R-load case the simplest of all controlled-rectifier topologies. ## 1.4 Critical Vocabulary (Must Memorise for KTU Viva) - **Firing angle $\alpha$** — the angle (from the **positive-going zero crossing** of $v_s$) at which the gate pulse is applied. Range for R load: $0 \leq \alpha \leq \pi$ (or $180^\circ$). - **Conduction angle $\gamma$** — the angular duration for which the SCR actually conducts. For R load: $\gamma = \pi - \alpha$. - **Extinction angle $\beta$** — the angle at which conduction ceases. For R load: $\beta = \pi$. - **Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV)** — the maximum reverse voltage the SCR must block. For R load: $\mathbf{PIV = V_m}$. - **Average (DC) output voltage $V_{dc}$** — arithmetic mean of the rectified waveform over a full cycle $0$ to $2\pi$. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mode-by-Mode Operation (R Load) The circuit operation over one full cycle of $v_s$ can be divided into **two distinct modes**: ### Mode 1 — Blocking Mode ($0 \leq \omega t < \alpha$) - Supply is in the **positive half-cycle**, so the SCR is **forward biased** ($v_{AK} > 0$). - However, **no gate pulse** has been applied yet. - SCR remains **OFF** (in blocking state). Output voltage: $v_o = 0$, output current: $i_o = 0$. - Voltage across SCR: $v_T = v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ (all the source voltage drops across the device). ### Mode 2 — Conduction Mode ($\alpha \leq \omega t \leq \pi$) - Gate pulse arrives at $\omega t = \alpha$. - SCR turns **ON** (latches) and behaves as a closed switch (ideal: $v_T = 0$). - Output voltage: $v_o = v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$. - Output current: $i_o = v_o / R = \dfrac{V_m \sin(\omega t)}{R}$. - Conduction ends at $\omega t = \pi$ because $i_o$ falls to zero naturally. ### Mode 3 — Reverse Blocking Mode ($\pi \leq \omega t \leq 2\pi$) - Supply is in the **negative half-cycle**; SCR is **reverse biased**. - $v_{AK} < 0$; SCR cannot conduct regardless of gate signal. - $v_o = 0$, $i_o = 0$. - The voltage across the SCR reaches its **most negative value of $-V_m$ at $\omega t = 3\pi/2$** — this defines the PIV. > [!TIP] > **Why $\alpha$ cannot exceed $\pi$ for R Load:** Beyond $\omega t = \pi$, the SCR is reverse biased. Applying a gate pulse at $\alpha > \pi$ is futile. Therefore, the **maximum useful firing angle for an R load is $\alpha = \pi$**, which gives $V_{dc} = 0$ (no conduction, no power). ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is **exam-critical**. All derivations in SECTION 3 stem from these expressions. Note the careful use of `\vert` (NOT pipe) to keep the markdown table valid. | # | Quantity | Formula | Unit / Range | |---|----------|---------|--------------| | 1 | Instantaneous source voltage | $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ | V | | 2 | Instantaneous output voltage | $v_o = \begin{cases} 0, & 0 \le \omega t < \alpha \\ V_m \sin(\omega t), & \alpha \le \omega t \le \pi \\ 0, & \pi \le \omega t \le 2\pi \end{cases}$ | V | | 3 | Instantaneous output current | $i_o = v_o / R$ | A | | 4 | **Average (DC) output voltage** | $V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}\bigl(1 + \cos \alpha\bigr)$ | V | | 5 | **RMS output voltage** | $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_m}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\!\left(\pi - \alpha + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)}$ | V | | 6 | RMS output current | $I_{rms} = V_{rms} / R$ | A | | 7 | Average output current | $I_{dc} = V_{dc} / R$ | A | | 8 | Form Factor (FF) | $\text{FF} = V_{rms} / V_{dc}$ | $\geq 1$, dimensionless | | 9 | Ripple Factor (RF) | $\text{RF} = \sqrt{\text{FF}^2 - 1}$ | dimensionless | | 10 | **Rectification Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{dc}^2}{V_{rms}^2} \times 100\% = \dfrac{40.6}{(1 + \cos\alpha)^2 \cdot \bigl[\pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\bigr]}\times 100$ | %, max 40.53 % at $\alpha=0$ | | 11 | Transformer Utilization Factor | $\text{TUF} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}\,V_{dc}^2}{V_m \, V_{rms}}$ | max $2\sqrt{2}/\pi^2 \approx 0.2865$ at $\alpha=0$ | | 12 | Supply Power Factor | $\text{PF} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}\,V_{rms}}{V_m} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi - \alpha + \sin 2\alpha/2}{\pi}}$ | max $0.707$ at $\alpha=0$ | | 13 | PIV of SCR | $\text{PIV} = V_m$ | V | | 14 | Conduction angle | $\gamma = \pi - \alpha$ | rad / deg | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **DC motor speed control** (low-power, fractional-HP applications) — historically the textbook application. - **Battery chargers** for low-voltage DC batteries. - **Lamp dimming circuits** (incandescent and halogen) using the firing-angle principle. - **Heater / furnace temperature control** in industrial ovens. - **Educational lab setup** to teach SCR triggering, commutation, and power-quality concepts. > [!NOTE] > In modern industry, the half-wave topology is **rarely used in production** because of its very poor TUF (~28.6 %) and high ripple. It is studied purely for **pedagogical reasons** — it builds the analytical foundation for full-wave and bridge converters that follow in KTU Module 3. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** Derivations in SECTION 3 must be reproduced **in full, step-by-step form** during the End Semester Evaluation. Marks are split as: (i) Setting up the integral limits = 2 marks, (ii) Correct integration = 3 marks, (iii) Final simplified expression = 2 marks. Skipping the integration steps leads to a flat 40 % deduction in this section. ## 3.1 Derivation of Average Output Voltage $V_{dc}$ By the formal definition of the average value of a periodic waveform over one full cycle $T$: $$ V_{dc} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} v_o(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) $$ **Step 1 — Substitute the piecewise expression for $v_o$.** Since $v_o = 0$ in the intervals $[0, \alpha]$ and $[\pi, 2\pi]$, the integral collapses to a single interval: $$ V_{dc} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) $$ **Step 2 — Pull the constant $V_m$ out and integrate.** $$ V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \left[-\cos(\omega t)\right]_{\alpha}^{\pi} $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the cosine terms at the upper and lower limits.** $$ V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \Bigl[ -\cos(\pi) + \cos(\alpha) \Bigr] $$ **Step 4 — Apply $\cos(\pi) = -1$ and simplify.** $$ V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \bigl[ 1 + \cos(\alpha) \bigr] $$ $$ \boxed{\,V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\bigl(1 + \cos \alpha\bigr)\,} $$ **Sanity Check:** - At $\alpha = 0$: $V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + 1) = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi} \approx 0.318\, V_m$ — matches the well-known **uncontrolled** half-wave rectifier value. ✓ - At $\alpha = \pi$: $V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 - 1) = 0$ — zero output, as expected when SCR never fires. ✓ - At $\alpha = \pi/2$: $V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + 0) = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi} \approx 0.159\,V_m$ — half the maximum. ✓ ## 3.2 Derivation of RMS Output Voltage $V_{rms}$ By the formal definition: $$ V_{rms}^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} v_o^2(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) $$ **Step 1 — Substitute the piecewise expression for $v_o$.** $$ V_{rms}^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) $$ **Step 2 — Use the trigonometric identity $\sin^2 \theta = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$.** $$ V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{4\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \bigl[1 - \cos(2\omega t)\bigr]\, d(\omega t) $$ **Step 3 — Perform the integration term-by-term.** $$ V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{4\pi} \left[ \omega t - \frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2} \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi} $$ **Step 4 — Apply the limits.** - At $\omega t = \pi$: $\pi - \dfrac{\sin(2\pi)}{2} = \pi - 0 = \pi$. - At $\omega t = \alpha$: $\alpha - \dfrac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}$. $$ V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{4\pi} \left[ \pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2} \right] $$ **Step 5 — Take the square root to obtain $V_{rms}$.** $$ \boxed{\,V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{2}\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)}\,} $$ **Sanity Check (at $\alpha = 0$):** $$ V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{2}\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - 0 + 0\right)} = \frac{V_m}{2} $$ This matches the RMS of a half sinusoid (uncontrolled half-wave rectifier). ✓ ## 3.3 Derivation of Form Factor (FF) By definition: $$ \text{FF} = \frac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}} $$ **Substituting the expressions derived above:** $$ \text{FF} = \frac{ \dfrac{V_m}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi - \alpha + \sin 2\alpha/2}{\pi}} }{ \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha) } $$ **Simplifying the constants and $V_m$:** $$ \text{FF} = \frac{\pi\sqrt{(\pi - \alpha + \sin 2\alpha/2)/\pi}}{1 + \cos\alpha} $$ $$ \boxed{\,\text{FF} = \frac{\pi}{(1 + \cos\alpha)}\sqrt{\frac{\pi - \alpha + \sin 2\alpha/2}{\pi}}\,} $$ **At $\alpha = 0$:** $\text{FF} = \dfrac{\pi}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi}{\pi}} = \dfrac{\pi}{2} \approx 1.571$. ✓ (matches standard textbook value) ## 3.4 Derivation of Ripple Factor (RF) The ripple factor quantifies the **AC (ripple) content** relative to the DC content. By the standard AC-circuit identity: $$ V_{rms}^2 = V_{dc}^2 + V_{ac,rms}^2 $$ **Step 1 — Isolate the AC component.** $$ V_{ac,rms}^2 = V_{rms}^2 - V_{dc}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Apply the formal definition of RF.** $$ \text{RF} = \frac{V_{ac,rms}}{V_{dc}} = \frac{\sqrt{V_{rms}^2 - V_{dc}^2}}{V_{dc}} $$ **Step 3 — Factor out $V_{dc}$.** $$ \text{RF} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}}\right)^2 - 1} = \sqrt{\text{FF}^2 - 1} $$ $$ \boxed{\,\text{RF} = \sqrt{\text{FF}^2 - 1}\,} $$ **At $\alpha = 0$:** $\text{RF} = \sqrt{(\pi/2)^2 - 1} = \sqrt{2.467 - 1} = \sqrt{1.467} \approx 1.211$. ✓ ## 3.5 Derivation of Rectification Efficiency $\eta$ Rectification efficiency is the ratio of **DC power delivered** to the load to the **total AC power drawn** from the source: $$ \eta = \frac{P_{dc}}{P_{ac}} \times 100\% $$ **Step 1 — Write each power in terms of voltage and resistance $R$.** $$ P_{dc} = \frac{V_{dc}^2}{R} \qquad\qquad P_{ac} = \frac{V_{rms}^2}{R} $$ **Step 2 — Form the ratio — the $R$ cancels.** $$ \eta = \frac{V_{dc}^2 / R}{V_{rms}^2 / R} = \left(\frac{V_{dc}}{V_{rms}}\right)^{\!2} \times 100\% = \frac{1}{\text{FF}^2}\times 100\% $$ $$ \boxed{\,\eta = \left(\frac{V_{dc}}{V_{rms}}\right)^{2} \times 100\% = \frac{1}{\text{FF}^{2}} \times 100\%\,} $$ **Maximum value (at $\alpha = 0$):** $$ \eta_{max} = \left(\frac{V_m/\pi}{V_m/2}\right)^2 \times 100\% = \left(\frac{2}{\pi}\right)^2 \times 100\% = \frac{4}{\pi^2} \times 100\% \approx 40.53\% $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Pearl:** The maximum theoretical efficiency of a **half-wave** rectifier is only **40.53 %**. This is one of the main reasons full-wave / bridge topologies (Module 3, upcoming topics) replaced half-wave circuits in industry — they can theoretically reach **81.06 %**. ## 3.6 Derivation of Transformer Utilization Factor (TUF) TUF compares the **DC output power** to the **VA rating of the transformer secondary**. $$ \text{TUF} = \frac{P_{dc}}{S_{sec}} = \frac{V_{dc} \cdot I_{dc}}{V_{rms,sec} \cdot I_{rms,sec}} $$ **Step 1 — Identify each quantity.** - $V_{dc}$ and $I_{dc} = V_{dc} / R$ → $P_{dc} = V_{dc}^2 / R$. - $V_{rms,sec} = V_m / \sqrt{2}$ (sinusoidal source). - For half-wave, the source current **equals** the load current → $I_{rms,sec} = V_{rms}/R$. **Step 2 — Substitute.** $$ \text{TUF} = \frac{V_{dc}^2 / R}{(V_m/\sqrt{2}) \cdot (V_{rms}/R)} = \frac{\sqrt{2}\, V_{dc}^2}{V_m \, V_{rms}} $$ **Step 3 — Insert the expressions for $V_{dc}$ and $V_{rms}$.** $$ \text{TUF} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{V_m \, V_{rms}} \cdot \left[\frac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)\right]^2 = \frac{\sqrt{2}\, V_m^2 (1+\cos\alpha)^2}{4\pi^2 \, V_m \, V_{rms}} $$ $$ \text{TUF} = \frac{\sqrt{2}\, V_m (1 + \cos\alpha)^2}{4\pi^2 \, V_{rms}} $$ **Step 4 — Replace $V_{rms}$ with its expression.** $$ \boxed{\,\text{TUF} = \frac{(1 + \cos\alpha)^2}{2\sqrt{2}\,\pi^2}\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{\pi - \alpha + \sin 2\alpha/2}}\,} $$ **Maximum value (at $\alpha = 0$):** $$ \text{TUF}_{max} = \frac{(1+1)^2}{2\sqrt{2}\pi^2}\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{\pi}} = \frac{4}{2\sqrt{2}\pi^2} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi^2} \approx 0.2865 $$ ## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style) > **Given:** $v_s = 200 \sin(314 t)$ V, $R = 10\,\Omega$, firing angle $\alpha = 60^\circ$. The supply feeds a single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier with R load. **Required:** $V_{dc}$, $V_{rms}$, $I_{dc}$, $I_{rms}$, FF, RF, $\eta$, TUF, PF, PIV. ### Solution Given: $V_m = 200$ V, $\alpha = 60^\circ = \pi/3$ rad, $R = 10\,\Omega$. **Step 1 — Average output voltage.** $$ V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos 60^\circ) = \frac{200}{2\pi}(1 + 0.5) = \frac{200 \times 1.5}{2\pi} = \frac{300}{2\pi} = \frac{150}{\pi} \approx 47.75 \text{ V} $$ **Step 2 — RMS output voltage.** $$ V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{2}\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)} $$ Substitute $\alpha = \pi/3$: $$ V_{rms} = \frac{200}{2}\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \frac{\pi}{3} + \frac{\sin(2\pi/3)}{2}\right)} $$ $$ V_{rms} = 100 \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left(\frac{2\pi}{3} + \frac{\sqrt{3}/2}{2}\right)} = 100 \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left(2.0944 + 0.4330\right)} $$ $$ V_{rms} = 100 \sqrt{\frac{2.5274}{\pi}} = 100 \sqrt{0.8045} = 100 \times 0.8970 \approx 89.70 \text{ V} $$ **Step 3 — Average and RMS currents.** $$ I_{dc} = \frac{V_{dc}}{R} = \frac{47.75}{10} = 4.775 \text{ A} $$ $$ I_{rms} = \frac{V_{rms}}{R} = \frac{89.70}{10} = 8.970 \text{ A} $$ **Step 4 — Form Factor.** $$ \text{FF} = \frac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}} = \frac{89.70}{47.75} \approx 1.879 $$ **Step 5 — Ripple Factor.** $$ \text{RF} = \sqrt{\text{FF}^2 - 1} = \sqrt{(1.879)^2 - 1} = \sqrt{3.531 - 1} = \sqrt{2.531} \approx 1.591 $$ **Step 6 — Rectification Efficiency.** $$ \eta = \left(\frac{V_{dc}}{V_{rms}}\right)^2 \times 100\% = \left(\frac{47.75}{89.70}\right)^2 \times 100\% \approx 28.32 \% $$ **Step 7 — Transformer Utilization Factor.** $$ \text{TUF} = \frac{\sqrt{2}\, V_{dc}^2}{V_m \, V_{rms}} = \frac{1.4142 \times (47.75)^2}{200 \times 89.70} = \frac{1.4142 \times 2280.06}{17940} = \frac{3224.4}{17940} \approx 0.1797 $$ **Step 8 — Supply Power Factor.** $$ \text{PF} = \frac{\sqrt{2}\, V_{rms}}{V_m} = \frac{1.4142 \times 89.70}{200} = \frac{126.85}{200} \approx 0.634 \text{ (lagging, due to harmonics)} $$ **Step 9 — PIV rating of SCR.** $$ \boxed{\text{PIV} = V_m = 200 \text{ V}} $$ ### Summary Table for the Numerical | Quantity | Value | Quantity | Value | |----------|-------|----------|-------| | $V_{dc}$ | **47.75 V** | FF | **1.879** | | $V_{rms}$ | **89.70 V** | RF | **1.591** | | $I_{dc}$ | **4.775 A** | $\eta$ | **28.32 %** | | $I_{rms}$ | **8.970 A** | TUF | **0.180** | | PIV | **200 V** | PF | **0.634** | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > Mermaid does not natively render sinusoidal waveforms. Below, the **circuit topology** is shown as a clean block-level architecture, and the **waveform timing sequence** is presented as a chronological state-flow diagram that mirrors the SCR's conduction behaviour. ## 4.1 Circuit Topology (Block-Level Architecture) ```mermaid graph LR nodeAC["AC SOURCE
Vs = Vm sin wt"]:::source nodeSec["SECONDARY
230 V 50 Hz"]:::transformer nodeAnode["ANODE A"]:::junction nodeSCR["THYRISTOR T1
SCR SWITCH"]:::thyristor nodeCathode["CATHODE K"]:::junction nodeGate["GATE G
PULSE at alpha"]:::gate nodeLoad["RESISTIVE LOAD
R LOAD"]:::load nodeReturn["RETURN PATH"]:::junction nodeAC -- primary --> nodeSec nodeSec -- top terminal --> nodeAnode nodeAnode --> nodeSCR nodeGate -- trigger pulse --> nodeSCR nodeSCR --> nodeCathode nodeCathode --> nodeLoad nodeLoad --> nodeReturn nodeReturn -- bottom terminal --> nodeSec classDef source fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef transformer fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef thyristor fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef gate fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef load fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef junction fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#555,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` ### Node Identification Table | Node ID | Real Component | Function | |---------|----------------|----------| | `nodeAC` | Single-phase AC mains | Source of sinusoidal voltage | | `nodeSec` | Transformer secondary | Steps down to required RMS level | | `nodeSCR` | Thyristor T1 | Controlled unidirectional switch | | `nodeGate` | Gate pulse generator | Fires the SCR at $\omega t = \alpha$ | | `nodeLoad` | Resistor R | Dissipative load; converts DC to heat | | `nodeReturn` | Return wire | Completes the loop with secondary | ## 4.2 Conduction-State Timing Diagram ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 direction LR stateA: STATE 1 BLOCKING
0 to alpha stateB: STATE 2 CONDUCTION
alpha to pi stateC: STATE 3 REVERSE BLOCK
pi to 2pi stateD: NEXT CYCLE 2pi to 2pi+alpha stateA --> stateB : Gate pulse applied
Vo = Vm sin wt stateB --> stateC : Current naturally
falls to zero stateC --> stateD : Negative half cycle
SCR reverse biased stateD --> stateB : Gate pulse in next
positive half cycle ``` ## 4.3 Waveform Description (Textual Reconstruction) The following four waveforms, all drawn against $\omega t$ on the horizontal axis, must be memorised for the KTU exam. They are usually the **first 4 marks** of any drawing-based question. | Region | $\omega t$ Range | $v_s$ | $i_g$ (gate) | $v_o$ | $i_o$ | $v_T$ (SCR) | |--------|------------------|-------|---------------|-------|-------|-------------| | 1 | $0 \to \alpha$ | Positive, rising | **0** (not yet applied) | 0 | 0 | $v_s$ (positive, peaks at $V_m$) | | 2 | $\alpha \to \pi$ | Positive, falls to 0 | Pulse present | $V_m\sin(\omega t)$ | $V_m\sin(\omega t)/R$ | $\approx 0$ (SCR ON) | | 3 | $\pi \to 2\pi$ | Negative | 0 (pulse off) | 0 | 0 | $v_s$ (negative, $\min = -V_m$) | > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Drawing Tip:** Always label the **three critical angles** on the $\omega t$ axis — (i) $\omega t = 0$ (start of positive half), (ii) $\omega t = \alpha$ (firing), and (iii) $\omega t = \pi$ (natural extinction). Use the same dashed vertical guidelines across all four waveforms to show synchronisation. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > **Mark Distribution as per KTU 2024 ESE Pattern (PCMRT302):** > - Part A: 3 marks each, no choice, **answer all** (typically 2 questions from this module per exam paper). > - Part B: 14 marks each, **Either/Or** choice (Internal Choice between the two 14-mark questions). > - Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** **Define firing angle and conduction angle for a single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier with R load. State their relationship and the permissible range.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Firing angle ($\alpha$):** The angle (measured from the positive-going zero-crossing of the supply voltage) at which the gate pulse is applied to the SCR to initiate conduction. (1 mark) - **Conduction angle ($\gamma$):** The angular duration (in radians or degrees) during which the SCR actually conducts current. (1 mark) - **Relationship:** $\gamma = \pi - \alpha$ (valid for R load only). - **Range:** $0 \leq \alpha \leq \pi$ (or $0^\circ \leq \alpha \leq 180^\circ$). (1 mark) --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **What is the Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV) of the thyristor in a single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier with R load? Why is it equal to $V_m$?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **PIV rating = $V_m$**, the peak value of the supply voltage. (1 mark) - **Reasoning:** During the negative half-cycle ($\pi \leq \omega t \leq 2\pi$), the SCR is reverse biased. The entire supply voltage $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ appears across the device (no current flows, so no drop across $R$). (1 mark) - The most negative value of $v_s$ is $-V_m$ at $\omega t = 3\pi/2$. The SCR must block this reverse voltage without breaking down. Hence, the **thyristor must be rated for PIV $\geq V_m$**. (1 mark) --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Either/Or Choice) > Each question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. **Answer ANY ONE full question (either Q1 OR Q2).** ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Theory + Derivation** #### Q1 (a) — Operation with Circuit & Waveforms **[7 marks]** > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Pattern]** With a neat circuit diagram and input-output waveforms, explain the working of a single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier supplying a purely resistive load. Clearly mark the firing angle $\alpha$ and the conduction period. Discuss how the output DC voltage varies with $\alpha$. **Model Solution:** **1. Circuit (1 mark):** Draw a single-phase AC source $v_s = V_m\sin(\omega t)$ connected in series with an SCR (anode to source, cathode to load $R$, gate to a pulse source), with the other end of $R$ returning to the source. Refer to the block diagram in SECTION 4.1. **2. Mode of Operation (3 × 1 mark = 3 marks):** - **Mode 1 ($0$ to $\alpha$):** SCR forward biased but not yet fired. $v_o = 0$, $i_o = 0$, $v_T = v_s$. - **Mode 2 ($\alpha$ to $\pi$):** Gate pulse applied. SCR conducts. $v_o = V_m\sin(\omega t)$, $i_o = V_m\sin(\omega t)/R$. - **Mode 3 ($\pi$ to $2\pi$):** SCR reverse biased. $v_o = 0$, $i_o = 0$, $v_T = v_s$ (negative, peak $-V_m$ at $3\pi/2$). **3. Waveforms (2 marks):** Draw $v_s$, $i_g$, $v_o$, $i_o$ vertically aligned with the same $\omega t$ axis. Mark $\alpha$ and $\pi$ with vertical dashed lines. Show conduction interval $[\alpha, \pi]$ shaded on $v_o$ and $i_o$. **4. Variation with $\alpha$ (1 mark):** $V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$. As $\alpha$ increases from $0$ to $\pi$, $V_{dc}$ **monotonically decreases** from $V_m/\pi$ to $0$. --- #### Q1 (b) — Derivation of $V_{dc}$ and $V_{rms}$ **[7 marks]** **Derive the expressions for the average and RMS output voltages of a single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier with R load.** **Model Solution:** See SECTION 3.1 and SECTION 3.2 for the **complete, step-by-step derivations**. The final boxed answers are: $$ V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha) \qquad\qquad V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{2}\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\!\left(\pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)} $$ **Mark Split:** - Setting up the integral with correct limits = 2 marks - Correct integration steps shown = 2 marks - Substitution of limits and final simplified form = 2 marks - Statement of units and limiting-case verification = 1 mark --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Numerical + Concept** #### Q2 (a) — Numerical Problem **[7 marks]** > **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Pattern]** A single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier is supplied from a $230$ V, $50$ Hz AC source and feeds a resistive load of $R = 20\,\Omega$. The thyristor is fired at an angle $\alpha = 45^\circ$. Calculate: > (i) Average output voltage, (ii) RMS output voltage, (iii) Average and RMS load currents, (iv) Rectification efficiency, (v) Peak Inverse Voltage. **Model Solution:** **Given:** $V_{rms,sec} = 230$ V → $V_m = 230 \times \sqrt{2} = 325.27$ V; $R = 20\,\Omega$; $\alpha = 45^\circ = \pi/4$. **(i) Average output voltage:** $$ V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos 45^\circ) = \frac{325.27}{2\pi}(1 + 0.7071) = \frac{325.27 \times 1.7071}{2\pi} = \frac{555.21}{6.2832} \approx 88.36 \text{ V} $$ **(ii) RMS output voltage:** $$ V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{2}\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)} $$ $$ V_{rms} = \frac{325.27}{2}\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \frac{\pi}{4} + \frac{\sin 90^\circ}{2}\right)} = 162.64 \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left(2.3562 + 0.5\right)} $$ $$ V_{rms} = 162.64 \sqrt{\frac{2.8562}{\pi}} = 162.64 \times 0.9534 \approx 155.07 \text{ V} $$ **(iii) Currents:** $$ I_{dc} = \frac{V_{dc}}{R} = \frac{88.36}{20} = 4.418 \text{ A} $$ $$ I_{rms} = \frac{V_{rms}}{R} = \frac{155.07}{20} = 7.754 \text{ A} $$ **(iv) Rectification efficiency:** $$ \eta = \left(\frac{V_{dc}}{V_{rms}}\right)^2 \times 100\% = \left(\frac{88.36}{155.07}\right)^2 \times 100\% = (0.5698)^2 \times 100\% \approx 32.47 \% $$ **(v) PIV rating:** $$ \boxed{\text{PIV} = V_m = 325.27 \text{ V}} $$ | Quantity | Computed Value | |----------|----------------| | $V_{dc}$ | **88.36 V** | | $V_{rms}$ | **155.07 V** | | $I_{dc}$ | **4.418 A** | | $I_{rms}$ | **7.754 A** | | $\eta$ | **32.47 %** | | PIV | **325.27 V** | --- #### Q2 (b) — Concept of TUF **[7 marks]** > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]** Explain the concept of **Transformer Utilization Factor (TUF)**. Derive its expression for a single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier with R load and find its maximum value. **Model Solution:** **1. Definition (2 marks):** TUF is defined as the ratio of the DC power delivered to the load to the **volt-ampere (VA) rating of the transformer secondary**: $$ \text{TUF} = \frac{P_{dc}}{S_{sec}} = \frac{V_{dc} \cdot I_{dc}}{V_{rms,sec} \cdot I_{rms,sec}} $$ It is a **figure of merit** indicating how effectively the transformer is being utilised. Lower TUF means the transformer must be physically oversized for the actual DC power delivered. **2. Derivation (4 marks):** See SECTION 3.6 for the **complete step-by-step derivation**. Key results: $$ \text{TUF} = \frac{\sqrt{2}\, V_{dc}^2}{V_m \, V_{rms}} $$ **3. Maximum value (1 mark):** At $\alpha = 0$: $$ \text{TUF}_{max} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi^2} \approx 0.2865 \;\; (\text{or } 28.65\%) $$ This is the **maximum possible TUF for any half-wave topology** and explains why half-wave circuits are rarely used in industry — the transformer is being utilised at less than 30 % of its rated capacity. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in This Topic (verified from past KTU answer scripts):** > 1. **Wrong integration limits (–2 marks):** Many students integrate from $0$ to $\pi$ instead of $\alpha$ to $\pi$. Always remember the **piecewise** nature of $v_o$. > 2. **Forgetting the $1/2\pi$ factor in $V_{dc}$ (–2 marks):** A common slip — students use $1/\pi$ instead of $1/2\pi$ when averaging over a full cycle. > 3. **Mixing up $V_m$ with $V_{rms}$ in TUF (–3 marks):** TUF uses $V_m$ (peak) in the denominator via $V_{rms,sec} = V_m/\sqrt{2}$. Using $V_s$ (RMS) directly is a frequent error. > 4. **PIV forgotten in numericals (–1 mark):** Always state PIV = $V_m$ as a separate line in the answer. > 5. **Form Factor written as 1 (–2 marks):** FF is always $>1$; for half-wave uncontrolled, FF = $\pi/2 \approx 1.57$, not unity. > 6. **No limiting-case check (–1 mark):** Examiners reward students who verify $V_{dc} = V_m/\pi$ at $\alpha = 0$ — it shows conceptual depth. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Night Revision Sheet):** - [x] Circuit consists of **one thyristor, one AC source, one R load** in series loop. - [x] **Firing angle $\alpha$** ∈ $[0, \pi]$ for R load. - [x] **Conduction angle** $\gamma = \pi - \alpha$ (R load only). - [x] **Natural extinction** at $\omega t = \pi$ (no forced commutation needed). - [x] $\boxed{V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)}$ - [x] $\boxed{V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_m}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\!\left(\pi - \alpha + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)}}$ - [x] $\boxed{\text{FF} = V_{rms}/V_{dc}}$, $\boxed{\text{RF} = \sqrt{\text{FF}^2 - 1}}$ - [x] $\boxed{\eta_{max} = 4/\pi^2 \approx 40.53\%}$ (at $\alpha = 0$) - [x] $\boxed{\text{TUF}_{max} = 2\sqrt{2}/\pi^2 \approx 0.2865}$ (at $\alpha = 0$) - [x] $\boxed{\text{PF}_{max} = 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707}$ (at $\alpha = 0$) - [x] $\boxed{\text{PIV} = V_m}$ - [x] **Half-wave** has poor TUF and high ripple → industry prefers full-wave/bridge. - [x] Always draw the **four waveforms** ($v_s, i_g, v_o, i_o$) in any 7-mark descriptive sub-question. - [x] Always write the **piecewise expression for $v_o$** before integrating. - [x] Verify final answers by checking the **$\alpha = 0$ limiting case** (uncontrolled half-wave). > [!TIP] > **One-Line Mnemonic for the 4 Critical Angles:** > **"F-A-P-E"** → **F**iring ($\alpha$) → conduction starts; **A**ttenuation begins (current ramps down); **P**ass through zero ($\omega t = \pi$) → **E**xtinction (SCR turns OFF).

topics.title: Fully Controlled Bridge Rectifier with R Load
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT302 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES & DRIVES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fully Controlled Bridge Rectifier with R Load**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fully Controlled Bridge Rectifier** is a power electronic converter topology that uses **four thyristors (SCRs)** arranged in a **full-bridge (H-bridge)** configuration to convert a single-phase or three-phase AC supply into a **controllable DC output**. The term "fully controlled" signifies that **all four switching devices are actively controllable** (unlike half-controlled or uncontrolled diode rectifiers), allowing the converter to operate in both **rectifier (inverter)** modes by varying the **firing angle (α)**.

When feeding a **purely resistive (R) load**, the converter delivers a **unidirectional but pulsating** DC voltage whose average magnitude is a cosine function of the firing angle α. This configuration is the **fundamental building block** for variable DC drives, DC motor armature voltage controllers, and HVDC transmission sub-systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A single-phase fully controlled bridge converter with R-load is a **high-weightage topic** in Module 3. Expect either a 7-mark sub-question or a full 14-mark derivation question. Mastery of the **average output voltage (V₀)** and **RMS output voltage (V_or)** derivations is **non-negotiable** for board examinations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Terminology Lockdown (KTU Examiner Vocabulary):**
> - **Converter** (not "rectifier" alone) — reflects the bidirectional power flow capability.
> - **Firing angle (α)** — the delay angle measured from the natural commutation point (zero-crossing of supply).
> - **Conduction angle** — duration for which a thyristor pair conducts.
> - **V₀** — average DC output voltage (the **most-asked symbol** in KTU papers).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **four-valve water pipeline system** connected to a water tank:
- The AC supply = water flowing back and forth in a pipe (alternating direction).
- The four thyristors = four manually operated valves (T1, T2, T3, T4) arranged in a cross pattern.
- The R load = a sprinkler that sprays water whenever it receives it.
- The **firing angle α** = the delay (in seconds) you wait before opening the valves after water starts flowing in the correct direction.

**What happens?**
- For the first half-cycle, water flows in one direction. You wait **α seconds**, then open valves T1 and T2. Water flows through the sprinkler until the flow naturally reverses.
- In the next half-cycle, you wait again, then open T3 and T4. Water flows again.
- The **longer you wait (larger α)**, the **less water reaches the sprinkler** (lower average output). This is exactly how a fully controlled rectifier reduces DC output voltage by increasing α.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Conduction Pairing:** "**T1-T2 Together, T3-T4 Together**." They always fire in diagonal pairs because the current must form a complete loop from source → load → back to source.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Supply voltage (peak) | V_m | **V (Volts)** |
| Supply voltage (RMS) | V_s | **V (Volts)** = V_m/√2 |
| Firing angle | α | **0° ≤ α ≤ 180°** (electrical) |
| Supply frequency | f | **50 Hz** (India) |
| Load resistance | R | **Ω** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Circuit Topology

A **single-phase fully controlled bridge rectifier with R load** consists of:
- **4 Thyristors:** T1, T2, T3, T4 arranged in bridge form.
- **Source:** Single-phase AC, v_s = V_m sin(ωt).
- **Load:** Pure resistance R connected between the two DC output terminals (cathode of T1/T3 and anode of T2/T4 junction).

The bridge is divided into two halves:
- **Positive half-bridge:** T1 (top), T2 (bottom) — conduct during the positive half of supply.
- **Negative half-bridge:** T3 (top), T4 (bottom) — conduct during the negative half of supply.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**Step 1: Natural Commutation Points**
The natural commutation points (where a diode would start conducting without delay) occur at **ωt = 0, π, 2π, 3π, ...**

**Step 2: Firing Pulse Sequence**
- T1 and T2 receive their **first simultaneous gate pulse at ωt = α** (positive half-cycle, delayed by α from zero-crossing).
- T3 and T4 receive their **first simultaneous gate pulse at ωt = (π + α)** (negative half-cycle, delayed by α from the π zero-crossing).

**Step 3: Conduction Pattern with R-Load**
| Interval (ωt) | Conducting Thyristors | Output Voltage v₀ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 → α | None | 0 |
| α → π | T1 and T2 | V_m sin(ωt) |
| π → (π+α) | None | 0 |
| (π+α) → 2π | T3 and T4 | -V_m sin(ωt) = V_m sin(ωt - π) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Point:** With a **pure R load**, the load current i₀ = v₀/R is always in phase with v₀. Since the thyristor cannot conduct negative current, **conduction stops exactly at ωt = π for T1-T2 and at ωt = 2π for T3-T4**. There is **no overlap** and no stored energy to sustain conduction beyond the supply voltage zero-crossing.

### 2.3 Why α Cannot Exceed 180° (π radians)

If α > π, T1-T2 would never be fired in the positive half-cycle, and v₀ would be zero for **both** halves → no output. Hence the **practical limit is α < π**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Instantaneous supply | v_s = V_m sin(ωt) | Single-phase AC input |
| Average output voltage | V₀ = (2V_m/π)·cos(α) | Most-asked KTU formula |
| RMS output voltage | V_or = V_m·√[(1/π)·(π - α + (1/2)·sin(2α))] | For R-load only |
| RMS thyristor current | I_TR = I_or/√2 | Per thyristor |
| Average thyristor current | I_TA = I₀/2 | Per thyristor |
| Average load current | I₀ = V₀/R | Ohmic load |
| RMS load current | I_or = V_or/R | Ohmic load |
| Output power | P₀ = I_or² · R = V_or²/R | DC side power |
| Input power factor | cos(φ) = (V_or/V_s)·(√2/π)·... | For R-load derivation |
| Efficiency | η = P_dc / P_ac | (typically 40-81% for R-load) |
| Rectification efficiency | η_rec = P_dc / (V_or·I_or) | With R-load, η_rec = 1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition Recall Table (Alpha Limits):**
> - **α = 0°:** Converter acts as uncontrolled rectifier (V₀ = 2V_m/π = maximum).
> - **α = 90°:** V₀ = 0 → converter outputs zero average voltage.
> - **α > 90°:** V₀ becomes **negative** → operation in **inverter mode** (with DC source, not with pure R-load as it cannot accept negative voltage across it — this is why pure R-load operation is limited to 0 ≤ α < π, and practically meaningful only for 0 ≤ α ≤ π/2).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Average Output Voltage V₀

**Given:**
- Supply voltage: v_s = V_m sin(ωt)
- T1 and T2 conduct from ωt = α to ωt = π.
- During conduction, output voltage v₀ = v_s = V_m sin(ωt).

**Step 1: Set up the integral for the average value over one full cycle (0 to 2π).**

The average of a periodic function over one period T is given by:
$$
V_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} v_0(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)
$$

**Step 2: Identify the regions where v₀ is non-zero.**

- From 0 to α: v₀ = 0 (no conduction).
- From α to π: v₀ = V_m sin(ωt) (T1-T2 conducting).
- From π to (π+α): v₀ = 0 (no conduction).
- From (π+α) to 2π: v₀ = -V_m sin(ωt) (T3-T4 conducting, output voltage equals negative of supply).

**Step 3: Substitute into the integral.**

$$
V_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \int_{0}^{\alpha} (0) \, d(\omega t) + \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) + \int_{\pi}^{\pi+\alpha} (0) \, d(\omega t) + \int_{\pi+\alpha}^{2\pi} (-V_m \sin(\omega t)) \, d(\omega t) \right]
$$

**Step 4: Solve the second integral.**

$$
\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = V_m \left[ -\cos(\omega t) \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi}
$$

Substituting the limits:
$$
= V_m \left[ -\cos(\pi) - (-\cos(\alpha)) \right]
$$
$$
= V_m \left[ -(-1) + \cos(\alpha) \right]
$$
$$
= V_m \left[ 1 + \cos(\alpha) \right]
$$

**Step 5: Solve the fourth integral.**

$$
\int_{\pi+\alpha}^{2\pi} (-V_m \sin(\omega t)) \, d(\omega t) = -V_m \left[ -\cos(\omega t) \right]_{\pi+\alpha}^{2\pi}
$$
$$
= V_m \left[ \cos(\omega t) \right]_{\pi+\alpha}^{2\pi}
$$
$$
= V_m \left[ \cos(2\pi) - \cos(\pi+\alpha) \right]
$$
$$
= V_m \left[ 1 - (-\cos(\alpha)) \right]
$$
$$
= V_m \left[ 1 + \cos(\alpha) \right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Symmetry Note:** The two non-zero integrals are equal because of the half-wave symmetry of the output waveform. This confirms our intuition that the positive and negative half conduction pulses contribute identically to the average value.

**Step 6: Add the two integrals and divide by 2π.**

$$
V_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ V_m(1 + \cos\alpha) + V_m(1 + \cos\alpha) \right]
$$
$$
V_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot 2 V_m (1 + \cos\alpha)
$$
$$
V_0 = \frac{V_m}{\pi} (1 + \cos\alpha)
$$

**Step 7: Apply the trigonometric identity to convert to the standard KTU form.**

Using the half-angle identity:
$$
1 + \cos\alpha = 2\cos^2\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)
$$

So:
$$
V_0 = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \cdot 2\cos^2\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos^2\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)
$$

Alternative standard form (using cos² to cos conversion):
$$
V_0 = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha \quad \text{is INCORRECT for R-load!}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM PITFALL — Read this carefully!**
> The formula **V₀ = (2V_m/π)·cos(α)** is the formula for a **single-phase fully controlled bridge rectifier with RLE load (highly inductive load)**, where the current is continuous and rectangular. **For a pure R-load, the correct formula is V₀ = (V_m/π)·(1 + cos α).**
> 
> Students routinely write the RL/RLF formula for R-load and **lose 7 full marks** in the KTU board exam. **Memorize both forms clearly distinguished by load type.**

### 3.2 Derivation of RMS Output Voltage V_or

**Step 1: Set up the RMS integral.**

The RMS value is:
$$
V_{or} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} v_0^2(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)}
$$

**Step 2: Split into the four regions.**

$$
V_{or}^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) + \int_{\pi+\alpha}^{2\pi} V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) \right]
$$

**Step 3: Use the half-angle identity for sin².**

$$
\sin^2(\omega t) = \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2}
$$

Substituting:
$$
V_{or}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \left[ \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2} \, d(\omega t) + \int_{\pi+\alpha}^{2\pi} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2} \, d(\omega t) \right]
$$

**Step 4: Solve the first integral.**

$$
\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2} \, d(\omega t) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ \omega t - \frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2} \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi}
$$
$$
= \frac{1}{2} \left[ \left(\pi - \frac{\sin(2\pi)}{2}\right) - \left(\alpha - \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right) \right]
$$
$$
= \frac{1}{2} \left[ \pi - 0 - \alpha + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2} \right]
$$
$$
= \frac{1}{2} \left[ (\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2} \right]
$$

**Step 5: Solve the second integral (using periodicity).**

Since sin²(x) has period π, the integral from (π+α) to 2π is the same as from α to (π - α) shifted. But we can compute directly:
$$
\int_{\pi+\alpha}^{2\pi} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2} \, d(\omega t) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ \omega t - \frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2} \right]_{\pi+\alpha}^{2\pi}
$$
$$
= \frac{1}{2} \left[ \left(2\pi - \frac{\sin(4\pi)}{2}\right) - \left(\pi+\alpha - \frac{\sin(2\pi+2\alpha)}{2}\right) \right]
$$
$$
= \frac{1}{2} \left[ 2\pi - 0 - \pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2} \right] \quad \text{(since } \sin(2\pi+2\alpha) = \sin(2\alpha)\text{)}
$$
$$
= \frac{1}{2} \left[ (\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2} \right]
$$

**Step 6: Sum the two integrals.**

Total = $\frac{1}{2}\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right] + \frac{1}{2}\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]$
$$
= (\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}
$$

**Step 7: Substitute back into the V_or² expression.**

$$
V_{or}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \left[ (\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2} \right]
$$

**Step 8: Final form.**

$$
V_{or}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \left[ \pi - \alpha + \frac{1}{2}\sin(2\alpha) \right]
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{or} = V_m \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \pi - \alpha + \frac{1}{2}\sin(2\alpha) \right]}}
$$

### 3.3 Special Case Verification: α = 0

At α = 0:
- V₀ = (V_m/π)·(1 + 1) = **2V_m/π** ✓ (Maximum average output, equivalent to uncontrolled bridge)
- V_or = V_m·√[(1/2π)·(π - 0 + 0)] = V_m·√(1/2) = **V_m/√2** ✓ (Equivalent to full sinusoid RMS, as expected when thyristors behave as diodes)

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (For Board Exam Practice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample KTU 2-mark numerical:** A single-phase fully controlled bridge rectifier has V_m = 311 V, R = 10 Ω, and α = 60°. Calculate V₀, I₀, and V_or.

**Solution:**
- V₀ = (V_m/π)·(1 + cos 60°) = (311/π)·(1 + 0.5) = (311/π)·1.5 = **148.5 V**
- I₀ = V₀/R = 148.5/10 = **14.85 A**
- V_or = 311·√[(1/2π)·(π - π/3 + (1/2)·sin 120°)]
- V_or = 311·√[(1/2π)·(2π/3 + (1/2)·(√3/2))]
- V_or = 311·√[(1/2π)·(2.094 + 0.433)]
- V_or = 311·√[(1/2π)·(2.527)]
- V_or = 311·√[0.4022] = 311·0.6342 = **197.24 V**

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**Q1. With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a single-phase fully controlled bridge rectifier with R load. Also draw the output voltage waveform for α = 30°.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A single-phase fully controlled bridge rectifier consists of four thyristors T1, T2, T3, T4 connected in bridge configuration across an AC source feeding a resistive load R. T1 and T2 are fired simultaneously at ωt = α during the positive half-cycle, conducting from α to π. T3 and T4 are fired at ωt = (π+α) during the negative half-cycle, conducting from (π+α) to 2π. The output voltage v₀ = V_m sin(ωt) appears across R only during the conduction intervals, remaining zero for the rest of the cycle. The waveform consists of two sinusoidal pulses per cycle, each of width (π - α), separated by gaps of width α.

For α = 30°: the pulse appears from 30° to 180° in the first half, and from 210° to 360° in the second half, with 30° gaps at 0°-30° and 180°-210°.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q2. Compare the output voltage expressions of a fully controlled bridge rectifier for R load and RLE load. Justify why they differ.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **For R-load:** V₀ = (V_m/π)·(1 + cos α). The output pulses are **discontinuous sinusoidal segments** because load current cannot be sustained when supply voltage reverses at ωt = π.
- **For RLE (highly inductive) load:** V₀ = (2V_m/π)·cos α. The load inductance forces **continuous conduction**, making the output waveform consist of two successive half-sinusoids without gaps.
- The difference arises because the **R-load cannot store energy** to maintain current through the supply zero-crossing, while the **inductance in RLE load acts as a current source**, sustaining thyristor conduction until the next pair is triggered.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **average output voltage** of a single-phase fully controlled bridge rectifier supplying a **purely resistive load**. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-phase fully controlled bridge rectifier is supplied from 230 V, 50 Hz AC mains and feeds a resistance of 10 Ω. If the firing angle α is 45°, calculate:
- (i) Average output voltage V₀
- (ii) Average output current I₀
- (iii) RMS output voltage V_or
- (iv) RMS thyristor current I_TR **(7 marks)**

**Complete Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation** (Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation — 7 marks breakdown: 1 mark for circuit, 2 marks for setting up the integral, 3 marks for solving the integral, 1 mark for final formula).

The final result:
$$
V_0 = \frac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)
$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Thyristors are ideal switches (zero forward voltage drop, infinite reverse breakdown voltage).
2. The source has zero internal impedance.
3. The load is purely resistive (no inductance or back-EMF).
4. Firing pulses are applied simultaneously to both thyristors in a conduction pair.
5. The supply is a perfect sinusoid.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** V_s (RMS) = 230 V, f = 50 Hz, R = 10 Ω, α = 45°

**Step 1:** Calculate V_m.
$$
V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_s = \sqrt{2} \cdot 230 = 325.27 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate V₀.
$$
V_0 = \frac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos 45°) = \frac{325.27}{\pi}(1 + 0.7071) = 103.53 \cdot 1.7071
$$
$$
\boxed{V_0 = 176.73 \text{ V}}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate I₀.
$$
I_0 = \frac{V_0}{R} = \frac{176.73}{10} = 17.67 \text{ A}
$$
$$
\boxed{I_0 = 17.67 \text{ A}}
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate V_or.
$$
V_{or} = V_m \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi}\left[\pi - \alpha + \frac{1}{2}\sin(2\alpha)\right]}
$$
$$
V_{or} = 325.27 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi}\left[\pi - \frac{\pi}{4} + \frac{1}{2}\sin(90°)\right]}
$$
$$
V_{or} = 325.27 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi}\left[2.356 + 0.5\right]}
$$
$$
V_{or} = 325.27 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot 2.856}
$$
$$
V_{or} = 325.27 \cdot \sqrt{0.4545}
$$
$$
V_{or} = 325.27 \cdot 0.6742
$$
$$
\boxed{V_{or} = 219.27 \text{ V}}
$$

**Step 5:** Calculate I_TR (RMS current through each thyristor).

Each thyristor conducts for half the period (two segments per cycle, but they share the total conduction). Since the conduction is symmetric:
$$
I_{TR} = \frac{I_{or}}{\sqrt{2}}
$$
where
$$
I_{or} = \frac{V_{or}}{R} = \frac{219.27}{10} = 21.93 \text{ A}
$$
$$
\boxed{I_{TR} = \frac{21.93}{\sqrt{2}} = 15.50 \text{ A}}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain with a **neat circuit diagram and waveforms** the operation of a single-phase fully controlled bridge rectifier with **R load** for firing angle α = 60°. Clearly mark the conduction intervals of each thyristor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a single-phase fully controlled bridge rectifier with R load, derive the expression for the **RMS output voltage**. Hence, calculate the **rectification efficiency** at α = 30°. **(7 marks)**

**Complete Model Solution:**

**(a) Operation and Waveforms** (7 marks: 2 marks for circuit, 3 marks for waveforms, 2 marks for explanation).

**Circuit Description:** The bridge consists of T1, T2, T3, T4 with anode of T1 and T3 connected to the positive supply terminal; cathode of T2 and T4 to the negative supply terminal. The R load is connected between the common cathode junction (T1, T3 cathodes) and the common anode junction (T2, T4 anodes).

**Waveform Description for α = 60°:**

| Interval | Conducting | Output v₀ |
|---|---|---|
| 0° → 60° | None | 0 V |
| 60° → 180° | T1, T2 | V_m sin(ωt) |
| 180° → 240° | None | 0 V |
| 240° → 360° | T3, T4 | -V_m sin(ωt) = V_m sin(ωt - π) |

The output waveform consists of two sinusoidal humps (one positive, one negative rectified) per cycle, each 120° wide, separated by 60° zero-voltage gaps.

**Operation Explanation:** T1 and T2 are forward-biased during 0 to π but only conduct from ωt = α (when the gate pulse is applied). They turn off naturally at π when v_s goes to zero. T3 and T4 fire at π+α. Load current i₀ = v₀/R is discontinuous.

**(b) Derivation of V_or and Efficiency:**

**Derivation:** (Refer to Section 3.2 of these notes — 4 marks for derivation, 1 mark for final formula).

$$
V_{or} = V_m \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi}\left[\pi - \alpha + \frac{1}{2}\sin(2\alpha)\right]}
$$

**Efficiency Calculation at α = 30°:**

The rectification efficiency is defined as:
$$
\eta_{rec} = \frac{P_{dc}}{P_{ac}} = \frac{V_0 \cdot I_0}{V_{or} \cdot I_{or}} = \frac{V_0^2 / R}{V_{or}^2 / R} = \frac{V_0^2}{V_{or}^2}
$$

**Step 1:** Calculate V₀ at α = 30°.
$$
V_0 = \frac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos 30°) = \frac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + 0.8660) = 0.5942 \, V_m
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate V_or at α = 30°.
$$
V_{or} = V_m \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi}\left[\pi - \frac{\pi}{6} + \frac{1}{2}\sin(60°)\right]}
$$
$$
V_{or} = V_m \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi}\left[2.618 + 0.4330\right]}
$$
$$
V_{or} = V_m \sqrt{\frac{3.051}{2\pi}} = V_m \sqrt{0.4855} = 0.6968 \, V_m
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate efficiency.
$$
\eta_{rec} = \frac{(0.5942)^2}{(0.6968)^2} = \frac{0.3531}{0.4855} = 0.7272
$$
$$
\boxed{\eta_{rec} = 72.72\%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — R-Load vs RLE-Load Confusion**
> 
> **Most-Common Mark-Deduction Triggers:**
> 1. **Wrong formula** — Writing V₀ = (2V_m/π)·cos(α) for R-load. This is the RLE-load formula. **Lose 3 marks immediately.**
> 2. **Forgetting zero-voltage gaps** — Drawing the waveform as if conduction were continuous from 0 to π. Always show the gap from 0 to α and from π to π+α.
> 3. **Ignoring the discontinuity of current** — For R-load, the current and voltage both become zero during the gap. Students often draw a continuous current waveform, which is **physically impossible** with R-load.
> 4. **Missing the unit (V/A)** in numerical answers.
> 5. **Not converting degrees to radians** when using the integral form. KTU strictly expects radians in integration limits.
> 6. **Omitting assumptions** in derivations. KTU board examiners allocate **1 full mark** for stating assumptions clearly.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Board Prep)**

- [x] **Topology:** 4 thyristors in H-bridge; load is R (resistive).
- [x] **Conduction Pairs:** T1-T2 (positive half), T3-T4 (negative half).
- [x] **Firing sequence:** T1-T2 at ωt = α; T3-T4 at ωt = (π + α).
- [x] **Output waveform:** Discontinuous sinusoidal pulses, NOT continuous.
- [x] **Master Formula (Average):** V₀ = (V_m/π)·(1 + cos α) — **NOT** (2V_m/π)·cos α!
- [x] **Master Formula (RMS):** V_or = V_m·√[(1/2π)·(π - α + (1/2)·sin 2α)]
- [x] **Maximum V₀:** Occurs at α = 0°, value = 2V_m/π.
