- [x] **Zero V₀:** Occurs at α = π (in theory); practically meaningful up to α = 90°.
- [x] **Inverter mode:** α > 90° → negative V₀ (requires DC source, not possible with passive R-load).
- [x] **Power:** P₀ = V_or² / R (since R-load has unity power factor internally).
- [x] **Current:** i₀ = v₀/R is in phase with v₀.
- [x] **Discontinuous conduction:** Always with R-load. Continuous conduction requires L > L_critical.
- [x] **Commutation:** Natural (line) commutation — thyristors turn off at supply voltage zero-crossing.
- [x] **Numerical conversion:** Always convert degrees to radians (e.g., 60° = π/3) before integration.
- [x] **Assumptions to declare:** Ideal thyristors, zero source impedance, no overlap, simultaneous firing of pair.
- [x] **Real-world applications:** DC motor speed control (low-power), battery chargers, lamp dimmers, HVDC sub-systems.
- [x] **R-load vs RLE-load comparison:** R = discontinuous, formula has (1+cos α); RLE = continuous, formula has cos α. **This is the #1 KTU exam differentiator.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Concept of Inverter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT302 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES & DRIVES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basic Concept of Inverter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Inverter** is a static power electronic converter device that converts **Direct Current (DC)** input power into **Alternating Current (AC)** output power, operating at a desired frequency and magnitude. The conversion is achieved through controlled switching of power semiconductor devices such as **MOSFETs, IGBTs, GTOs, or Thyristors**, typically arranged in an H-bridge or three-phase bridge topology.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard Textbook Terminology):**
> "A DC-to-AC converter is known as an **Inverter**. The function of an inverter is to change a DC input voltage to a symmetrical AC output voltage of desired magnitude and frequency."

The fundamental block representation is:

$$
\boxed{V_{DC \, \text{(Input)}} \xrightarrow{\text{Inverter}} V_{AC \, \text{(Output)}} = V_m \sin(\omega t)}
$$

where $V_m$ is the peak output AC voltage and $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the desired angular frequency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Naming Convention Used in KTU Board Exams:**
> The term **"Converter"** in the strict KTU power electronics context refers to the family of devices: Rectifier (AC→DC), Chopper (DC→DC), Cycloconverter (AC→AC at lower frequency), and **Inverter (DC→AC)**. Students must NOT confuse "Inverter" with "Cycloconverter".

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Battery-to-Wall-Socket" Device

Imagine you are camping in a remote forest with only a **12 V DC car battery**, but you want to run your **230 V, 50 Hz household fan**.

| Real-World Object | Electrical Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| DC Car Battery (12 V) | DC Source ($V_s$) |
| Household AC Mains (230 V, 50 Hz) | Required AC Output ($V_{o}$, $f_o$) |
| Device that boosts voltage and changes nature | **INVERTER** (with internal step-up transformer or PWM boost control) |

The inverter essentially acts as a **"frequency translator + voltage booster"** — it slices the steady DC voltage into a series of timed pulses, which after filtering (or via transformer action), reconstruct a sinusoidal AC waveform usable by AC loads.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain English Summary:** *DC goes in → Switches chop it at a fast rate → AC comes out.*

---

## 1.3 Core Terminology Every KTU Student Must Memorize

The following terms appear repeatedly in KTU 2024 question papers and must be committed to memory:

- **VSI (Voltage Source Inverter):** The DC input is a **stiff voltage source** (e.g., battery, capacitor-fed DC link). Output is a controlled **voltage waveform**.
- **CSI (Current Source Inverter):** The DC input is a **stiff current source** (large series inductor with current source). Output is a controlled **current waveform**.
- **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation):** A switching technique to vary the output voltage magnitude by modulating the **duty cycle** of the switching pulses.
- **Square-Wave Inverter:** A simple inverter outputting a pure square wave (highest low-order harmonics).
- **Quasi-Square Wave (or Quasi-Square) Inverter:** A square wave with a small notch (pulse width modulation at fundamental level) to control RMS voltage.
- **IGBT / MOSFET:** Preferred self-commutating switches in modern inverters (frequencies from 1 kHz to 20 kHz).
- **THD (Total Harmonic Distortion):** A measure of harmonic pollution; for ideal square wave, **THD ≈ 48.34 %**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Notation Convention** (use these symbols in your answer scripts):
> - $V_s$ → DC Source Voltage
> - $V_o$ → Output (AC) Voltage
> - $I_s$ → DC Source Current
> - $I_o$ → Output (AC) Current
> - $f_o$ → Output Frequency
> - $R$ → Pure Resistive Load
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Inverters (High-Frequency KTU Question)

The KTU 2024 module-3 syllabus demands mastery of the following classification tree. Examiners frequently pose 3-mark or 5-mark questions on this hierarchy.

### A. Based on Type of Input Source
1. **Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** — fed from constant DC voltage.
2. **Current Source Inverter (CSI)** — fed from constant DC current.

### B. Based on Number of Output Phases
1. **Single-Phase Inverter** — Half-Bridge and Full-Bridge.
2. **Three-Phase Inverter** — 180° mode and 120° mode conduction.

### C. Based on Switching Device Used
1. **MOSFET-based Inverter** (low to medium power, high frequency).
2. **IGBT-based Inverter** (medium power industrial drives).
3. **Thyristor-based Inverter** (high power; requires forced commutation).

### D. Based on Output Waveform
1. **Square-Wave Inverter** (fixed output, simple).
2. **Quasi-Square Wave Inverter** (controllable output via pulse-width adjustment).
3. **SPWM Inverter** (Sinusoidal PWM — low THD, high quality).

---

## 2.2 Working Principle of a Basic Single-Phase Full-Bridge Inverter

The single-phase full-bridge VSI consists of **four controlled switches** (S1, S2, S3, S4) and **four freewheeling diodes** (D1, D2, D3, D4) connected in anti-parallel across each switch. The DC source $V_s$ feeds the bridge.

| Switching State | ON Switches | Output Voltage $V_{AB}$ | Current Path |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| State I (0 to T/2) | S1 and S2 | $+V_s$ | Current flows from +V_s → S1 → Load → S2 → -V_s |
| State II (T/2 to T) | S3 and S4 | $-V_s$ | Current flows from +V_s → S3 → Load → S4 → -V_s |
| Freewheeling (inductive load) | D1 and D2 OR D3 and D4 | 0 | Reactive current circulates through diodes |

The switches operate in **complementary pairs**: S1–S2 are ON together for half a cycle, then S3–S4 are ON together for the next half cycle. This produces a **symmetrical square-wave output** of magnitude $\pm V_s$ across the load.

---

## 2.3 Why Inverters Are Indispensable in Engineering

Inverters are the **heart of modern AC drives**. The KTU 2024 syllabus positions them in Module 3 because every variable-frequency drive (VFD) used to control induction motors and synchronous motors in industries relies on a rectifier–DC link–inverter power chain. Real-world applications include:

- **Industrial Motor Drives** — controlling speed of 3-phase induction motors in pumps, fans, and conveyors.
- **Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS)** — converting battery DC to AC during mains failure.
- **Solar Photovoltaic (PV) Grid-Tie Systems** — converting solar-panel DC to grid-synchronized AC.
- **Electric Vehicles (EVs)** — battery-to-motor conversion.
- **HVDC Transmission** — long-distance bulk power transfer.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every formula a KTU 2024 student is expected to recall for inverter numerical problems. Avoid using the `$\vert$` symbol inside table rows — use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Condition / Mode | Unit |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | RMS output voltage of **Square-Wave Full-Bridge VSI** | $V_{o,\text{rms}} = V_s$ | Pure square wave, full-bridge | Volts |
| 2 | RMS output voltage of **Square-Wave Half-Bridge VSI** | $V_{o,\text{rms}} = \dfrac{V_s}{2}$ | Pure square wave, half-bridge | Volts |
| 3 | Average output voltage of Square-Wave Inverter | $V_{o,\text{avg}} = 0$ (over full cycle) | Symmetrical square wave | Volts |
| 4 | Fundamental RMS component (square wave) | $V_{o1,\text{rms}} = \dfrac{4 \, V_s}{\pi \sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2} \, V_s}{\pi}$ | Full-bridge square wave | Volts |
| 5 | Fourier series of square wave output | $v_o(t) = \dfrac{4 V_s}{\pi} \displaystyle\sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty} \dfrac{\sin(n\omega t)}{n}$ | Full-bridge, ideal switches | Volts |
| 6 | THD of ideal square wave | $\text{THD} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\pi^2}{8} - 1\right)} \approx 0.4834 = 48.34\,\%$ | Pure square wave | Percent |
| 7 | Quasi-Square-Wave RMS output | $V_{o,\text{rms}} = V_s \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{2\alpha}{\pi}}$ | Pulse width $= \pi - 2\alpha$, where $\alpha$ is the notch half-angle | Volts |
| 8 | Distortion Factor (DF) of square wave | $\text{DF} = \dfrac{V_{o1,\text{rms}}}{V_{o,\text{rms}}} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \approx 0.9003$ | Fundamental content relative to total | — |
| 9 | Harmonic Factor (HF) | $\text{HF} = \dfrac{1}{\text{DF}} \approx 1.11$ | Measure of harmonic pollution | — |
| 10 | Output frequency | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{\text{Switching freq.}}{2}$ | Determined by switching pattern | Hz |

---

## 2.5 The 'Why and How' Behind Each Step

**Why four switches in a full bridge?**
Because to produce an AC output from a single-polarity DC source, you need to **reverse the polarity across the load periodically**. A half-bridge produces only $\pm V_s/2$ (lower utilization), while the full-bridge doubles this to $\pm V_s$ (better DC-bus utilization).

**How is the output frequency controlled?**
By **gating the switches at the desired switching frequency**. The fundamental frequency of the output is half the switching frequency (since one complete AC cycle requires two half-cycles of switching).

**How is the output magnitude controlled?**
Three main methods are taught in KTU:
1. **External AC link chopper (classic):** Adjusting the input DC magnitude.
2. **Quasi-square wave (pulse-width modulation at fundamental level):** Inserting a notch to reduce RMS.
3. **SPWM (Sinusoidal PWM):** Varying the modulation index $m_a$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Fourier Series of a Square-Wave Inverter Output

The square-wave output of a single-phase full-bridge VSI is a **periodic odd function** of magnitude $\pm V_s$. The Fourier series of any periodic odd function contains only **sine terms**.

The general Fourier series is:

$$
v_o(t) = a_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \left[ a_n \cos(n\omega t) + b_n \sin(n\omega t) \right]
$$

For an **odd square wave** symmetric about the origin:
- $a_0 = 0$ (zero average over a full cycle)
- $a_n = 0$ (odd symmetry eliminates cosine terms)
- Only $b_n$ survives

**Step 1: Compute the sine coefficient $b_n$.**

$$
b_n = \frac{2}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v_o(t) \sin(n\omega t) \, dt
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the square-wave piecewise definition.**

Over one period, the output is $+V_s$ for $0 < t < T/2$ and $-V_s$ for $T/2 < t < T$.

$$
b_n = \frac{2}{T} \left[ \int_{0}^{T/2} (+V_s)\sin(n\omega t)\, dt + \int_{T/2}^{T} (-V_s)\sin(n\omega t)\, dt \right]
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the first integral.**

$$
\int_{0}^{T/2} V_s \sin(n\omega t) \, dt = V_s \left[ -\frac{\cos(n\omega t)}{n\omega} \right]_{0}^{T/2}
$$

$$
= -\frac{V_s}{n\omega} \left[ \cos\!\left(\frac{n\omega T}{2}\right) - \cos(0) \right]
$$

Since $\omega T = 2\pi$:

$$
= -\frac{V_s}{n\omega} \left[ \cos(n\pi) - 1 \right] = \frac{V_s}{n\omega} \left[ 1 - \cos(n\pi) \right]
$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the second integral.**

$$
\int_{T/2}^{T} (-V_s)\sin(n\omega t) \, dt = \frac{V_s}{n\omega} \left[ \cos(n\omega T) - \cos\!\left(\frac{n\omega T}{2}\right) \right]
$$

$$
= \frac{V_s}{n\omega} \left[ \cos(2n\pi) - \cos(n\pi) \right] = \frac{V_s}{n\omega} \left[ 1 - \cos(n\pi) \right]
$$

**Step 5: Combine the two integrals and substitute back into $b_n$.**

$$
b_n = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{2V_s}{n\omega} \left[ 1 - \cos(n\pi) \right] = \frac{4V_s}{T \cdot n\omega} \left[ 1 - \cos(n\pi) \right]
$$

Since $T\omega = 2\pi$:

$$
b_n = \frac{4V_s}{2n\pi} \left[ 1 - \cos(n\pi) \right] = \frac{2V_s}{n\pi} \left[ 1 - \cos(n\pi) \right]
$$

**Step 6: Apply parity of $n$ to obtain the final closed-form expression.**

- For **even $n$:** $\cos(n\pi) = +1 \;\Rightarrow\; b_n = 0$ (even harmonics vanish).
- For **odd $n$:** $\cos(n\pi) = -1 \;\Rightarrow\; b_n = \dfrac{4V_s}{n\pi}$.

$$
\boxed{\,v_o(t) = \frac{4V_s}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty} \frac{\sin(n\omega t)}{n}\,}
$$

This is the **canonical KTU result** for the square-wave inverter Fourier series.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: RMS Value of the Square-Wave Output

**Step 1: Set up the RMS definition.**

$$
V_{o,\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v_o^{2}(t)\, dt}
$$

**Step 2: Insert the square wave — the integrand becomes $V_s^2$ throughout.**

$$
V_{o,\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \left[ \int_{0}^{T/2} V_s^{2} \, dt + \int_{T/2}^{T} (-V_s)^{2} \, dt \right]}
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the integrals.**

$$
= \sqrt{\frac{V_s^{2}}{T} \left[ \frac{T}{2} + \frac{T}{2} \right]} = \sqrt{\frac{V_s^{2}}{T} \cdot T} = \sqrt{V_s^{2}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_{o,\text{rms}} = V_s\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why the **full-bridge square-wave VSI** has the highest possible DC-bus utilization factor among simple square-wave schemes: $V_{o,\text{rms}}$ equals the source voltage exactly.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Fundamental RMS Component and THD of Square Wave

From the Fourier series, the **fundamental peak** (n = 1) is:

$$
V_{o1,\text{peak}} = \frac{4V_s}{\pi}
$$

The fundamental RMS is:

$$
V_{o1,\text{rms}} = \frac{V_{o1,\text{peak}}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{4V_s}{\pi\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}\,V_s}{\pi} \approx 0.9003 \, V_s
$$

The **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** is defined as:

$$
\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_{o,\text{rms}}^{2} - V_{o1,\text{rms}}^{2}}}{V_{o1,\text{rms}}}
$$

**Step 1: Square the RMS values.**

$$
V_{o,\text{rms}}^{2} = V_s^{2} \qquad ; \qquad V_{o1,\text{rms}}^{2} = \frac{8V_s^{2}}{\pi^{2}}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the harmonic RMS.**

$$
V_{\text{harm,rms}} = \sqrt{V_s^{2} - \frac{8V_s^{2}}{\pi^{2}}} = V_s \sqrt{1 - \frac{8}{\pi^{2}}}
$$

**Step 3: Form the THD ratio.**

$$
\text{THD} = \frac{V_s \sqrt{1 - 8/\pi^{2}}}{2\sqrt{2}\,V_s/\pi} = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{1 - \frac{8}{\pi^{2}}}
$$

**Step 4: Simplify and evaluate.**

$$
\text{THD} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^{2}}{8} - 1} \approx \sqrt{1.2337 - 1} \approx \sqrt{0.2337} \approx 0.4834
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\text{THD}_{\text{square wave}} \approx 48.34\,\%\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** This number 48.34 % is a **"favourite" KTU 2024 question**. Memorize the formula $\text{THD} = \sqrt{\pi^{2}/8 - 1}$ as well as the numerical value.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Quasi-Square-Wave RMS Output

In a quasi-square wave, a **notch of width $2\alpha$** is removed from the centre of each half-cycle. The remaining pulse width is $\pi - 2\alpha$ (in electrical radians). The RMS is then:

$$
V_{o,\text{rms}}^{2} = \frac{1}{\pi} \left[ \int_{\alpha}^{\pi-\alpha} V_s^{2}\, d(\omega t) \right] = \frac{V_s^{2}}{\pi} \left[ (\pi - \alpha) - \alpha \right] = \frac{V_s^{2}}{\pi} (\pi - 2\alpha)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_{o,\text{rms}} = V_s \sqrt{1 - \frac{2\alpha}{\pi}}\,}
$$

**Significance:** As $\alpha$ varies from $0$ (pure square wave, $V_{o,\text{rms}} = V_s$) to $\pi/2$ (zero output), the RMS can be smoothly controlled — this is one of the **classical KTU 2024 voltage-control techniques**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Square Wave vs Quasi-Square Wave vs SPWM

| Parameter | Square Wave | Quasi-Square Wave | SPWM |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Output Waveform | Pure $\pm V_s$ | $\pm V_s$ with central notch | Pulses of varying width |
| $V_{o,\text{rms}}$ | $V_s$ | $V_s \sqrt{1 - 2\alpha/\pi}$ | $m_a \cdot V_s/2$ (single phase, bipolar) |
| THD | ~48.34 % | Lower (drops as $\alpha$ increases) | Lowest (5–10 % with $m_f > 20$) |
| Switching Frequency | $f_o$ | $f_o$ | High (kHz range) |
| Complexity | Low | Moderate | High |
| DC Bus Utilization | Best (100 %) | Adjustable | Adjustable via $m_a$ |
| KTU 2024 Relevance | Must know | Must know | Module 4 territory |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Tree of Inverters (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block maps the **complete classification hierarchy** of inverters, following the KTU 2024 syllabus structure. All node IDs are alphanumeric, and labels are kept as plain uppercase text to comply with rendering safeguards.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[INVERTER DC TO AC CONVERTER] --> B[BASED ON INPUT SOURCE]
    A --> C[BASED ON OUTPUT PHASES]
    A --> D[BASED ON SWITCHING DEVICE]
    A --> E[BASED ON OUTPUT WAVEFORM]

    B --> B1[VSI VOLTAGE SOURCE]
    B --> B2[CSI CURRENT SOURCE]

    C --> C1[SINGLE PHASE]
    C --> C2[THREE PHASE]
    C1 --> C1A[HALF BRIDGE]
    C1 --> C1B[FULL BRIDGE]
    C2 --> C2A[180 DEGREE MODE]
    C2 --> C2B[120 DEGREE MODE]

    D --> D1[MOSFET INVERTER]
    D --> D2[IGBT INVERTER]
    D --> D3[THYRISTOR INVERTER]

    E --> E1[SQUARE WAVE]
    E --> E2[QUASI SQUARE WAVE]
    E --> E3[SPWM SINE PWM]
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Single-Phase Full-Bridge VSI

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **block-level functional architecture** showing the signal flow and switching logic of a full-bridge VSI — adapted in lieu of a hand-drawn physical schematic.

```mermaid
graph LR
    DC[DC SOURCE VS] --> FB[H BRIDGE POWER STAGE FOUR SWITCHES S1 S2 S3 S4]
    FB --> D[ANTI PARALLEL FREEWHEELING DIODES]
    FB --> LOAD[AC LOAD R OR RL]
    GATE[GATING LOGIC AND CONTROL CIRCUIT] --> FB
    FB -. FEEDBACK .-> GATE
    LOAD --> OUT[OUTPUT VOLTAGE VO T]

    subgraph POWER STAGE
        FB
        D
    end

    subgraph SIGNAL STAGE
        GATE
    end
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- The **DC SOURCE** provides the constant input voltage $V_s$.
- The **H-BRIDGE POWER STAGE** contains four switches arranged in two legs; diagonal pairs (S1, S2) and (S3, S4) conduct alternately every half cycle.
- The **FREEWHEELING DIODES** allow reactive current circulation when the load is inductive.
- The **GATING LOGIC** generates complementary firing pulses at the desired output frequency.
- The **AC LOAD** receives the resulting square-wave voltage $v_o(t)$ of magnitude $\pm V_s$.

---

## 4.3 Output Waveform Timing Diagram (Conceptual)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **timing relationship** between the gate pulses, switch states, and the resulting load voltage for a single-phase full-bridge square-wave VSI.

```mermaid
graph LR
    T[TIME AXIS] --> P1[STATE 1 0 TO T HALF]
    T --> P2[STATE 2 T HALF TO T]
    P1 --> S1ON[S1 ON]
    P1 --> S2ON[S2 ON]
    P1 --> S3OFF[S3 OFF]
    P1 --> S4OFF[S4 OFF]
    P1 --> VOPOS[VO EQUALS PLUS VS]
    P2 --> S1OFF[S1 OFF]
    P2 --> S2OFF[S2 OFF]
    P2 --> S3ON[S3 ON]
    P2 --> S4ON[S4 ON]
    P2 --> VONEG[VO EQUALS MINUS VS]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualization Reminder:** A real waveform sketch in your KTU answer script should show the **square wave alternating between $+V_s$ and $-V_s$** with period $T = 1/f_o$, alongside the gate pulses for the four switches drawn below it. Always label both axes (Voltage in V vs Time in s).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, May 2024 Style]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**Q: Define an inverter. Distinguish between a Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) and a Current Source Inverter (CSI).**

**Model Answer (Point-Wise, as per KTU valuation key):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** An inverter is a power electronic converter that converts DC input voltage/current into AC output voltage/current of desired magnitude and frequency.
2. **VSI Characteristics (1 mark):** In a VSI, the input is a stiff DC voltage source (low source impedance). The output is a controlled **voltage waveform**, independent of load current.
3. **CSI Characteristics (1 mark):** In a CSI, the input is a stiff DC current source (high source inductance). The output is a controlled **current waveform**, and the switches must block forward voltage (requires reverse-blocking devices or series diodes with SCRs).

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**Q: Why are anti-parallel diodes connected across the switches in a VSI? What is their function?**

**Model Answer (Point-Wise, as per KTU valuation key):**

1. **Reason (1 mark):** The load in most inverter applications (motors, transformers) is **inductive** and stores energy in its magnetic field.
2. **Function (1 mark):** When a switch is turned OFF, the stored inductive energy must have a path to flow; otherwise, destructive voltage spikes will damage the switch.
3. **Mechanism (1 mark):** The **anti-parallel freewheeling diodes** provide this path, allowing the stored energy to **circulate back to the DC source** (regenerative braking in drives) or **freewheel across the load** during switch transitions, protecting the switches from over-voltage.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - December 2023 Style Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

**Q: (a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a single-phase full-bridge voltage source inverter supplying an R load. Draw the output voltage waveform and the gating signals for the four switches. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Fourier series expression for the output voltage of the above inverter. Hence determine the RMS value of the fundamental component. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Circuit Description (2 marks):**
Draw the H-bridge consisting of switches S1, S2, S3, S4 (e.g., IGBTs) with anti-parallel diodes D1–D4, connected across a DC source $V_s$. The R load is connected between the midpoints of the two legs (points A and B).

**Step 2 — Switching Sequence (2 marks):**
- During **0 to T/2:** S1 and S2 are ON, S3 and S4 are OFF. Current flows: $+V_s \rightarrow S1 \rightarrow R \rightarrow S2 \rightarrow -V_s$. Output $V_{AB} = +V_s$.
- During **T/2 to T:** S3 and S4 are ON, S1 and S2 are OFF. Current reverses through the load. Output $V_{AB} = -V_s$.

**Step 3 — Output Waveform (2 marks):**
The load voltage $V_o$ is a square wave of magnitude $\pm V_s$ with period $T = 1/f_o$. Sketch the square wave; label the four gating pulses (g1, g2, g3, g4) below, with g1–g2 high in the first half and g3–g4 high in the second half.

**Step 4 — Freewheeling Action (1 mark):**
For a pure R load, no freewheeling current flows. (Mention that for RL loads, diodes D1/D2 or D3/D4 conduct during commutation to provide the inductive current path.)

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Fourier Series (3 marks):**
Refer to **Derivation 1 in Section 3.1** above. The final result is:

$$
v_o(t) = \frac{4V_s}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty} \frac{\sin(n\omega t)}{n}
$$

where $\omega = 2\pi f_o$.

**Step 2 — Fundamental Peak (2 marks):**
For $n = 1$:

$$
V_{o1,\text{peak}} = \frac{4V_s}{\pi}
$$

**Step 3 — Fundamental RMS (2 marks):**

$$
V_{o1,\text{rms}} = \frac{V_{o1,\text{peak}}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{4V_s}{\pi\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}\,V_s}{\pi} \approx 0.9003 \, V_s
$$

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)

**Q: (a)** Explain the working of a single-phase half-bridge voltage source inverter. Derive an expression for its RMS output voltage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A single-phase full-bridge square-wave inverter supplies a load of $R = 10\,\Omega$ from a DC source of $V_s = 220\,\text{V}$. Determine: (i) the RMS output voltage, (ii) the RMS output current, (iii) the average DC source current, and (iv) the THD of the output. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Circuit Description (2 marks):**
A half-bridge VSI uses **two switches** (S1, S2) and **two capacitors** ($C_1, C_2$) across the DC source. The load is connected between the midpoint of the switches and the midpoint of the capacitors. Each capacitor holds $V_s/2$.

**Step 2 — Switching Sequence (2 marks):**
- S1 ON, S2 OFF: load voltage = $+V_s/2$.
- S1 OFF, S2 ON: load voltage = $-V_s/2$.

**Step 3 — Waveform (1 mark):**
Output is a square wave of amplitude $\pm V_s/2$.

**Step 4 — RMS Derivation (2 marks):**
Square wave of magnitude $V_s/2$ has RMS equal to its peak (for a constant-amplitude square wave):

$$
\boxed{\,V_{o,\text{rms}} = \frac{V_s}{2}\,}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:** $R = 10\,\Omega$, $V_s = 220\,\text{V}$, single-phase full-bridge square-wave inverter.

**Step 1 — RMS Output Voltage (1 mark):**

$$
V_{o,\text{rms}} = V_s = 220\,\text{V}
$$

**Step 2 — RMS Output Current (1 mark):**

$$
I_{o,\text{rms}} = \frac{V_{o,\text{rms}}}{R} = \frac{220}{10} = 22\,\text{A}
$$

**Step 3 — Average DC Source Current (3 marks):**
For a square wave, the DC source supplies current to the load only during the positive half-cycle (S1, S2 ON). The current during this half is $+V_s/R = 22\,\text{A}$, and during the other half, it returns $-V_s/R = -22\,\text{A}$ (i.e., the source actually receives current for the negative half-cycle in a passive R load only on average zero basis — but with inductive kick, the net average through the source is the difference).

For a **pure R load**, the average DC source current is **zero** because the input current is symmetric. However, in real inverter problems, KTU expects the following (considering only positive half-cycle for energy transfer):

$$
I_{s,\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T/2} \frac{V_s}{R}\, dt = \frac{V_s}{2R} = \frac{220}{2 \times 10} = 11\,\text{A}
$$

$$
I_{s,\text{avg}} = 11\,\text{A}
$$

**Step 4 — THD (2 marks):**

$$
\text{THD} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^{2}}{8} - 1} \approx 0.4834 = 48.34\,\%
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing RMS and Average for square wave:** For a square wave of magnitude $V_s$, $V_{o,\text{rms}} = V_s$ (NOT $V_s/2$). Half the batch writes the wrong value.
> 2. **Forgetting to state "over a full cycle" for average voltage:** Average over a full cycle is zero for a symmetric square wave. If asked for the average over a half-cycle, the answer is $V_s$.
> 3. **Drawing the circuit without anti-parallel diodes:** Even for R loads, examiners in KTU 2024 deduct 1 mark if the diodes are omitted — it is a standard part of a "neat circuit diagram".
> 4. **Missing the direction of current in the switching explanation:** Always specify whether current is positive (during S1–S2 ON) or negative (during S3–S4 ON) — this earns you an extra mark in the key.
> 5. **Not labelling the waveform axes:** The horizontal axis MUST be labelled "Time (s)" and the vertical axis "Output Voltage (V)". Unlabelled waveforms lose 0.5 mark.
> 6. **THD numerical substitution errors:** Practice writing $\pi^{2}/8 \approx 1.2337$ and the final answer 48.34 % in **one step** to save time in the 14-mark questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] An **inverter** is a DC-to-AC power electronic converter.
- [x] **VSI** is fed by a stiff DC voltage source; **CSI** by a stiff DC current source.
- [x] **Half-bridge VSI** uses 2 switches and 2 capacitors; output amplitude is $\pm V_s/2$.
- [x] **Full-bridge VSI** uses 4 switches in an H-bridge; output amplitude is $\pm V_s$.
- [x] **Anti-parallel (freewheeling) diodes** are essential across each switch for inductive load protection.
- [x] Square wave RMS output (full-bridge) = $V_s$; for half-bridge = $V_s/2$.
- [x] Fourier series of square-wave inverter: $v_o(t) = (4V_s/\pi) \sum \sin(n\omega t)/n$ — only **odd harmonics** present.
- [x] Fundamental RMS = $2\sqrt{2} V_s / \pi \approx 0.9003 V_s$.
- [x] **THD of pure square wave = 48.34 %** — a KTU favourite numerical.
- [x] Quasi-square wave allows RMS control via notch angle $\alpha$: $V_{o,\text{rms}} = V_s \sqrt{1 - 2\alpha/\pi}$.
- [x] **SPWM** (covered in Module 4) is the modern method to reduce THD and vary both magnitude and frequency.
- [x] Output frequency $f_o$ is controlled by the gating-pulse frequency (half the switching frequency for one full AC cycle).
- [x] Real-world applications: motor drives, UPS, solar PV, EVs, HVDC.
- [x] Always state the **standard KTU symbols**: $V_s$, $V_o$, $I_s$, $I_o$, $f_o$, $R$, $RL$.
- [x] Always **label all axes and switches** in the circuit diagram to secure full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical Drives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT302 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES & DRIVES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Electrical Drives ## SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) > [!NOTE] > **Electrical Drive (KTU 2024 Definition):** > An **Electrical Drive** is a highly integrated electromechanical energy conversion system used to control the motion (speed, torque, and position) of a mechanical load — such as a fan, pump, conveyor belt, robotic arm, or electric vehicle — by efficiently converting electrical energy from the source into mechanical energy at the shaft, using a power electronic converter/modulator, a suitable electric motor, and a sensing/control unit. In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, an electrical drive is not just "a motor"; it is the **complete system** comprising the source, the power electronic controller, the motor, the mechanical transmission, and the feedback/control loop working in unison. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Drivetrain of Industry" Imagine you are driving a car. The car does not simply have an engine — it has a **fuel tank (energy source)**, a **gearbox/clutch (transmission)**, an **engine (motor)**, a **driveshaft (mechanical coupling)**, and a **steering wheel with brake/throttle pedals (controller + feedback)**. The engine alone cannot drive the car; it is the entire *drivetrain system* that does. An **electrical drive is exactly this** — but for industrial machines: | Car Component | Electrical Drive Equivalent | Function | |---|---|---| | Fuel Tank | Electrical Power Source (Mains / Battery) | Stores / supplies raw energy | | Gearbox + Clutch | Power Electronic Converter (Chopper / Inverter) | Modulates voltage / frequency / current | | Engine | Electric Motor (DC / Induction / PMSM) | Converts electrical → mechanical energy | | Driveshaft | Mechanical Load Coupling | Transmits torque to the load | | Steering Wheel + Sensors | Controller + Sensors (PI / FOC) | Regulates speed, torque, position | > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield KTU Insight:** > In KTU 2024 ESE answers, examiners expect students to clearly state that an **Electrical Drive = Source + Power Modulator + Motor + Mechanical Load + Controller**. Defining it merely as "a motor" will fetch partial marks only. --- ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Quantities (Bolded) The performance of every electrical drive is characterized by **four golden quantities**, all bolded below because they appear in **virtually every KTU numerical**: - **Speed ($N$)** — measured in **rpm (revolutions per minute)** or **rad/s**. - **Torque ($T$)** — measured in **N·m (Newton-metre)**. - **Power ($P$)** — measured in **W (Watts)** or **kW**. - **Efficiency ($\eta$)** — dimensionless, expressed as a percentage (**%**). Their fundamental relationships are universal across all drives: $$P = T \cdot \omega = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$$ where $\omega$ is angular speed in **rad/s**, $N$ in **rpm**, and $T$ in **N·m**. --- ### 1.4 Why Study Electrical Drives? (Engineering Relevance) - **Industry adoption** — drives control **>70%** of all industrial motion worldwide (pumps, compressors, CNC machines, EVs, robotics). - **Energy efficiency** — variable speed drives can save **20–50%** of electrical energy in fan/pump applications. - **Precision control** — modern drives achieve speed accuracy of **±0.01%** in CNC and servo applications. - **Sustainable future** — electric vehicle powertrains and renewable energy systems are entirely electrical drives. ## SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Block Diagram of an Electrical Drive System Every electrical drive, regardless of whether it drives a tiny toy or a steel rolling mill, has the **same five functional blocks**: 1. **Power Source** — Utility mains (1-phase / 3-phase AC), DC battery, solar PV, or generator. 2. **Power Modulator (Converter)** — Rectifier, Chopper, Inverter, or Cycloconverter. It adjusts voltage, current, and frequency as required by the motor. 3. **Electric Motor** — DC motor, Induction motor, Synchronous motor, PMSM, BLDC, SRM, Stepper motor, etc. 4. **Mechanical Load + Transmission** — The driven machine (pump, fan, conveyor) and any coupling/gearbox. 5. **Controller + Sensors** — PI controller, Field-Oriented Control (FOC), V/F control, along with speed/torque/position feedback sensors (encoder, tacho, Hall sensor). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Favourite Concept:** The **Power Modulator** is what differentiates a *fixed-speed drive* from a *variable-speed drive*. A motor connected directly to the mains without a modulator is **not** considered a complete modern electrical drive — it is merely an unregulated motor. --- ### 2.2 Classification of Electrical Drives (High-Yield) #### A. Based on Power Conversion Stage | Type | Energy Conversion | Example | |---|---|---| | **Single-Stage Conversion** | AC → Mechanical | Direct AC induction motor from mains | | **Two-Stage Conversion** | AC → DC → Mechanical | DC motor with rectifier (without chopper) | | **Three-Stage Conversion** | AC → DC → AC → Mechanical | VFD-fed induction motor drive (rectifier + inverter) | #### B. Based on Number of Motors per Drive | Type | Description | Typical Application | |---|---|---| | **Individual Drive** | One motor drives one load | Domestic fan, washing machine | | **Group Drive** | One motor drives many loads through shafts/belts | Small workshops, older textile mills | | **Multi-Motor Drive** | Separate motor for each load motion | Paper mills, rolling mills, cranes, printing presses | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Modern industry **always prefers multi-motor drives** because each load can be controlled independently. A question like *"Why are group drives obsolete?"* is a frequent 3-mark question. --- ### 2.3 Comparison of Drive Types (Selection Logic) | Parameter | Individual Drive | Group Drive | Multi-Motor Drive | |---|---|---|---| | Cost | High (multiple motors) | Low | Highest | | Flexibility | High | Very Low | Highest | | Efficiency | High | Lower (transmission losses) | High | | Reliability | High (one motor failure affects one load) | Low (one motor stops many loads) | Highest (independent operation) | | Maintenance | Easy | Difficult | Easy | | Speed Control | Independent per load | All loads run together | Independent per load | | Modern Use | Common | Obsolete | Universal (KTU 2024 emphasis) | --- ### 2.4 Motor Characteristics vs Load Characteristics (Speed–Torque Curves) This is one of the **most heavily tested topics** in KTU Module 4. The selection of a motor depends on how its **speed-torque characteristic** matches the **load's torque demand**. #### Common Motor Characteristics | Motor Type | Speed-Torque Relationship | Behaviour | |---|---|---| | **DC Shunt Motor** | Roughly constant speed, linear torque increase | Constant speed drive | | **DC Series Motor** | $T \propto 1/N^2$ (inverse square) | High starting torque, traction use | | **Induction Motor (Squirrel Cage)** | Drop in speed with load (slip increases) | General purpose industrial drive | | **Synchronous Motor** | Truly constant speed (independent of load) | Constant speed, power factor correction | #### Common Load Characteristics | Load Type | Torque vs Speed | Examples | |---|---|---| | **Constant Torque Load** | $T \propto N^0$ | Conveyors, hoists, cranes | | **Torque $\propto$ Speed (Linear)** | $T \propto N$ | Mixers, viscous friction loads | | **Torque $\propto N^2$ (Parabolic)** | $T \propto N^2$ | Fans, blowers, centrifugal pumps | | **Constant Power Load** | $T \propto 1/N$ | Machine tool spindles, winding machines | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Selection Rule (High-Yield for KTU):** > A motor is correctly selected only if its **steady-state operating point** lies on its natural characteristic curve *and* it can supply the load torque at the desired speed **with adequate margin for transient overload**. --- ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical power from torque and angular speed | W | | 2 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Angular speed from rpm | rad/s | | 3 | $P_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | Output mechanical power | W | | 4 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{p}$ | Synchronous speed of AC machine | rpm | | 5 | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Slip of induction motor | dimensionless | | 6 | $T \propto s$ for small $s$ (linear region) | Linear torque-slip relation (motoring region) | N·m | | 7 | $T_{\text{max}} \propto \dfrac{V^2}{f^2}$ for V/F control | Maximum torque under V/F inverter drive | N·m | | 8 | $\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2$ | Fan / pump torque-power law (affinity law) | N·m | | 9 | $\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$ | Cubic power law for fans/pumps | W | | 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | Drive efficiency | % | | 11 | $T_{\text{starting}} / T_{\text{rated}}$ | Starting torque ratio (typically 1.5 to 2.5) | ratio | | 12 | $G D^2$ | Flywheel effect / load inertia | kg·m² | > [!WARNING] > **CRITICAL LaTeX Safety Note (For You, The Student):** > In the table above, **vertical bars are intentionally avoided**. For example, instead of writing $\vert G D^2 \vert$, the symbol is kept clean. If your KTU answer script uses pipes inside a table, it can break the rendering and confuse the examiner. Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ only in plain-text equations, not inside tables. --- ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Steel Rolling Mill:** Multi-motor drive with precise torque control per stand. - **Electric Vehicle (EV):** PMSM or Induction motor drive with FOC for high efficiency. - **HVAC System:** Variable frequency drive (VFD) on fan/pump — saves 30% energy. - **CNC Machine Tool:** Servo drive for sub-micrometre positioning. - **Lift / Elevator:** Worm-gear induction motor drive with counterweight. ## SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples ### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Speed, Torque, and Power We begin with the most fundamental drive equation. When a motor rotates at an angular speed $\omega$, in time $dt$ it sweeps an angle $d\theta = \omega \, dt$. The mechanical work done by the shaft is: $$dW = T \cdot d\theta = T \cdot \omega \, dt$$ Dividing both sides by $dt$, we obtain mechanical power: $$\frac{dW}{dt} = P_{\text{mech}} = T \cdot \omega$$ Since $\omega$ in rad/s and $N$ in rpm are related by $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$: $$P = T \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$ Solving for torque (to obtain the form used in KTU numericals): $$T = \frac{60 \cdot P}{2\pi N}$$ **Conversion Logic:** Every time $N$ is given in rpm and $P$ in watts, the factor $60 / (2\pi) \approx 9.5488$ converts between them. Memorize this constant. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Power and Torque from Rated Values (KTU-Style) **Question (3-mark KTU type):** A 3-phase induction motor rated at **5 kW, 1440 rpm** is driving a fan. Calculate the rated torque at the shaft. **Model Solution (Step-by-step):** **Step 1 — Identify the given quantities.** Output power: $P = 5 \text{ kW} = 5000 \text{ W}$ Speed: $N = 1440 \text{ rpm}$ **Step 2 — Apply the formula** $T = \dfrac{60 \cdot P}{2\pi N}$ $$T = \frac{60 \times 5000}{2\pi \times 1440}$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate numerator and denominator.** $$T = \frac{300{,}000}{9047.79}$$ **Step 4 — Final answer.** $$T \approx 33.16 \text{ N·m}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification:** Since $1440 \text{ rpm} \approx 150.8 \text{ rad/s}$, we can cross-check using $T = P / \omega = 5000 / 150.8 \approx 33.16 \text{ N·m}$. ✓ --- ### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Slip Calculation (KTU Favourite 7-Mark Numerical) **Question:** A 4-pole, 3-phase induction motor is supplied at **50 Hz**. The rotor runs at **1440 rpm**. Calculate the synchronous speed, the slip, and the rotor frequency. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Synchronous speed** using $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{p}$. $$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$ **Step 2 — Slip** using $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$. $$s = \frac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = \frac{60}{1500} = 0.04$$ **Step 3 — Rotor frequency** $f_r = s \cdot f$. $$f_r = 0.04 \times 50 = 2 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 4 — Summary table for the answer script.** | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | Synchronous speed $N_s$ | **1500 rpm** | | Slip $s$ | **0.04** (or **4%**) | | Rotor frequency $f_r$ | **2 Hz** | --- ### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Fan Affinity Law (High-Yield 7-Mark Problem) **Question:** A centrifugal pump driven by an induction motor at **1450 rpm** delivers **10 kW**. The pump speed is to be reduced to **1160 rpm** by a VFD. Assuming affinity laws hold, calculate the new torque and the new power. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify the given values.** $N_1 = 1450 \text{ rpm}$, $P_1 = 10 \text{ kW}$ $N_2 = 1160 \text{ rpm}$ **Step 2 — Calculate rated torque at $N_1$.** $$T_1 = \frac{60 \times 10{,}000}{2\pi \times 1450} = 65.85 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 3 — Apply fan torque affinity law:** $T_2 / T_1 = (N_2 / N_1)^2$ $$\frac{T_2}{65.85} = \left(\frac{1160}{1450}\right)^2 = (0.8000)^2 = 0.6400$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $T_2$.** $$T_2 = 65.85 \times 0.6400 \approx 42.14 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 5 — Apply cubic power law:** $P_2 / P_1 = (N_2 / N_1)^3$ $$P_2 = 10 \times (0.8000)^3 = 10 \times 0.5120 = 5.12 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 6 — Physical interpretation.** By reducing speed from 1450 rpm to 1160 rpm (only a **20%** reduction), power consumption dropped from **10 kW to 5.12 kW** — a saving of **48.8%**. This is the *entire reason* VFDs are installed on fans and pumps. --- ### 3.5 Motor Selection Logic (Analytical Matrix) For KTU 14-mark questions on motor selection, the matrix below is the examiner-expected reasoning: | Step | Question to Ask | Decision Criteria | |---|---|---| | 1 | What is the load's nature? | Constant torque / Constant power / Fan-pump (parabolic)? | | 2 | What starting torque is needed? | High (≥2× rated) → DC series / Slip-ring IM / Cage with VFD | | 3 | What speed regulation is acceptable? | Tight (±1%) → Sync motor / DC shunt; Loose → Cage IM | | 4 | What is the duty cycle? | Continuous → Class F insulation; Intermittent → Class B | | 5 | What is the environment? | Hazardous → Flameproof enclosure; Dusty → Totally enclosed | | 6 | What is the power supply? | Single-phase → Split-phase / shaded-pole; 3-phase → Any industrial motor | | 7 | Is variable speed needed? | Yes → VFD-driven AC motor or DC chopper-fed motor | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always close your selection answer with a sentence like: > *"Therefore, a 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor with V/F control is selected, because the load is a centrifugal pump (parabolic torque) requiring variable speed with high starting torque and minimal maintenance."* > This is the **concluding sentence pattern** that fetches full marks in KTU valuation. ## SECTION 4 : Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — The Electrical Drive System This Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete signal and power flow of an electrical drive, exactly as KTU 2024 examiners expect in answers: ```mermaid flowchart LR A[SOURCE
3-Phase AC Mains
or DC Battery] --> B[POWER MODULATOR
Rectifier / Chopper / Inverter / Cycloconverter] B --> C[ELECTRIC MOTOR
DC / Induction / PMSM / SRM] C --> D[MECHANICAL LOAD
Pump / Fan / Conveyor / Robot] D -.-> E[SENSORS
Speed / Torque / Position Encoder] E -.-> F[CONTROLLER
PI / FOC / V-F Control] F -.-> B style A fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#d97706,color:#000 style B fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000 style C fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000 style D fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d,color:#000 style E fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#6d28d9,color:#000 style F fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000 ``` **Reading Guide for Students:** - Solid arrows (→) denote **power flow**. - Dotted arrows (-.->) denote **feedback/control signals**. - The loop Source → Modulator → Motor → Load → Sensor → Controller → Modulator is a **closed-loop drive**. --- ### 4.2 Classification Tree — Types of Electrical Drives ```mermaid flowchart TB ROOT[ELECTRICAL DRIVES] ROOT --> P[Based on Power Stages] ROOT --> M[Based on Number of Motors] ROOT --> C[Based on Control Mode] ROOT --> A[Based on Application] P --> P1[Single Stage
AC to Mechanical] P --> P2[Two Stage
AC-DC to Mechanical] P --> P3[Three Stage
AC-DC-AC to Mechanical
VFD] M --> M1[Individual Drive
One Motor One Load] M --> M2[Group Drive
One Motor Many Loads] M --> M3[Multi Motor Drive
Many Motors Many Loads] C --> C1[Open Loop Drive
No Feedback] C --> C2[Closed Loop Drive
With Feedback] A --> A1[Constant Torque
Conveyor / Hoist] A --> A2[Constant Power
Lathe / Winder] A --> A3[Parabolic Torque
Fan / Pump / Blower] style ROOT fill:#fcd34d,stroke:#92400e,color:#000 style P fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000 style M fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#14532d,color:#000 style C fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d,color:#000 style A fill:#ddd6fe,stroke:#5b21b6,color:#000 ``` --- ### 4.3 Speed-Torque Characteristic Matching (Block Architecture) When matching motor to load, the **operating point** must lie at the intersection of the motor's natural curve and the load's required curve: ```mermaid flowchart LR L[LOAD CURVE
T varies with N] -->|intersect| OP[STEADY-STATE
OPERATING POINT] M[MOTOR CHARACTERISTIC
T_motor as a function of N] -->|intersect| OP OP -->|check| SAFE{Is T_motor >= T_load
at all N?} SAFE -- Yes --> OK[MOTOR SUITABLE] SAFE -- No --> REJ[REJECT - SELECT BIGGER MOTOR] style L fill:#fde68a,stroke:#a16207,color:#000 style M fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#075985,color:#000 style OP fill:#86efac,stroke:#166534,color:#000 style OK fill:#86efac,stroke:#166534,color:#000 style REJ fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Practical Engineering Insight:** The diagram above reflects how real-world engineers (e.g., at L&T, Siemens, ABB) use software tools like MATLAB/Simulink and ANSYS Maxwell to perform motor-load matching *before* any physical installation. KTU expects students to describe this matching logic in 7-mark answers. ## SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **KTU University Exam Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Part A carries **3 marks** per question. Answer must be concise (3–4 sentences + diagram if needed). --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define an Electrical Drive. List any four advantages of using electrical drives over mechanical drives in modern industry.** **Model Answer:** An electrical drive is an integrated system comprising an electrical power source, a power electronic converter, an electric motor, and a controller, used to regulate the speed, torque, and position of a mechanical load. **Four advantages:** 1. **High efficiency** — modern VFD-fed drives achieve **>95%** efficiency. 2. **Flexible control** — easy speed and torque control via power electronics. 3. **Low maintenance** — no fuel, no combustion, no oil change; only bearing lubrication. 4. **Clean operation** — no exhaust gases, suitable for indoor and hazardous environments. --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Compare Individual Drive and Group Drive. Why are multi-motor drives preferred in modern industries?** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Individual Drive | Group Drive | |---|---|---| | Definition | One motor for one load | One motor for many loads | | Speed control | Independent | All loads same speed | | Cost | Higher | Lower | | Reliability | High | Low (one motor failure stops all) | | Application | Modern industry | Obsolete (small workshops) | **Multi-motor drives are preferred** because each load has its own independently controlled motor, offering **superior flexibility**, **higher reliability**, and **better fault isolation** — essential in rolling mills, paper plants, and CNC machines. --- ### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Answer **one** full question from each module (14 marks). Most module questions carry sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. --- ### 🔹 QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]` **Q.A (a)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the functional elements of an electrical drive system. Describe the role of each block. (7 marks)* **Model Answer:** The five functional blocks of an electrical drive are: 1. **Source** — supplies raw electrical energy (AC mains, DC battery, solar PV). 2. **Power Modulator** — regulates voltage/current/frequency via converters (rectifier, chopper, inverter, cycloconverter). 3. **Motor** — electromechanical energy converter (DC/IM/PMSM/SRM). 4. **Mechanical Load** — the driven machine (pump, fan, conveyor, robot). 5. **Controller + Sensors** — senses speed/torque/position and modulates the converter to maintain desired operation. **Conclusion sentence (mandatory):** *Together, these blocks form a closed-loop system that delivers precise, efficient, and reliable motion control to industrial loads.* --- **Q.A (b)** *A 4-pole, 50 Hz, 3-phase induction motor runs at 1440 rpm. If it drives a constant-torque load of 50 N·m, calculate: (i) synchronous speed, (ii) slip, (iii) rotor frequency, (iv) developed mechanical power. (7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(i) Synchronous speed:** $$N_s = \frac{120 f}{p} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$ **(ii) Slip:** $$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \frac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = 0.04 \text{ or } 4\%$$ **(iii) Rotor frequency:** $$f_r = s \cdot f = 0.04 \times 50 = 2 \text{ Hz}$$ **(iv) Mechanical power developed:** $$P = \frac{2\pi N_r T}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1440 \times 50}{60}$$ $$P = 7539.8 \text{ W} \approx 7.54 \text{ kW}$$ **Result:** The motor develops **7.54 kW** of mechanical power at **4% slip**. --- ### 🔹 QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]` **Q.B (a)** *Classify electrical drives. Explain the working of a multi-motor drive with two suitable examples. (7 marks)* **Model Answer:** **Classification of electrical drives:** 1. Based on power stages — single / two / three-stage conversion drives. 2. Based on number of motors — **individual**, **group**, **multi-motor** drives. 3. Based on control mode — open-loop and closed-loop drives. 4. Based on application — constant-torque, constant-power, parabolic-torque drives. **Multi-Motor Drive:** Each load motion is driven by its own dedicated motor, all coordinated by a central controller. Each motor can run at a different speed and torque. **Example 1 — Rolling Mill:** A steel rolling mill has multiple stands (roughing, intermediate, finishing). Each stand is driven by a separate high-horsepower DC motor. The speeds of all motors are coordinated so that the steel strip is reduced uniformly without breaking. **Example 2 — Paper Manufacturing Machine:** A paper machine has sections for forming, pressing, drying, and winding. Each section has its own motor drive, precisely synchronised so the paper sheet does not tear or stretch. Modern drives use field-oriented control (FOC) with encoder feedback. --- **Q.B (b)** *A centrifugal pump requires 12 kW at 1450 rpm. Using VFD control, the speed is reduced to 50% of rated. Calculate the new torque and the new power consumed. Comment on the energy savings. (7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Rated values:** $$N_1 = 1450 \text{ rpm}, \quad P_1 = 12 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 2 — Rated torque:** $$T_1 = \frac{60 \times 12{,}000}{2\pi \times 1450} = 79.04 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 3 — New speed:** $$N_2 = 0.50 \times 1450 = 725 \text{ rpm}$$ **Step 4 — New torque (using fan affinity law $T \propto N^2$):** $$T_2 = T_1 \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 = 79.04 \times (0.5)^2 = 19.76 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 5 — New power (using cubic law $P \propto N^3$):** $$P_2 = P_1 \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 = 12 \times (0.5)^3 = 1.5 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 6 — Energy savings:** $$\text{Savings} = 12 - 1.5 = 10.5 \text{ kW}, \quad \text{Percentage} = \frac{10.5}{12} \times 100 = 87.5\%$$ **Comment:** A **50% reduction in speed** leads to an **87.5% reduction in power consumption** for centrifugal pumps — this remarkable cubic relationship is the economic foundation for installing VFDs in HVAC, water pumping, and industrial fan systems worldwide. --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks in Electrical Drive questions (KTU 2024 trend):** > > 1. **Defining a drive as "just a motor"** — a 14-mark answer expecting a *system definition* will lose **at least 4 marks** for this. Always mention *Source + Modulator + Motor + Load + Controller*. > 2. **Forgetting units** — writing $T = 79.04$ without **N·m** loses the unit mark. KTU strictly enforces SI units. > 3. **Wrong power formula** — using $P = 2\pi NT$ instead of $P = 2\pi NT/60$ is a common slip; the factor of **60** must be present because $N$ is in rpm. > 4. **Ignoring the conclusion sentence** in selection questions — every 7-mark selection answer must end with *"Therefore, motor X is selected because…"*. > 5. **No diagram in 7-mark questions** — even if not asked, a block diagram always secures an additional 1–2 marks. --- ### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Electrical Drives** - ⭐ An **electrical drive** = **Source + Power Modulator + Motor + Mechanical Load + Controller**. - ⭐ **Power Modulator** is what differentiates a modern variable-speed drive from a fixed-speed motor. - ⭐ **Individual Drive** → one motor, one load (modern standard). - ⭐ **Group Drive** → one motor, many loads (obsolete, used only in tiny workshops). - ⭐ **Multi-Motor Drive** → separate motor per load motion (industrial standard for paper, steel, printing). - ⭐ Master the **fundamental equation** $P = 2\pi N T / 60$ — every numerical uses it. - ⭐ **Synchronous speed** $N_s = 120 f / p$; **Slip** $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$; **Rotor frequency** $f_r = s \cdot f$. - ⭐ **Fan/Pump Affinity Laws:** $T \propto N^2$ and $P \propto N^3$ — extremely high-yield for KTU. - ⭐ Motor characteristics: DC shunt (constant speed), DC series (high starting torque, traction), IM (general purpose), Sync motor (truly constant speed, PF correction). - ⭐ Load characteristics: **Constant torque** (conveyors, hoists), **Constant power** (lathes), **Parabolic** (fans, pumps). - ⭐ A motor is correctly selected only if its operating torque $\geq$ load torque at **all operating speeds**, with margin for **transient overload**. - ⭐ Always end selection answers with a **concluding justification sentence**. - ⭐ Always include **SI units** (N·m, rpm, kW, Hz, rad/s) and a **neat block diagram** to maximize marks. - ⭐ Closed-loop drives use **sensors + controllers** (PI, FOC) and outperform open-loop drives in accuracy and dynamic response.

topics.title: Advantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT302 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES & DRIVES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Advantages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Module 4 – Solid-State / Power Electronic Control of DC Drives**, the topic "**Advantages**" refers to the systematic enumeration of the technical, operational, economic, and performance-related benefits of using **power-electronic converter and chopper-based speed control schemes** for separately excited DC motors (and, by extension, DC shunt motors) over conventional electromechanical methods such as **armature resistance control, field-weakening control using rheostats, and Ward–Leonard (MG-set) control**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> *"Solid-state speed control of DC motors using single-phase and three-phase phase-controlled converters (rectifiers), DC–DC choppers, and closed-loop feedback schemes provides superior speed regulation, faster transient response, higher efficiency, compact hardware, and easier interfacing with digital controllers compared to classical rheostatic and Ward–Leonard methods."*

The advantages are evaluated under the following standard performance parameters:
- **Speed range** (ratio of maximum to minimum no-load speeds)
- **Speed regulation** at a given load and speed
- **Torque–speed characteristic linearity** (constant-torque vs. constant-power region)
- **Transient response** (rise time, settling time, overshoot)
- **Power conversion efficiency** (typically **85% – 95%** for solid-state drives)
- **Power factor** at the AC mains side (for converter circuits)
- **Cost, weight, and floor space** of the controller hardware

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world analogy: Manual vs. Automatic Transmission in a Car**
>
> Imagine driving a car with **three control options**:
>
> 1. **Conventional Rheostatic Control (Manual Hand-Brake Style):** A huge resistor is inserted in series with the armature. Speed is reduced by *wasting* electrical energy as heat in the resistor. Sluggish, inefficient, and the resistor becomes burning hot — like pressing the brake pedal and the accelerator together.
>
> 2. **Ward–Leonard Control (Hybrid Engine):** A separate motor–generator set acts as a variable-voltage source. Smooth, but bulky, noisy, and needs a 3-phase supply to spin the M-G set first. The "boot-up" time is large.
>
> 3. **Solid-State Converter / Chopper Drive (Modern Automatic Transmission):** A handful of thyristors, IGBTs, or MOSFETs chop or phase-shift the supply voltage precisely and almost instantaneously. **No moving parts, no warm-up time, no bulky rotating mass, efficiency > 90%**, and the motor speed tracks the reference with millisecond accuracy — like a modern CVT gearbox.
>
> The **advantages** of option (3) over (1) and (2) is precisely what Module 4 expects you to master.

**Key physical constants / metrics to memorize (in bold):**

| Metric | Typical Solid-State Value |
|---|---|
| Efficiency ($\eta$) | **85 % – 95 %** |
| Speed range | **20 : 1 to 100 : 1** (closed-loop) |
| Speed regulation | **± 0.1 % to ± 1 %** |
| Dynamic response (bandwidth) | **10 – 100 rad/s** |
| Power factor (1-φ converter) | **0.3 – 0.7 (lagging)** |
| Power factor (3-φ converter) | **0.85 – 0.95** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Core Advantages of Solid-State DC Drives

The following ordered list forms the standard KTU answer template for a 7-mark / 14-mark question on this topic. Each advantage is explained with the underlying physics.

### Advantage 1 — High Efficiency
- In **rheostatic control**, the speed reduction is achieved by dissipating $I_a^2 R_{ext}$ as heat. The efficiency is *load-dependent* and drops sharply at low speeds.
- In **solid-state control**, the controlling device (SCR/IGBT) has a forward drop of only **1 V – 2 V**. The conduction loss $P_{loss} = V_T \cdot I_a$ is small.
- For a **chopper at duty cycle $\alpha$**, the average armature voltage is $V_a = \alpha V_s$, and the **chopper efficiency itself is 95 % – 99 %**, giving an *overall drive efficiency* of **85 % – 95 %**.

### Advantage 2 — Fast Dynamic Response
- A **thyristor** turns ON in **microseconds** ($\mu s$); an **IGBT** switches in **0.2 – 1 $\mu s$**.
- The armature time constant $T_a = \dfrac{L_a}{R_a}$ is typically **5 – 50 ms**.
- Combined, the closed-loop bandwidth reaches **20 – 100 rad/s**, far superior to the mechanical inertia-limited response of Ward–Leonard drives.

### Advantage 3 — Wide and Continuous Speed Range
- The **no-load speed** is given by $N_0 = \dfrac{V_a}{k\phi}$ (neglecting $R_a$ drop).
- By varying $V_a$ smoothly from **near 0 V to rated voltage**, the speed varies proportionally — providing a **continuous, stepless** speed range of **20 : 1 to 100 : 1** in closed-loop.

### Advantage 4 — Excellent Speed Regulation
- Speed regulation is defined as
$$
\text{SR} = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100 \,\%
$$
- With **closed-loop tachogenerator feedback**, regulation improves from **5 % – 10 %** (open-loop converter) to **± 0.1 %** (precision drive).

### Advantage 5 — Compact, Lightweight, and Maintenance-Free
- A 10 kW Ward–Leonard set weighs **300 – 500 kg**; the equivalent solid-state controller weighs **5 – 10 kg**.
- **No rotating parts** ⇒ no bearings, no brushes (in chopper-fed separately-excited motor), no lubrication ⇒ **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) > 50,000 hours**.

### Advantage 6 — Regenerative Braking Capability
- A **fully-controlled converter** or a **two-quadrant chopper** allows power flow from the motor back to the supply (during overspeed / downhill loads).
- Regenerated energy is **recovered** rather than dissipated, unlike dynamic-braking resistors.

### Advantage 7 — Easy Interface with Digital / Microprocessor Controllers
- The firing angle $\alpha$ of a converter or the duty cycle $\alpha$ of a chopper can be set by a **microcontroller, DSP, or PLC**.
- Enables **programmable soft-start**, **S-curve acceleration**, and **industry 4.0** integration.

### Advantage 8 — Better Power Quality and Reduced Acoustic Noise
- Modern **PWM choppers** (switching at **2 – 20 kHz**) move the acoustic noise outside the audible band.
- **Three-phase converters** inherently have less **harmonic distortion** and better displacement power factor than single-phase ones.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these ten equations — they appear in nearly every 14-mark question on Module 4.**

| # | Quantity | Equation | Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Back EMF | $E_b = k \phi \, \omega = \dfrac{V \phi \, N}{60}$ | V (volts) | EMF equation of DC motor |
| 2 | Armature voltage equation | $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$ | V | Steady-state voltage balance |
| 3 | No-load speed | $N_0 = \dfrac{V_a}{k \phi}$ | rpm | Reference speed at no load |
| 4 | Loaded speed | $N = \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{k \phi}$ | rpm | Speed under load |
| 5 | Torque | $T = k \phi \, I_a$ | N·m | Electromagnetic torque |
| 6 | Power developed | $P_d = E_b \, I_a = T \cdot \omega$ | W | Internal mechanical power |
| 7 | Speed regulation | $\text{SR} = \dfrac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100$ | % | Performance metric |
| 8 | Converter avg. output (1-φ FWR) | $V_0 = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ | V | 1-φ full converter output |
| 9 | Converter avg. output (3-φ FWR) | $V_0 = \dfrac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ | V | 3-φ full converter output |
| 10 | Chopper output voltage | $V_0 = \alpha V_s$ | V | Step-down chopper with duty cycle $\alpha$ |

**Note on notation:** in this sheet $V_a$ is the average armature voltage, $V_s$ is the chopper source voltage, $V_m$ is the peak phase voltage, and $V_{mL}$ is the peak line-to-line voltage.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial applications exploiting these advantages:**
> - **Rolling mills, paper mills, textile mills:** require 100:1 speed range and regenerative braking.
> - **Electric locomotives (e.g., Indian Railways Rajdhani / Shatabdi):** chopper-controlled DC traction motors.
> - **Robotics and CNC machines:** closed-loop DC servo drives (historically) for sub-millisecond response.
> - **Battery-operated EVs (early generation):** chopper-fed series DC motor.
> - **Printing presses:** require precise tension and speed regulation (±0.5 %).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Efficiency Comparison – Rheostatic vs. Chopper Control

**Problem statement:** A **220 V, 100 A, 1500 rpm** separately excited DC motor has $R_a = 0.1 \, \Omega$. Compare the efficiency of (a) rheostatic control and (b) chopper control ($\alpha = 0.5$) when the motor delivers a constant torque at **half-rated speed**.

### Step 1 — Back EMF at rated condition
At rated load, the armature drop is $I_a R_a = 100 \times 0.1 = 10 \,\text{V}$. Therefore,
$$
E_{b,\text{rated}} = V - I_a R_a = 220 - 10 = 210 \,\text{V}
$$
This $E_b \propto N$ holds, so at **half-rated speed** ($N = 750$ rpm),
$$
E_b = \frac{1}{2} \times 210 = 105 \,\text{V}
$$

### Step 2 — Case (a) Rheostatic Control
Since the torque is constant, $I_a$ remains approximately **100 A** (neglecting saturation). The applied voltage must equal the rated voltage (otherwise the motor cannot develop rated torque at rated $I_a$). The external resistance $R_{ext}$ is used to dissipate the extra voltage.
$$
V = E_b + I_a (R_a + R_{ext})
$$
$$
220 = 105 + 100(0.1 + R_{ext})
$$
$$
R_{ext} = \frac{220 - 105 - 10}{100} = 1.05 \,\Omega
$$
Power input,
$$
P_{in} = V \cdot I_a = 220 \times 100 = 22{,}000 \,\text{W}
$$
Power lost in $R_{ext}$,
$$
P_{ext} = I_a^2 \cdot R_{ext} = 100^2 \times 1.05 = 10{,}500 \,\text{W}
$$
Efficiency of the *controller*,
$$
\eta_{\text{rheo}} = \frac{P_d}{P_{in}} = \frac{E_b I_a}{V I_a} = \frac{105}{220} \times 100 = 47.7 \,\%
$$

### Step 3 — Case (b) Chopper Control
The chopper is fed by a source $V_s$ chosen so that the **average armature voltage** equals the back EMF plus the armature drop at the new operating point:
$$
V_0 = E_b + I_a R_a = 105 + 10 = 115 \,\text{V}
$$
With $V_0 = \alpha V_s$, for $\alpha = 0.5$ ⇒ $V_s = 230 \,\text{V}$. The chopper is essentially lossless (assume IGBT drop $V_{CE(sat)} = 1.5$ V). Power input from source,
$$
P_{in} = V_s \cdot I_{s,avg} = V_s \cdot \alpha I_a = 230 \times 0.5 \times 100 = 11{,}500 \,\text{W}
$$
Mechanical power developed,
$$
P_d = E_b \cdot I_a = 105 \times 100 = 10{,}500 \,\text{W}
$$
Efficiency,
$$
\eta_{\text{chopper}} = \frac{P_d}{P_{in}} = \frac{10{,}500}{11{,}500} \times 100 = 91.3 \,\%
$$

### Step 4 — Conclusion
$$
\boxed{\eta_{\text{chopper}} \approx 91.3 \,\% \quad \gg \quad \eta_{\text{rheo}} \approx 47.7 \,\%}
$$
This **43.6 % efficiency gain** at half speed is the single most powerful proof of the *efficiency advantage* of solid-state control — a classic 14-mark KTU derivation.

## 3.2 Derivation: Speed Regulation with Closed-Loop Control

### Step 1 — Open-loop speed
For an armature-voltage-controlled motor, taking partial derivatives,
$$
N = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{k \phi}
$$
Define the motor gain $K_m = \dfrac{1}{k \phi}$ (rpm/V). Then,
$$
N(s) = K_m \left[ V_a(s) - R_a I_a(s) \right]
$$

### Step 2 — Load disturbance
A step increase in load torque $T_L$ increases $I_a$, which (open-loop) **reduces** $N$ by
$$
\Delta N_{OL} = -K_m R_a \Delta I_a
$$

### Step 3 — Closed-loop correction
A tachogenerator of gain $K_t$ (V/rpm) feeds back the actual speed. The error $e = V_{ref} - K_t N$ drives the converter, which adjusts $V_a$. Solving the loop,
$$
\frac{N(s)}{V_{ref}(s)} = \frac{K_m K_{conv}}{1 + K_m K_{conv} K_t}
$$
where $K_{conv}$ is the converter gain. By the **high loop-gain** principle, when $K_m K_{conv} K_t \gg 1$,
$$
N \approx \frac{V_{ref}}{K_t}
$$
Hence the **speed becomes independent of $I_a$ and $R_a$**, giving near-perfect regulation.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Rheostatic vs. Ward–Leonard vs. Solid-State)

| Performance Parameter | Rheostatic Control | Ward–Leonard | Solid-State Converter/Chopper |
|---|---|---|---|
| Efficiency at low speed | **Poor (40 – 60 %)** | **Good (70 – 85 %)** | **Excellent (85 – 95 %)** |
| Speed range | **2 : 1** | **10 : 1** | **20 : 1 – 100 : 1** |
| Speed regulation | **Poor (5 – 15 %)** | **Good (1 – 5 %)** | **Excellent (±0.1 %)** |
| Transient response | **Slow (>> 1 s)** | **Moderate (100 ms)** | **Fast (< 10 ms)** |
| Regenerative braking | **Not possible** | **Possible (with M-G set)** | **Easily possible** |
| Hardware size & weight | **Medium** | **Very large (M-G set)** | **Very compact** |
| Maintenance | **High (brushes, resistors)** | **Very high (rotating set)** | **Very low (no moving parts)** |
| Initial cost | **Low** | **Very high** | **Moderate** |
| Operating cost (energy) | **High** | **Moderate** | **Low** |
| Suitability for digital control | **Not suitable** | **Difficult** | **Highly suitable** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion (memorize this single line for the exam):**
> *"Solid-state control dominates in **efficiency, size, response, and controllability**; Ward–Leonard is now obsolete except in regenerative high-power test rigs; rheostatic control survives only in very low-cost, non-critical applications."*

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Solid-State DC Drive

The figure below maps the **functional architecture** of a closed-loop speed-controlled DC drive using a phase-controlled converter. Each block represents a distinct sub-system whose interaction enables the advantages listed above.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ref[Reference Speed Vref] --> sumJ[Summing Junction]
    tach[Tacho Generator] --> sumJ
    sumJ --> err[Error Signal Ve]
    err --> ctrl[PI Controller]
    ctrl --> firing[Firing Circuit]
    firing --> conv[1-Phase / 3-Phase Converter]
    conv[1-Phase / 3-Phase Converter] --> arm[DC Motor Armature]
    arm --> load[Mechanical Load]
    arm --> tach
    load --> fb[Back EMF Feedback]
    fb --> arm
```

**Reading the diagram (functionally):**

1. **Reference Speed (`Vref`):** user-set DC voltage, typically **0 – 10 V**.
2. **Tacho Generator:** produces a voltage $K_t \cdot N$ proportional to actual speed.
3. **Summing Junction:** computes error $V_e = V_{ref} - K_t N$.
4. **PI Controller:** amplifies and integrates the error; gains $K_P$ and $K_I$ set the dynamic response.
5. **Firing Circuit:** converts the controller output to a **firing angle $\alpha$** (delay relative to zero-crossing of the AC mains).
6. **Converter:** adjusts average output $V_0 = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$, which becomes the armature voltage $V_a$.
7. **DC Motor Armature:** develops torque $T = k\phi I_a$ and drives the load.
8. **Negative Feedback loop:** speed sensed continuously, error continuously nulled → **self-correcting system** → high regulation.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Two-Quadrant Chopper (for Regenerative Braking)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[DC Source Vs] -->|forward| chop[Chopper Switch T1 and Diode D1]
    chop --> motor[DC Motor]
    motor -->|regen current| chop2[Freewheel and Regen Path T2 and D2]
    chop2 --> src
    motor --> load2[Mechanical Load]
```

**Why this proves the regenerative-braking advantage:**
- During **motoring**, T1 is ON (current flows from source → motor).
- During **regeneration** (overhauling load, e.g., a descending crane or downhill EV), the motor back EMF exceeds the supply voltage, T2 is gated ON, and the current **flows back into the source**, recovering kinetic energy as electrical energy — impossible in a rheostatic circuit.

## 4.3 Comparative Topology Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SS[Speed Control Methods of DC Motor] --> ARM[Armature Side]
    SS --> FLD[Field Side]
    ARM --> RHEO[Rheostatic Control]
    ARM --> WL[Ward-Leonard M-G Set]
    ARM --> CONV[Phase-Controlled Converter]
    ARM --> CHOP[Chopper DC-DC]
    FLD --> FW[Field Weakening]
    CONV --> ONE[1-Phase Half / Full]
    CONV --> THREE[3-Phase Half / Full]
    CHOP --> STEP[Step-Down Buck]
    CHOP --> TWOQ[Two-Quadrant]
    CHOP --> FOURQ[Four-Quadrant Class E Chopper]
```

**Key take-away from the topology map:**
- All four **solid-state branches** (1-φ converter, 3-φ converter, step-down chopper, four-quadrant chopper) are what Module 4 calls *modern drives*.
- They collectively displace **rheostatic** and **Ward–Leonard** branches, and the rest of the module teaches their analysis.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. Define the term "speed regulation" of a DC motor. Why is solid-state control preferred over rheostatic control from the speed-regulation standpoint? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Speed regulation** is defined as the change in motor speed (from no-load to full-load) expressed as a percentage of full-load speed at constant armature voltage and constant field current.
$$
\text{Speed Regulation (SR)} = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100 \,\%
$$
**Why solid-state control is preferred:**
- In **rheostatic control**, the same full armature voltage is applied at all loads, so the $I_a R_a$ drop directly causes a proportional speed drop. SR is typically **5 % – 15 %**.
- In **solid-state converter/chopper control with closed-loop tachogenerator feedback**, the converter continuously adjusts the armature voltage to compensate for the $I_a R_a$ drop, achieving a regulation of **± 0.1 % – 1 %**. Hence solid-state control is preferred. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2. List any **four advantages** of solid-state speed control of DC motors over conventional Ward–Leonard control. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks — one mark per advantage, with a one-line justification):**

1. **Higher efficiency** — Solid-state drives achieve **85 % – 95 %** efficiency, whereas Ward–Leonard suffers a *double energy conversion* (AC → M → G → DC) with overall efficiency of **70 % – 80 %**.
2. **Compact size and low weight** — A solid-state controller for 10 kW weighs ~**10 kg**, replacing a Ward–Leonard M-G set of ~**400 kg**.
3. **Fast dynamic response** — Converter firing-angle adjustment occurs within **a few milliseconds**, compared with the **hundreds of milliseconds** needed to re-excite the Ward–Leonard generator field.
4. **Low maintenance** — No rotating parts (no bearings, no brushes), giving MTBF of **> 50,000 hours** versus **~ 20,000 hours** for M-G sets. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — with Internal Choice)

### ➤ Question A — OR — Question B (Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A. (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the closed-loop speed control of a separately excited DC motor using a phase-controlled converter. State its **advantages and limitations**. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (already drawn in Section 4.1 above — to be redrawn in exam):** Reference → Comparator → PI Controller → Firing Circuit → Converter → Armature → Tacho feedback.

**Operating Principle:**
1. A reference voltage $V_{ref}$ (proportional to desired speed) is compared with the tacho feedback voltage $V_t = K_t N$.
2. The error $V_e = V_{ref} - V_t$ is processed by a **PI controller**, which gives proportional + integral action to eliminate steady-state error.
3. The controller output sets the **firing angle $\alpha$** of a 1-φ or 3-φ fully-controlled converter feeding the armature.
4. As load increases, $N$ momentarily drops, $V_e$ rises, $\alpha$ decreases, $V_0$ rises, restoring $N$.

**Advantages (any 5):**
1. Excellent speed regulation (**± 0.1 %**).
2. Fast dynamic response (bandwidth **20 – 100 rad/s**).
3. Wide speed range (**100 : 1**).
4. Compact, maintenance-free, **85 – 95 %** efficient.
5. Easy integration with **microcontroller / PLC** for programmable soft-start and S-curve.
6. Regenerative braking possible with dual converter.

**Limitations (any 2):**
1. **Displacement power factor** of 1-φ converters is low (**0.3 – 0.7**), worsening at low speeds (high $\alpha$).
2. **Line-current harmonics** injected back to the AC mains — requires filters.
3. Sensitive to **AC supply disturbances** (sags, transients).
4. SCR-based converters have **commutation failure** risk at high loads.

**[7 marks — block diagram 2 marks, principle 2 marks, advantages 2 marks, limitations 1 mark]**

---

### **Question A. (b)** A **220 V, 1500 rpm, 100 A** separately excited DC motor has armature resistance $R_a = 0.1 \, \Omega$. It is controlled by a **chopper** with a source voltage of $V_s = 220$ V. The motor operates at a duty cycle $\alpha = 0.6$ and delivers rated torque. Calculate: **(i)** the motor speed, **(ii)** the input power from the source, **(iii)** the efficiency. Compare it with rheostatic control at the same operating point. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $V = 220$ V (rated), $N_r = 1500$ rpm, $I_{a,rated} = 100$ A, $R_a = 0.1 \, \Omega$, $V_s = 220$ V, $\alpha = 0.6$.

**Step 1 — Back EMF constant and rated EMF:**
$$
E_{b,rated} = V - I_a R_a = 220 - 100(0.1) = 210 \,\text{V at } N_r = 1500 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 2 — Armature voltage with chopper:**
$$
V_a = \alpha V_s = 0.6 \times 220 = 132 \,\text{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Torque constant:**
Since torque is *rated*, $I_a = 100$ A, so $T = k\phi I_a = $ rated torque. The back EMF at the new speed is,
$$
E_b = V_a - I_a R_a = 132 - 100(0.1) = 122 \,\text{V}
$$

**(i) Motor speed:**
$$
N = \frac{E_b}{E_{b,rated}} \times N_r = \frac{122}{210} \times 1500 = 871.4 \,\text{rpm}
$$

**(ii) Input power from source:**
$$
P_{in} = V_s \cdot I_{s,avg} = V_s \cdot \alpha I_a = 220 \times 0.6 \times 100 = 13{,}200 \,\text{W}
$$

**(iii) Efficiency:**
$$
P_d = E_b \cdot I_a = 122 \times 100 = 12{,}200 \,\text{W}
$$
$$
\eta_{\text{chopper}} = \frac{P_d}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{12{,}200}{13{,}200} \times 100 = 92.42 \,\%
$$

**Comparison with rheostatic control:** at the same speed 871 rpm with rated torque, rheostatic control would still apply the full **220 V** to the armature, dissipating the extra $220 - 132 = 88$ V across an external resistor with the same 100 A, i.e. $P_{R_{ext}} = 88 \times 100 = 8{,}800$ W lost as heat. The rheostatic efficiency is only
$$
\eta_{\text{rheo}} = \frac{E_b \cdot I_a}{V \cdot I_a} \times 100 = \frac{12{,}200}{22{,}000} \times 100 = 55.5 \,\%
$$
**Conclusion:** the chopper control is **36.9 % more efficient**, confirming the **efficiency advantage** of solid-state control. **[7 marks — each sub-part ~1.5 marks, comparison 1.5 marks]**

---

### **Question B. (a)** Explain with necessary equations the **Ward–Leonard method** of speed control of a DC motor. Discuss **its advantages and disadvantages**, and justify why solid-state converters are gradually replacing it. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Ward–Leonard Principle:**
A 3-φ induction motor (or synchronous motor) M₁ drives a DC generator G. The output of G feeds the armature of the main DC motor M₂ whose field is held constant.
