Pin"] --> B["DC Motor
Converts to mechanical"] B --> C{"Power Balance
Tm vs TL"} C -- Tm greater than TL --> D["Excess Power
Stored in Flywheel
Pe = Tm - TL"] C -- TL greater than Tm --> E["Deficit Power
Supplied by Flywheel
Pd = TL - Tm"] D --> F["Flywheel KE Increases
omega increases"] E --> G["Flywheel KE Decreases
omega decreases"] F --> H["Load driven at
near-uniform torque"] G --> H ``` > [!NOTE] > The flywheel itself **stores no net energy** over a complete cycle — it only *buffers* energy. In steady state, the energy stored during light-load intervals is exactly the energy released during peak-load intervals. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define load equalization. List any **four** industrial applications where it is employed. (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** Load equalization is the technique of inserting an energy-storage element (typically a flywheel) between an electric motor and a fluctuating load, so that the motor delivers an approximately **constant average torque** while the storage element absorbs energy during light loads and releases it during peaks. **Four applications:** 1. Rolling mills in the steel industry. 2. Cranes, hoists and mine winders. 3. Punch presses and shearing machines. 4. Reciprocating compressors and electric locomotives. ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Why is a flywheel more effective for load equalization when used with a **series motor** than with a **shunt motor**? (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** A DC series motor has a **drooping (highly variable) speed–torque characteristic**, so during a peak load the speed falls appreciably. This larger speed drop translates to a greater release of kinetic energy from the flywheel per cycle, allowing a smaller flywheel to achieve the same smoothing. A DC shunt motor has nearly constant speed — its speed drop is small and most of the peak must still be supplied by the motor. Therefore the flywheel (kinetic buffering) gives larger benefit with a series motor. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A #### **(a)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]** Explain, with a neat block diagram, the **principle of load equalization** using a flywheel. Clearly distinguish the peak-load and light-load intervals and write the energy balance equation. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** 1. **Definition (2 marks):** Load equalization is the process of smoothing the torque demand seen by an electric motor by interposing a flywheel (energy-storage device) between the motor and the load. 2. **Block diagram description (2 marks):** The motor drives the load shaft through a flywheel mounted on the shaft. The flywheel has moment of inertia $J$ referred to the motor shaft. 3. **Two operating intervals (2 marks):** - **Peak load** ($T_{L} > T_{m}$): The flywheel **decelerates**, releasing kinetic energy: $\Delta E = \dfrac{1}{2}J(\omega_{\max}^{2} - \omega_{\min}^{2})$. - **Light load** ($T_{L} < T_{m}$): The flywheel **accelerates**, absorbing kinetic energy back to its original speed. 4. **Energy balance equation (1 mark):** $$\dfrac{1}{2}J(\omega_{\max}^{2} - \omega_{\min}^{2}) = (T_{L1} - T_{m})\,t_{1}\,\omega_{\text{avg}} = (T_{m} - T_{L2})\,t_{2}\,\omega_{\text{avg}}$$ #### **(b)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]** A rolling mill driven by a DC motor requires peak torque of **1500 N·m for 8 s** and light torque of **300 N·m for 14 s**. The motor torque is constant. Mean speed is **600 rpm** with a permissible speed variation of **±8 %**. Calculate: (i) the motor torque, (ii) the moment of inertia of the flywheel. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **(i) Motor torque** (using Derivation 1): $$T_{m} = \dfrac{T_{L1}\,t_{1} + T_{L2}\,t_{2}}{t_{1} + t_{2}} = \dfrac{(1500)(8) + (300)(14)}{8 + 14} = \dfrac{12000 + 4200}{22} = \dfrac{16200}{22}$$ $$\boxed{T_{m} \approx 736.4 \text{ N·m}}$$ **(ii) Moment of inertia of flywheel:** Speed swing: $\pm 8\%$ of 600 rpm gives $N_{\max} = 648$ rpm, $N_{\min} = 552$ rpm. Total swing: $\Delta N = 648 - 552 = 96$ rpm. Net torque during peak: $T_{L1} - T_{m} = 1500 - 736.4 = 763.6$ N·m. Using the exact formula (Derivation 2): $$J = \dfrac{(T_{L1} - T_{m})\,t_{1} \times 60}{2\pi\,\Delta N} = \dfrac{(763.6)(8)(60)}{2\pi(96)} = \dfrac{366528}{603.19}$$ $$\boxed{J \approx 607.6 \text{ kg·m}^{2}}$$ --- ### Question B #### **(a)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** Derive the expression for the **moment of inertia of a flywheel** required for load equalization in terms of the peak load torque $T_{L1}$, duration of peak load $t_{1}$, motor torque $T_{m}$, and the total speed fluctuation $\Delta N$. State clearly all assumptions. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Assumptions (1 mark):** 1. Motor torque $T_{m}$ is constant. 2. Load torque has only two levels — $T_{L1}$ (peak) and $T_{L2}$ (light). 3. System returns to its initial speed at the end of every cycle (steady state). 4. Mean angular speed $\omega_{\text{avg}} = (\omega_{\max} + \omega_{\min})/2$ is used for energy calculations. **Derivation (6 marks):** **Step 1:** Energy released by the flywheel during the peak-load interval equals the product of net (decelerating) torque, time, and mean angular speed: $$\Delta E = (T_{L1} - T_{m})\,t_{1}\,\omega_{\text{avg}}$$ **Step 2:** The same energy equals the change in rotational kinetic energy of the flywheel: $$\Delta E = \dfrac{1}{2}\,J\,(\omega_{\max}^{2} - \omega_{\min}^{2})$$ **Step 3:** Equating and factorising the difference of squares: $$\dfrac{1}{2}\,J\,(\omega_{\max} + \omega_{\min})(\omega_{\max} - \omega_{\min}) = (T_{L1} - T_{m})\,t_{1}\left(\dfrac{\omega_{\max} + \omega_{\min}}{2}\right)$$ **Step 4:** Cancelling $(\omega_{\max} + \omega_{\min})/2$: $$J\,(\omega_{\max} - \omega_{\min}) = (T_{L1} - T_{m})\,t_{1}$$ **Step 5:** Solve for $J$, then convert $\Delta\omega$ to $\Delta N$: $$J = \dfrac{(T_{L1} - T_{m})\,t_{1}}{\Delta\omega}, \quad \text{where} \quad \Delta\omega = \dfrac{2\pi\,\Delta N}{60}$$ **Final result:** $$\boxed{J = \dfrac{(T_{L1} - T_{m})\,t_{1} \times 60}{2\pi\,\Delta N} \quad \text{[kg·m}^{2}\text{]}}$$ #### **(b)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** A DC series motor drives a hoist through a gear train of ratio **K = 5** (motor runs 5× faster than hoist). The load on the hoist alternates between **2000 N·m for 6 s** and **500 N·m for 14 s** (referred to the *hoist* shaft). The motor torque is constant. The mean speed of the hoist is **40 rpm**, and the speed fluctuation allowed is **±10 %**. Compute: (i) the equivalent $J$ referred to the **motor shaft**, (ii) the energy stored in the flywheel at mean speed. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **(i) Equivalent $J$ referred to motor shaft:** First, compute the motor (average) torque. Torques referred to the *motor* shaft are divided by $K = 5$: - Peak torque at motor shaft: $T_{L1} = 2000/5 = 400$ N·m - Light torque at motor shaft: $T_{L2} = 500/5 = 100$ N·m $$T_{m} = \dfrac{(400)(6) + (100)(14)}{6 + 14} = \dfrac{2400 + 1400}{20} = \dfrac{3800}{20} = 190 \text{ N·m}$$ Mean motor speed: $N_{\text{motor}} = K \times N_{\text{hoist}} = 5 \times 40 = 200$ rpm. Total speed swing: $\Delta N = 0.20 \times 200 = 40$ rpm. Net peak torque at motor: $T_{L1} - T_{m} = 400 - 190 = 210$ N·m. $$J_{\text{motor}} = \dfrac{(210)(6)(60)}{2\pi(40)} = \dfrac{75600}{251.33} \approx 300.8 \text{ kg·m}^{2}$$ $$\boxed{J_{\text{motor}} \approx 300.8 \text{ kg·m}^{2}}$$ **(ii) Energy stored in the flywheel at mean speed:** $\omega_{\text{avg}} = 2\pi(200)/60 = 20\pi/3$ rad/s. $$E = \dfrac{1}{2}J\omega_{\text{avg}}^{2} = \dfrac{1}{2}(300.8)\left(\dfrac{20\pi}{3}\right)^{2} = \dfrac{1}{2}(300.8)\left(\dfrac{400\pi^{2}}{9}\right)$$ $$E = (150.4)(139.626) \approx 21\,000 \text{ J}$$ $$\boxed{E \approx 21.0 \text{ kJ}}$$ --- ## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places where KTU students lose marks in Load Equalization problems:** > 1. **± notation confusion:** When the problem states “±10 % speed variation”, students must compute **total swing** $\Delta N = 0.20 \times N_{\text{mean}}$, **not** $0.10 \times N_{\text{mean}}$. This single mistake costs **2–3 marks** instantly. > 2. **Forgetting to refer torques/inertia to the motor shaft** when a gear train is present. $T_{\text{motor}} = T_{\text{load}}/K$ and $J_{\text{motor}} = J_{\text{load}}/K^{2}$. > 3. **Incorrect units in the $J$ formula:** $t$ must be in seconds, $N$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m. Mixing SI with CGS loses marks. > 4. **Writing energy balance as $\Delta E = (T_{L1} - T_{m})\,t_{1}$ (missing $\omega_{\text{avg}}$)** — this is dimensionally inconsistent. Always include $\omega_{\text{avg}}$ explicitly in the derivation step. > 5. **Not stating the assumption** that the motor torque is constant — examiners specifically allocate marks for this in 7-mark derivations. --- ## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** Load equalization uses a **flywheel** to decouple the motor from a fluctuating load, so the motor delivers near-constant average torque. - **Energy storage:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2}J\omega^{2}$ — the flywheel is a kinetic-energy buffer. - **Two phases:** - *Peak load:* $T_{L} > T_{m}$ → flywheel **releases** KE, speed **falls**. - *Light load:* $T_{L} < T_{m}$ → flywheel **stores** KE, speed **rises**. - **Motor torque formula:** $T_{m} = \dfrac{T_{L1}\,t_{1} + T_{L2}\,t_{2}}{t_{1} + t_{2}}$ — the time-weighted average. - **Flywheel inertia formula:** $J = \dfrac{(T_{L1} - T_{m})\,t_{1} \times 60}{2\pi\,\Delta N}$ where $\Delta N$ is **total** speed swing. - **Speed conversion:** $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$; $\Delta\omega = 2\pi \Delta N / 60$. - **Gear-train reflection:** $T_{\text{motor}} = T_{\text{load}}/K$, $J_{\text{motor}} = J_{\text{load}}/K^{2}$. - **Most effective with series motors** because their large speed drop releases more KE. - **Typical applications:** rolling mills, mine hoists, cranes, punch presses, locomotives, reciprocating compressors. - **Modern alternative:** In modern EV drives, regenerative braking and supercapacitor/battery banks replace the mechanical flywheel for load equalization.
